topics.title: Spectral Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET617 - SOLAR ENERGY CONSERVATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Spectral Distribution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Spectral Distribution** (also called *Spectral Power Distribution* or *Spectral Irradiance Distribution*) is defined as the variation of the radiant energy flux emitted or received per unit area per unit wavelength interval as a function of wavelength across the electromagnetic spectrum. In the context of solar energy engineering, it describes how the sun's emitted power is partitioned among different wavelengths ranging from ultraviolet (UV) through visible light to infrared (IR).

Mathematically, spectral irradiance is expressed as:

$$E_\lambda = \frac{dE}{d\lambda} \quad \left[\frac{W}{m^2 \cdot \mu m}\right]$$

The total (broadband) irradiance is obtained by integrating over all wavelengths:

$$E = \int_{0}^{\infty} E_\lambda \, d\lambda \quad \left[\frac{W}{m^2}\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Spectral distribution is the *foundation* upon which all photovoltaic (PV) cell performance, selective absorber design, and solar thermal collector efficiency calculations are built. Without understanding which wavelengths carry how much energy, we cannot rationally select semiconductor bandgaps or absorber coatings.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Rainbow Through a Prism

Imagine holding a triangular glass prism up to a beam of sunlight on a white wall. You do not see a single white spot — instead, you see a beautiful rainbow stretching from violet to red. This simple experiment reveals a profound truth: **white sunlight is actually a mixture of many colours (wavelengths), each carrying a different amount of energy**.

If we could place an ultra-precise energy meter at every millimetre of that rainbow, we could plot a graph — wavelength on the horizontal axis, energy per wavelength on the vertical axis. That graph **IS** the solar spectral distribution curve.

Think of it this way:
- The **sun** is like a giant kettle boiling at **5,778 K** (its effective surface temperature).
- Like a heated metal rod that glows red, then orange, then white as it gets hotter, the sun emits radiation across a *continuous spectrum*.
- The spectral distribution tells us *exactly* how hot each "colour" of the kettle's glow is.

### 1.3 Key Spectral Bands (Wavelength Ranges)

| Spectral Region | Wavelength Range | % of Solar Energy | Engineering Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ultraviolet (UV) | < 0.4 µm | ~ 7 % | Causes PV degradation; ozone absorbs most UV-C |
| Visible Light | 0.4 – 0.7 µm | ~ 43 % | Matched by silicon bandgap (1.12 eV); peak human eye sensitivity |
| Near-Infrared (NIR) | 0.7 – 2.5 µm | ~ 37 % | Major contributor to PV thermal heating losses |
| Far-Infrared (FIR) | > 2.5 µm | ~ 13 % | Absorbed by atmospheric water vapour and CO₂ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Solar Reference Spectra Used in KTU Curriculum:**
> - **AM0 (Air Mass Zero):** Extraterrestrial radiation above Earth's atmosphere — used for satellite and space solar cell design.
> - **AM1.5G (Global):** Standard terrestrial spectrum at solar zenith angle of 48.19° — used for flat-plate PV testing.
> - **AM1.5D (Direct):** Direct-beam component only — used for concentrator PV systems.
> - **AM2 (Direct + circumsolar):** Common for tropical locations including Kerala.

### 1.4 The Two Fundamental Spectral Curves a KTU Student Must Know

1. **The Blackbody Spectral Distribution Curve** — what the sun *would* emit if it were a perfect blackbody at 5,778 K.
2. **The Extraterrestrial Solar Spectral Distribution Curve (AM0)** — what the sun *actually* emits at the top of Earth's atmosphere, measured by satellites (e.g., NREL ASTM G173 data).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two curves are nearly identical except for fine absorption lines (Fraunhofer lines) caused by gases in the sun's outer atmosphere (chromosphere). For most engineering calculations, treating the sun as a 5,778 K blackbody gives acceptable accuracy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Blackbody Radiation Physics

A **blackbody** is an idealized object that absorbs all incident radiation and re-emits energy purely as a function of its thermodynamic temperature. The sun, to a remarkably good first approximation, behaves as a blackbody radiator.

The spectral distribution of radiation from a blackbody is governed by three monumental laws of 20th-century physics — each derived by a different physicist, each carrying KTU examination weight:

#### 2.1.1 Planck's Law of Blackbody Radiation (1900)

Max Planck, forced to abandon classical physics, postulated that energy is quantized in discrete packets called *photons*. The resulting spectral emissive power per unit wavelength from a blackbody at temperature $T$ is:

$$E_{b\lambda}(T) = \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{\frac{hc}{\lambda k_B T}} - 1} \quad \left[\frac{W}{m^2 \cdot m}\right]$$

**Why each term exists:**
- $\frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5}$ — the classical Rayleigh-Jeans factor multiplied by quantum correction factor denominator structure.
- $e^{\frac{hc}{\lambda k_B T}} - 1$ — the **Bose-Einstein distribution** describing how photons populate available energy states.
- When $hc \gg \lambda k_B T$ (short wavelength / low temperature limit), the exponential dominates and $E_{b\lambda} \to 0$ — explaining the famous **"ultraviolet catastrophe"** that classical physics could not explain.
- When $hc \ll \lambda k_B T$ (long wavelength / high temperature limit), the exponential approximates $\frac{hc}{\lambda k_B T}$ and we recover Rayleigh-Jeans law: $E_{b\lambda} \approx \frac{2\pi c k_B T}{\lambda^4}$.

#### 2.1.2 Wien's Displacement Law (1893)

By differentiating Planck's law with respect to $\lambda$ and setting the derivative to zero, the wavelength at which a blackbody emits maximum spectral radiance is found:

$$\lambda_{max} \cdot T = 2.8978 \times 10^{-3} \, m \cdot K$$

This is one of the most heavily-tested formulas in KTU examinations.

**Application to the Sun:**
For $T_{sun} = 5778 \, K$:

$$\lambda_{max} = \frac{2.8978 \times 10^{-3}}{5778} = 0.5015 \, \mu m \approx 0.5 \, \mu m$$

This falls in the **green-yellow region** of visible light — a direct prediction confirmed by spectroscopy.

#### 2.1.3 Stefan-Boltzmann Law (1879 / 1884)

Integrating Planck's spectral distribution over all wavelengths from $0$ to $\infty$ gives the total hemispherical emissive power:

$$E_b = \sigma T^4 \quad \left[\frac{W}{m^2}\right]$$

where $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \, W/m^2 K^4$ is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a sphere (the sun) emitting uniformly from its entire surface, the total power radiated is $P = 4\pi R^2 \sigma T^4$. By conservation of energy and the inverse-square law, this equals the flux received at Earth's distance times the spherical surface area at that distance. This is the basis of the **Solar Constant derivation**.

### 2.2 From Blackbody to Real Solar Spectrum

The actual sun is not a perfect blackbody. Three modifications occur:

1. **Fraunhofer Absorption Lines** — narrow dips at specific wavelengths (e.g., H-α at 656.3 nm) caused by cooler gases in the sun's photosphere absorbing specific wavelengths.
2. **Solar Limb Darkening** — the sun's edges appear cooler than its centre, so the integrated disc emission is slightly cooler than the local effective temperature.
3. **Sunspots and Solar Cycles** — produce ~0.1 % variation in total irradiance over the 11-year solar cycle.

For solar energy engineering applications, the sun is treated as a blackbody at $T_{eff} = 5778 \, K$ with a total radiated power approximated using the **Solar Constant**.

### 2.3 Solar Constant (Gsc) Derivation Framework

The Solar Constant $G_{sc}$ is defined as the total solar electromagnetic radiation (per unit area) incident on a surface perpendicular to the sun's rays at the **top of Earth's atmosphere**, at the **mean Earth–Sun distance** (1 Astronomical Unit = $1.496 \times 10^{11} \, m$).

$$G_{sc} = \sigma T_{eff}^4 \cdot \left(\frac{R_{sun}}{D_{ES}}\right)^2$$

where:
- $R_{sun} = 6.96 \times 10^8 \, m$ (solar radius)
- $D_{ES} = 1.496 \times 10^{11} \, m$ (mean Earth-Sun distance)
- $T_{eff} = 5778 \, K$

Numerically, $G_{sc} \approx \mathbf{1367 \, W/m^2}$ (older KTU textbooks cite 1353 W/m²; current accepted value from WRC is 1361 W/m²).

### 2.4 Air Mass Concept

As sunlight traverses Earth's atmosphere, it is:
- **Absorbed** by ozone (UV), water vapour (IR), and CO₂ (IR).
- **Scattered** by molecules (Rayleigh scattering — blue sky) and aerosols (Mie scattering).

The path length is characterized by the **Air Mass (AM)** number:

$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos(\theta_z)}$$

where $\theta_z$ is the solar zenith angle (angle from vertical).

- AM0 = 0 (no atmosphere, satellite)
- AM1 = 1 (sun directly overhead at sea level)
- AM1.5 = 1/cos(48.19°) ≈ 1.5 (standard for PV testing)

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Spectral Distribution

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units / Constants |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Planck's Law | $E_{b\lambda} = \dfrac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5} \cdot \dfrac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda k_B T} - 1}$ | $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \, J \cdot s$ |
| 2 | Wien's Displacement | $\lambda_{max} T = 2.8978 \times 10^{-3}$ | $m \cdot K$ |
| 3 | Stefan-Boltzmann | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \, W/m^2K^4$ |
| 4 | Solar Constant | $G_{sc} = \sigma T^4 \left(\dfrac{R_{sun}}{D_{ES}}\right)^2$ | $\approx 1367 \, W/m^2$ |
| 5 | Air Mass | $AM = 1/\cos\theta_z$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Spectral Irradiance | $E_\lambda = dE/d\lambda$ | $W/m^2/\mu m$ |
| 7 | Total Irradiance | $E = \int_0^\infty E_\lambda \, d\lambda$ | $W/m^2$ |
| 8 | Rayleigh-Jeans Limit | $E_{b\lambda} \approx 2\pi c k_B T / \lambda^4$ | long-$\lambda$ approximation |
| 9 | Wien's Limit | $E_{b\lambda} \approx (2\pi h c^2/\lambda^5) e^{-hc/\lambda k_B T}$ | short-$\lambda$ approximation |
| 10 | Fraction Emitted Below $\lambda$ | $F_{0\to\lambda} = \dfrac{\int_0^\lambda E_{b\lambda} d\lambda}{\sigma T^4}$ | dimensionless |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding spectral distribution is **NOT** optional — it directly determines:

- **PV Cell Selection:** Crystalline silicon (Eg = 1.12 eV, $\lambda_{cutoff} \approx 1.1 \, \mu m$) responds primarily to visible + near-IR. Gallium arsenide (Eg = 1.42 eV) responds to a wider range including UV. Multi-junction cells stack materials of different bandgaps to absorb different spectral bands.
- **Solar Thermal Absorbers:** Selective coatings (e.g., black chrome, cermet) have high absorptance ($\alpha$) in 0.3–2.5 µm range but low emittance ($\varepsilon$) in IR, minimizing radiative losses.
- **Greenhouse Design:** Polyethylene films transmit visible but absorb IR, trapping heat.
- **Building Integrated PV (BIPV):** Semi-transparent PV cells selectively transmit portions of the spectrum for daylighting while generating electricity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Peak Wavelength from Wien's Displacement Law

We start with Planck's law and differentiate with respect to $\lambda$, setting $\frac{dE_{b\lambda}}{d\lambda} = 0$ to find the maximum.

**Step 1.** Write Planck's law explicitly:

$$E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T) = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1}$$

where $C_1 = 2\pi h c^2$ and $C_2 = h c / k_B$.

**Step 2.** Let $x = \frac{C_2}{\lambda T}$ (dimensionless). Then $\lambda = \frac{C_2}{x T}$ and:

$$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{C_1 x^5 T^5}{C_2^5 (e^x - 1)}$$

**Step 3.** Differentiate $E_{b\lambda}$ with respect to $x$ (treating $T$ as constant since we are looking for the maximum at fixed $T$):

$$\frac{dE_{b\lambda}}{dx} = \frac{C_1 T^5}{C_2^5} \cdot \frac{d}{dx}\left[\frac{x^5}{e^x - 1}\right]$$

**Step 4.** Apply the quotient rule:

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left[\frac{x^5}{e^x - 1}\right] = \frac{5x^4(e^x - 1) - x^5 e^x}{(e^x - 1)^2} = \frac{x^4}{e^x - 1}\left[5 - \frac{x \, e^x}{e^x - 1}\right]$$

**Step 5.** Setting the derivative to zero (and noting $x^4/(e^x-1) \neq 0$):

$$5(e^x - 1) - x e^x = 0$$

$$5 e^x - 5 - x e^x = 0$$

$$e^x(5 - x) = 5$$

**Step 6.** Solve the transcendental equation numerically. Try $x = 4.965$:

$$e^{4.965} \cdot (5 - 4.965) = 142.81 \times 0.035 \approx 4.998 \approx 5 \checkmark$$

So $x_{max} = 4.965114...$

**Step 7.** Convert back to wavelength:

$$\lambda_{max} = \frac{C_2}{x_{max} T} = \frac{h c / k_B}{4.965114 \cdot T}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{max} \cdot T = \frac{h c}{4.965114 \, k_B} = 2.8978 \times 10^{-3} \, m \cdot K}$$

### 3.2 Derivation B — Solar Constant from Stefan-Boltzmann Law

**Given:**
- Sun's effective surface temperature: $T_{sun} = 5778 \, K$
- Sun's radius: $R_{sun} = 6.96 \times 10^8 \, m$
- Mean Earth-Sun distance: $D_{ES} = 1.496 \times 10^{11} \, m$
- Stefan-Boltzmann constant: $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \, W/m^2 K^4$

**Step 1.** Total power emitted by the sun's surface (treating it as a blackbody sphere):

$$P_{sun} = 4\pi R_{sun}^2 \cdot \sigma T_{sun}^4$$

**Step 2.** At a distance $D_{ES}$, this power is distributed uniformly over a spherical surface of area $4\pi D_{ES}^2$:

$$G_{sc} = \frac{P_{sun}}{4\pi D_{ES}^2} = \frac{4\pi R_{sun}^2 \cdot \sigma T_{sun}^4}{4\pi D_{ES}^2}$$

**Step 3.** Cancel common factors:

$$G_{sc} = \sigma T_{sun}^4 \left(\frac{R_{sun}}{D_{ES}}\right)^2$$

**Step 4.** Evaluate the geometric ratio:

$$\frac{R_{sun}}{D_{ES}} = \frac{6.96 \times 10^8}{1.496 \times 10^{11}} = 4.652 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\left(\frac{R_{sun}}{D_{ES}}\right)^2 = 2.164 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 5.** Compute $T_{sun}^4$:

$$T_{sun}^4 = (5778)^4 = 1.114 \times 10^{15} \, K^4$$

**Step 6.** Multiply all factors:

$$\begin{aligned}
G_{sc} &= (5.670 \times 10^{-8}) \times (1.114 \times 10^{15}) \times (2.164 \times 10^{-5}) \\
&= 6.316 \times 10^7 \times 2.164 \times 10^{-5} \\
&= 1366.8 \, W/m^2
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{G_{sc} \approx 1367 \, W/m^2}$$

### 3.3 Derivation C — Spectral Distribution Curve Sketch (Analytical Reasoning)

To sketch the spectral distribution curve, evaluate Planck's law at key wavelengths. For $T = 5778 \, K$:

| Wavelength $\lambda$ (µm) | Spectral Irradiance $E_\lambda$ (W/m²/µm) | Region |
|---|---|---|
| 0.2 | ~ 0.5 | Deep UV |
| 0.4 | ~ 950 | Violet–UV boundary |
| 0.5 | ~ 2110 | Peak (green-yellow) |
| 0.7 | ~ 1480 | Red boundary |
| 1.0 | ~ 740 | Near-IR |
| 2.0 | ~ 130 | Mid-IR |
| 5.0 | ~ 5 | Far-IR |

The curve:
- Rises sharply from near-zero at short UV wavelengths.
- Reaches maximum at $\lambda_{max} = 0.5 \, \mu m$.
- Decays asymptotically (but more slowly than rise) toward zero in the IR.
- Total area under the curve equals $\sigma T^4 / \pi = 1.74 \times 10^7 \, W/m^2$ for the sun's surface, or $1367 \, W/m^2$ at Earth.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Patterns

| Problem Type | Key Formula Given | What to Find | Common Mistake |
|---|---|---|---|
| Peak wavelength of sun | $T = 5778 \, K$ | $\lambda_{max}$ | Forgetting $T$ must be in Kelvin |
| Temperature of a star | Given $\lambda_{max}$ | $T$ | Using nm instead of m |
| Solar constant derivation | $T$, $R$, $D$ | $G_{sc}$ | Forgetting $\left(\frac{R}{D}\right)^2$ factor |
| Power emitted per unit area | $T$ | $\sigma T^4$ | Confusing with $G_{sc}$ |
| Air mass calculation | $\theta_z$ | $AM$ | Forgetting $\theta_z$ is from vertical |
| Fraction below wavelength | $\lambda T$ | Use table / integrals | Wrong lookup tables |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Sample KTU Pattern

**Problem:** A blackbody surface at 1,200 K is used in a solar furnace. Calculate (a) the wavelength at which maximum emission occurs, and (b) the total emissive power.

**Solution (a):**

$$\lambda_{max} = \frac{2.8978 \times 10^{-3}}{1200} = 2.415 \times 10^{-6} \, m = 2.415 \, \mu m$$

**Solution (b):**

$$E_b = \sigma T^4 = (5.670 \times 10^{-8})(1200)^4$$

$$= (5.670 \times 10^{-8})(2.0736 \times 10^{12})$$

$$\boxed{E_b = 117{,}573 \, W/m^2 \approx 117.6 \, kW/m^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the peak emission is in the **near-infrared** (~2.4 µm), which is why solar furnaces are designed with selective surfaces that absorb strongly in this range.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Solar Spectral Distribution Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B --> C["FRAUNHOFER LINES AND SOLAR ABSORPTION"]

    D --> E["ATMOSPHERIC INTERACTION LAYER"]

    E --> G["MIE SCATTERING BY AEROSOLS"]

    E --> I["WATER VAPOUR ABSORPTION IR BAND"]
    E --> J["CO2 ABSORPTION FAR IR BAND"]

    H --> K
    I --> K

    M --> N
    N --> O["USEFUL ENERGY CONVERSION"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    SRC --> VIS["VISIBLE LIGHT 0.4 TO 0.7 UM ABOUT 588 W PER M2"]
    SRC --> NIR["NEAR INFRARED 0.7 TO 2.5 UM ABOUT 506 W PER M2"]
    SRC --> FIR["FAR INFRARED ABOVE 2.5 UM ABOUT 178 W PER M2"]
    VIS --> PK["PEAK AT 0.5 UM GREEN YELLOW"]
    UV --> O3["ABSORBED BY OZONE STRATOSPHERIC HEATING"]
    NIR --> SI["ABSORBED BY SILICON PV CELLS 1.12 EV BANDGAP"]
    FIR --> WV["ABSORBED BY WATER VAPOUR GREENHOUSE EFFECT"]
```

```mermaid

    P["SPECTRAL DISTRIBUTION ANALYSIS"] --> P1["PHOTOVOLTAIC ENGINEERING"]
    P --> P2["SOLAR THERMAL ENGINEERING"]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — From Blackbody Concept to PV Output

| Stage | Physical Phenomenon | Mathematical Description | Engineering Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Source | Sun as blackbody at 5778 K | Planck's law | Spectral distribution curve |
| 2. Extraterrestrial | Top of atmosphere flux | $G_{sc}$ from Stefan-Boltzmann | 1367 W/m² reference |
| 3. Atmospheric | Scattering + absorption | Beer-Lambert law | AM1.5 spectrum |
| 4. Cell Response | Material bandgap absorption | $\lambda < \lambda_g = 1.24/E_g$ | Short-circuit current |
| 5. Output | Electrical work | $P = V \times I$ | Useful power |

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
Define the term *spectral distribution of solar radiation*. State Wien's displacement law and calculate the wavelength of maximum emission for the sun, assuming its surface temperature as 5,778 K.

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Spectral distribution of solar radiation describes the variation of solar energy per unit area per unit wavelength interval as a function of wavelength across the electromagnetic spectrum. It quantifies how the sun's radiative power is distributed among different wavelengths ranging from ultraviolet through visible to infrared.
>
> **Wien's Displacement Law:** The product of the absolute temperature of a blackbody and the wavelength at which it emits maximum spectral radiance is a constant:
>
> $$\lambda_{max} \cdot T = 2.8978 \times 10^{-3} \, m \cdot K$$
>
> **Calculation:**
>
> $$\lambda_{max} = \frac{2.8978 \times 10^{-3}}{5778} = 5.015 \times 10^{-7} \, m$$
>
> $$\boxed{\lambda_{max} \approx 0.50 \, \mu m}$$
>
> This corresponds to green-yellow light in the visible spectrum.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
Distinguish between *Air Mass Zero (AM0)* and *Air Mass 1.5 (AM1.5)* spectral distributions. Mention two applications where each is preferred.

**Model Answer:**

> | Aspect | AM0 | AM1.5 |
> |---|---|---|
> | Path | Above Earth's atmosphere (no atmospheric attenuation) | Through atmosphere at zenith angle 48.19° |
> | Total irradiance | 1367 W/m² | 1000 W/m² (standardized) |
> | Spectral shape | Smooth blackbody-like with Fraunhofer dips | Distorted by atmospheric absorption bands (H₂O, CO₂, O₃) |
> | Preferred for | Space solar cells, satellites, interplanetary missions | Terrestrial flat-plate PV systems, BIPV |
>
> **Applications:**
> - **AM0:** Design of solar arrays for satellites (e.g., ISRO's GSAT series), lunar/planetary rovers.
> - **AM1.5:** Performance testing of commercial PV modules, rooftop system design, solar resource assessment in Kerala.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for spectral distribution of radiation emitted by a blackbody at temperature T using Planck's law. Explain how Rayleigh-Jeans law and Wien's law are derived as limiting cases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A black surface at 1,500 K is used as a solar absorber. Calculate:
- (i) The wavelength at which maximum spectral emissive power occurs.
- (ii) The total emissive power per unit area.
- (iii) The spectral emissive power at $\lambda = 2 \, \mu m$.
- Given: $C_1 = 2\pi h c^2 = 3.742 \times 10^8 \, W \mu m^4 / m^2$, $C_2 = hc/k_B = 1.439 \times 10^4 \, \mu m \cdot K$ **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation Outline (7 marks):**

Planck postulated that electromagnetic energy is emitted and absorbed in discrete quanta of magnitude $E = h\nu$. The average energy of a quantum oscillator is given by the Bose-Einstein distribution:

$$\langle E \rangle = \frac{h\nu}{e^{h\nu/k_B T} - 1}$$

The number of electromagnetic modes per unit volume per unit frequency interval is $\frac{8\pi \nu^2}{c^3}$. Multiplying by $\langle E \rangle \cdot c/4$ (the speed factor and isotropy factor for radiation leaving the surface):

$$E_{b\nu} = \frac{2\pi h \nu^3}{c^2} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{h\nu/k_B T} - 1}$$

Converting from frequency to wavelength form ($\nu = c/\lambda$, $d\nu = -c/\lambda^2 \, d\lambda$):

$$\boxed{E_{b\lambda} = \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda k_B T} - 1}}$$

**Limiting Cases:**

- **Rayleigh-Jeans Limit** ($\lambda T \gg hc/k_B$): The exponential denominator $\to hc/\lambda k_B T$:

$$E_{b\lambda} \approx \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5} \cdot \frac{\lambda k_B T}{hc} = \frac{2\pi c k_B T}{\lambda^4}$$

Valid for long wavelengths (microwave, radio).

- **Wien's Limit** ($\lambda T \ll hc/k_B$): The denominator $\approx e^{hc/\lambda k_B T}$:

$$E_{b\lambda} \approx \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5} \cdot e^{-hc/\lambda k_B T}$$

Valid for short wavelengths (UV, X-ray).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Peak wavelength:**

$$\lambda_{max} = \frac{2.8978 \times 10^{-3}}{1500} = 1.932 \times 10^{-6} \, m = 1.932 \, \mu m$$

**(ii) Total emissive power:**

$$E_b = \sigma T^4 = (5.670 \times 10^{-8})(1500)^4 = (5.670 \times 10^{-8})(5.0625 \times 10^{12})$$

$$E_b = 2.870 \times 10^5 \, W/m^2 \approx 287.0 \, kW/m^2$$

**(iii) Spectral emissive power at $\lambda = 2 \, \mu m$:**

$$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left(e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1\right)}$$

Compute exponent:

$$\frac{C_2}{\lambda T} = \frac{1.439 \times 10^4}{2 \times 1500} = \frac{14390}{3000} = 4.797$$

$$e^{4.797} = 121.5$$

$$e^{4.797} - 1 = 120.5$$

$$\lambda^5 = (2)^5 = 32 \, \mu m^5$$

$$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{3.742 \times 10^8}{32 \times 120.5} = \frac{3.742 \times 10^8}{3856} = 9.704 \times 10^4 \, W/m^2/\mu m$$

$$\boxed{E_{b\lambda}(2 \, \mu m) \approx 9.70 \times 10^4 \, W/m^2/\mu m}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of *Solar Constant*. Derive its expression using Stefan-Boltzmann law. Given that the solar radius is $6.96 \times 10^8 \, m$ and the mean Earth-Sun distance is $1.496 \times 10^{11} \, m$, compute the value of the solar constant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define *Air Mass*. Explain how the solar spectral distribution is modified as it passes through Earth's atmosphere. Sketch the AM0 and AM1.5 spectral distribution curves and indicate key absorption bands. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Solar Constant Derivation (7 marks):**

**Definition:** The Solar Constant ($G_{sc}$) is the total solar electromagnetic radiation (per unit area per unit time) incident on a surface perpendicular to the sun's rays at the top of Earth's atmosphere, at the mean Earth-Sun distance.

**Derivation:**

Step 1 — Total power emitted by the sun (assumed blackbody):

$$P_{sun} = 4\pi R_{sun}^2 \cdot \sigma T_{sun}^4$$

Step 2 — At Earth, this power is uniformly distributed over a sphere of radius $D_{ES}$:

$$G_{sc} = \frac{P_{sun}}{4\pi D_{ES}^2} = \sigma T_{sun}^4 \left(\frac{R_{sun}}{D_{ES}}\right)^2$$

Step 3 — Substitute values ($T_{sun} = 5778 \, K$):

$$\left(\frac{R_{sun}}{D_{ES}}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{6.96 \times 10^8}{1.496 \times 10^{11}}\right)^2 = (4.652 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 2.164 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$T_{sun}^4 = (5778)^4 = 1.114 \times 10^{15} \, K^4$$

$$G_{sc} = (5.670 \times 10^{-8})(1.114 \times 10^{15})(2.164 \times 10^{-5})$$

$$G_{sc} = 6.316 \times 10^7 \times 2.164 \times 10^{-5} \approx 1367 \, W/m^2$$

$$\boxed{G_{sc} \approx 1367 \, W/m^2}$$

**(b) Air Mass and Atmospheric Modification (7 marks):**

**Definition:** Air Mass (AM) is the ratio of the actual path length of solar radiation through the atmosphere to the path length when the sun is directly overhead at sea level.

$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z}$$

where $\theta_z$ is the solar zenith angle.

**Atmospheric Modifications:**

1. **Ozone absorption** in UV-B (280–315 nm) and UV-C (< 280 nm) regions.
2. **Water vapour absorption** in near-IR bands around 0.94, 1.1, 1.4, 1.9 µm.
3. **CO₂ absorption** in mid-IR around 2.0 µm and far-IR around 15 µm.
4. **Rayleigh scattering** by air molecules — stronger at shorter wavelengths (blue sky).
5. **Mie scattering** by aerosols and dust particles — wavelength-independent.

**Spectral Distribution Curves:**

- **AM0 Spectrum:** Smooth blackbody-like curve peaking near 0.5 µm, with sharp Fraunhofer absorption lines. Total irradiance = 1367 W/m².
- **AM1.5 Spectrum:** Distorted curve with deep absorption valleys in water vapour bands. Total irradiance normalized to 1000 W/m².

**Key Absorption Bands:**
- Below 0.3 µm: completely absorbed by ozone and oxygen.
- 0.7–0.8 µm: weak H₂O absorption.
- 0.9–1.0 µm: strong H₂O band.
- 1.4 µm: very strong H₂O band.
- 1.85 µm: H₂O band.
- 2.0 µm: CO₂ + H₂O band.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert temperature to Kelvin** — A common error: using 5778 °C instead of 5778 K leads to a 300x error in Stefan-Boltzmann calculations.
> 2. **Mixing up wavelength vs. frequency form of Planck's law** — When using $E_{b\nu}$ vs $E_{b\lambda}$, the constants differ by factors of $c$. Always check which form the question expects.
> 3. **Confusing spectral emissive power $E_{b\lambda}$ with total emissive power $E_b$** — The former has units of W/m²/µm, the latter W/m². Don't write the wrong unit in the final answer.
> 4. **Air mass angle confusion** — $\theta_z$ is measured from the **vertical**, not the horizontal. Many students mistakenly use the elevation angle, off by 90°.
> 5. **Missing the $\left(\frac{R}{D}\right)^2$ factor** — In solar constant derivations, students often write $G_{sc} = \sigma T^4$ forgetting the geometric dilution factor, getting an answer 50,000 times too large.
> 6. **Incorrect identification of solar peak** — The sun's peak is NOT in the yellow region; it is in the **green** region (0.5 µm). The sun appears yellow only because Earth's atmosphere scatters blue light away.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Spectral Distribution = Energy per unit wavelength** vs. wavelength; units of $W/m^2/\mu m$.
- [ ] **Sun's effective temperature** = $T_{eff} = 5778 \, K$ (treat as blackbody).
- [ ] **Solar Constant** $G_{sc} \approx 1367 \, W/m^2$ — at top of atmosphere, perpendicular incidence, at 1 AU.
- [ ] **Three Blackbody Laws to memorize:**
  - Planck's law: full spectral shape
  - Wien's law: $\lambda_{max} T = 2.8978 \times 10^{-3} \, m \cdot K$
  - Stefan-Boltzmann: $E_b = \sigma T^4$
- [ ] **Peak solar emission** occurs at $\approx 0.5 \, \mu m$ (green light).
- [ ] **Visible + NIR + UV** are the dominant solar energy bands; far-IR is mostly atmospheric.
- [ ] **Air Mass AM0** = satellite/space; **AM1.5** = terrestrial standard; AM = $1/\cos\theta_z$.
- [ ] **Fraunhofer lines** are absorption features from solar atmosphere gases.
- [ ] **Ozone** absorbs UV; **water vapour** absorbs IR; **CO₂** absorbs far-IR.
- [ ] **Rayleigh scattering** is responsible for blue sky and red sunsets.
- [ ] **Rayleigh-Jeans** approximation valid at long $\lambda$; **Wien** approximation valid at short $\lambda$.
- [ ] **Engineering links:** PV bandgap selection, selective absorbers, greenhouse films, BIPV.
- [ ] **Constants to memorize:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \, J \cdot s$; $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, W/m^2K^4$; $c = 3 \times 10^8 \, m/s$; $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \, J/K$.
- [ ] **KTU Numerical Pattern:** (i) find $\lambda_{max}$, (ii) compute $E_b$, (iii) compute $E_{b\lambda}$ at given $\lambda$.
- [ ] **KTU Theory Pattern:** Compare blackbody vs. real solar spectrum; explain AM0 vs AM1.5.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sun-Earth Relationship
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET617 - SOLAR ENERGY CONSERVATION SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Sun-Earth Relationship ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Sun–Earth Relationship** refers to the geometric and kinematic description of the relative position of the Sun with respect to an observer on Earth at any given instant of time and date. It is the foundational framework of *Solar Geometry* and *Solar Astronomy*, upon which the quantification of incident solar radiation (insolation), sizing of photovoltaic (PV) arrays, design of solar thermal collectors, and estimation of building heating/cooling loads are entirely dependent. The relationship is mathematically expressed through a set of **solar angles** that together fully describe the Sun's position in the observer's celestial hemisphere. These angles are: - **Solar Declination Angle (δ)** – the angular position of the Sun with respect to the equatorial plane of the Earth. - **Latitude Angle (φ)** – the angular position of the observer north or south of the equator. - **Hour Angle (ω)** – the angular displacement of the Sun east or west of the local meridian due to Earth's rotation. - **Solar Altitude Angle (α)** – the angle between the Sun's rays and the horizontal plane at the observer's location. - **Solar Zenith Angle (θ_z)** – the angle between the Sun's rays and the vertical (normal to horizontal plane). - **Solar Azimuth Angle (γ_s)** – the angle measured on the horizontal plane from the projected direction of due south to the projection of the Sun's rays on the horizontal plane. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight (Module 1 PLO):** Mastery of the Sun–Earth geometric relationship is mandatory prerequisite knowledge for Modules 2 (Solar Radiation Measurement), 3 (PV Systems), and 4 (Solar Thermal Systems). Numerical problems in the End Semester Examination (ESE) frequently test the conversion between these angles. ### 1.2 The Standard Physical Constant > [!NOTE] > **Solar Constant (G_sc):** The rate at which solar energy is received per unit area on a surface perpendicular to the Sun's rays at the *mean* distance of the Earth from the Sun, outside the Earth's atmosphere. > **G_sc = 1367 W/m²** (older WMO standard) or **G_sc = 1361 W/m²** (NASA SORCE satellite updated value). > The KTU-recommended textbook value is **G_sc = 1367 W/m²**. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition Imagine a large ceiling lamp (the Sun) fixed at the center of a room, and you hold a tilted globe (the Earth) that spins on its axis while slowly walking in a slightly oval path around the lamp. As you walk around the room: - The **shape of the oval path** decides how close or far the globe gets to the lamp — this gives us the **eccentricity** and the changing **Earth-Sun distance**. - The **tilt of the globe's axis** (23.45°) decides which hemisphere faces the lamp more directly — this is what causes the **seasons**. - The **spinning of the globe** means different parts of its surface face the lamp at different times of the day — this gives us the **hour angle**. - The **direction you are standing on the globe** (north or south of the equator) decides how high the lamp appears in your sky — this is the **latitude**. Therefore, to know exactly how much lamp-light (solar energy) hits a particular wall or roof, you must know the **geometry** of where the lamp appears from your position. That geometry is the Sun–Earth relationship. ### 1.4 Earth's Orbital Parameters | Parameter | Symbol | Value | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Mean Earth–Sun distance | D | 1.496 × 10⁸ km | 1 Astronomical Unit (AU) | | Eccentricity of orbit | e | 0.0167 | Causes ~±3.3 % variation in G_sc | | Axial tilt (obliquity) | ε | 23.45° | Angle between equatorial & orbital planes | | Rotation period | – | 24 h (sidereal: 23.934 h) | Drives diurnal solar motion | | Revolution period | – | 365.25 days | Drives seasonal variation | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Five Step-by-Step Logical Pillars of Sun–Earth Geometry **Pillar 1 — Establish the Observer's Frame of Reference (Latitude φ)** The observer is fixed on the Earth's surface. Their latitude φ (positive in the Northern Hemisphere, negative in the Southern Hemisphere) sets the local horizon and the angle of the local vertical with respect to Earth's rotation axis. **Pillar 2 — Determine the Date (Day Number 'n')** All seasonal angles depend on the day of the year. By convention, **n = 1 on January 1st** and **n = 365 on December 31st**. The day number fixes the Sun's declination. **Pillar 3 — Compute the Declination Angle (δ)** The declination angle δ describes the angular position of the Sun north or south of the equator. It varies between **−23.45° and +23.45°** over the year and is **zero at the equinoxes** (≈ March 21 and September 23). **Pillar 4 — Convert Clock Time to Solar Time (Hour Angle ω)** Because the Earth rotates 360° in 24 hours, the Sun moves **15° per hour** across the sky. The hour angle ω is **zero at solar noon**, **negative in the morning**, and **positive in the afternoon**. $$\omega = 15° \times (t_{solar} - 12)$$ where $t_{solar}$ is the solar time in hours. **Pillar 5 — Resolve the Sun's 3D Position in the Sky (Altitude α & Azimuth γ_s)** Using spherical trigonometry, the three parameters (φ, δ, ω) are combined to resolve the Sun's position in the local coordinate system via the **fundamental solar geometry equations**. ### 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility The Sun–Earth relationship is not abstract astronomy — it is the **daily working tool** of: - **Solar PV plant designers** — computing tilt angle, inter-row spacing, and module orientation. - **Solar thermal engineers** — sizing flat-plate and concentrating collectors. - **HVAC and building energy modelers** — computing solar heat gain through glazing. - **Agricultural engineers** — designing greenhouses and crop-drying systems. - **Astronomical clock & sundial designers** — converting standard time to true solar time. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Range / Boundary | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Day-angle, Γ (radians) | $\Gamma = \dfrac{2\pi (n-1)}{365}$ | 0 to 2π | | 2 | Declination, δ (degrees) | $\delta = 23.45 \, \sin\!\left[\dfrac{360(284+n)}{365}\right]$ | −23.45° to +23.45° | | 3 | Eccentricity correction factor, E₀ | $E_0 = 1 + 0.033 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{360\,n}{365}\right)$ | ~0.967 to 1.033 | | 4 | Extraterrestrial normal irradiance, G_on | $G_{on} = G_{sc}\,E_0$ | ~1322 to 1410 W/m² | | 5 | Hour angle, ω | $\omega = 15(t_{solar}-12)$ | −180° to +180° | | 6 | Solar altitude, α | $\sin \alpha = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$ | 0° to 90° | | 7 | Solar zenith, θ_z | $\theta_z = 90° - \alpha$ | 0° to 90° | | 8 | Solar azimuth, γ_s | $\cos\gamma_s = \dfrac{\sin\alpha\sin\phi - \sin\delta}{\cos\alpha\cos\phi}$ | 0° to 360° | | 9 | Sunrise/sunset hour angle, ω_s | $\cos\omega_s = -\tan\phi \tan\delta$ | 0° to 180° | | 10 | Day length, N | $N = \dfrac{2}{15}\cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)$ | 0 to 24 hours | | 11 | Air mass (Kasten-Young), AM | $AM = \dfrac{1}{\cos\theta_z + 0.50572(96.07995-\theta_z)^{-1.6364}}$ | ≥ 1 | | 12 | Simple air mass (Kastrov) | $AM \approx \dfrac{1}{\cos\theta_z}$ | θ_z < 90° | | 13 | Equation of time, EoT (minutes) | $EoT = 9.87\sin 2B - 7.53\cos B - 1.5\sin B$, where $B = 360(n-81)/365$ | −14 to +16 min | | 14 | Solar time, t_solar | $t_{solar} = t_{local} + EoT/60 + 4(\lambda_{std}-\lambda_{loc}) - DST$ | hours | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Memo:** When writing declination on the answer sheet, the angle is *always* in **degrees** if you use the formula on row 2; if you replace 360 with $2\pi$ and 365 with 365, then δ comes out in **radians**. Mixing units is the #1 cause of wrong answers in KTU ESE. ### 2.4 The Cosine Law of Incidence — the Bridge to Tilted Surfaces For a tilted surface with tilt angle β from horizontal and surface azimuth γ, the **angle of incidence θ_i** between the incoming beam and the normal to the surface is: $$\cos\theta_i = \sin\delta\sin\phi\cos\beta - \sin\delta\cos\phi\sin\beta\cos\gamma + \cos\delta\cos\phi\cos\beta\cos\omega + \cos\delta\sin\phi\sin\beta\cos\gamma\cos\omega + \cos\delta\sin\beta\sin\gamma\sin\omega$$ This single equation is the *Rosetta Stone* for computing beam radiation on *any* tilted surface — the central numerical problem in KTU solar energy papers. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Declination Angle (Cooper's Equation) **Starting premise:** Earth's rotation axis is tilted by 23.45° with respect to the orbital plane (the ecliptic). On the day of the **summer solstice** (n ≈ 172 for the Northern Hemisphere), the Sun is directly overhead at the Tropic of Cancer (δ = +23.45°). The declination then varies sinusoidally throughout the year. **Step 1 — Define the day angle Γ (in radians) for day number n:** $$\Gamma = \frac{2\pi (n-1)}{365}$$ This converts a day of the year into a fraction of the orbital revolution, normalized to start at n = 1. **Step 2 — Write the sinusoidal variation:** Since δ is maximum (+23.45°) when Γ corresponds to the summer solstice (n ≈ 172), we require: $$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin(\Gamma + \Gamma_0)$$ where Γ₀ is a phase shift chosen so that the sine peaks at n = 172. **Step 3 — Solve for Γ₀:** We need $\sin(\Gamma + \Gamma_0) = 1$ when $n = 172$, i.e., when $\Gamma = \dfrac{2\pi(171)}{365} = 1.7209\pi$. Thus, $\Gamma_0 + 1.7209\pi = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$, which gives $\Gamma_0 = -1.2209\pi$. **Step 4 — Rewrite in the standard "day-number" form:** Multiplying the phase shift back into the day number: $$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{2\pi}{365}(n - 1 - 81.5)\right]$$ Re-approximating to the textbook form (and converting radians to degrees by replacing $2\pi/365$ with $360/365$ and shifting the day count from $(n-1-81.5)$ to $(n-81)$, a small adjustment absorbed in the empirical constant): $$\boxed{\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{360^\circ(284 + n)}{365}\right]}$$ This is **Cooper's Equation (1969)** — the most-used declination formula in KTU textbooks. ### 3.2 Derivation of the Eccentricity Correction Factor E₀ **Starting premise:** Kepler's 1st Law — Earth's orbit is an ellipse with the Sun at one focus. The Earth–Sun distance r varies through the year, so the intensity of the beam outside the atmosphere at normal incidence, $G_{on}$, varies inversely with the square of the ratio r/D (where D is the mean distance). **Step 1 — Kepler's geometric result:** $$\left(\frac{D}{r}\right)^2 = 1 + e^2 + 2e\cos\nu$$ where e is the eccentricity and ν is the true anomaly (angle from perihelion). **Step 2 — Expand for small e (e = 0.0167):** Drop $e^2$ as negligible: $$\left(\frac{D}{r}\right)^2 \approx 1 + 2e\cos\nu$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the standard day-angle form of ν ≈ Γ − 0.0327 sin Γ, and use e = 0.0167:** The maximum deviation corresponds to a cosine-amplitude of $2e = 2(0.0167) = 0.0334$, and the phase aligns such that perihelion occurs near day n = 3 (January 3). **Step 4 — Final empirical form:** $$\boxed{E_0 = 1 + 0.033 \cos\!\left(\frac{360^\circ n}{365}\right)}$$ **Step 5 — Application:** $$G_{on} = G_{sc} \cdot E_0 = 1367 \left[1 + 0.033 \cos\!\left(\frac{360^\circ n}{365}\right)\right] \;\text{W/m}^2$$ Maximum value: 1367 × 1.033 = **1412 W/m²** (early January). Minimum value: 1367 × 0.967 = **1322 W/m²** (early July). ### 3.3 Derivation of the Solar Altitude Angle (α) **Starting premise:** The Sun's position vector in the local horizon coordinate system is given by the **direction cosines** derived from spherical trigonometry applied to the celestial triangle (pole–zenith–Sun). **Step 1 — Identify the three sides of the celestial spherical triangle:** - From zenith to celestial pole = 90° − φ - From zenith to Sun = 90° − α - From celestial pole to Sun = 90° − δ **Step 2 — Apply the Cosine Rule of Spherical Trigonometry:** For the side opposite the hour-angle ω (which is the angle at the pole between the meridian and the Sun): $$\cos(90^\circ - \alpha) = \cos(90^\circ - \phi)\cos(90^\circ - \delta) + \sin(90^\circ - \phi)\sin(90^\circ - \delta)\cos\omega$$ **Step 3 — Simplify using cos(90° − x) = sin x and sin(90° − x) = cos x:** $$\boxed{\sin\alpha = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega}$$ ### 3.4 Derivation of the Solar Azimuth Angle (γ_s) **Step 1 — Apply the Sine Rule of Spherical Trigonometry to the same celestial triangle:** $$\frac{\sin\gamma_s}{\sin(90^\circ - \delta)} = \frac{\sin\omega}{\sin(90^\circ - \alpha)}$$ **Step 2 — Simplify:** $$\sin\gamma_s = \frac{\cos\delta \sin\omega}{\cos\alpha}$$ **Step 3 — Alternative cosine form (more useful for computational purposes):** Using the cosine rule on the third side gives: $$\boxed{\cos\gamma_s = \frac{\sin\alpha \sin\phi - \sin\delta}{\cos\alpha \cos\phi}}$$ **Step 4 — Sign convention for KTU:** - Morning hours (ω < 0): γ_s is measured **east of south** → take γ_s = −|γ_s|. - Afternoon hours (ω > 0): γ_s is measured **west of south** → take γ_s = +|γ_s|. ### 3.5 Derivation of the Sunrise/Sunset Hour Angle (ω_s) and Day Length **Step 1 — At sunrise/sunset, the altitude α = 0°, so sin α = 0:** $$0 = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega_s$$ **Step 2 — Solve for cos ω_s:** $$\cos\omega_s = -\frac{\sin\phi\sin\delta}{\cos\phi\cos\delta} = -\tan\phi\tan\delta$$ **Step 3 — The sunset hour angle is therefore:** $$\boxed{\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)}$$ **Step 4 — Day length N (in hours), since ω_s is in degrees:** $$N = \frac{2\,\omega_s}{15^\circ/\text{hr}}$$ $$\boxed{N = \frac{2}{15}\cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)}$$ ### 3.6 Derivation of the Air Mass **Step 1 — Geometric definition:** Air mass is the ratio of the optical path length of sunlight through the atmosphere to the path length if the Sun were directly overhead (θ_z = 0). For a plane-parallel atmosphere: $$AM = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z}$$ **Step 2 — Empirical correction (Kasten-Young, 1989) — accounts for Earth's curvature and atmospheric refraction for θ_z up to 90°:** $$\boxed{AM = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z + 0.50572\,(96.07995^\circ - \theta_z)^{-1.6364}}}$$ This is the form KTU expects for **AM0, AM1, AM1.5** specifications. **Step 3 — Standard AM values used in solar energy:** - AM0 (space) = 0 - AM1 (Sun at zenith) = 1 - AM1.5 (Sun at 48.2° from zenith) = 1.5 → **Standard test condition for PV modules** --- ### 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU ESE Style) **Problem:** On **March 21** at a location with **latitude φ = 13° N** and **longitude 75° E**, find (a) declination, (b) solar altitude at **11:00 AM local solar time**, (c) solar azimuth at that instant, and (d) air mass. Standard meridian for IST is 82.5° E. **Solution:** **Day number for March 21:** n = 31 (Jan) + 28 (Feb) + 21 = **80** **(a) Declination:** $$\delta = 23.45 \sin\!\left[\frac{360(284+80)}{365}\right] = 23.45 \sin(359.01^\circ) \approx 23.45 \times (-0.0175) \approx -0.41^\circ$$ Since March 21 is the vernal equinox, we expect δ ≈ 0°, and −0.41° is consistent. ✓ **(b) Hour angle at 11:00 AM solar time:** $$\omega = 15^\circ \times (11 - 12) = -15^\circ$$ **Solar altitude:** $$\sin\alpha = \sin(13^\circ)\sin(-0.41^\circ) + \cos(13^\circ)\cos(-0.41^\circ)\cos(-15^\circ)$$ $$\sin\alpha = (0.225)(-0.0072) + (0.974)(0.99998)(0.9659)$$ $$\sin\alpha = -0.00162 + 0.9409 = 0.9393$$ $$\alpha = \sin^{-1}(0.9393) = 69.95^\circ \approx 70.0^\circ$$ **(c) Solar azimuth:** $$\cos\gamma_s = \frac{\sin(70^\circ)\sin(13^\circ) - \sin(-0.41^\circ)}{\cos(70^\circ)\cos(13^\circ)}$$ $$\cos\gamma_s = \frac{(0.9397)(0.2250) - (-0.0072)}{(0.3420)(0.9744)} = \frac{0.2117 + 0.0072}{0.3332} = \frac{0.2189}{0.3332} = 0.6570$$ $$\gamma_s = \cos^{-1}(0.6570) = 48.93^\circ$$ Since it is morning (ω = −15° < 0), γ_s is east of south: **γ_s = −48.93°**. **(d) Air mass:** $$\theta_z = 90^\circ - 70^\circ = 20^\circ$$ $$AM = \frac{1}{\cos(20^\circ) + 0.50572(96.08 - 20)^{-1.6364}}$$ $$AM = \frac{1}{0.9397 + 0.50572 \times (76.08)^{-1.6364}} = \frac{1}{0.9397 + 0.50572 \times 0.00119} \approx \frac{1}{0.9403} \approx 1.063$$ **Result:** AM ≈ **1.06** (typical near-noon clear-sky value). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture — Solar Geometry Engine The Sun–Earth relationship, when implemented computationally, follows a strict hierarchical data-flow: ```mermaid flowchart TD INPUT_DATE[Day Number n] --> DECL[Compute Declination delta] INPUT_LAT[Latitude phi] --> ALPHA[Compute Altitude alpha] INPUT_LON[Longitude lambda] --> EOT[Compute Equation of Time] INPUT_TIME[Local Clock Time] --> EOT EOT --> TSOL[Solar Time t_solar] TSOL --> OMEGA[Hour Angle omega] DECL --> ALPHA DECL --> OMEGA DECL --> OMEGA2[Sunrise Hour Angle omega_s] OMEGA --> ALPHA OMEGA --> AZIMUTH[Azimuth gamma_s] INPUT_AZ[Surface Azimuth gamma] --> INCIDENCE[Angle of Incidence theta_i] INPUT_TILT[Surface Tilt beta] --> INCIDENCE ALPHA --> ZENITH[Zenith theta_z] ALPHA --> AZIMUTH ALPHA --> INCIDENCE AZIMUTH --> INCIDENCE ZENITH --> AIRMASS[Air Mass AM] OMEGA2 --> DAYLEN[Day Length N] INPUT_LAT2[Latitude phi] --> OMEGA2 DECL --> DAYLEN ALPHA --> IB[Beam Radiation on Tilted Surface Ib] INCIDENCE --> IB AIRMASS --> CLEARSKY[Clear Sky Model] CLEARSKY --> GHI[Global Horizontal Irradiance GHI] IB --> POA[Plane-of-Array Irradiance POA] GHI --> POA POA --> OUTPUT[PV Yield / Solar Thermal Gain] ``` ### 4.2 Schematic — Earth–Sun Geometry in 3D (Coordinate Reference) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Celestial_Coordinates A1[Declination delta
Equatorial Plane] --> C1 B1[Hour Angle omega
Local Meridian] --> C1 C1[Sun Position in
Celestial Sphere] end subgraph Horizon_Coordinates C1 --> D1 D1[Solar Altitude alpha
above horizon] C1 --> E1 E1[Solar Azimuth gamma_s
from North/South] end subgraph Observer_Frame F1[Latitude phi] --> C1 G1[Date n] --> A1 H1[Local Solar Time] --> B1 end ``` ### 4.3 Annual Variation of Declination — Sinusoidal Profile ``` δ (degrees) +23.45 ┤ ╭──╮ │ ╭──╯ ╰──╮ │ ╭╯ ╰╮ 0 ┤─────────╯╰───────────────╰────────────── n │ ╱ ╲ │ ╱ ╲ -23.45 ┤ ╱ ╲ └───────────────────────────────────────── Jan 1 Dec 31 ↑ ↑ Winter Solstice Summer Solstice (S) Winter Solstice (N) ``` | Event | Approximate Date (n) | δ | |---|---|---| | Winter Solstice (N) | Dec 21 (n = 355) | −23.45° | | Vernal Equinox | Mar 21 (n = 80) | 0° | | Summer Solstice (N) | Jun 21 (n = 172) | +23.45° | | Autumnal Equinox | Sep 23 (n = 266) | 0° | ### 4.4 Annual Variation of E₀ — Eccentricity Factor ``` E₀ 1.033 ┤ ╭╮ │ ╭╯ ╰╮ 1.000 ┤───╯──────╰────────────── n │ ╭╯ ╰╮ │╭╯ ╰╮ 0.967 ┤╯ ╰─ └────────────────────── Jan 3 Jul 4 ↑ Perihelion ↑ Aphelion (closest) (farthest) ``` ### 4.5 Block-Level Flow of a Solar Geometry Computation ```mermaid flowchart TD A[User Inputs:
Latitude phi, Longitude lambda,
Date n, Local Time t] --> B[Compute Declination delta
Cooper Eq.] A --> C[Compute Equation of Time EoT] A --> D[Longitude Correction 4 min/deg] B --> H[Compute Solar Time] C --> H D --> H H --> I[Compute Hour Angle omega] A --> J[Compute Tilt beta, Azimuth gamma] I --> K[Compute Altitude alpha,
Zenith theta_z, Azimuth gamma_s] B --> K J --> L[Compute Incidence Angle theta_i] K --> L L --> M[Compute Beam Radiation on Tilted Surface Ib] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)** --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]** Define the following terms with neat sketches: *(a)* Solar declination angle *(b)* Hour angle *(c)* Solar zenith angle. **Model Answer:** **(a) Solar Declination Angle (δ):** It is the angular position of the Sun north or south of the equatorial plane of the Earth. It varies between **−23.45° and +23.45°** through the year. It is zero at the equinoxes and maximum (+23.45°) at the summer solstice (June 21) for the Northern Hemisphere. Mathematically, $$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{360(284+n)}{365}\right]$$ where n is the day of the year. **(b) Hour Angle (ω):** It is the angular displacement of the Sun east or west of the local meridian due to Earth's rotation of 15° per hour. It is **zero at solar noon**, negative before noon, and positive after noon. $$\omega = 15^\circ (t_{\text{solar}} - 12)$$ **(c) Solar Zenith Angle (θ_z):** It is the angle between the Sun's rays and the local vertical (normal to the horizontal plane). It is the complement of the solar altitude: $\theta_z = 90^\circ - \alpha$. It varies from 0° (Sun overhead) to 90° (Sun at horizon). **Sketch:** A vertical line marked "ZENITH", a horizontal plane marked "HORIZON", and an inclined ray from the upper-right representing the Sun's rays, with θ_z between vertical and the ray, and α between horizontal and the ray. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is meant by the *solar constant*? Explain why the extraterrestrial radiation on a normal surface varies by about ±3.3 % during the year. **Model Answer:** The **solar constant G_sc** is the rate at which solar energy is received per unit area on a surface oriented perpendicular to the Sun's rays, at the *mean* Earth–Sun distance, *outside* the atmosphere. The accepted value is **G_sc = 1367 W/m²**. **Reason for ±3.3 % variation:** The Earth's orbit is an ellipse (not a circle) with the Sun at one focus and an eccentricity **e = 0.0167**. As the Earth moves along this orbit, the Earth–Sun distance varies from a minimum (perihelion, ~1.471 × 10⁸ km, around January 3) to a maximum (aphelion, ~1.521 × 10⁸ km, around July 4). Since the solar intensity falls off as the inverse square of the distance, the extraterrestrial normal irradiance $G_{on} = G_{sc} E_0$, where $$E_0 = 1 + 0.033 \cos\!\left(\frac{360 n}{365}\right)$$ varies from **0.967 × 1367 = 1322 W/m²** to **1.033 × 1367 = 1412 W/m²**, giving exactly the ±3.3 % variation observed. --- ### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the **declination angle** of the Sun. State clearly the assumptions made and the boundary values of declination. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a location at latitude **φ = 28.6° N** and longitude **77.2° E**, calculate on **June 15**: (i) Solar declination (ii) Solar altitude angle at **2:00 PM** local standard time (Standard meridian = 82.5° E) (iii) Solar azimuth angle at that instant (iv) Air mass **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A:** **(a) Derivation of Declination Angle (7 marks):** [As derived in Section 3.1 of these notes. Student must reproduce:] **Assumptions:** 1. Earth's orbital eccentricity is neglected in setting the seasonal phase; only the axial tilt is considered for δ. 2. The variation of δ through the year is sinusoidal. 3. δ is +23.45° on the summer solstice (Northern Hemisphere, n ≈ 172) and −23.45° on the winter solstice (n ≈ 355). **Derivation:** Define day angle $\Gamma = \dfrac{2\pi(n-1)}{365}$. Since δ varies sinusoidally and reaches its peak on day 172, we write: $$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin(\Gamma + \Gamma_0)$$ Setting $\Gamma + \Gamma_0 = \pi/2$ at $n = 172$ (so $\Gamma = 2\pi(171)/365$): $$\Gamma_0 = \frac{\pi}{2} - \frac{2\pi \times 171}{365} = \frac{\pi}{2} - 0.9367\pi = -0.4367\pi$$ So $$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{2\pi}{365}(n - 1) - 0.4367\pi\right]$$ $$= 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{2\pi}{365}(n - 1 - 81.5)\right]$$ Empirically simplified to the standard Cooper's equation (radians → degrees, day offset shifted): $$\boxed{\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{360(284 + n)}{365}\right]}$$ **Boundary values:** - Maximum: δ = +23.45° at summer solstice (June 21, n = 172) - Minimum: δ = −23.45° at winter solstice (Dec 21, n = 355) - δ = 0° at equinoxes (March 21 and September 23) --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** φ = 28.6° N, longitude = 77.2° E, Standard meridian λ_std = 82.5° E, Date = June 15, Time = 2:00 PM LST. **Step 1 — Day number:** n = 31 + 28 + 31 + 30 + 31 + 15 = **166** **Step 2 — (i) Declination:** $$\delta = 23.45 \sin\!\left[\frac{360(284 + 166)}{365}\right] = 23.45 \sin\!\left(\frac{360 \times 450}{365}\right)$$ $$= 23.45 \sin(443.84^\circ) = 23.45 \sin(83.84^\circ) = 23.45 \times 0.9943$$ $$\boxed{\delta = +23.32^\circ}$$ **Step 3 — Longitude correction to solar time:** $$\Delta t = 4 \times (82.5 - 77.2) = 4 \times 5.3 = 21.2 \text{ minutes}$$ (Subtract because location is west of standard meridian.) So solar time at 2:00 PM LST: $$t_{\text{solar}} = 14{:}00 - 21.2 \text{ min} = 13{:}38.8$$ (Neglecting Equation of Time for this part — full-credit answer would include EoT ≈ −0.5 min on June 15, negligible here.) **Step 4 — Hour angle:** $$\omega = 15^\circ \times (13.647 - 12) = 15 \times 1.647 = 24.71^\circ$$ **Step 5 — (ii) Solar altitude:** $$\sin\alpha = \sin(28.6^\circ)\sin(23.32^\circ) + \cos(28.6^\circ)\cos(23.32^\circ)\cos(24.71^\circ)$$ $$= (0.4787)(0.3957) + (0.8780)(0.9184)(0.9083)$$ $$= 0.1894 + 0.7324 = 0.9218$$ $$\alpha = \sin^{-1}(0.9218) = \boxed{67.18^\circ}$$ **Step 6 — (iii) Solar azimuth (afternoon, ω > 0, so γ_s positive = west of south):** $$\cos\gamma_s = \frac{\sin\alpha \sin\phi - \sin\delta}{\cos\alpha \cos\phi}$$ $$= \frac{(0.9218)(0.4787) - 0.3957}{(0.3877)(0.8780)} = \frac{0.4413 - 0.3957}{0.3404} = \frac{0.0456}{0.3404} = 0.1339$$ $$\gamma_s = \cos^{-1}(0.1339) = \boxed{77.31^\circ \text{ (West of South)}}$$ **Step 7 — (iv) Air mass (Kasten-Young):** $$\theta_z = 90^\circ - 67.18^\circ = 22.82^\circ$$ $$AM = \frac{1}{\cos(22.82^\circ) + 0.50572(96.08 - 22.82)^{-1.6364}}$$ $$= \frac{1}{0.9218 + 0.50572 \times (73.26)^{-1.6364}} = \frac{1}{0.9218 + 0.50572 \times 0.00135}$$ $$= \frac{1}{0.9218 + 0.000683} = \frac{1}{0.9225}$$ $$\boxed{AM \approx 1.084}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Style — Alternative Choice]** **(a)** With the help of a neat celestial-sphere diagram, derive the **fundamental equation for solar altitude angle** in terms of latitude φ, declination δ, and hour angle ω. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A solar collector is installed at latitude **10° N** with a **tilt angle β = 15°** facing **due south (γ = 0°)**. Compute the **angle of incidence** of the solar beam on **March 1** at **10:30 AM** local apparent solar time. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B:** **(a) Derivation of Solar Altitude Equation (7 marks):** [Refer to Section 3.3 of these notes. Key steps to reproduce:] 1. Sketch the celestial triangle with vertices at **P** (celestial pole), **Z** (zenith), and **S** (Sun). 2. Mark sides: arc PZ = 90° − φ, arc PS = 90° − δ, arc ZS = 90° − α. 3. Mark angle at P = ω (hour angle). 4. Apply spherical cosine rule: $$\cos(ZS) = \cos(PZ)\cos(PS) + \sin(PZ)\sin(PS)\cos\omega$$ 5. Substitute: $$\sin\alpha = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$$ **Diagram description (text form):** - Vertical axis pointing up labeled "ZENITH (Z)". - A horizontal plane labeled "HORIZON". - An axis inclined at angle (90° − φ) from the horizon labeled "POLAR AXIS (P)". - A point "S" representing the Sun at altitude α above horizon, with an arc from zenith to Sun subtending angle (90° − α). - The hour angle ω is the angle measured at P between the meridian (PZ) and the great circle to the Sun (PS). --- **(b) Angle of Incidence Calculation (7 marks):** **Given:** φ = 10° N, β = 15°, γ = 0° (due south), date = March 1, time = 10:30 AM solar time. **Step 1 — Day number for March 1:** n = 31 + 28 + 1 = **60** **Step 2 — Declination:** $$\delta = 23.45 \sin\!\left[\frac{360(284+60)}{365}\right] = 23.45 \sin\!\left(\frac{360 \times 344}{365}\right)$$ $$= 23.45 \sin(339.29^\circ) = 23.45 \times (-0.3450) = \boxed{-8.09^\circ}$$ **Step 3 — Hour angle at 10:30 AM solar time:** $$\omega = 15^\circ \times (10.5 - 12) = 15 \times (-1.5) = \boxed{-22.5^\circ}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the incidence-angle equation with γ = 0°:** For a south-facing surface, γ = 0°, the general equation simplifies to: $$\cos\theta_i = \sin\delta\sin(\phi - \beta) + \cos\delta\cos(\phi - \beta)\cos\omega$$ *(Alternative — use the full equation with γ = 0, β = 15°.)* Plugging in: $$\cos\theta_i = \sin(-8.09^\circ)\sin(10^\circ - 15^\circ) + \cos(-8.09^\circ)\cos(10^\circ - 15^\circ)\cos(-22.5^\circ)$$ $$= (-0.1408)\sin(-5^\circ) + (0.9900)\cos(-5^\circ)\cos(22.5^\circ)$$ $$= (-0.1408)(-0.0872) + (0.9900)(0.9962)(0.9239)$$ $$= 0.01228 + 0.91115 = 0.92343$$ $$\theta_i = \cos^{-1}(0.92343) = \boxed{22.60^\circ}$$ **Interpretation:** The beam strikes the tilted collector surface at 22.6° from the surface normal — a reasonably small angle, indicating near-optimal interception of the beam at this date and time. --- ### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Sun–Earth Relationship Problems:** > > 1. **Unit Confusion (–2 to –3 marks):** Mixing degrees and radians in trigonometric functions. **Always** check whether the formula has 360 (degrees) or 2π (radians). The δ formula **δ = 23.45 sin(360(284+n)/365)** is in **degrees**; the calculator **must be in DEG mode**. > > 2. **Sign Error on Declination (–1 mark):** December declination is **negative** for the Northern Hemisphere. Always use the sine value as-is; don't take the absolute. > > 3. **Forgetting Longitude Correction (–2 marks):** Solar time ≠ Local clock time. The examiner explicitly tests the conversion: $t_{\text{solar}} = t_{\text{local}} + \text{EoT}/60 + 4(\lambda_{\text{std}} - \lambda_{\text{loc}})$. > > 4. **Wrong Azimuth Sign (–1 mark):** For ω > 0 (afternoon), γ_s is **positive** (west of south in N. Hemisphere convention); for ω < 0 (morning), γ_s is **negative**. > > 5. **Declination Out of Range (–1 mark):** If your answer comes out to ±25° or more, you almost certainly used the wrong formula or wrong units. > > 6. **Equation of Time Omitted:** Many KTU toppers lose marks by *not even mentioning* EoT. A standard answer should state it (≈ ±16 min) and show it was applied if precision warrants. > > 7. **No Sketch in Long Answer (–1 to –2 marks):** Part (a) of any 7-mark derivation **must include a celestial-sphere diagram**. Marks are explicitly allocated for it. --- ### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)** - **Solar constant G_sc = 1367 W/m²** is the energy flux *outside* the atmosphere at mean Earth–Sun distance. - **Earth's orbit is elliptical (e = 0.0167)**, not circular — this causes ±3.3 % annual variation in G_on. - **Earth's axial tilt = 23.45°** is the *only* physical reason we have seasons and the *only* reason declination is nonzero. - **Day number n = 1** on January 1; **n = 365** on December 31. **n = 80** is the vernal equinox (δ ≈ 0), **n = 172** is the summer solstice (δ ≈ +23.45°). - **Declination formula (Cooper's):** $\delta = 23.45 \sin\!\left[\dfrac{360(284+n)}{365}\right]$ — use DEGREE mode on calculator. - **Eccentricity factor:** $E_0 = 1 + 0.033\cos\!\left(\dfrac{360n}{365}\right)$. - **Hour angle:** $\omega = 15^\circ (t_{\text{solar}} - 12)$; negative before noon, positive after. - **Solar time conversion:** $t_{\text{solar}} = t_{\text{local}} + \dfrac{\text{EoT}}{60} + 4(\lambda_{\text{std}} - \lambda_{\text{loc}})$. - **Altitude:** $\sin\alpha = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$. - **Zenith:** $\theta_z = 90^\circ - \alpha$. - **Azimuth:** $\cos\gamma_s = \dfrac{\sin\alpha\sin\phi - \sin\delta}{\cos\alpha\cos\phi}$; afternoon = positive (west). - **Sunrise hour angle:** $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)$. - **Day length:** $N = \dfrac{2}{15}\cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)$ hours. - **Air mass (simple):** $AM = 1/\cos\theta_z$. - **Air mass (Kasten-Young, accurate up to 90°):** $AM = \dfrac{1}{\cos\theta_z + 0.50572(96.07995 - \theta_z)^{-1.6364}}$. - **Equation of Time (Spencer, 1971):** $EoT = 9.87\sin 2B - 7.53\cos B - 1.5\sin B$, $B = \dfrac{360(n-81)}{365}$ (minutes). - **Angle of incidence on tilted surface:** the 5-term cosine law combining δ, φ, β, γ, ω — the central formula for tilted-surface beam radiation. - **Standard test conditions:** AM1.5G with G = 1000 W/m², cell temperature 25°C — used universally for rating PV modules. - **Solstices and Equinoxes** are the four anchor points of the solar geometry calendar — memorize the day numbers (80, 172, 266, 355) and δ values (0, +23.45, 0, −23.45). - **Always include a labeled celestial-sphere diagram** in any derivation question — KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for the sketch.

topics.title: Extraterrestrial Radiation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET617 - SOLAR ENERGY CONSERVATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Extraterrestrial Radiation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Extraterrestrial Radiation (ETR)**, denoted as $I_0$ or $G_0$, is defined as the solar radiation incident on a surface located at the top of the Earth's atmosphere, *before* any attenuation, scattering, or absorption takes place within the atmosphere. It represents the unfiltered solar energy flux that would be received on a plane perpendicular to the Sun's rays outside the Earth's atmosphere, evaluated at the mean Earth–Sun distance.

$$I_0 = I_{sc} \cdot E_0 \cdot \cos(\theta_z)$$

where:
- $I_{sc}$ = **Solar Constant** (W/m²)
- $E_0$ = Eccentricity correction factor (dimensionless)
- $\theta_z$ = Solar zenith angle (degrees or radians)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solar Constant ($I_{sc}$):** The solar constant is the rate at which solar energy is received on a unit area perpendicular to the Sun's rays at the top of the atmosphere, at the **mean Earth–Sun distance of 1 Astronomical Unit (1 AU ≈ 1.496 × 10⁸ km)**, when the Earth is at its mean distance from the Sun.
>
> **Standard Accepted Value:** $I_{sc} = \mathbf{1367 \ W/m^2}$ (WMO/ASHRAE standard)
> (Modern satellite measurements give 1361 W/m², but KTU textbooks and ASHRAE use **1367 W/m²**.)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing on the roof of a skyscraper with a sun-collector facing the Sun. Before the sunlight reaches you, it has to pass through the entire atmosphere — clouds, dust, water vapor, ozone — all of which "soak up" or scatter a portion of the energy. If we could lift that same collector on a magic platform **above the atmosphere** (about 100 km up), we would receive **more** and **cleaner** sunlight because nothing has yet blocked any of it.

That unfiltered, pristine solar energy reaching the top of the atmosphere is what we call **Extraterrestrial Radiation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Extra"–"Terrestrial"?**
> The prefix *Extra* means "outside of" and *Terrestrial* refers to "Earth". So *Extra-Terrestrial* literally means **"outside the Earth"** — i.e., outside the atmosphere.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solar Constant | $I_{sc}$ | **1367** | W/m² |
| Mean Earth–Sun Distance | 1 AU | 1.496 × 10⁸ | km |
| Earth's Orbital Eccentricity | $e$ | 0.0167 | – |
| Tilt of Earth's Axis | – | 23.45° | degrees |
| Earth's Rotational Speed | – | 15°/hour | degrees/hour |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The extraterrestrial radiation is **NOT constant** throughout the year because the Earth's orbit is elliptical. The actual Earth–Sun distance varies by ±1.7%, which causes a ±3.4% variation in $I_0$ over the year.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Factors Influencing Extraterrestrial Radiation

The extraterrestrial radiation available at the top of the atmosphere on a horizontal surface depends on **three primary factors**:

1. **Time of Year** (Day number, $n$): Controls the eccentricity correction factor $E_0$ and solar declination $\delta$.
2. **Time of Day** (Hour angle, $\omega$): Controls the Sun's position in the sky.
3. **Geographic Latitude** ($\phi$): Determines the angle at which Sun's rays strike the location.

### 2.2 Decomposition of Each Factor

### (A) Eccentricity Correction Factor ($E_0$)

The Earth follows an **elliptical orbit** around the Sun. As the Earth moves closer to the Sun (perihelion — around January 3), the incident radiation increases; when it moves farther (aphelion — around July 4), the radiation decreases. This is corrected using:

$$E_0 = 1 + 0.033 \cdot \cos\left(\frac{360 \cdot n}{365}\right)$$

where $n$ = day of the year (January 1 = 1, December 31 = 365).

**Physical Interpretation:**
- When $n = 1$ (January 1): $E_0 ≈ 1.033$ (maximum, perihelion)
- When $n = 184$ (July 4): $E_0 ≈ 0.967$ (minimum, aphelion)

### (B) Solar Declination Angle ($\delta$)

The Earth's rotational axis is tilted at **23.45°** to the plane of its orbit. The **solar declination** is the angle between the Sun's rays and the Earth's equatorial plane. It varies from **+23.45°** (Summer Solstice, June 21) to **−23.45°** (Winter Solstice, December 21).

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(n + 284)\right]$$

Alternative form using Cooper's equation:

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \cdot \sin\left[\frac{2\pi}{365}(n - 81)\right]$$

### (C) Hour Angle ($\omega$)

The **hour angle** is the angular displacement of the Sun east or west of the local meridian (the north–south line directly overhead) due to Earth's rotation. It is **zero at solar noon**, negative in the morning, positive in the afternoon.

$$\omega = 15^\circ \cdot (t_{solar} - 12)$$

where $t_{solar}$ is the local solar time in hours.

- 1 hour = **15°** of Earth's rotation
- Morning: $\omega < 0$
- Afternoon: $\omega > 0$
- Sunrise/Sunset: $\omega = \pm \omega_s$

### (D) Latitude ($\phi$)

The angle north (+) or south (−) of the equator. Trivially constant for a given location, but affects the **cosine of the angle of incidence**.

### (E) Solar Zenith Angle ($\theta_z$)

The angle between the Sun's rays and the **vertical (zenith)** at the observer's location:

$$\cos(\theta_z) = \sin(\phi)\sin(\delta) + \cos(\phi)\cos(\delta)\cos(\omega)$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet**. Memorize these for direct 3-mark and 14-mark questions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Boundary / Special Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Solar Constant | $I_{sc} = 1367 \ W/m^2$ | At 1 AU, perpendicular incidence |
| 2 | Eccentricity Factor | $E_0 = 1 + 0.033\cos(360n/365)$ | $n$ = day of year |
| 3 | Solar Declination | $\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin[360(n+284)/365]$ | $-23.45^\circ \le \delta \le +23.45^\circ$ |
| 4 | Hour Angle | $\omega = 15^\circ(t_{solar} - 12)$ | $\omega = 0$ at solar noon |
| 5 | Sunset Hour Angle | $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}[-\tan(\phi)\tan(\delta)]$ | Used in daily integration |
| 6 | Cosine of Zenith | $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$ | $\cos\theta_z = 0$ at sunset |
| 7 | ETR on Horizontal Surface (Instantaneous) | $I_{0H} = I_{sc} \cdot E_0 \cdot \cos\theta_z$ | When $\omega < \omega_s$ |
| 8 | ETR on Tilted Surface | $I_{0T} = I_{sc} \cdot E_0 \cdot \cos\theta$ | $\theta$ = angle of incidence on tilt |
| 9 | Daily ETR on Horizontal | $H_{0} = \frac{24 \cdot 3600 \cdot I_{sc}}{\pi} \cdot E_0 \cdot \left[\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \omega_s\sin\phi\sin\delta\right]$ | Integrated over $\omega = -\omega_s$ to $+\omega_s$ |
| 10 | Monthly Average Daily ETR | $H_{0,m} = \frac{24 \cdot 3600 \cdot I_{sc}}{\pi} \cdot \overline{E_0} \cdot \left[\cos\phi\cos\overline{\delta}\sin\overline{\omega_s} + \overline{\omega_s}\sin\phi\sin\overline{\delta}\right]$ | Overbars denote monthly means |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** Engineers use these formulas to:
> - Size **photovoltaic (PV) arrays** and **solar thermal collectors**.
> - Predict **peak solar power generation** before plant installation.
> - Calculate **clearness index** ($K_T = H/H_0$) to assess the fraction of extraterrestrial radiation that actually reaches the ground — a key metric for site selection.
> - Design **solar tracking systems** with precise angular control.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Extraterrestrial Radiation on a Horizontal Surface ($I_{0H}$)

### Step 1 — Start with the General Extraterrestrial Flux on a Surface Normal to Sun's Rays

When a surface is **perpendicular** to the solar beam at the top of the atmosphere, the radiant flux density received is:

$$I_{on} = I_{sc} \cdot E_0$$

This is the "normal incidence" extraterrestrial radiation, accounting for the elliptical orbit.

### Step 2 — Generalize to an Arbitrary Surface (Angle of Incidence $\theta$)

When the surface makes an angle $\theta$ with the Sun's rays, the projected area intercepting the beam is reduced by a factor $\cos\theta$:

$$I_0 = I_{sc} \cdot E_0 \cdot \cos\theta$$

### Step 3 — Specialize to a Horizontal Surface

For a horizontal surface, the angle of incidence $\theta$ equals the solar zenith angle $\theta_z$. We need a workable expression for $\cos\theta_z$ in terms of measurable angles.

**Using the Spherical Trigonometry of the Sun–Earth Geometry:**

Let:
- $\phi$ = Latitude of location
- $\delta$ = Solar declination
- $\omega$ = Hour angle

The angle between the Sun's direction and the local zenith is given by the fundamental astronomical relationship:

$$\cos(\theta_z) = \sin(\phi)\sin(\delta) + \cos(\phi)\cos(\delta)\cos(\omega)$$

**Geometric Justification (textual):** The first term, $\sin(\phi)\sin(\delta)$, represents the projection of the Sun's rays along the local vertical axis when the Sun is in the meridian plane (i.e., at noon, $\omega = 0$, only this term remains, leaving $\cos(\theta_{z,noon}) = \sin(\phi)\sin(\delta) + \cos(\phi)\cos(\delta) = \cos(\phi-\delta)$, which is correct). The second term accounts for the deviation as the Sun moves away from the meridian.

### Step 4 — Combine to Obtain the Instantaneous Horizontal ETR

$$\boxed{\,I_{0H} = I_{sc} \cdot E_0 \cdot \left[\sin(\phi)\sin(\delta) + \cos(\phi)\cos(\delta)\cos(\omega)\right]\,}$$

This expression is valid **only when the Sun is above the horizon**, i.e., when $\cos(\theta_z) > 0$ or equivalently $\vert \omega \vert < \omega_s$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sunset Hour Angle ($\omega_s$)

### Step 1 — Condition for Sunrise/Sunset

At sunrise and sunset, the Sun is exactly on the horizon, meaning the zenith angle $\theta_z = 90°$, so $\cos(\theta_z) = 0$.

### Step 2 — Substitute into the Cosine Expression

$$\cos(90^\circ) = 0 = \sin(\phi)\sin(\delta) + \cos(\phi)\cos(\delta)\cos(\omega_s)$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $\cos(\omega_s)$

$$\cos(\phi)\cos(\delta)\cos(\omega_s) = -\sin(\phi)\sin(\delta)$$

$$\cos(\omega_s) = -\frac{\sin(\phi)\sin(\delta)}{\cos(\phi)\cos(\delta)} = -\tan(\phi)\tan(\delta)$$

### Step 4 — Final Expression

$$\boxed{\,\omega_s = \cos^{-1}\left[-\tan(\phi)\tan(\delta)\right]\,}$$

This angle is in **radians** if you used the radian form, or **degrees** if you used degrees. Always ensure unit consistency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Cases:**
> - At equator ($\phi = 0$): $\omega_s = 90°$ for all days (12-hour day, 12-hour night).
> - At summer solstice, polar regions can have $\omega_s > 90°$ (midnight sun) — the formula automatically gives valid values.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Daily Integrated Extraterrestrial Radiation on a Horizontal Surface ($H_0$)

This is one of the **most important KTU derivations** and frequently appears for 7-mark sub-questions.

### Step 1 — Set Up the Integration

The daily integrated radiation $H_0$ (in J/m²/day) is obtained by integrating the instantaneous $I_{0H}$ over the duration of daylight. Since the Earth rotates at 15°/hour, we convert time $dt$ (in seconds) to hour angle $d\omega$:

$$dt = \frac{24 \cdot 3600}{2\pi} \, d\omega \quad \text{(seconds per radian)}$$

Thus, integration from sunrise ($-\omega_s$) to sunset ($+\omega_s$):

$$H_0 = \int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} I_{0H} \cdot \frac{24 \cdot 3600}{2\pi} \, d\omega$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $I_{0H}$

$$H_0 = \frac{24 \cdot 3600 \cdot I_{sc} \cdot E_0}{2\pi} \int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} \left[\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega\right] d\omega$$

Note: $I_{sc}$, $E_0$, $\phi$, $\delta$ are constants over a single day (since the day length is short compared to Earth's orbital period).

### Step 3 — Split the Integral

$$H_0 = \frac{24 \cdot 3600 \cdot I_{sc} \cdot E_0}{2\pi} \left[ \sin\phi\sin\delta \int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} d\omega + \cos\phi\cos\delta \int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} \cos\omega \, d\omega \right]$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate Each Integral Separately

**First Integral:**

$$\int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} d\omega = \omega_s - (-\omega_s) = 2\omega_s$$

**Second Integral:**

$$\int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} \cos\omega \, d\omega = \sin\omega \Big|_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} = \sin(\omega_s) - \sin(-\omega_s) = 2\sin(\omega_s)$$

### Step 5 — Substitute Back and Simplify

$$H_0 = \frac{24 \cdot 3600 \cdot I_{sc} \cdot E_0}{2\pi} \left[ \sin\phi\sin\delta \cdot 2\omega_s + \cos\phi\cos\delta \cdot 2\sin\omega_s \right]$$

$$H_0 = \frac{24 \cdot 3600 \cdot I_{sc} \cdot E_0}{\pi} \left[ \omega_s \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta \sin\omega_s \right]$$

### Step 6 — Final Canonical Form

$$\boxed{\,H_0 = \frac{24 \cdot 3600 \cdot I_{sc} \cdot E_0}{\pi} \left[\cos\phi\cos\delta \sin\omega_s + \omega_s \sin\phi\sin\delta\right]\,}$$

This is the **Liu and Jordan (1960) equation**, which is the gold standard for daily ETR on horizontal surfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Check:**
> - $I_{sc}$ is in W/m² = J/(s·m²)
> - Time factor: $24 \cdot 3600$ seconds/day
> - $\omega_s$ must be in **radians**
> - Result: $H_0$ in J/m²/day (or divide by 10⁶ to get MJ/m²/day)

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Model Problem)

**Problem:** Calculate the daily extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface at a location with latitude $\phi = 13°\text{N}$ on the **summer solstice** ($n = 172$).

**Given:**
- $\phi = 13°$
- $n = 172$ (summer solstice)
- $I_{sc} = 1367$ W/m²

### Solution:

**Step A — Compute $\delta$:**

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(172 + 284)\right]$$

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360 \cdot 456}{365}\right] = 23.45^\circ \cdot \sin(449.75^\circ)$$

Since $\sin$ is periodic: $\sin(449.75°) = \sin(449.75° - 360°) = \sin(89.75°) ≈ 1.000$

$$\delta ≈ 23.45°$$ (confirms summer solstice)

**Step B — Compute $E_0$:**

$$E_0 = 1 + 0.033 \cdot \cos\left[\frac{360 \cdot 172}{365}\right] = 1 + 0.033 \cdot \cos(169.64°)$$

$$E_0 = 1 + 0.033 \cdot (-0.984) = 1 - 0.0325 = 0.9675$$

**Step C — Compute $\omega_s$:**

$$\tan(\phi)\tan(\delta) = \tan(13°)\tan(23.45°) = (0.2309)(0.4335) = 0.1001$$

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-0.1001) = 95.74°$$

Converting to radians: $\omega_s = 95.74 \times \pi/180 = 1.671$ rad

**Step D — Compute the Trigonometric Bracket:**

$$\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s = \cos(13°)\cos(23.45°)\sin(95.74°)$$
$$= (0.9744)(0.9178)(0.9950) = 0.8900$$

$$\omega_s \sin\phi\sin\delta = (1.671)\sin(13°)\sin(23.45°)$$
$$= (1.671)(0.2250)(0.3979) = 0.1496$$

Sum: $0.8900 + 0.1496 = 1.0396$

**Step E — Compute $H_0$:**

$$H_0 = \frac{24 \cdot 3600 \cdot 1367 \cdot 0.9675}{\pi} \cdot 1.0396$$

$$H_0 = \frac{31{,}536{,}000 \cdot 1367 \cdot 0.9675}{3.1416} \cdot 1.0396$$

$$H_0 = \frac{31{,}536{,}000 \cdot 1322.6}{3.1416} \cdot 1.0396$$

$$H_0 = \frac{4.171 \times 10^{10}}{3.1416} \cdot 1.0396 = 1.328 \times 10^{10} \cdot 1.0396$$

$$\boxed{\,H_0 \approx 1.380 \times 10^{10} \ \text{J/m}^2/\text{day} = 13.80 \ \text{MJ/m}^2/\text{day}\,}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sun–Earth Geometric Relationships (Mermaid Block Diagram)

This diagram maps the geometric and astronomical inputs that determine extraterrestrial radiation at any point on Earth.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[SUN Source of ETR] --> B[Earth Orbital Geometry]
    B --> C1[Day of Year n]
    B --> C2[Latitude phi]
    B --> C3[Solar Time t]
    C1 --> D1[Eccentricity Factor E0]
    C1 --> D2[Solar Declination delta]
    C3 --> D3[Hour Angle omega]
    C2 --> E[Angle of Incidence theta]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D1 --> F[Extraterrestrial Radiation I0]
    E --> F
    F --> G1[Horizontal Surface I0H]
    F --> G2[Tilted Surface I0T]
    F --> G3[Daily Integrated H0]
    G3 --> H[Application: Clearness Index KT]
    G1 --> H
    H --> I[Solar Collector Design Sizing and Site Selection]
```

## 4.2 Hierarchical Decomposition of the ETR Calculation Chain

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input: Latitude phi and Day n] --> B[Compute declination delta]
    A --> C[Compute E0]
    B --> D[Compute sunset hour angle omega_s]
    D --> E[Compute daily ETR H0]
    C --> E
    E --> F[Output H0 in MJ per m2 per day]
    A2[Input: phi, delta, omega] --> G[Compute cosine of zenith]
    G --> H[Compute instantaneous I0H]
    C --> H
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture of ETR Determination System

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Module: Astronomical Constants] --> A[Solar Constant I_sc = 1367 W/m2]
    B1[Module: Orbital Mechanics] --> B[Eccentricity Correction E0 n]
    C1[Module: Axis Tilt Model] --> C[Declination delta n]
    D1[Module: Earth Rotation] --> D[Hour Angle omega t]
    E1[Module: Site Coordinates] --> E[Latitude phi]
    A --> F[Core Engine: Zenith Angle Calculator]
    B --> F
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Output: Instantaneous I0H on Horizontal]
    F --> H[Integrator: Daily H0]
    G --> I[End User: Solar Engineer]
    H --> I
```

> [!NOTE]
> The three diagrams above together illustrate: (1) the causal chain of inputs to ETR, (2) the computational workflow, and (3) the modular decomposition of the calculation system. For KTU 14-mark derivations, the examiner expects students to draw **functional block diagrams** showing the relationships between $I_{sc}$, $E_0$, $\delta$, $\omega$, $\phi$, and the final $I_0$ or $H_0$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Solar Constant" and state its value. Mention why extraterrestrial radiation is not exactly equal to the solar constant throughout the year.**
