**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The **Solar Constant** is defined as the rate at which solar energy is received per unit area on a surface **perpendicular to the Sun's rays at the top of the Earth's atmosphere**, at the **mean Earth–Sun distance of 1 Astronomical Unit (1 AU)**.

**Value (0.5 mark):** $I_{sc} = \mathbf{1367 \ W/m^2}$ (standard WMO/ASHRAE value).

**Reason for variation (1 mark):** The Earth follows an **elliptical orbit** with eccentricity $e = 0.0167$. Hence the Earth–Sun distance varies between 0.983 AU (perihelion, ~Jan 3) and 1.017 AU (aphelion, ~July 4). The extraterrestrial radiation $I_0$ is inversely proportional to the square of the Earth–Sun distance, so it varies by approximately ±3.4% over the year. The eccentricity correction factor is:

$$E_0 = 1 + 0.033 \cos\left(\frac{360n}{365}\right)$$

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is solar declination? Derive an expression for the same.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Solar declination ($\delta$) is the angle between the line joining the centre of the Sun to the centre of the Earth and the projection of this line on the Earth's equatorial plane. It represents the angular position of the Sun north or south of the equator.

**Derivation (2 marks):** The Earth's axis is tilted at $23.45°$ to the plane of its orbit (the ecliptic). As the Earth orbits the Sun, the apparent position of the Sun shifts north and south. Modeling this as a sinusoidal function of the day of the year $n$ (with $n = 1$ for Jan 1, $n = 365$ for Dec 31), and using the boundary condition that the maximum declination of $+23.45°$ occurs at the summer solstice (around $n = 172$):

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(n + 284)\right]$$

---

## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module Choice Rule:** Each Part B question carries an **internal choice**. You must attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### ✍️ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **daily integrated extraterrestrial radiation ($H_0$) on a horizontal surface** at any location on Earth. Clearly define each term used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A solar collector is to be installed at a location with latitude $\phi = 28.6°$N. Calculate the **daily extraterrestrial radiation** on a horizontal surface on **March 21** (Spring Equinox). Take $I_{sc} = 1367$ W/m². **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The instantaneous extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface is:

$$I_{0H} = I_{sc} \cdot E_0 \cdot \cos\theta_z$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Using spherical trigonometry, the cosine of the zenith angle is expressed in terms of latitude $\phi$, declination $\delta$, and hour angle $\omega$:

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Hence:

$$I_{0H} = I_{sc} E_0 [\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega]$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** To get the daily integrated value, integrate from sunrise ($-\omega_s$) to sunset ($+\omega_s$). The conversion between time and hour angle is $dt = (24 \times 3600/2\pi) d\omega$:

$$H_0 = \int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} I_{0H} \cdot \frac{24 \cdot 3600}{2\pi} d\omega$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Substituting and treating $I_{sc}$, $E_0$, $\phi$, $\delta$ as constants over a single day:

$$H_0 = \frac{24 \cdot 3600 \cdot I_{sc} E_0}{2\pi} \int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} [\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega] d\omega$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Splitting the integral:

$$\int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} d\omega = 2\omega_s \quad ; \quad \int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} \cos\omega \, d\omega = 2\sin\omega_s$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Final expression (Liu and Jordan equation):

$$\boxed{H_0 = \frac{24 \cdot 3600 \cdot I_{sc} \cdot E_0}{\pi} \left[\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \omega_s \sin\phi\sin\delta\right]}$$

where $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}[-\tan\phi\tan\delta]$ (in radians).

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $\phi = 28.6°$, $n = 81$ (March 21), $I_{sc} = 1367$ W/m².

**Step 1 — Compute $\delta$ (1.5 marks):**

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(81 + 284)\right] = 23.45^\circ \cdot \sin(360.0^\circ)$$

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \cdot 0 = 0° \quad \text{(correct: equinox)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $E_0$ (1 mark):**

$$E_0 = 1 + 0.033\cos\left[\frac{360 \cdot 81}{365}\right] = 1 + 0.033\cos(79.89°)$$

$$E_0 = 1 + 0.033 \cdot 0.175 = 1 + 0.00578 = 1.00578$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\omega_s$ (1.5 marks):**

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}[-\tan(28.6°)\tan(0°)] = \cos^{-1}[0] = 90° = 1.5708 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate trigonometric terms (1.5 marks):**

$$\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s = \cos(28.6°)(1)\sin(90°) = 0.8782 \cdot 1 \cdot 1 = 0.8782$$

$$\omega_s \sin\phi\sin\delta = 1.5708 \cdot \sin(28.6°) \cdot 0 = 0$$

Sum $= 0.8782$

**Step 5 — Compute $H_0$ (1.5 marks):**

$$H_0 = \frac{24 \cdot 3600 \cdot 1367 \cdot 1.00578}{\pi} \cdot 0.8782$$

$$H_0 = \frac{31{,}536{,}000 \cdot 1374.9}{3.1416} \cdot 0.8782 = \frac{4.336 \times 10^{10}}{3.1416} \cdot 0.8782$$

$$H_0 = 1.380 \times 10^{10} \cdot 0.8782 = 1.212 \times 10^{10} \ \text{J/m}^2/\text{day}$$

$$\boxed{H_0 \approx 12.12 \ \text{MJ/m}^2/\text{day}}$$

---

### ✍️ **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **various angles used in solar geometry**. Define solar declination, hour angle, zenith angle, and altitude angle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **variation of extraterrestrial radiation with the time of year, time of day, and latitude**. Show how these variations affect the design of solar collectors. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

**Angles in Solar Geometry:**

1. **Solar Declination ($\delta$)** (1.5 marks): As defined earlier — angle between Sun–Earth line and equatorial plane. Varies from $-23.45°$ to $+23.45°$ annually.

2. **Hour Angle ($\omega$)** (1.5 marks): Angular displacement of the Sun east or west of the local meridian. $\omega = 0$ at solar noon; $\omega = +15°$ at 1 PM; $\omega = -15°$ at 11 AM.

3. **Zenith Angle ($\theta_z$)** (1.5 marks): Angle between the Sun's rays and the **vertical (zenith)** at the observer. $\theta_z = 0°$ when Sun is directly overhead; $\theta_z = 90°$ at sunrise/sunset.

4. **Altitude Angle ($\alpha$)** (1 mark): Angle between the Sun's rays and the **horizontal**. $\alpha = 90° - \theta_z$.

**Neat sketch of solar geometry (1.5 marks):** Draw a vertical line at observer, with the zenith above and horizon horizontal. Show the Sun's ray as a slanted line making angle $\theta_z$ with the vertical (or $\alpha$ with the horizontal). Mark declination and hour angle on a celestial sphere diagram. *(Use the functional block diagrams from Section 4 as reference for showing the angular relationships.)*

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**1. Variation with Time of Year (2 marks):**
- Caused by two effects: Earth's orbital eccentricity (captured by $E_0$) and axial tilt (captured by $\delta$).
- $E_0$ ranges from 0.967 (aphelion) to 1.033 (perihelion) → ±3.4% variation.
- $\delta$ varies sinusoidally between $-23.45°$ and $+23.45°$ → drastically affects the noon altitude of the Sun. In summer, the Sun is high (smaller $\theta_z$ → larger $I_{0H}$); in winter, the Sun is low (larger $\theta_z$ → smaller $I_{0H}$).

**2. Variation with Time of Day (2.5 marks):**
- At sunrise ($\omega = -\omega_s$): $I_{0H} = 0$ (Sun on horizon).
- As Sun rises towards meridian, $\omega$ approaches 0, and $\cos\theta_z$ increases → $I_{0H}$ increases.
- At solar noon ($\omega = 0$): $I_{0H}$ reaches its **maximum daily value**.
- After noon, $I_{0H}$ decreases symmetrically back to 0 at sunset.
- This daily bell-shaped curve is the basis for sizing battery storage in off-grid solar systems.

**3. Variation with Latitude (2.5 marks):**
- At the **equator** ($\phi = 0$): $\omega_s = 90°$ always, and the Sun rises high in the sky twice a year. $H_0$ is high and seasonally symmetric.
- At **mid-latitudes** ($\phi = 30°$–$45°$): strong seasonal variation; high summer $H_0$, low winter $H_0$.
- At **poles** ($\phi = 90°$): six months of continuous daylight/night; integration gives very different $H_0$ values.
- **Design impact:** Tilt angle of solar collectors is usually set to $\phi$ for annual optimum, $\phi - 15°$ for summer optimum, and $\phi + 15°$ for winter optimum.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in ETR Problems:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in $\omega_s$** — Students frequently plug $\omega_s$ in **degrees** into the formula. The integral yields correct values only if $\omega_s$ is in **radians**. The factor $24 \cdot 3600/\pi$ assumes radian input.
> 2. **Forgetting $E_0$** — Many students omit the eccentricity factor thinking $I_{sc}$ is sufficient. Examiners explicitly check whether you included $E_0$.
> 3. **Using solar declination wrong on Spring Equinox** — On March 21 (n=81), $\delta$ is **exactly 0°** — not 23.45°, not 0.5°. Using $n=80$ or $n=82$ shifts the value. Memorize: equinox = $\delta = 0$, solstice = $\delta = \pm 23.45°$.
> 4. **Not converting MJ to J or vice versa** — KTU may give $H_0$ in either unit. Always check: $1 \ \text{MJ} = 10^6 \ \text{J}$.
> 5. **Mixing latitude signs** — Northern hemisphere latitudes are positive; southern hemisphere latitudes are negative. Declination is positive in northern summer, negative in southern summer. Sign errors in $\sin\phi\sin\delta$ lead to wrong answers.
> 6. **Drawing incomplete diagrams** — A 7-mark derivation without the final boxed expression loses 1–2 marks. Always present the **final boxed result**.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Extraterrestrial Radiation:**

- 🔹 **Solar Constant:** $I_{sc} = 1367 \ W/m^2$ (WMO/ASHRAE standard).
- 🔹 **Eccentricity Factor:** $E_0 = 1 + 0.033 \cos(360n/365)$ — corrects for elliptical orbit; varies ±3.4% annually.
- 🔹 **Solar Declination:** $\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin[360(n+284)/365]$; range $\pm 23.45°$.
- 🔹 **Hour Angle:** $\omega = 15°(t_{solar} - 12)$; positive in afternoon, negative in morning.
- 🔹 **Sunset Hour Angle:** $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}[-\tan\phi\tan\delta]$ — used in daily integration; in radians!
- 🔹 **Cosine of Zenith:** $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$.
- 🔹 **Instantaneous Horizontal ETR:** $I_{0H} = I_{sc} E_0 \cos\theta_z$.
- 🔹 **Daily Integrated Horizontal ETR (Liu–Jordan):** $H_0 = \frac{24 \cdot 3600 \cdot I_{sc} E_0}{\pi}[\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \omega_s\sin\phi\sin\delta]$.
- 🔹 **Key Boundary Values:**
  - Summer solstice (n=172): $\delta = +23.45°$
  - Winter solstice (n=355): $\delta = -23.45°$
  - Equinoxes (n=81, n=264): $\delta = 0°$
  - Perihelion (n≈3): $E_0$ maximum
  - Aphelion (n≈184): $E_0$ minimum
- 🔹 **Clearness Index** (utility concept): $K_T = H/H_0$ — fraction of ETR that actually reaches the ground.
- 🔹 **Units of $H_0$:** J/m²/day (SI), divide by $10^6$ for MJ/m²/day.
- 🔹 **Design Rule of Thumb:** Tilt angle of fixed solar collectors $\approx \phi$ for annual optimum.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Revolution of Earth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET617 - SOLAR ENERGY CONSERVATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Revolution of Earth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Revolution of the Earth** is the continuous, elliptical orbital motion of the Earth around the Sun, completed in approximately **365.25 days** (one tropical year). This motion, combined with the **23.45° tilt** of Earth's rotational axis (obliquity), is the primary astronomical cause of seasonal variation, solar declination shift, and the variable intensity of extraterrestrial solar radiation received at any given location on Earth.

In the context of **Solar Energy Conversion Systems**, the revolution of the Earth governs three engineering-critical parameters:

1. **Solar Declination Angle ($\delta$)** – the angular position of the Sun north or south of the celestial equator.
2. **Equation of Time (EoT)** – the deviation of apparent solar time from mean solar time.
3. **Variation in Earth-Sun Distance ($d$ or $r$)** – causing the **eccentricity correction factor** ($E_0$) for extraterrestrial irradiance.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a tilted spinning top running around a circular track.**
> - The spinning top is the **Earth**, spinning once a day (rotation).
> - The tilted axis of the top (leaning 23.45° from vertical) is the **Earth's axial tilt**.
> - The track around a center lamp is the **Earth's orbit (revolution)** around the Sun.
> - As the top runs around the lamp, sometimes the top of the tilt faces the lamp (summer in the Northern Hemisphere), sometimes it leans away (winter). This is why we have **seasons**, even though the Sun's energy output is essentially constant.

The key engineering insight is: **the Sun is always shining with nearly the same intensity (≈ 1361 W/m²), but the geometry of Earth's orbit and tilt changes *how much* of that energy falls on a particular spot on Earth at a particular time.** That is the foundation of all solar collector design.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Mean Earth-Sun Distance | $1\,\text{AU}$ | $1.496 \times 10^{11}$ **m** |
| Semi-major axis of Earth's orbit | $a$ | $1.496 \times 10^{11}$ **m** |
| Orbital eccentricity | $e$ | $0.0167$ |
| Sidereal year (one revolution) | $T$ | $365.256$ **days** |
| Tropical year (seasons cycle) | $T_{trop}$ | $365.242$ **days** |
| Axial tilt (obliquity) | $\varepsilon$ | $23.45°$ |
| Mean orbital velocity | $v_{orb}$ | $29.78$ **km/s** |
| Solar constant | $I_{sc}$ | $1361$ **W/m²** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For all KTU 2024 numerical problems, the **accepted standard value of the Solar Constant is $I_{sc} = 1367$ W/m²** (older WMO standard) unless a specific problem statement dictates otherwise. Always read the question carefully.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Kepler's Laws of Planetary Motion (Foundation of Revolution)

Earth's revolution is mathematically described by **Johannes Kepler's three laws (1609–1619)**, derived empirically and later proven by Newton via gravitation.

### Kepler's First Law (Law of Ellipses)
> The orbit of the Earth is an **ellipse** with the Sun at **one of the two foci**.

### Kepler's Second Law (Law of Equal Areas)
> The line joining the Earth and the Sun sweeps out **equal areas during equal intervals of time**.

This means Earth moves **faster at perihelion** (closest to Sun, ~January 3) and **slower at aphelion** (farthest from Sun, ~July 4).

### Kepler's Third Law (Law of Harmonies)
> The square of the orbital period of a planet is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis of its orbit.

$$T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{G M_s} \, a^3$$

where:
- $T$ = orbital period
- $a$ = semi-major axis
- $G$ = universal gravitational constant = $6.674 \times 10^{-11}$ N·m²/kg²
- $M_s$ = mass of the Sun = $1.989 \times 10^{30}$ kg

## 2.2 Earth's Orbital Parameters

The Earth's orbit is an **ellipse**, not a perfect circle. The geometry of this ellipse is defined by:

- **Perihelion ($r_{per}$)** = $a(1 - e) \approx 1.471 \times 10^{11}$ m
- **Aphelion ($r_{ap}$)** = $a(1 + e) \approx 1.521 \times 10^{11}$ m
- **Mean distance** = $a = 1.496 \times 10^{11}$ m

> [!NOTE]
> Although the distance variation is about **3.3%** (perihelion is 1.47 × 10¹¹ m vs aphelion 1.52 × 10¹¹ m), it causes approximately a **6.9% variation in extraterrestrial solar irradiance** because irradiance follows an **inverse-square law** with distance.

## 2.3 Solar Declination Angle ($\delta$)

The **solar declination angle** is the angle between the line joining the Earth's center to the Sun and the Earth's equatorial plane. It varies from **$-23.45°$** (Winter Solstice, Dec 21) to **$+23.45°$** (Summer Solstice, Jun 21).

$$ \delta = 23.45° \times \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right] $$

where $n$ = day of the year (Jan 1 = 1, Dec 31 = 365).

A more accurate (Cooper's) form:

$$ \delta = 23.45° \times \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(n - 81)\right] $$

For KTU, the first form (with $284$) is more commonly used in answer keys.

## 2.4 Equation of Time (EoT)

The **Equation of Time** corrects for the fact that the actual solar day varies throughout the year due to two combined effects:
1. **Earth's orbital eccentricity** (non-uniform speed)
2. **Axial tilt** (obliquity)

$$ E_oT = 9.87 \sin(2B) - 7.53 \cos(B) - 1.5 \sin(B) $$

where $B = \frac{360}{364}(n - 81)$ degrees.

The value of EoT varies from about **$-14$ minutes** to **$+16$ minutes** through the year.

## 2.5 Eccentricity Correction Factor ($E_0$)

Because Earth's distance from the Sun changes, the **extraterrestrial normal irradiance** $I_{on}$ on a surface perpendicular to the Sun's rays also varies:

$$ E_0 = \left(\frac{d_0}{d}\right)^2 = 1 + 0.033 \cos\left(\frac{360\, n}{365}\right) $$

So:

$$ I_{on} = I_{sc} \cdot E_0 $$

> $E_0 \approx 1.033$ at perihelion (maximum) and $E_0 \approx 0.967$ at aphelion (minimum).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Use Case | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Kepler's Third Law | $T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{GM_s} a^3$ | Compute orbital period | s, m |
| Perihelion distance | $r_{per} = a(1 - e)$ | Closest approach | m |
| Aphelion distance | $r_{ap} = a(1 + e)$ | Farthest approach | m |
| Mean Orbital Velocity | $v_{orb} = \frac{2\pi a}{T}$ | Average speed of Earth | m/s |
| Solar Declination | $\delta = 23.45° \sin\left[\frac{360(284+n)}{365}\right]$ | Sun's angular position | degrees |
| Eccentricity Factor | $E_0 = 1 + 0.033 \cos\left(\frac{360\,n}{365}\right)$ | Distance correction | dimensionless |
| Extraterrestrial Irradiance | $I_{on} = I_{sc} \cdot E_0$ | Beam radiation on $A_{\perp}$ | W/m² |
| Equation of Time | $E_oT = 9.87 \sin(2B) - 7.53 \cos(B) - 1.5 \sin(B)$ | Solar time correction | minutes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU examinations, **deriving $\delta$ from the basic geometry of the tilted Earth orbit** is worth 4–5 marks when asked. Memorize the form **$\delta = 23.45° \sin(\text{angular day})$** — the angle inside sine changes depending on the textbook, but the **23.45° amplitude** is non-negotiable.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Solar PV Plant Design** – tilt angle of panels is set using the **maximum declination angle** ($+23.45°$ for sites in the Northern Hemisphere).
2. **Solar Thermal Collectors** – seasonal tracking strategies rely on accurate $\delta$ values.
3. **Agricultural / HVAC Loads** – seasonal variation of solar input drives cooling/heating load calculations.
4. **Satellite Orbit Design** – Kepler's laws directly govern mission planning for Earth-observation satellites that study solar irradiance.
5. **Smart Grid & Storage Sizing** – battery storage for off-grid solar systems is sized based on the **winter solstice** (lowest solar input).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Kepler's Third Law from Newton's Law of Gravitation

**Starting point:** Newton's law of universal gravitation provides the centripetal force for circular (or near-circular) orbital motion.

**Step 1 — Equate gravitational force to centripetal force.**

For Earth of mass $m_e$ orbiting the Sun of mass $M_s$ at radius $a$ (semi-major axis for an ellipse; radius for a circle), Newton's second law for circular motion states:

$$F_{grav} = F_{centripetal}$$

**Step 2 — Write the gravitational force.**

$$F_{grav} = \frac{G \, M_s \, m_e}{a^2}$$

**Step 3 — Write the centripetal force.**

$$F_{cent} = \frac{m_e \, v^2}{a} = m_e \, \omega^2 \, a = m_e \left(\frac{2\pi}{T}\right)^2 a$$

**Step 4 — Equate both forces.**

$$\frac{G \, M_s \, m_e}{a^2} = m_e \left(\frac{2\pi}{T}\right)^2 a$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $m_e$ and rearrange.**

$$\frac{G \, M_s}{a^2} = \frac{4\pi^2 \, a}{T^2}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $T^2$.**

$$T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{G \, M_s} \, a^3$$

**This is Kepler's Third Law.**

**Step 7 — Numerical verification for Earth.**

$$T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{(6.674 \times 10^{-11})(1.989 \times 10^{30})} (1.496 \times 10^{11})^3$$

$$T^2 = \frac{39.478}{1.327 \times 10^{20}} \times 3.348 \times 10^{33}$$

$$T^2 = 2.973 \times 10^{-19} \times 3.348 \times 10^{33} = 9.954 \times 10^{14} \, \text{s}^2$$

$$T = \sqrt{9.954 \times 10^{14}} \approx 9.977 \times 10^{7} \, \text{s}$$

Converting to days:

$$T = \frac{9.977 \times 10^{7}}{86400} \approx 365.25 \, \text{days} \;\;\checkmark$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Solar Declination Angle

**Geometric Setup.** The Earth's axis of rotation is tilted by angle $\varepsilon = 23.45°$ from the perpendicular to the orbital plane (the **ecliptic**). On any given day of the year, the Earth-Sun line makes an angle with the equatorial plane; this angle is the **solar declination** $\delta$.

**Step 1 — Day-counting convention.**

Let $n$ be the **day of the year**, with $n = 1$ on January 1. The Earth completes one full orbit in $N = 365$ days, sweeping a full $360°$.

**Step 2 — Angular position on the orbit.**

The Earth has completed a fraction $\frac{n}{N}$ of its orbit since January 1, which corresponds to an orbital angle of:

$$\theta = \frac{360}{365} \times n \quad \text{(degrees)}$$

**Step 3 — Reference the Vernal Equinox (March 21).**

The Vernal Equinox occurs on approximately day $n = 81$. The declination is **zero** at the equinoxes and **maximum ($\pm 23.45°$)** at the solstices. To synchronize $\delta = 0$ at $n = 81$, we shift the argument:

$$\theta' = \frac{360}{365} (n - 81)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the sine projection.**

The declination is the projection of the axial tilt onto the Earth-Sun line. For a circular orbit (a very good approximation since $e = 0.0167$ is small), this projection is sinusoidal:

$$\boxed{\;\delta = 23.45° \times \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(n - 81)\right]\;}$$

**Step 5 — Verification at solstices.**

- **Summer Solstice (June 21, $n = 172$):**

$$\delta = 23.45° \times \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(172 - 81)\right] = 23.45° \times \sin(89.75°) \approx +23.43° \;\checkmark$$

- **Winter Solstice (December 21, $n = 355$):**

$$\delta = 23.45° \times \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(355 - 81)\right] = 23.45° \times \sin(270.25°) \approx -23.43° \;\checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Alternative form (used in KTU key):**

Many textbooks write the formula as:

$$\delta = 23.45° \times \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right]$$

This is algebraically equivalent because:

$$\frac{360}{365}(n - 81) = \frac{360}{365}(n + 284) - 360$$

and $\sin(\alpha - 360°) = \sin(\alpha)$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Eccentricity Correction Factor

**Step 1 — Inverse-square law of radiation.**

The energy flux at distance $d$ from the Sun is:

$$I_{on} = \frac{P_s}{4\pi d^2}$$

where $P_s$ is the Sun's total radiative power (luminosity) and $4\pi d^2$ is the surface area of a sphere of radius $d$.

**Step 2 — Reference at mean distance $d_0$ (1 AU).**

At $d = d_0 = 1$ AU, the flux is the solar constant $I_{sc}$:

$$I_{sc} = \frac{P_s}{4\pi d_0^2}$$

**Step 3 — Take the ratio.**

$$\frac{I_{on}}{I_{sc}} = \frac{d_0^2}{d^2} = \left(\frac{d_0}{d}\right)^2$$

**Step 4 — Define the eccentricity factor.**

$$E_0 = \left(\frac{d_0}{d}\right)^2$$

**Step 5 — Express $d$ in terms of day of year.**

Earth's orbit is an ellipse, and the distance varies sinusoidally (to first order in $e$):

$$d = d_0 \left[1 - e \cos\left(\frac{360\,n}{365}\right)\right]$$

**Step 6 — Expand $E_0$ for small $e$.**

$$E_0 = \frac{1}{[1 - e\cos\theta]^2} \approx 1 + 2e\cos\theta + \ldots$$

**Step 7 — Insert $e = 0.0167$.**

$$2e = 2 \times 0.0167 \approx 0.0334 \approx 0.033$$

$$\boxed{\;E_0 = 1 + 0.033 \cos\left(\frac{360\, n}{365}\right)\;}$$

**Step 8 — Verification.**

At $n = 1$ (close to perihelion, $n_{perihelion} \approx 3$):

$$E_0 = 1 + 0.033 \cos(0.99°) \approx 1.033 \;\checkmark$$

At $n = 185$ (close to aphelion, $n_{aphelion} \approx 186$):

$$E_0 = 1 + 0.033 \cos(182.5°) \approx 0.967 \;\checkmark$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Key Differences to Remember

| Feature | Perihelion | Aphelion |
|---|---|---|
| Approximate Date | January 3 | July 4 |
| Distance ($d$) | $1.471 \times 10^{11}$ m | $1.521 \times 10^{11}$ m |
| $E_0$ | $\approx 1.033$ (max) | $\approx 0.967$ (min) |
| Earth's Orbital Speed | $\approx 30.3$ km/s (max) | $\approx 29.3$ km/s (min) |
| Northern Hemisphere Season | Winter | Summer |
| Solar Input at Top of Atmosphere | $\sim 1413$ W/m² | $\sim 1321$ W/m² |
| Solar Declination ($\delta$) | $\approx -23°$ | $\approx +23°$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Counter-intuitive point (favorite KTU question):** During Northern Hemisphere winter, the Earth is actually **closest** to the Sun (perihelion). This proves that the **seasons are caused by axial tilt, not by distance variation**. The ~6.9% irradiance difference is small compared to the ~40% difference caused by the Sun's low altitude (large zenith angle) in winter.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Earth's Elliptical Orbit — Top-Down View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sun at Focus F1] --- B((Perihelion\nr = 1.471e11 m\nJan 3))]
    A --- C((Earth on Day n))]
    C --- D((Aphelion\nr = 1.521e11 m\nJul 4))]
    A --- E((Focus F2\nEmpty))
    B --- F((Mean Distance\nr = 1.496e11 m\n1 AU))]
    F --- D
    C -.->|Revolution Path| G((Counter-clockwise\nOrbit 365.25 days))
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Sun sits at one focus** of the elliptical orbit (Kepler's First Law).
- Earth moves **counter-clockwise** when viewed from above the North Pole.
- Earth is at **perihelion** (closest) in early January and at **aphelion** (farthest) in early July.

## 4.2 Axial Tilt and Declination — Side View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OrbitPlane["Ecliptic Plane - Orbital Path"]
        direction LR
        H((Jun 21\nSummer Solstice\nN. Hemis)) --> I((Sep 22\nAutumnal Equinox))
        I --> J((Dec 21\nWinter Solstice\nN. Hemis))
        J --> K((Mar 21\nVernal Equinox))
        K --> H
    end
    subgraph AxisView["Earth Axis Tilt 23.45 degrees"]
        Tilt["Tilt always points\ntoward Polaris\nNorth Star"]
    end
    H -.->|delta = +23.45 deg| Sun1[Sun Rays]
    I -.->|delta = 0 deg| Sun2[Sun Rays]
    J -.->|delta = -23.45 deg| Sun3[Sun Rays]
    K -.->|delta = 0 deg| Sun4[Sun Rays]
```

**Key takeaway:** The axis **always points in the same direction in space** (toward Polaris). As Earth revolves, the **relative angle between the Earth-Sun line and the equatorial plane** (i.e., $\delta$) changes, producing seasons.

## 4.3 Annual Cycle of Declination — Engineering Reference

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([Day n = 1\nJan 1]) --> N1[delta approx minus 23 deg]
    N1 --> N2[delta = 0\nMar 21 Vernal Equinox]
    N2 --> N3[delta = +23.45 deg\nJun 21 Summer Solstice]
    N3 --> N4[delta = 0\nSep 22 Autumnal Equinox]
    N4 --> N5[delta = -23.45 deg\nDec 21 Winter Solstice]
    N5 --> End([Day n = 365\nDec 31])
    N1 -.->|Sinusoidal Variation| N5
```

**Engineering use:** This sinusoidal $\delta(n)$ curve is used to compute the **optimum tilt angle** for a fixed solar panel at any latitude $\phi$:

$$\beta_{opt} \approx \phi - \delta$$

## 4.4 Energy Flow — From Sun to Solar Collector

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sun Core Fusion\nP = 3.828e26 W] --> B[Photosphere Emission\nSurface T = 5778 K]
    B --> C[Extraterrestrial Irradiance\nI_on = I_sc x E0\n1361 W per m2 at 1 AU]
    C --> D[Atmospheric Attenuation\nAbsorption + Scattering]
    D --> E[Ground-Level Global Irradiance\nI = I_b + I_d]
    E --> F[Solar PV / Thermal Collector\nElectrical / Thermal Output]
    F --> G[Load or Grid]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Revolution of the Earth** enters this diagram at the step **$I_{on} = I_{sc} \cdot E_0(n)$** — the revolution determines $E_0$ for any given day $n$. **This is the only place in the chain where revolution directly affects the energy value**; tilt and rotation affect the *angle*, not the *magnitude*, of the incoming beam.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Solar Declination Angle. Write its formula and state its maximum and minimum values. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> The **Solar Declination Angle ($\delta$)** is defined as the angle between the line joining the centre of the Earth to the centre of the Sun, and the Earth's equatorial plane.

**Formula:**

$$\delta = 23.45° \times \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(n - 81)\right]$$

where $n$ = day of the year.

**Extreme values:**

- **Maximum:** $\delta = +23.45°$ on the **Summer Solstice (June 21)**.
- **Minimum:** $\delta = -23.45°$ on the **Winter Solstice (December 21)**.
- Declination is **zero** on the **Equinoxes** (March 21 and September 22).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students write $\delta = 23.45° \times \sin(\theta)$ without the **amplitude 23.45°**, or forget to state the units as **degrees**. Examiners will deduct 0.5–1 mark for this.

---

### Q2. What is meant by the eccentricity correction factor? Why is it required? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> The **eccentricity correction factor ($E_0$)** is the ratio of the **actual extraterrestrial irradiance** $I_{on}$ on a surface normal to the Sun's rays, to the **solar constant** $I_{sc}$, on a given day of the year.

**Formula:**

$$E_0 = \left(\frac{d_0}{d}\right)^2 = 1 + 0.033 \cos\left(\frac{360\,n}{365}\right)$$

**Why it is required:**
- The Earth's orbit is an **ellipse** (Kepler's First Law), not a perfect circle.
- The Earth-Sun distance $d$ varies from $1.471 \times 10^{11}$ m (perihelion) to $1.521 \times 10^{11}$ m (aphelion) — about a **3.4%** variation.
- Since irradiance follows the **inverse-square law** ($I \propto 1/d^2$), the actual beam intensity at the top of the atmosphere varies by about **6.9%** over the year.
- $E_0$ corrects the standard solar constant to give the **day-specific extraterrestrial irradiance**: $I_{on} = I_{sc} \cdot E_0$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Revolution, Orbit & Kepler's Laws `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** State and explain **Kepler's three laws of planetary motion** with neat diagrams. Derive Kepler's Third Law from Newton's law of gravitation. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** For the Earth's orbit around the Sun, given that the semi-major axis is $a = 1.496 \times 10^{11}$ m and the eccentricity is $e = 0.0167$, calculate:
  (i) the perihelion and aphelion distances,
  (ii) the mean orbital velocity, and
  (iii) the variation in extraterrestrial solar irradiance between perihelion and aphelion. Take $I_{sc} = 1367$ W/m². **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Kepler's Laws:**

**1. Law of Ellipses:** *All planets, including Earth, move in elliptical orbits with the Sun at one focus.*

**2. Law of Equal Areas:** *The line joining a planet to the Sun (the radius vector) sweeps out equal areas in equal intervals of time.* This implies the planet moves **faster near perihelion** and **slower near aphelion**.

**3. Law of Harmonies:** *The square of the orbital period of a planet is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis of its orbit.*

$$T^2 \propto a^3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{GM_s}a^3$$

**Derivation of Kepler's Third Law:**

For a planet of mass $m$ in a (near-)circular orbit of radius $a$ around a central body of mass $M_s$, Newton's law of gravitation provides the centripetal force:

$$F_{grav} = \frac{G M_s m}{a^2}$$

The required centripetal force for circular motion is:

$$F_{cent} = m \omega^2 a = m \left(\frac{2\pi}{T}\right)^2 a$$

Equating:

$$\frac{G M_s m}{a^2} = m \left(\frac{4\pi^2}{T^2}\right) a$$

Rearranging:

$$T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{G M_s} a^3 \quad \blacksquare$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $a = 1.496 \times 10^{11}$ m, $e = 0.0167$, $I_{sc} = 1367$ W/m².

**(i) Perihelion and Aphelion distances:**

$$r_{per} = a(1 - e) = 1.496 \times 10^{11} \times (1 - 0.0167)$$

$$r_{per} = 1.496 \times 10^{11} \times 0.9833 = 1.471 \times 10^{11} \, \text{m}$$

$$r_{ap} = a(1 + e) = 1.496 \times 10^{11} \times (1 + 0.0167)$$

$$r_{ap} = 1.496 \times 10^{11} \times 1.0167 = 1.521 \times 10^{11} \, \text{m}$$

**(ii) Mean orbital velocity:**

The period of the Earth is $T = 365.25 \times 24 \times 3600 = 3.156 \times 10^{7}$ s.

$$v_{orb} = \frac{2\pi a}{T} = \frac{2\pi \times 1.496 \times 10^{11}}{3.156 \times 10^{7}}$$

$$v_{orb} = \frac{9.40 \times 10^{11}}{3.156 \times 10^{7}} \approx 2.978 \times 10^{4} \, \text{m/s} \approx 29.78 \, \text{km/s}$$

**(iii) Variation in extraterrestrial irradiance:**

At perihelion, using the inverse-square law:

$$I_{on,per} = I_{sc} \left(\frac{a}{r_{per}}\right)^2 = 1367 \times \left(\frac{1.496 \times 10^{11}}{1.471 \times 10^{11}}\right)^2$$

$$I_{on,per} = 1367 \times (1.0170)^2 = 1367 \times 1.0343 \approx 1414 \, \text{W/m}^2$$

At aphelion:

$$I_{on,ap} = I_{sc} \left(\frac{a}{r_{ap}}\right)^2 = 1367 \times \left(\frac{1.496 \times 10^{11}}{1.521 \times 10^{11}}\right)^2$$

$$I_{on,ap} = 1367 \times (0.9836)^2 = 1367 \times 0.9674 \approx 1322 \, \text{W/m}^2$$

**Variation:**

$$\Delta I = I_{on,per} - I_{on,ap} = 1414 - 1322 = 92 \, \text{W/m}^2$$

**Percentage variation:**

$$\frac{\Delta I}{I_{sc}} \times 100\% = \frac{92}{1367} \times 100\% \approx 6.73\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. Students often use $T = 365$ days instead of $365.25$ days, giving a velocity error of ~0.07%. (Loss: 0.5 mark.)
> 2. Forgetting to **square the distance ratio** in part (iii). (Loss: 1 mark.)
> 3. Writing the answer in km/s but without stating the unit clearly. (Loss: 0.5 mark.)
> 4. Not mentioning the **tropical year** ($365.242$ days) vs **sidereal year** ($365.256$ days) — for solar energy work, the **tropical year (365.25)** is the correct reference. (Loss: 0.5 mark.)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Solar Declination, Eccentricity Factor & Equation of Time `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **solar declination angle** $\delta$ as a function of the day of the year $n$. State the values of $\delta$ at the equinoxes and solstices. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** For the 100th day of the year, calculate:
  (i) the solar declination angle,
  (ii) the eccentricity correction factor, and
  (iii) the extraterrestrial normal irradiance. Take $I_{sc} = 1367$ W/m². **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Derivation of $\delta(n)$:**

*(Full derivation as in Section 3.2.)*

The Earth's rotational axis is tilted at $\varepsilon = 23.45°$ from the perpendicular to the ecliptic plane. As Earth revolves around the Sun with day-count $n$ (Jan 1 = 1), the Earth-Sun line makes a continually varying angle with the equatorial plane.

**Step 1 — Orbital angle since Vernal Equinox (March 21 ≈ day 81):**

$$\theta = \frac{360}{365}(n - 81) \quad \text{(in degrees)}$$

**Step 2 — Project axial tilt onto Earth-Sun line:**

For small eccentricity, this projection is sinusoidal:

$$\delta = 23.45° \times \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(n - 81)\right]$$

**Values at key dates:**

| Event | Date | Day $n$ | $\delta$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vernal Equinox | March 21 | 81 | $0°$ |
| Summer Solstice | June 21 | 172 | $+23.45°$ |
| Autumnal Equinox | September 22 | 265 | $0°$ |
| Winter Solstice | December 21 | 355 | $-23.45°$ |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $n = 100$, $I_{sc} = 1367$ W/m².

**(i) Solar declination angle:**

$$\delta = 23.45° \times \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(100 - 81)\right]$$

$$\delta = 23.45° \times \sin\left[\frac{360 \times 19}{365}\right]$$

$$\delta = 23.45° \times \sin(18.74°)$$

$$\delta = 23.45° \times 0.3214 \approx 7.54°$$

So $\delta \approx +7.54°$ (Sun is north of the equator, moving toward the Tropic of Cancer).

**(ii) Eccentricity correction factor:**

$$E_0 = 1 + 0.033 \cos\left(\frac{360 \times 100}{365}\right)$$

$$E_0 = 1 + 0.033 \cos(98.63°)$$

$$E_0 = 1 + 0.033 \times (-0.1484)$$

$$E_0 \approx 1 - 0.00490 \approx 0.9951$$

**(iii) Extraterrestrial normal irradiance:**

$$I_{on} = I_{sc} \times E_0 = 1367 \times 0.9951$$

$$\boxed{I_{on} \approx 1360.3 \, \text{W/m}^2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):**
> 1. **Degree-Radian trap:** When using a calculator, ensure the mode is set to **DEG**, not RAD. A common student error gives a wildly different $\delta$. (Loss: 2 marks.)
> 2. **Sign of cosine:** Day 100 is **after aphelion** (July 4 ≈ day 185), so the cosine argument is near $90°$+ and gives a **slightly negative correction**. Always check the sign.
> 3. **Day-count convention:** January 1 = day 1 (not day 0). Some books use day 0, which shifts $\delta$ by ~1°. Follow the **standard convention $n = 1$ on Jan 1**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Bullet Points — Memorize for ESE:**

- **Revolution period** of Earth = **365.25 days** (tropical year for solar energy work).
- **Orbit shape** = Ellipse with **eccentricity $e = 0.0167$** (Kepler's 1st Law).
- **Axial tilt (obliquity)** = $\varepsilon = 23.45°$ — this is the **cause of seasons**, not distance variation.
- **Perihelion** ≈ Jan 3, $r_{per} = 1.471 \times 10^{11}$ m (Earth moves **fastest** here).
- **Aphelion** ≈ Jul 4, $r_{ap} = 1.521 \times 10^{11}$ m (Earth moves **slowest** here).
- **Mean orbital velocity** = **29.78 km/s** (use $v = 2\pi a / T$).
- **Solar declination formula (KTU standard):**
$$\delta = 23.45° \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(n - 81)\right]$$
- **Eccentricity factor (KTU standard):**
$$E_0 = 1 + 0.033 \cos\left(\frac{360 n}{365}\right)$$
- **Extraterrestrial normal irradiance:** $I_{on} = I_{sc} \cdot E_0$, with $I_{sc} = 1367$ W/m².
- **Kepler's 3rd Law:** $T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{GM_s} a^3$ (derivable from Newton's gravitation — **derivation is a guaranteed 4–5 mark question**).
- **Equation of Time (EoT)** combines eccentricity + obliquity effects; range is **$-14$ to $+16$ minutes**.
- **Engineering applications:** Solar panel tilt optimization, seasonal collector design, satellite mission planning, off-grid battery sizing.
- **Mnemonic to remember solstice dates:** *June Sol = Sun up North (N. Hem summer) → $\delta = +23.45°$*. *December Sol = Sun down South → $\delta = -23.45°$*.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Examiner's Tip:** When a question asks *"Explain the role of Earth's revolution in solar energy"*, structure your answer in **three layers**: (1) astronomical cause (ellipse + tilt), (2) measurable parameters ($\delta$, $E_0$, EoT), (3) engineering consequences (panel tilt, seasonal radiation, storage sizing). A 7-mark answer must touch **all three layers** to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Seasons
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET617 - SOLAR ENERGY CONSERVATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Seasons

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Seasons** are periodic climatic variations caused by the **23.45° axial tilt** of the Earth's rotational axis relative to the plane of its orbit (the **ecliptic plane**), combined with the Earth's annual revolution around the Sun. This tilt causes varying solar incidence angles and day lengths at any given latitude throughout the year, producing the four conventional seasons: **Spring (Vernal)**, **Summer**, **Autumn (Autumnal)**, and **Winter**.

The key astronomical drivers of the seasons are:

- **Axial Tilt (Obliquity)**: The Earth's axis is tilted at **23.45°** from the perpendicular to the ecliptic plane. This value varies slowly between **22.1° and 24.5°** over a 41,000-year cycle.
- **Revolution**: Earth completes one orbit around the Sun in **365.25 days** in an **elliptical path** with eccentricity **e = 0.0167**.
- **Rotation**: Earth spins on its own axis once every **24 hours**, giving rise to the diurnal cycle.
- **Direction of Tilt**: The North Pole consistently points toward **Polaris** (the North Star) throughout the year; the orientation in space is fixed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Misconception**: Seasons are *NOT* caused by the Earth's varying distance from the Sun. In fact, Earth is **closest to the Sun (Perihelion)** around **January 3rd** and **farthest (Aphelion)** around **July 4th** — counter-intuitive to Northern Hemisphere seasons. It is the **tilt**, not the **distance**, that governs seasons.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a flashlight (the Sun) shining straight down onto a globe (the Earth). If the globe is held **upright**, every point on its surface receives roughly the same intensity of light. Now **tilt the globe** by 23.45° and walk it in a circle around the flashlight.

- When the **North Pole tilts toward the Sun** → the Northern Hemisphere receives more direct (concentrated) rays and longer daylight → **Summer** in the North.
- When the **North Pole tilts away from the Sun** → the Northern Hemisphere receives slanted (spread-out) rays and shorter daylight → **Winter** in the North.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy**: Think of a torchlight beam on a wall. When the wall is **perpendicular** to the beam, you get a small bright circle (high intensity — Summer). When the wall is **slanted**, the same energy spreads over a larger oval area (lower intensity — Winter). This is exactly how solar energy gets distributed seasonally on Earth.

---

### 1.3 Key Astronomical Constants

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Axial Tilt (Obliquity) | ε | **23.45°** | Tilt of Earth's axis from ecliptic perpendicular |
| Orbital Eccentricity | e | **0.0167** | Deviation of Earth's orbit from a circle |
| Semi-Major Axis | a | **149.6 × 10⁶ km** | Mean Earth-Sun distance (1 AU) |
| Perihelion Distance | r_p | **147.1 × 10⁶ km** | Closest approach to Sun (~Jan 3) |
| Aphelion Distance | r_a | **152.1 × 10⁶ km** | Farthest from Sun (~Jul 4) |
| Orbital Period | T | **365.25 days** | One revolution around the Sun |
| Rotation Period | — | **24 hours** | One spin on axis (solar day) |

---

### 1.4 The Four Key Solar Positions

| Event | Approximate Date (N. Hemisphere) | Solar Declination (δ) | Day Length at Equator | Characteristic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Vernal Equinox (Spring)** | March 21 | **0°** | 12 hr | Sun crosses equator northward |
| **Summer Solstice** | June 21 | **+23.45°** | 12 hr | Maximum N. Hemisphere insolation |
| **Autumnal Equinox (Fall)** | September 23 | **0°** | 12 hr | Sun crosses equator southward |
| **Winter Solstice** | December 22 | **−23.45°** | 12 hr | Minimum N. Hemisphere insolation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Seasons Occur — The Governing Physics

The seasons arise from the **changing solar declination angle (δ)** throughout the year, which in turn modifies three critical quantities at any given latitude (φ):

1. **The solar altitude angle (α)** at solar noon
2. **The day length (N)** — hours of sunshine
3. **The intensity of solar radiation** per unit horizontal area (insolation)

#### Step-by-Step Logic of Seasonal Variation

- **Step 1 — The Earth revolves around the Sun with a fixed axial tilt**.
  - *Why*: The gravitational pull of the Sun on Earth's equatorial bulge causes a **precession**, but the *direction* of the tilt remains constant in space.
- **Step 2 — As Earth moves along its orbit, different hemispheres "face" the Sun more directly**.
  - *How*: When the Northern Hemisphere is tilted toward the Sun, the solar rays strike the ground more *perpendicularly*, concentrating energy over a smaller surface area.
- **Step 3 — The solar declination (δ) varies sinusoidally between −23.45° and +23.45°**.
  - *Why*: δ is the angle between the Sun's rays and the equatorial plane. At the solstices, δ reaches its extreme values.
- **Step 4 — Day length (N) varies with latitude and δ**.
  - *How*: At higher latitudes, day length becomes much longer in summer and much shorter in winter, amplifying the seasonal energy imbalance.
- **Step 5 — The combination of high noon altitude and long day length → Summer; low altitude and short day length → Winter**.

---

### 2.2 The Solar Declination Angle (δ)

The **solar declination** is the angle between the Sun's rays and the Earth's equatorial plane. It is the *single most important parameter* governing seasonality.

**Governing Equation (Cooper's Equation):**

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360^\circ}{365} \cdot (n + 284)\right]$$

where:
- $\delta$ = Solar declination angle (in degrees)
- $n$ = Day of the year (January 1 = 1, December 31 = 365)

**Boundary Conditions:**
- Summer Solstice (n = 172): $\delta = +23.45°$
- Winter Solstice (n = 355): $\delta = -23.45°$
- Equinoxes (n = 81, n = 266): $\delta = 0°$

---

### 2.3 Day Length (Sunshine Hours)

The number of hours between sunrise and sunset is given by:

$$N = \frac{2}{15} \cdot \cos^{-1}\left(-\tan(\phi) \cdot \tan(\delta)\right)$$

where:
- $N$ = Day length in hours
- $\phi$ = Latitude of the location (in degrees, positive for N. Hemisphere)
- $\delta$ = Solar declination (in degrees)

**Special Cases:**
- At the **Equator** ($\phi = 0°$): $N = 12$ hours *every day of the year*.
- At the **Arctic/Antarctic Circle** ($\phi = 66.5°$): $N = 24$ hours on the summer solstice and $N = 0$ hours on the winter solstice.
- At the **Poles** ($\phi = 90°$): $N = 6$ months of daylight followed by 6 months of darkness.

---

### 2.4 Hour Angle (ω) and Solar Time

The **hour angle** measures the angular displacement of the Sun from the local meridian:

$$\omega = 15^\circ \cdot (t_{\text{solar}} - 12)$$

where:
- $\omega$ = Hour angle (in degrees, negative before noon, positive after noon)
- $t_{\text{solar}}$ = Local solar time (in hours, 0–24)

**Sunrise and sunset hour angles are always equal in magnitude and opposite in sign:**

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}\left(-\tan(\phi) \cdot \tan(\delta)\right)$$

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Solar Declination | $\delta = 23.45 \cdot \sin\left(\frac{360}{365}(n+284)\right)$ | degrees | Cooper's Equation |
| 2 | Hour Angle | $\omega = 15 \cdot (t_{\text{solar}} - 12)$ | degrees | $\omega = 0°$ at solar noon |
| 3 | Day Length | $N = \frac{2}{15} \cos^{-1}\left(-\tan\phi \cdot \tan\delta\right)$ | hours | Valid for $\vert\phi + \delta\vert < 90°$ |
| 4 | Sunrise/Sunset Hour Angle | $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}\left(-\tan\phi \cdot \tan\delta\right)$ | degrees | $\omega_s \in [0°, 90°]$ |
| 5 | Altitude Angle (noon) | $\alpha_n = 90° - \vert\phi - \delta\vert$ | degrees | At solar noon |
| 6 | Zenith Angle (noon) | $\theta_z = \vert\phi - \delta\vert$ | degrees | $\theta_z + \alpha = 90°$ |
| 7 | Earth-Sun Distance Factor | $\left(\frac{r_0}{r}\right)^2 = 1 + 0.033 \cos\left(\frac{360n}{365}\right)$ | dimensionless | Eccentricity correction |
| 8 | Solar Constant | $G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2$ | W/m² | Extraterrestrial irradiance |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding seasons is **not abstract astronomy** — it has direct engineering applications:

- **Solar PV System Design**: Tilt angle and azimuth of solar panels are optimized using the *average* or *seasonal* declination. Fixed-tilt panels are typically set to **latitude ± 15°** for summer/winter optimization.
- **Solar Thermal Collectors**: Seasonal variation in declination dictates the *monthly optimum tilt* of flat-plate collectors.
- **Building Design**: Orientation of windows (south-facing in N. Hemisphere) leverages low winter sun (good for heating) while blocking high summer sun (for cooling).
- **Agricultural Planning**: Crop selection and irrigation scheduling depend heavily on seasonal solar availability.
- **Solar Tracking Systems**: Single-axis and dual-axis trackers continuously adjust for the daily *and* seasonal position of the Sun.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Solar Declination Equation (Cooper's Equation)

The solar declination is not constant; it varies throughout the year because the Earth's rotational axis is tilted by **23.45°** with respect to the ecliptic plane. We can derive Cooper's empirical formula by considering the geometry of Earth's orbit.

#### Step 1: Geometry of the Earth-Sun System

Consider the Earth at two positions: at the **Vernal Equinox** (n = 81) and at an arbitrary day of the year. The Sun-Earth line, projected onto the equatorial plane, makes an angle with the Earth's equatorial plane. That angle is the **solar declination (δ)**.

#### Step 2: Maximum Declination

At the **Summer Solstice** (n = 172), the Sun is directly over the **Tropic of Cancer (23.45° N)**. Therefore:

$$\delta_{\max} = +23.45^\circ$$

This is the *amplitude* of the declination variation.

#### Step 3: Sinusoidal Variation Assumption

Observation shows that δ varies approximately **sinusoidally** with the day of the year. A general sinusoidal form is:

$$\delta = A \cdot \sin(B \cdot (n - C))$$

where A, B, C are constants to be determined.

#### Step 4: Apply the Boundary Conditions

**Condition 1**: The maximum declination is +23.45° at n = 172.

$$\delta_{\max} = A = 23.45^\circ$$

**Condition 2**: The declination is zero at the **Vernal Equinox**, n = 81.

$$0 = 23.45 \cdot \sin(B \cdot (81 - 172))$$

$$0 = \sin(-91 \cdot B) \implies B \cdot 91 = -180^\circ$$

$$B = -\frac{180^\circ}{91} \approx -1.978^\circ/\text{day}$$

The *negative sign* arises because the sine function reaches its maximum *before* n = 172 if we consider the convention used by Cooper. To express the declination with the equinox at n = 81, we shift the argument:

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360^\circ}{365} \cdot (n - 81)\right]$$

**Condition 3**: Shifting the reference so that the formula yields δ = 0 at n = 81 *and* the maximum at n = 172:

Using the identity that a phase shift of (n − 81) gives a peak at n = 81 + 365/4 = 81 + 91.25 ≈ 172, the form is consistent. The convention used in Cooper's formula is:

$$\boxed{\delta = 23.45^\circ \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360^\circ}{365} \cdot (n + 284)\right]}$$

The constant **284** comes from the fact that December 31st is *n* = 365, and we want the phase of the sine to correspond to the day after the winter solstice. The winter solstice occurs at n = 355, and 355 + (365 − 355)/2 ≈ 357.5; using a slightly different starting reference gives n = 284 such that the formula peaks at n = 172.

#### Step 5: Verification

- For n = 172 (Summer Solstice): $\frac{360}{365}(172 + 284) = \frac{360 \cdot 456}{365} = 449.75° \equiv 89.75°$
  - $\sin(89.75°) \approx 0.99999$
  - $\delta \approx 23.45°$ ✓
- For n = 81 (Vernal Equinox): $\frac{360}{365}(81 + 284) = \frac{360 \cdot 365}{365} = 360° \equiv 0°$
  - $\sin(0°) = 0$ ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Day Length Equation

#### Step 1: Sunrise Condition

At sunrise (or sunset), the Sun is exactly on the horizon, so the **altitude angle (α) = 0°**. The altitude angle is given by:

$$\sin\alpha = \sin\phi \cdot \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cdot \cos\delta \cdot \cos\omega$$

#### Step 2: Set α = 0° (Sun on Horizon)

$$\sin\phi \cdot \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cdot \cos\delta \cdot \cos\omega_s = 0$$

Solving for $\cos\omega_s$:

$$\cos\omega_s = -\frac{\sin\phi \cdot \sin\delta}{\cos\phi \cdot \cos\delta} = -\tan\phi \cdot \tan\delta$$

#### Step 3: Solve for the Hour Angle at Sunrise/Sunset

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}\left(-\tan\phi \cdot \tan\delta\right)$$

#### Step 4: Convert Hour Angle to Hours

Since the Earth rotates **15° per hour** (360° / 24 hr):

$$1 \text{ hour} = 15^\circ$$

The total day length is the time from sunrise ($\omega = -\omega_s$) to sunset ($\omega = +\omega_s$):

$$N = \frac{2 \cdot \omega_s}{15} \text{ (in hours)}$$

#### Step 5: Final Day Length Formula

$$\boxed{N = \frac{2}{15} \cdot \cos^{-1}\left(-\tan\phi \cdot \tan\delta\right)}$$

---

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem**: Determine the solar declination, day length, and noon altitude angle for **Thiruvananthapuram (φ = 8.5° N)** on **June 21** (Summer Solstice, n = 172).

#### Step 1: Calculate Solar Declination

$$\delta = 23.45 \cdot \sin\left(\frac{360}{365}(172 + 284)\right)$$

$$\delta = 23.45 \cdot \sin\left(\frac{360 \cdot 456}{365}\right) = 23.45 \cdot \sin(449.75°)$$

$$449.75° - 360° = 89.75°$$

$$\delta = 23.45 \cdot \sin(89.75°) = 23.45 \cdot 0.99999 \approx 23.45°$$

#### Step 2: Calculate Day Length

$$N = \frac{2}{15} \cdot \cos^{-1}\left(-\tan(8.5°) \cdot \tan(23.45°)\right)$$

$$\tan(8.5°) \approx 0.1493, \quad \tan(23.45°) \approx 0.4335$$

$$\text{Product} = -0.1493 \times 0.4335 = -0.0647$$

$$N = \frac{2}{15} \cdot \cos^{-1}(-0.0647) = \frac{2}{15} \cdot 93.71° = 12.49 \text{ hours}$$

#### Step 3: Calculate Noon Altitude Angle

$$\alpha_n = 90° - \vert\phi - \delta\vert = 90° - \vert 8.5° - 23.45° \vert = 90° - 14.95° = 75.05°$$

**Result**: On June 21 in Thiruvananthapuram, the Sun rises to a high noon altitude of **75.05°**, with a day length of approximately **12.5 hours**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Seasonal Variation at Different Latitudes

| Latitude | Summer Solstice Day Length | Winter Solstice Day Length | Annual Insolation Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0° (Equator)** | 12.0 hr | 12.0 hr | Minimal variation |
| **23.45° (Tropic)** | 13.5 hr | 10.5 hr | Moderate variation |
| **45° (Mid-latitude)** | 15.5 hr | 8.5 hr | Large variation |
| **66.5° (Arctic Circle)** | 24.0 hr | 0.0 hr | Extreme variation |
| **90° (Pole)** | 6 months | 6 months | Midnight sun / polar night |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

```mermaid
    F --> G

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, OEMET617]**

**Q1. Why do seasons occur on Earth? Explain the role of axial tilt.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Seasons occur due to the **23.45° axial tilt (obliquity)** of the Earth's rotational axis with respect to the plane of its orbit (ecliptic). As the Earth revolves around the Sun, the tilt causes the **solar declination angle (δ)** to vary sinusoidally between +23.45° and −23.45° throughout the year. This in turn changes the **noon altitude angle**, **day length**, and **insolation intensity** at any given latitude, producing the four seasons. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. Distinguish between Perihelion and Aphelion.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Perihelion**: The point in Earth's orbit where it is *closest* to the Sun, occurring around **January 3** at a distance of **147.1 × 10⁶ km**.
- **Aphelion**: The point where Earth is *farthest* from the Sun, occurring around **July 4** at a distance of **152.1 × 10⁶ km**.
- The difference is due to the **elliptical orbit** with eccentricity **e = 0.0167**. Notably, seasons are *not* caused by this distance variation but by the axial tilt. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, OEMET617]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **solar declination angle** as a function of the day of the year. State clearly all boundary conditions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A solar collector is installed at **Delhi (φ = 28.6° N)**. Calculate the **solar declination, day length, and noon altitude angle** on the **Summer Solstice (n = 172)** and **Winter Solstice (n = 355)**. Comment on the implications for solar collector design. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation** — Follows Section 3.1 above. The student should:
- State the sinusoidal form: $\delta = A \sin(B(n - C))$
- Identify amplitude A = 23.45° from maximum declination at summer solstice
- Apply equinox condition: δ = 0 at n = 81 to determine the phase
- Use the periodicity (365 days) to find the frequency B = 360°/365
- Arrive at Cooper's equation: $\delta = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(n + 284)\right]$ **(7 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**Summer Solstice (n = 172):**
- $\delta_s = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(172 + 284)\right] = 23.45 \sin(89.75°) = +23.45°$ **(1 mark)**
- $N_s = \frac{2}{15} \cos^{-1}\left(-\tan 28.6° \cdot \tan 23.45°\right)$
  - $\tan 28.6° = 0.5454$, $\tan 23.45° = 0.4335$
  - Product = −0.2364
  - $\cos^{-1}(-0.2364) = 103.68°$
  - $N_s = \frac{2 \times 103.68}{15} = 13.82$ hours **(2 marks)**
- $\alpha_{n,s} = 90° - \vert 28.6° - 23.45° \vert = 90° - 5.15° = 84.85°$ **(1 mark)**

**Winter Solstice (n = 355):**
- $\delta_w = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(355 + 284)\right] = 23.45 \sin(629.75°) = 23.45 \sin(269.75°) = -23.45°$ **(1 mark)**
- $N_w = \frac{2}{15} \cos^{-1}\left(-\tan 28.6° \cdot \tan(-23.45°)\right) = \frac{2}{15} \cos^{-1}(0.2364) = \frac{2 \times 76.32}{15} = 10.18$ hours **(1 mark)**
- $\alpha_{n,w} = 90° - \vert 28.6° - (-23.45°) \vert = 90° - 52.05° = 37.95°$ **(1 mark)**

**Comment**: The day length varies from 10.18 hr (winter) to 13.82 hr (summer) — a difference of **3.64 hours**. The noon altitude drops from 84.85° to 37.95°. This large seasonal variation means a **fixed-tilt solar collector at latitude angle** would underperform in both seasons. A **seasonal tilt adjustment** (summer: φ − 15°, winter: φ + 15°) or a **dual-axis tracker** is recommended. **(1 mark — for design comment)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **astronomical basis of seasons** with neat diagrams. Discuss the variation of solar declination throughout the year. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a location at latitude **φ = 13° N (Bengaluru)**, determine the **date(s)** when the day length is exactly **12 hours**. Verify your answer using the declination formula. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** — Follows Sections 1 and 2 above. The student should:
- Define axial tilt, ecliptic plane, and the geometry of Earth's orbit
- Draw a labeled diagram (use Mermaid schematic from Section 4)
- Show δ = +23.45° at summer solstice, 0° at equinoxes, −23.45° at winter solstice
- Discuss the sinusoidal variation with the day of the year
- Note that seasons in the two hemispheres are *opposite* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For day length N = 12 hours:

$$12 = \frac{2}{15} \cos^{-1}\left(-\tan 13° \cdot \tan\delta\right)$$

$$90° = \cos^{-1}\left(-\tan 13° \cdot \tan\delta\right)$$

$$\cos 90° = -\tan 13° \cdot \tan\delta$$

$$0 = -\tan 13° \cdot \tan\delta \implies \tan\delta = 0 \implies \delta = 0°$$ **(3 marks)**

The declination is **zero** on the **equinoxes**:
- **Vernal Equinox**: n = 81 → March 22
- **Autumnal Equinox**: n = 266 → September 21 **(2 marks)**

**Verification using Cooper's equation:**
- For n = 81: $\delta = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(81 + 284)\right] = 23.45 \sin(360°) = 0°$ ✓ **(1 mark)**
- For n = 266: $\delta = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(266 + 284)\right] = 23.45 \sin(541.48°) = 23.45 \sin(181.48°) = -0.07° \approx 0°$ ✓ **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit Errors**: Students frequently forget to convert hour angle from degrees to hours (divide by 15). Marks are deducted for stating day length in degrees.
> 2. **Sign of Declination**: Confusing +23.45° and −23.45° for summer and winter solstices. The summer solstice in the **Northern** Hemisphere has **δ = +23.45°**.
> 3. **Latitude Sign Convention**: Always use **positive** latitude for Northern Hemisphere and **negative** for Southern Hemisphere. Mixing signs gives nonsensical results.
> 4. **Forgetting Cooper's "+284" Constant**: Writing $\delta = 23.45 \sin(360n/365)$ gives the wrong phase — declination peaks on the wrong day.
> 5. **Arccos Range Error**: $\cos^{-1}$ returns values in [0°, 180°]. The hour angle $\omega_s$ must be in [0°, 90°]; use the principal value.
> 6. **Distance vs Tilt Misconception**: In long-answer questions, students often state that Earth is closer to the Sun in summer. This is a *factual error* and costs marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Seasons"**

- **Earth's Axial Tilt**: Fixed at **23.45°** relative to the ecliptic — this is the **sole astronomical cause of seasons**.
- **Earth's Orbit**: Elliptical with **e = 0.0167**, period = **365.25 days**; Perihelion (~Jan 3) and Aphelion (~Jul 4) do *not* cause seasons.
- **Cooper's Equation** (must memorize):
  $$\delta = 23.45^\circ \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360^\circ}{365} \cdot (n + 284)\right]$$
- **Four Cardinal Solar Positions**:
  - Vernal Equinox: n = 81, δ = 0°
  - Summer Solstice: n = 172, δ = +23.45°
  - Autumnal Equinox: n = 266, δ = 0°
  - Winter Solstice: n = 355, δ = −23.45°
- **Day Length Formula** (must memorize):
  $$N = \frac{2}{15} \cdot \cos^{-1}\left(-\tan\phi \cdot \tan\delta\right)$$
- **Hour Angle at Sunrise/Sunset**:
  $$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}\left(-\tan\phi \cdot \tan\delta\right)$$
- **Noon Altitude Angle**:
  $$\alpha_n = 90° - \vert\phi - \delta\vert$$
- **Special Latitude Behavior**:
  - Equator (φ = 0°): N = 12 hours *all year*
  - Arctic Circle (φ = 66.5°): 24-hr daylight at summer solstice
  - Poles (φ = 90°): 6 months day, 6 months night
- **Engineering Implications**:
  - Fixed PV tilt ≈ latitude angle (for annual optimum)
  - Seasonal adjustment: Summer tilt = φ − 15°, Winter tilt = φ + 15°
  - Dual-axis tracking captures full seasonal + daily variation
- **Solar Constant**: $G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2$ (extraterrestrial, on a surface perpendicular to Sun's rays at 1 AU).
- **Earth-Sun Distance Correction Factor**:
  $$\left(\frac{r_0}{r}\right)^2 = 1 + 0.033 \cos\left(\frac{360n}{365}\right)$$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Position of Sun in the Sky
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET617 - SOLAR ENERGY CONSERVATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Position of Sun in the Sky

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The **Position of the Sun in the Sky** refers to the precise angular coordinates of the sun relative to an observer on Earth's surface at any given moment, expressed through a set of solar geometry angles that govern the intensity, direction, and availability of solar radiation reaching a given location.

### Fundamental Solar Angles (KTU Core Vocabulary)

The position of the sun in the celestial sphere is defined by a set of **five primary angular parameters** that an engineer MUST internalize:

| S.No | Solar Angle | Symbol | Range / Domain |
|------|-------------|--------|----------------|
| 1 | Latitude | $\phi$ | $-90^\circ$ to $+90^\circ$ |
| 2 | Declination | $\delta$ | $-23.45^\circ$ to $+23.45^\circ$ |
| 3 | Hour Angle | $\omega$ | $-180^\circ$ to $+180^\circ$ (or $\pm$ 12 hrs) |
| 4 | Altitude / Elevation | $\alpha$ | $0^\circ$ to $90^\circ$ |
| 5 | Azimuth | $\gamma_s$ | $0^\circ$ to $360^\circ$ |
| 6 | Zenith Angle | $\theta_z$ | $0^\circ$ to $90^\circ$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Other critical auxiliary angles** include the **Solar Incidence Angle (θ)**, **Profile / Wall Azimuth (γᵥᵥ)**, **Surface Tilt (β)**, and the **Angle of Incidence on Tilted Surfaces** — these are *not* part of the sun's *position* itself, but they *depend* on it.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in an open field in Kerala (say, Kochi — latitude ≈ 10° N). You hold a ball on a string straight up in the air at **noon**. That is roughly where the sun appears. Now walk toward the **North Pole** — the ball would appear to tilt toward the south. Walk toward the **equator** — the ball would appear almost directly overhead. 

The "ball on the string" represents the **zenith direction** (straight up). The sun's position is the *angle* by which it deviates from this zenith — both in **how high it is** (altitude) and **which compass direction it sits** (azimuth). This angular deviation changes continuously with:
- **Time of day** (Earth's rotation) → Hour Angle
- **Day of year** (Earth's orbit + axial tilt) → Declination
- **Where you stand on Earth** → Latitude & Longitude

The entire field of **solar engineering** — designing PV panels, solar thermal collectors, tracking systems, and shading analysis — reduces to knowing these five angles accurately.

### Governing Physical Constants

- **Solar Constant (Gsc):** $\mathbf{G_{sc} = 1361 \text{ W/m}^2}$ (extraterrestrial radiation on a surface perpendicular to the sun's rays at mean Earth–Sun distance)
- **Axial Tilt / Obliquity of Earth:** $\mathbf{\varepsilon = 23.45^\circ}$
- **Eccentricity of Earth's orbit:** $e \approx 0.0167$
- **Mean Earth–Sun distance:** 1 AU ≈ $1.496 \times 10^{11}$ m
- **Earth's rotation rate:** 15°/hour (relative to sun)

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters:** Without knowing the sun's position, you cannot size a solar panel, calculate its tilt, predict its annual energy yield, or design a sun-tracking system. *Every* solar engineering calculation begins here.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Fixed/Quasi-Fixed Angles (depend on observer + day)

#### (A) Latitude ($\phi$)
The angle between the equatorial plane and the observer's location measured along a meridian. Positive in **Northern Hemisphere**, negative in **Southern Hemisphere**.

#### (B) Declination ($\delta$) — "Seasonal Tilt of Earth"
The angular position of the sun at solar noon with respect to the equatorial plane. It is purely a function of the **day of the year** (n).

**Cooper's Equation (Most commonly used in KTU exams):**

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \cdot \sin\left(\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right)$$

where:
- $n$ = day of the year (Jan 1 = 1, Dec 31 = 365)
- $\delta$ is in **degrees**
- $23.45^\circ$ is the maximum declination (at summer/winter solstices)

**Spencer's Equation (More accurate, Fourier-series based):**

$$\delta = 180^\circ/\pi \cdot \left( \begin{aligned} &0.006918 - 0.399912\cos(B) + 0.070257\sin(B) \\ &- 0.006758\cos(2B) + 0.000907\sin(2B) \\ &- 0.002697\cos(3B) + 0.001480\sin(3B) \end{aligned} \right)$$

where $B = 360^\circ(n-1)/365$ in **radians** for trigonometric arguments, but expressed in degrees above.

**Key Declination Reference Points (KTU frequently tested):**

| Date | Day (n) | Declination ($\delta$) | Event |
|------|---------|----------------------|-------|
| Mar 21 | 80 | $0^\circ$ | Vernal Equinox |
| Jun 21 | 172 | $+23.45^\circ$ | Summer Solstice (N. Hem.) |
| Sep 23 | 266 | $0^\circ$ | Autumnal Equinox |
| Dec 22 | 355/356 | $-23.45^\circ$ | Winter Solstice (N. Hem.) |

#### (C) Hour Angle ($\omega$) — "Time of Day Angle"
The angular displacement of the sun **east or west** of the local meridian due to Earth's rotation of 15° per hour.

$$\omega = 15^\circ \cdot (\text{Solar Time} - 12:00)$$

- **Negative before solar noon** (sun is in the east)
- **Zero at solar noon** (sun on the meridian)
- **Positive after solar noon** (sun is in the west)
- **Sunrise/Sunset** corresponds to $\omega = \pm \omega_s$ (sunrise/sunset hour angle)

### 2.2 The Two Derived Angles (Position of Sun in Observer's Sky)

#### (D) Altitude / Solar Elevation Angle ($\alpha$)
The angle between the sun's rays and the **horizontal plane**.

$$\sin(\alpha) = \sin(\phi)\sin(\delta) + \cos(\phi)\cos(\delta)\cos(\omega)$$

#### (E) Zenith Angle ($\theta_z$)
The angle between the sun's rays and the **vertical (zenith)**. By definition: $\theta_z = 90^\circ - \alpha$.

$$\cos(\theta_z) = \sin(\phi)\sin(\delta) + \cos(\phi)\cos(\delta)\cos(\omega)$$

#### (F) Azimuth Angle ($\gamma_s$)
The compass direction of the sun's projection on the horizontal plane, measured **clockwise from North**.

$$\cos(\gamma_s) = \frac{\sin(\delta)\cos(\phi) - \cos(\delta)\sin(\phi)\cos(\omega)}{\cos(\alpha)}$$

$$\sin(\gamma_s) = \frac{-\cos(\delta)\sin(\omega)}{\cos(\alpha)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> The sign convention for azimuth varies by textbook. KTU follows: **North = 0°, East = +90°, South = ±180°, West = -90° or 270°** in some conventions. Always check the convention in the question paper.

### 2.3 Sunrise, Sunset, and Day Length

The hour angle at **sunrise / sunset** ($\omega_s$) is found by setting $\alpha = 0$ (sun on horizon):

$$\cos(\omega_s) = -\tan(\phi)\tan(\delta)$$

**Day Length:**

$$N_d = \frac{2}{15} \cdot \omega_s \;\; \text{(in hours)}$$

$$N_d = \frac{2}{15} \cdot \cos^{-1}\left(-\tan(\phi)\tan(\delta)\right)$$

### 2.4 Solar Time vs. Clock Time (Equation of Time + Longitude Correction)

Solar geometry requires **Solar Time (ST)**, not clock time. The conversion:

$$\text{Solar Time} = \text{Standard Time} + E_oT + 4(\text{Standard Meridian} - \text{Local Longitude}) \;\; \text{[minutes]}$$

**Equation of Time (minutes):**

$$E_{oT} = 9.87\sin(2B) - 7.53\cos(B) - 1.5\sin(B)$$

where $B = 360^\circ(n-1)/365$ (in degrees).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Declination | $\delta = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284+n)\right]$ | degrees |
| 2 | Hour Angle | $\omega = 15(\text{ST} - 12)$ | degrees |
| 3 | Altitude | $\sin\alpha = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$ | degrees |
| 4 | Zenith Angle | $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$ | $\theta_z = 90^\circ - \alpha$ |
| 5 | Azimuth | $\cos\gamma_s = \dfrac{\sin\delta\cos\phi - \cos\delta\sin\phi\cos\omega}{\cos\alpha}$ | degrees, from N |
| 6 | Sunrise/Sunset Hour Angle | $\cos\omega_s = -\tan\phi\tan\delta$ | degrees |
| 7 | Day Length | $N_d = \frac{2}{15}\cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)$ | hours |
| 8 | Equation of Time | $E_{oT} = 9.87\sin 2B - 7.53\cos B - 1.5\sin B$ | minutes, $B = 360(n-1)/365$ |
| 9 | Solar Time | $ST = CT + E_{oT} + 4(\text{SM} - L)$ | minutes |

> **Units in trigonometric functions:** Always convert degrees to radians ONLY if the calculator/function requires it. Cooper's equation outputs in degrees directly. Spencer's equation uses $180/\pi$ conversion.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Solar Position Matters |
|-------------|----------------------------|
| **Fixed PV Panel Tilt** | Tilt angle = $\phi - \delta$ (year-round optimum) or seasonal adjustment $\phi \pm 15^\circ$ |
| **Single-Axis Trackers** | Hour-angle tracking needs $\omega$ vs. ST mapping |
| **Dual-Axis Trackers** | Need all of $\alpha, \gamma_s, \omega$ in real time |
| **Shading Analysis** | Building shadow lengths depend on $\alpha$ |
| **Concentrated Solar Power** | Mirror/parabolic geometry must match $\gamma_s, \alpha$ within $< 0.5^\circ$ accuracy |
| **Daylighting Design** | Glare & natural light depend on azimuth & altitude |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Altitude / Zenith Angle Equation

**Setup:** Spherical triangle on the celestial sphere with vertices at:
- **Zenith (Z)** — directly above observer
- **Pole (P)** — celestial pole
- **Sun (S)**

The three sides of this spherical triangle are:
- $\text{Zenith to Pole} = 90^\circ - \phi$ (co-latitude)
- $\text{Zenith to Sun} = \theta_z = 90^\circ - \alpha$ (zenith angle)
- $\text{Pole to Sun} = 90^\circ - \delta$ (co-declination)

The angle at **Pole (P)** is the **hour angle** $\omega$.

Apply the **Spherical Law of Cosines** for sides:

$$\cos(\text{side opposite to angle}) = \cos(\text{adjacent}_1)\cos(\text{adjacent}_2) + \sin(\text{adjacent}_1)\sin(\text{adjacent}_2)\cos(\text{angle between})$$

Mapping to our triangle: side $ZS$ opposite to angle $P$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos(ZS) &= \cos(ZP)\cos(PS) + \sin(ZP)\sin(PS)\cos(\omega)
\end{aligned}$$

Substitute each side:

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos(ZS) &= \cos(90^\circ - \alpha) = \sin(\alpha) \\
\cos(ZP) &= \cos(90^\circ - \phi) = \sin(\phi) \\
\cos(PS) &= \cos(90^\circ - \delta) = \sin(\delta) \\
\sin(ZP) &= \sin(90^\circ - \phi) = \cos(\phi) \\
\sin(PS) &= \sin(90^\circ - \delta) = \cos(\delta)
\end{aligned}$$

So the equation becomes:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sin(\alpha) &= \sin(\phi)\sin(\delta) + \cos(\phi)\cos(\delta)\cos(\omega)
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **fundamental altitude equation**. **Q.E.D.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Azimuth Angle Equation

Use the **Spherical Law of Cosines for angles** (the dual form), applied to the same triangle with vertex angle at the **Zenith (Z)**, which equals the azimuth $\gamma_s$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos(Z) &= -\cos(P)\cos(S) + \sin(P)\sin(S)\cos(\text{side opposite to } Z)
\end{aligned}$$

Alternatively, use the **Five-Part Formula** (Cotangent form). The most direct route is from a second application of the Law of Cosines, this time solving for the side $\cos(PS)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos(PS) &= \cos(ZS)\cos(ZP) + \sin(ZS)\sin(ZP)\cos(\gamma_s)
\end{aligned}$$

Substitute:

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos(90^\circ - \delta) &= \cos(90^\circ - \alpha)\cos(90^\circ - \phi) + \sin(90^\circ - \alpha)\sin(90^\circ - \phi)\cos(\gamma_s)
\end{aligned}$$

Simplify:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sin(\delta) &= \sin(\alpha)\sin(\phi) + \cos(\alpha)\cos(\phi)\cos(\gamma_s)
\end{aligned}$$

Rearrange to solve for $\cos(\gamma_s)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos(\gamma_s) &= \frac{\sin(\delta) - \sin(\alpha)\sin(\phi)}{\cos(\alpha)\cos(\phi)}
\end{aligned}$$

Now substitute the previously derived $\sin(\alpha) = \sin(\phi)\sin(\delta) + \cos(\phi)\cos(\delta)\cos(\omega)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos(\gamma_s) &= \frac{\sin(\delta) - \sin(\phi)[\sin(\phi)\sin(\delta) + \cos(\phi)\cos(\delta)\cos(\omega)]}{\cos(\alpha)\cos(\phi)} \\
\cos(\gamma_s) &= \frac{\sin(\delta) - \sin^2(\phi)\sin(\delta) - \sin(\phi)\cos(\phi)\cos(\delta)\cos(\omega)}{\cos(\alpha)\cos(\phi)} \\
\cos(\gamma_s) &= \frac{\sin(\delta)[1 - \sin^2(\phi)] - \sin(\phi)\cos(\phi)\cos(\delta)\cos(\omega)}{\cos(\alpha)\cos(\phi)} \\
\cos(\gamma_s) &= \frac{\sin(\delta)\cos^2(\phi) - \sin(\phi)\cos(\phi)\cos(\delta)\cos(\omega)}{\cos(\alpha)\cos(\phi)}
\end{aligned}$$

Factor $\cos(\phi)$ from the numerator:

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos(\gamma_s) &= \frac{\cos(\phi)[\sin(\delta)\cos(\phi) - \sin(\phi)\cos(\delta)\cos(\omega)]}{\cos(\alpha)\cos(\phi)} \\
\cos(\gamma_s) &= \frac{\sin(\delta)\cos(\phi) - \cos(\delta)\sin(\phi)\cos(\omega)}{\cos(\alpha)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Q.E.D.** — this matches the standard textbook result.

For $\sin(\gamma_s)$, apply the **spherical law of sines**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\sin(\gamma_s)}{\sin(PS)} &= \frac{\sin(\omega)}{\sin(ZS)} \\
\frac{\sin(\gamma_s)}{\sin(90^\circ - \delta)} &= \frac{\sin(\omega)}{\sin(90^\circ - \alpha)} \\
\frac{\sin(\gamma_s)}{\cos(\delta)} &= \frac{\sin(\omega)}{\cos(\alpha)} \\
\sin(\gamma_s) &= \frac{\cos(\delta)\sin(\omega)}{\cos(\alpha)}
\end{aligned}$$

The negative sign convention in the standard formula accounts for the **east-of-meridian / west-of-meridian** distinction.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Sunrise/Sunset Hour Angle

At sunrise/sunset, the sun is on the **horizon**, so the **altitude $\alpha = 0$** and consequently $\cos(\alpha) = 1$, $\sin(\alpha) = 0$.

Set the altitude equation to zero:

$$\begin{aligned}
0 &= \sin(\phi)\sin(\delta) + \cos(\phi)\cos(\delta)\cos(\omega_s) \\
-\cos(\phi)\cos(\delta)\cos(\omega_s) &= \sin(\phi)\sin(\delta) \\
\cos(\omega_s) &= -\frac{\sin(\phi)\sin(\delta)}{\cos(\phi)\cos(\delta)} \\
\cos(\omega_s) &= -\tan(\phi)\tan(\delta)
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
\omega_s &= \cos^{-1}[-\tan(\phi)\tan(\delta)]
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> If $\cos(\omega_s) < -1$ (e.g., at high latitudes in winter), the equation has no real solution — meaning the sun **does not rise** (polar night). If $\cos(\omega_s) > 1$, the sun does not set (midnight sun).

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Analytical Matrix Style)

> **Problem:** Find the **solar altitude, azimuth, zenith angle**, and **day length** for a location at $\phi = 13^\circ$ N (Trivandrum) on **March 21 (n = 80)** at **Solar Time = 10:00 AM**.

**Step 1: Compute Declination**

$$\delta = 23.45 \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + 80)\right]$$

$$\delta = 23.45 \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360 \times 364}{365}\right] = 23.45 \cdot \sin(358.85^\circ)$$

$$\delta = 23.45 \cdot (-0.0193) \approx -0.453^\circ \approx 0^\circ$$

(Confirms: equinox → $\delta \approx 0^\circ$)

**Step 2: Compute Hour Angle**

$$\omega = 15^\circ \times (10 - 12) = -30^\circ$$

(Negative → before noon → sun is in the east)

**Step 3: Compute Altitude**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sin(\alpha) &= \sin(13^\circ)\sin(0^\circ) + \cos(13^\circ)\cos(0^\circ)\cos(-30^\circ) \\
\sin(\alpha) &= 0 + (0.9744)(1)(0.8660) \\
\sin(\alpha) &= 0.8438 \\
\alpha &= \sin^{-1}(0.8438) \approx 57.6^\circ
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Compute Zenith Angle**

$$\theta_z = 90^\circ - 57.6^\circ = 32.4^\circ$$

**Step 5: Compute Azimuth**

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos(\gamma_s) &= \frac{\sin(0^\circ)\cos(13^\circ) - \cos(0^\circ)\sin(13^\circ)\cos(-30^\circ)}{\cos(57.6^\circ)} \\
\cos(\gamma_s) &= \frac{0 - (1)(0.2250)(0.8660)}{0.5365} \\
\cos(\gamma_s) &= \frac{-0.1949}{0.5365} = -0.3632 \\
\gamma_s &= \cos^{-1}(-0.3632) \approx 111.3^\circ \;\;\text{(measured ESE, i.e., morning sun in east)}
\end{aligned}$$

(At 10 AM in Trivandrum on equinox, the sun is in the **east-southeast**, as expected.)

**Step 6: Compute Day Length**

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos(\omega_s) &= -\tan(13^\circ)\tan(0^\circ) = 0 \\
\omega_s &= 90^\circ \\
N_d &= \frac{2 \times 90^\circ}{15^\circ/\text{hr}} = 12 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

(Equinox → 12 hours of daylight everywhere on Earth ✓)

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: How Each Input Affects the Output

| Input Variable | Increase Effect on Altitude ($\alpha$) | Increase Effect on Day Length ($N_d$) |
|----------------|---------------------------------------|---------------------------------------|
| Latitude $\phi$ (toward pole) | $\uparrow$ in summer, $\downarrow$ in winter (complex) | $\uparrow$ in summer, $\downarrow$ in winter |
| Declination $\delta$ (toward +) | $\uparrow$ in N. Hem., $\downarrow$ in S. Hem. | $\uparrow$ in N. Hem. summer |
| Hour Angle $\vert\omega\vert$ | $\downarrow$ (always) | No effect |
