## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Celestial Sphere Geometry — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    Observer[Observer at Latitude phi] -->|Zenith direction up| Z[Z: Zenith Point]
    Observer -->|Tilted by phi from zenith| NP[CP: Celestial North Pole]
    Z -->|90 deg - alpha| Sun[S: Sun Position]
    Z -->|90 deg - phi| NP
    NP -->|Hour angle omega| Sun
    Sun -->|90 deg - delta| Equator[CE: Celestial Equator]
    Observer -->|Phi above Equator| Equator

    ZAngular[Angle at Z = Azimuth gamma_s] -.-> Z
    NPAngular[Angle at NP = Hour Angle omega] -.-> NP
    SunAngular[Angle at S = 90 - alpha or 90 - zenith] -.-> Sun
```

**Interpretation:** The celestial sphere triangle $\Delta Z\!-\!NP\!-\!S$ has sides:
- $Z\!-\!S = 90^\circ - \alpha = \theta_z$
- $Z\!-\!NP = 90^\circ - \phi$
- $NP\!-\!S = 90^\circ - \delta$

and angles:
- At $Z$ → Azimuth $\gamma_s$
- At $NP$ → Hour Angle $\omega$
- At $S$ → $90^\circ - \alpha$ (parallactic angle)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Inputs: phi, n, Solar Time] --> B[Compute Declination delta]

    C --> D
    B --> E[Compute Day Length Nd]
    A --> E
    D --> F[Compute Zenith theta_z]
    D --> G[Compute Azimuth gamma_s]
    A --> G
    C --> G

    F --> H[Final Solar Position Vector]
    G --> H

    H --> I[PV Panel Tilt Optimization]
    H --> J[Solar Tracker Control]

```

### 4.3 Sun's Apparent Path Through the Sky (Annual Cycle)

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Summer_Solstice
    SS1[Jun 21: delta = +23.45 deg] --> SS2[Sun rises NE of East]
    SS2 --> SS3[Highest noon altitude in N. Hem.]
    SS3 --> SS4[Sun sets NW of West]
    end

    subgraph Equinoxes

    end
```

### 4.4 Sun-Tracking System Architecture (Functional Block)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Sensor[Sun Position Sensor / LDR Array] -->|Raw irradiance data| MCU[Microcontroller]
    RTC[Real-Time Clock + GPS Module] -->|Date, time, latitude, longitude| MCU
    MCU -->|Calculates alpha, gamma_s, omega| Driver[Motor Driver Unit]
    Driver -->|Azimuth motor signal| AZ_Motor[Azimuth Tracking Motor]
    Driver -->|Elevation motor signal| EL_Motor[Elevation Tracking Motor]

```

### 4.5 Solar Position Vector Decomposition

```mermaid
graph TD
    SunVec[Sun Vector of magnitude I_bn] -->|Project onto horizontal| Horiz[Horizontal Component = I_bn cos alpha]
    SunVec -->|Project onto vertical Zenith| Vert[Vertical Component = I_bn sin alpha]

    Horiz -->|North-South component| NSComp[NS = I_bn cos alpha cos gamma_s]
    Horiz -->|East-West component| EWComp[EW = I_bn cos alpha sin gamma_s]

    Vert -->|Beam on horizontal surface| BeamH[Beam on Horizontal = I_bn cos theta_z = I_bn sin alpha]
    BeamH -->|Used in Hottel-Whillier model| Collector[Solar Collector Useful Gain]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the following solar angles: (i) Solar declination angle, (ii) Hour angle, and (iii) Zenith angle.*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Solar Declination Angle ($\delta$):** It is the angle between the line joining the centers of the sun and the earth, and the projection of this line on the equatorial plane. It varies between $-23.45^\circ$ and $+23.45^\circ$ throughout the year, being **zero at the equinoxes** and maximum at the **solstices**. Given by $\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284+n)\right]$ where $n$ is the day of the year.

**(ii) Hour Angle ($\omega$):** It is the angular displacement of the sun from the local meridian due to the earth's rotation at 15° per hour. It is **zero at solar noon, negative before noon, and positive after noon**, expressed as $\omega = 15^\circ \times (ST - 12)$ where ST is the solar time in hours.

**(iii) Zenith Angle ($\theta_z$):** It is the angle between the sun's rays and the **vertical (zenith direction)**. It is complementary to the altitude: $\theta_z = 90^\circ - \alpha$. It is critical for calculating the **angle of incidence** of beam radiation on horizontal and tilted surfaces.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Explain the concept of "Equation of Time" and state its physical significance in solar energy calculations.*

**Model Answer:**

The **Equation of Time (EoT)** is the difference between **apparent solar time** (true sun position) and **mean solar time** (average, as kept by clocks), arising from two effects:
1. **Eccentricity of Earth's orbit** — Earth's speed varies (faster near perihelion in January, slower near aphelion in July).
2. **Axial tilt (obliquity)** — Projection of the sun's apparent motion onto the equatorial plane is non-uniform.

**Formula (Spencer, in minutes):**

$$E_{oT} = 9.87 \sin(2B) - 7.53 \cos(B) - 1.5 \sin(B), \quad B = \frac{360(n-1)}{365}$$

**Significance in solar engineering:**
- Required to convert **clock time → solar time** for accurate hour-angle computation.
- Affects the **scheduling of solar tracking** and **grid synchronization** of PV systems.
- Without it, sun-tracking errors can reach up to **±16 minutes** ($\approx 4^\circ$ hour-angle error), translating to several degrees of pointing error in solar concentrators.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **solar altitude angle** in terms of latitude $\phi$, declination $\delta$, and hour angle $\omega$ using the **spherical trigonometry** approach. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a location in **Kerala at $\phi = 10^\circ$ N, longitude 76° E**, on **15th March (n = 74)** at **Indian Standard Time (IST) = 2:30 PM**, determine the **(i) declination angle, (ii) hour angle, (iii) altitude angle, (iv) zenith angle, and (v) azimuth angle**. Use the standard meridian for IST = 82.5°E. **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**(a) Derivation of Altitude Angle:**

Consider the celestial sphere with the observer at the centre. Form the spherical triangle $Z\!-\!NP\!-\!S$ where:
- $Z$ = Zenith
- $NP$ = North Celestial Pole
- $S$ = Sun's position

**Sides:**
- Arc $Z\!-\!S = 90^\circ - \alpha$ (where $\alpha$ is the altitude)
- Arc $Z\!-\!NP = 90^\circ - \phi$ (co-latitude)
- Arc $NP\!-\!S = 90^\circ - \delta$ (co-declination)

**Angle at $NP$** = Hour angle $\omega$

Apply the **Spherical Law of Cosines** for sides:

$$\cos(ZS) = \cos(ZP)\cos(PS) + \sin(ZP)\sin(PS)\cos(\omega)$$

Substitute:

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos(90^\circ - \alpha) &= \cos(90^\circ - \phi)\cos(90^\circ - \delta) + \sin(90^\circ - \phi)\sin(90^\circ - \delta)\cos(\omega) \\
\sin(\alpha) &= \sin(\phi)\sin(\delta) + \cos(\phi)\cos(\delta)\cos(\omega)
\end{aligned}$$

This is the required expression for solar altitude.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Declination (n = 74)**

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + 74)\right] = 23.45^\circ \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360 \times 358}{365}\right]$$

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \cdot \sin(353.10^\circ) = 23.45^\circ \cdot (-0.1219) = -2.86^\circ$$

**Step 2: Equation of Time (for converting IST to Solar Time)**

$$B = \frac{360(74-1)}{365} = 71.84^\circ$$

$$E_{oT} = 9.87\sin(2 \times 71.84^\circ) - 7.53\cos(71.84^\circ) - 1.5\sin(71.84^\circ)$$

$$E_{oT} = 9.87 \cdot \sin(143.68^\circ) - 7.53 \cdot \cos(71.84^\circ) - 1.5 \cdot \sin(71.84^\circ)$$

$$E_{oT} = 9.87 \cdot (0.5928) - 7.53 \cdot (0.3096) - 1.5 \cdot (0.9509)$$

$$E_{oT} = 5.851 - 2.331 - 1.426 = 2.094 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 3: Solar Time**

$$ST = CT + E_{oT} + 4(\text{Standard Meridian} - \text{Local Longitude})$$

$$ST = 14{:}30 + 2.094 + 4(82.5 - 76) = 14{:}30 + 2.094 + 26 = 14{:}30 + 28.09 \text{ min}$$

$$ST = 14{:}58.09 \;\; (\text{i.e., almost 3:00 PM solar time})$$

**Step 4: Hour Angle**

$$\omega = 15^\circ \times (14.968 - 12) = 15^\circ \times 2.968 = 44.52^\circ$$

**Step 5: Altitude**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sin(\alpha) &= \sin(10^\circ)\sin(-2.86^\circ) + \cos(10^\circ)\cos(-2.86^\circ)\cos(44.52^\circ) \\
&= (0.1736)(-0.0499) + (0.9848)(0.9988)(0.7133) \\
&= -0.00866 + 0.7014 \\
&= 0.6927
\end{aligned}$$

$$\alpha = \sin^{-1}(0.6927) = 43.84^\circ$$

**Step 6: Zenith Angle**

$$\theta_z = 90^\circ - 43.84^\circ = 46.16^\circ$$

**Step 7: Azimuth**

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos(\gamma_s) &= \frac{\sin(-2.86^\circ)\cos(10^\circ) - \cos(-2.86^\circ)\sin(10^\circ)\cos(44.52^\circ)}{\cos(43.84^\circ)} \\
&= \frac{(-0.0499)(0.9848) - (0.9988)(0.1736)(0.7133)}{0.7212} \\
&= \frac{-0.0491 - 0.1236}{0.7212} = \frac{-0.1727}{0.7212} = -0.2394
\end{aligned}$$

$$\gamma_s = \cos^{-1}(-0.2394) = 103.85^\circ$$

(Sun is in the south-east, slightly west of south, at 2:30 PM IST — physically correct for mid-afternoon in tropics.)

---

#### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With the help of neat diagrams, explain the **solar azimuth angle** and **solar altitude angle**. Define the **hour angle** and explain how it is related to **solar time**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a location at $\phi = 28.6^\circ$ N (Delhi), determine the **day length** and **sunrise hour angle** on the **summer solstice (n = 172)** and the **winter solstice (n = 355)**. Also calculate the **solar altitude at solar noon** on both days. **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**(a) Conceptual Explanation:**

Refer to Section 1.1 (above) for full theoretical explanation. The model answer should include:

**Solar Azimuth ($\gamma_s$):** Compass direction of the sun's projection on the horizontal plane, measured clockwise from North. In the **Northern Hemisphere** between sunrise and sunset, the sun's azimuth swings from approximately 90° (East) at sunrise → 180° (South) at solar noon (for $\phi > \delta$) → 270° (West) at sunset.

**Solar Altitude ($\alpha$):** Angle between the sun's rays and the horizontal plane. Varies from 0° (sunrise/sunset) to maximum at solar noon.

**Hour Angle ($\omega$):** Angular measure of time elapsed since solar noon, based on Earth's rotation of **15°/hour**. Defined as:
$$\omega = 15^\circ \times (ST - 12)$$
where ST is the **Solar Time** in hours (not clock time). At solar noon, $\omega = 0$; at 1 PM, $\omega = +15^\circ$; at 11 AM, $\omega = -15^\circ$.

**Solar Time vs Clock Time:**
$$\text{Solar Time} = \text{Clock Time} + E_{oT} + 4(\text{Standard Meridian} - \text{Longitude})$$

The relationship between solar time and hour angle is **linear** at 15°/hour because of Earth's constant rotational speed (in the short term).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Summer Solstice (n = 172):**

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + 172)\right] = 23.45^\circ \cdot \sin(449.26^\circ)$$

Reduce $449.26 - 360 = 89.26^\circ$:

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \cdot \sin(89.26^\circ) = 23.45^\circ \cdot 0.99986 \approx 23.45^\circ$$

**Sunrise Hour Angle:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos(\omega_s) &= -\tan(28.6^\circ)\tan(23.45^\circ) \\
&= -(0.5454)(0.4335) \\
&= -0.2364
\end{aligned}$$

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-0.2364) = 103.68^\circ$$

**Day Length:**

$$N_d = \frac{2 \times 103.68^\circ}{15^\circ/\text{hr}} = 13.82 \text{ hours} \;\; (\approx 13 \text{ h } 49 \text{ min})$$

**Noon Altitude (ω = 0):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sin(\alpha_{\text{noon}}) &= \sin(28.6^\circ)\sin(23.45^\circ) + \cos(28.6^\circ)\cos(23.45^\circ)\cos(0) \\
&= (0.4786)(0.3977) + (0.8781)(0.9175)(1) \\
&= 0.1903 + 0.8057 = 0.9960
\end{aligned}$$

$$\alpha_{\text{noon}} = \sin^{-1}(0.9960) = 85.16^\circ$$

---

**Winter Solstice (n = 355):**

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + 355)\right] = 23.45^\circ \cdot \sin(629.26^\circ)$$

Reduce: $629.26 - 360 = 269.26^\circ$. Note $\sin(269.26^\circ) = \sin(269.26 - 360) = \sin(-90.74^\circ) = -0.99995 \approx -1$

$$\delta \approx -23.45^\circ$$

**Sunrise Hour Angle:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos(\omega_s) &= -\tan(28.6^\circ)\tan(-23.45^\circ) = +(0.5454)(0.4335) = +0.2364
\end{aligned}$$

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(0.2364) = 76.32^\circ$$

**Day Length:**

$$N_d = \frac{2 \times 76.32^\circ}{15^\circ/\text{hr}} = 10.18 \text{ hours} \;\; (\approx 10 \text{ h } 11 \text{ min})$$

**Noon Altitude (ω = 0):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sin(\alpha_{\text{noon}}) &= \sin(28.6^\circ)\sin(-23.45^\circ) + \cos(28.6^\circ)\cos(-23.45^\circ) \\
&= (0.4786)(-0.3977) + (0.8781)(0.9175) \\
&= -0.1903 + 0.8057 = 0.6154
\end{aligned}$$

$$\alpha_{\text{noon}} = \sin^{-1}(0.6154) = 37.98^\circ$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Summer Solstice | Winter Solstice |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| Declination | $+23.45^\circ$ | $-23.45^\circ$ |
| Sunrise $\omega_s$ | $103.68^\circ$ | $76.32^\circ$ |
| Day Length | 13.82 h | 10.18 h |
| Noon Altitude | $85.16^\circ$ | $37.98^\circ$ |

> The difference in day length ($13.82 - 10.18 = 3.64$ hours) and noon altitude ($85.16 - 37.98 = 47.18^\circ$) is what causes the **strong seasonal variation in solar energy harvest** at Delhi's latitude — directly relevant to KTU problems on annual collector yield.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Solar Geometry Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert clock time to solar time** (5–10% marks lost). Most students use IST directly in the hour-angle formula — WRONG. Always apply the Equation of Time + longitude correction.
>
> 2. **Mixing degree/radian modes** in calculators. Cooper's formula outputs degrees, but inverse trig functions (sin⁻¹, cos⁻¹, tan⁻¹) require the input in the correct mode.
>
> 3. **Sign errors in Cooper's declination formula** — students often forget the `(284 + n)` term, leading to declination values off by ~6 months.
>
> 4. **Azimuth sign convention** — for KTU, the standard is: North = 0°, East = +90°, South = 180°, West = 270° (clockwise from North). Some international conventions measure from South. Always state your convention in the answer.
>
> 5. **Declination angle domain** — students sometimes compute $\delta > 23.45°$ due to arithmetic error, which is physically impossible.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to mention units** in the final answer — KTU strictly deducts 0.5–1 mark for missing "degrees" in the final numerical answer.
>
> 7. **Hour angle sign** — students often get $\omega$ as positive in the morning. Use ST-12 with ST in 24-hour format (e.g., 9 AM = 9, not 9:00; 3 PM = 15, not 3).

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Five Primary Angles** — Latitude, Declination, Hour Angle, Altitude, Azimuth.
- [ ] **Declination Formula** — $\delta = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284+n)\right]$ — **MUST MEMORIZE.**
- [ ] **Hour Angle Formula** — $\omega = 15(ST-12)$ where ST is in hours.
- [ ] **Altitude Equation** — $\sin\alpha = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$ — **MUST MEMORIZE.**
- [ ] **Zenith** — $\theta_z = 90^\circ - \alpha$.
- [ ] **Azimuth** — Use both $\cos\gamma_s$ and $\sin\gamma_s$ equations to resolve the **quadrant ambiguity**.
- [ ] **Sunrise/Sunset** — $\cos\omega_s = -\tan\phi\tan\delta$.
- [ ] **Day Length** — $N_d = \frac{2\omega_s}{15}$ in hours.
- [ ] **Solar Time** — Always convert clock time using $E_{oT}$ and longitude correction before computing $\omega$.
- [ ] **Equinoxes** (Mar 21, Sep 23): $\delta = 0$, day length = 12 hours everywhere.
- [ ] **Solstices** (Jun 21, Dec 22): $\delta = \pm 23.45^\circ$, max/min day length.
- [ ] **Polar Day/Night** — when $\cos\omega_s > 1$ or $< -1$, the sun never sets/rises.
- [ ] **Trigonometric Spherical Law of Cosines** — the foundation of all solar geometry derivations.
- [ ] **Unit discipline** — always state "degrees" or "radians" in the final answer.
- [ ] **Sign convention** — North = 0°, East = +90°, South = 180°, West = 270° (KTU default).
- [ ] **Application link** — solar angles feed into: panel tilt, tracking algorithms, incidence angle on tilted surfaces, beam radiation intensity $I_b = I_{bn}\cos\theta_i$.
- [ ] **Cooper vs Spencer** — Cooper is simpler; Spencer is more accurate (Fourier series).
- [ ] **Atmospheric effects** — air mass and solar position are linked: $AM = 1/\sin\alpha$ for $\alpha > 0$ (Kasten-Young modified formula is more accurate).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Position of Sun with Respect to the Centre of the Earth. Role of Atmosphere on Solar Radiation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET617 - SOLAR ENERGY CONSERVATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Position of Sun with Respect to the Centre of the Earth. Role of Atmosphere on Solar Radiation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Position of the Sun with Respect to the Centre of the Earth

The **Position of the Sun with respect to the Centre of the Earth** is a fundamental concept in solar engineering that describes the geometric location of the sun in the celestial sphere relative to an observer (or a horizontal plane) located on Earth's surface. Because the Earth is tilted on its axis by **23.45°** and orbits the sun in an elliptical path, the sun's apparent position in the sky continuously changes throughout the day (diurnal variation) and throughout the year (seasonal variation). This position is quantified using a set of **solar angles** — namely the solar declination, hour angle, altitude angle, zenith angle, and azimuth angle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solar Geometry (KTU Syllabus Definition):** Solar geometry is the branch of solar engineering that deals with determining the angular position of the sun in the sky with respect to a specific location on Earth, using mathematical relationships between the Earth-Sun geometry and the observer's local coordinates (latitude, longitude, and time).

#### Conceptual Analogy — "The Torch and the Tilted Globe"

Imagine holding a flashlight (the Sun) in a dark room, pointing it at a tilted globe (the Earth). As you rotate the globe on its axis (Earth's daily rotation) and slowly walk it in a circle around the flashlight (Earth's yearly revolution), the bright spot (sunlight) on the globe keeps shifting — sometimes falling on the Northern Hemisphere (summer in the north), sometimes on the Southern Hemisphere (winter in the north). Now, if you stand at a fixed point on the globe and try to "catch" that flashlight beam, you would have to know:
- **Where** in the sky to look (altitude/azimuth).
- **When** during the day the light arrives (hour angle).
- **What time of year** changes the height of the light (declination).

This is exactly what a solar engineer computes to optimize the tilt of a **photovoltaic (PV) panel** or a **solar thermal collector**.

### 1.2 Role of Atmosphere on Solar Radiation

The Earth's atmosphere is a complex, multi-layered blanket of gases (primarily **N₂ ≈ 78%**, **O₂ ≈ 21%**, **Ar ≈ 0.93%**, **CO₂ ≈ 0.04%**, plus variable water vapor, ozone, and aerosols) that significantly alters the solar radiation reaching the ground. The extraterrestrial radiation (outside the atmosphere) is a relatively constant **1367 W/m²** (the Solar Constant), but by the time sunlight reaches the Earth's surface, its intensity, spectral composition, and direction have all been modified through three primary physical mechanisms:
- **Atmospheric Absorption** (by O₃, H₂O, CO₂)
- **Atmospheric Scattering** (Rayleigh, Mie, and non-selective)
- **Reflection** (by clouds and ground-albedo)

> [!NOTE]
> **Atmospheric Attenuation (KTU Definition):** The reduction in the intensity, spectral purity, and directness of solar radiation as it traverses the Earth's atmosphere, caused by absorption (selective molecular conversion to heat), scattering (redirection of photons), and reflection (large-particle bounce-back) is collectively termed *atmospheric attenuation*.

#### Conceptual Analogy — "The Dirty Window Pane"

Think of the atmosphere as a slightly dusty glass window pane through which sunlight must pass before entering your room. A clean window lets almost all light through (similar to the high-sun, dry-day desert). A dirty, foggy window blocks, diffuses, and redirects light (similar to a humid, cloudy, polluted atmosphere). The "dirtiness" is parameterized by an **Air Mass (AM)** number and an **extinction coefficient (k)**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Solar Constant | $G_{sc}$ | **1367 W/m²** (also reported as 1353 W/m²) |
| Earth's Axial Tilt | $\varepsilon$ | **23.45°** |
| Mean Earth-Sun Distance | $r_0$ | **1.496 × 10⁸ km** (1 AU) |
| Eccentricity of Earth's Orbit | $e$ | **0.0167** |
| Earth's Angular Velocity | $\omega_{Earth}$ | **15°/hour** |
| Total Solar Irradiance at TOA | $I_{sc}$ | **1367 W/m²** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Earth-Sun Geometry — Step-by-Step Logic

The position of the sun is defined through five interconnected angles:

#### Step 1 — Latitude ($\phi$)
The latitude is the angular distance of an observer north (+) or south (–) of the Equator. For Kerala, $\phi \approx 10°\text{N}$ to $12.5°\text{N}$.

#### Step 2 — Solar Declination Angle ($\delta$)
- **What it is:** The angle between the line joining the centres of the Sun and Earth, projected on the Earth's equatorial plane. It defines the seasonal "tilt" of the sun.
- **Why it changes:** Earth's axis is tilted at **23.45°**, so as Earth revolves, the sun appears to oscillate between **+23.45°** (Summer Solstice, June 21) and **–23.45°** (Winter Solstice, December 21).
- **How:** Given by **Cooper's Equation** (used in nearly all KTU numerical problems):

$$
\delta = 23.45^\circ \times \sin\left( \frac{360}{365} (284 + n) \right)
$$

where $n$ = day of the year (Jan 1 = 1, Dec 31 = 365).

#### Step 3 — Hour Angle ($\omega$)
- **What it is:** The angular displacement of the sun east or west of the local meridian due to Earth's rotation of **15° per hour**.
- **Why it changes:** Earth rotates once in 24 h, giving 360°/24 h = 15°/h.
- **How:**

$$
\omega = 15^\circ \times (Solar\,Time - 12)
$$

$\omega$ is **negative in morning**, **zero at solar noon**, and **positive in afternoon**.

#### Step 4 — Solar Zenith Angle ($\theta_z$) and Altitude Angle ($\alpha$)
- **What they are:** The zenith angle is the angle between the vertical (zenith) and the sun's rays. The altitude angle is its complement — the angle of the sun above the horizon.
- **How:**

$$
\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega
$$

$$
\alpha = 90^\circ - \theta_z
$$

#### Step 5 — Solar Azimuth Angle ($\gamma_s$)
- **What it is:** The angle measured on the horizontal plane from the local North (or South, depending on convention) to the projection of the sun's rays on the horizontal plane.
- **How (Sukhatme's convention — measured from South, positive towards West):**

$$
\sin\gamma_s = \frac{\cos\delta \sin\omega}{\cos\alpha}
$$

- **How (Duffie & Beckman convention — measured from North):**

$$
\cos\gamma_s = \frac{\sin\delta \cos\phi - \cos\delta \sin\phi \cos\omega}{\cos\alpha}
$$

#### Step 6 — Sunrise / Sunset Hour Angle ($\omega_s$)
At sunrise/sunset, $\alpha = 0$, so $\cos\theta_z = 0$, leading to:

$$
\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta)
$$

**Day Length** $= \dfrac{2\omega_s}{15^\circ}$ hours.

### 2.2 The Atmosphere — Step-by-Step Logic

#### Step 1 — Extraterrestrial Radiation ($I_{on}$)
The radiation just outside Earth's atmosphere varies slightly because the Earth's orbit is elliptical. This variation is captured by the **eccentricity correction factor** $E_0$:

$$
E_0 = 1 + 0.033 \cos\left(\frac{360\,n}{365}\right)
$$

$$
I_{on} = G_{sc} \times E_0 = 1367 \left[1 + 0.033 \cos\left(\frac{360\,n}{365}\right)\right] \;\; \text{W/m}^2
$$

#### Step 2 — Air Mass (AM)
The **Air Mass** is the ratio of the actual path length of solar radiation through the atmosphere to the path length when the sun is directly overhead (zenith).

$$
AM = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z} = \frac{1}{\sin\alpha}
$$

| Air Mass Label | Meaning | $\theta_z$ | Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AM 0** | Outside atmosphere | — | Space, satellites |
| **AM 1** | Sun at zenith | 0° | Equator at equinox noon |
| **AM 1.5** | Standard test condition | 48.19° | Industry benchmark for PV |
| **AM 38.18** | Sunrise/sunset | 90° | Horizon (theoretical) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** The standard AM 1.5 spectrum corresponds to a solar zenith angle of 48.19°, corresponding to $\sec\theta_z = 1.5$. This is the industry reference for rating PV modules (e.g., a "250 W panel" is rated at AM 1.5, 1000 W/m²).

#### Step 3 — Atmospheric Attenuation Mechanisms

1. **Absorption (≈ 20%)** — Photons are absorbed and converted into internal energy by specific molecules:
   - **Ozone (O₃):** absorbs UV (< 0.38 $\mu$m).
   - **Water Vapor (H₂O):** absorbs near-IR (0.7–2 $\mu$m).
   - **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂):** absorbs far-IR (> 2 $\mu$m).

2. **Scattering (≈ 7%)** — Photons are redirected without loss of energy:
   - **Rayleigh Scattering** (molecules $\ll \lambda$): why the sky is blue.
   - **Mie Scattering** (aerosols $\approx \lambda$): why hazy skies look white.
   - **Non-selective Scattering** (large droplets $\gg \lambda$): clouds look grey.

3. **Reflection (≈ 25%)** — Photons bounce back to space; mainly due to **clouds** (albedo $\approx 0.6$).

#### Step 4 — Beer's Law (Lambert's Law)
After traversing an air mass $m$, the **direct (beam) normal radiation** is:

$$
I_{bn} = I_{on} \times e^{-k \cdot m}
$$

where $k$ is the **atmospheric extinction coefficient** (typically 0.1 to 0.3 in clear sky; higher in polluted regions).

#### Step 5 — Components of Terrestrial Radiation
The radiation received on a horizontal surface (a flat PV panel or ground) consists of:
- **Direct (Beam) Radiation ($I_b$)** — arrives in a straight line from the sun.
- **Diffuse Radiation ($I_d$)** — scattered radiation from sky and surroundings.
- **Reflected Radiation ($I_r$)** — reflected from ground or nearby obstacles.

$$
\boxed{\;I_G = I_b + I_d + I_r\;}
$$

The **Clearness Index ($K_T$)** quantifies atmospheric transparency:

$$
K_T = \frac{I_G}{I_{on}}
$$

| $K_T$ Range | Sky Condition |
|---|---|
| **0.0 – 0.3** | Overcast / Cloudy |
| **0.3 – 0.6** | Partly cloudy |
| **0.6 – 0.8** | Mostly clear |
| **0.8 – 1.0** | Very clear |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Validity / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Solar Declination | $\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{360}{365}(284+n)\right]$ | $n$ = day of year | Cooper's equation, $\pm 0.5^\circ$ accuracy |
| 2 | Hour Angle | $\omega = 15^\circ (T_{sol} - 12)$ | $T_{sol}$ = solar time (h) | –ve morning, +ve afternoon |
| 3 | Zenith Angle | $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$ | All angles in degrees | Foundation of all geometric calcs |
| 4 | Altitude Angle | $\alpha = 90^\circ - \theta_z$ | Degrees | Above horizontal |
| 5 | Azimuth Angle | $\cos\gamma_s = \dfrac{\sin\delta\cos\phi - \cos\delta\sin\phi\cos\omega}{\cos\alpha}$ | Degrees from North | Duffie–Beckman |
| 6 | Sunrise Hour Angle | $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta)$ | Degrees | Day length $= 2\omega_s/15$ h |
| 7 | Eccentricity Factor | $E_0 = 1 + 0.033\cos\!\left(\frac{360n}{365}\right)$ | Dimensionless | $\pm 3.3\%$ annual variation |
| 8 | Extraterrestrial Radiation | $I_{on} = G_{sc}\,E_0$ | W/m² | $G_{sc}=1367$ W/m² |
| 9 | Air Mass | $AM = \dfrac{1}{\cos\theta_z}$ | Dimensionless | Valid $\theta_z < 90^\circ$ |
| 10 | Beer's Law | $I_{bn} = I_{on}\,e^{-k\cdot AM}$ | W/m² | $k$ = extinction coeff. |
| 11 | Global Radiation | $I_G = I_b + I_d + I_r$ | W/m² | On horizontal surface |
| 12 | Clearness Index | $K_T = \dfrac{I_G}{I_{on}}$ | Dimensionless | 0 (cloudy) to 1 (clear) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PV System Design:** The tilt angle of a solar panel is set equal to the latitude ($\phi$) to maximize annual energy yield. The "Sun-tracking" systems use $\alpha$ and $\gamma_s$ in real-time.
- **Solar Thermal Collectors:** Evacuated tube collectors and parabolic troughs are oriented using solar zenith/azimuth angles.
- **Building Integrated PV (BIPV):** Architects use sun-path diagrams to design shading and overhangs.
- **Atmospheric Studies:** Air mass and clearness index feed directly into clear-sky models (e.g., Hottel, ASHRAE).
- **Satellite Operations:** AM 0 spectrum is used for space-grade solar cells.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Zenith Angle ($\theta_z$)

**Starting Picture:** Consider the celestial sphere with the observer at the centre. The sun is at a point making an angle $\theta_z$ from the local vertical (zenith). The observer's latitude is $\phi$, the sun's declination is $\delta$, and the hour angle is $\omega$.

Using the **spherical law of cosines** on the celestial triangle formed by the zenith, the sun, and the celestial pole:

$$
\cos\theta_z = \cos(90^\circ - \phi)\cos(90^\circ - \delta) + \sin(90^\circ - \phi)\sin(90^\circ - \delta)\cos\omega
$$

Applying the co-function identity $\cos(90^\circ - x) = \sin x$:

$$
\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega
$$

This is the **fundamental equation** of solar geometry.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Sunrise Hour Angle ($\omega_s$)

At sunrise (or sunset), the sun is on the horizon, so $\alpha = 0^\circ$ and $\theta_z = 90^\circ$, hence $\cos\theta_z = 0$. Substituting into the zenith equation:

$$
0 = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega_s
$$

Rearranging for $\cos\omega_s$:

$$
\cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega_s = -\sin\phi \sin\delta
$$

$$
\cos\omega_s = -\frac{\sin\phi \sin\delta}{\cos\phi \cos\delta} = -\tan\phi \tan\delta
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta)\;}
$$

**Day Length** $= \dfrac{2\omega_s}{15^\circ}$ hours.

### 3.3 Derivation of Air Mass (AM)

Consider sunlight entering the atmosphere at zenith angle $\theta_z$. The geometric path length through the atmosphere is:

$$
L = \frac{h}{\cos\theta_z} = h \sec\theta_z
$$

where $h$ is the effective atmospheric thickness (≈ scale height). At the zenith ($\theta_z = 0$), the path length is $h$. The Air Mass is the ratio of the actual path to the zenith path:

$$
\boxed{\;AM = \frac{L}{h} = \sec\theta_z = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z} = \frac{1}{\sin\alpha}\;}
$$

For a more accurate model (Kasten & Young, 1989):

$$
AM = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z + 0.50572\,(96.07995^\circ - \theta_z)^{-1.6364}}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation of Beer's Law (Exponential Attenuation)

Let $I$ be the intensity of a parallel beam of radiation after passing through a thickness $dx$ of an absorbing medium. The incremental loss is proportional to the intensity and the thickness:

$$
dI = -k\,I\,dx
$$

where $k$ is the **extinction (attenuation) coefficient** in m⁻¹ (or km⁻¹). Separating variables:

$$
\frac{dI}{I} = -k\,dx
$$

Integrating from the top of the atmosphere (where $I = I_{on}$) to the surface (where $I = I_{bn}$), with the path length $L = AM \cdot h$:

$$
\int_{I_{on}}^{I_{bn}} \frac{dI}{I} = -k \int_0^{AM} dx
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{I_{bn}}{I_{on}}\right) = -k \cdot AM
$$

$$
\boxed{\;I_{bn} = I_{on}\,e^{-k\cdot AM}\;}
$$

This is the classical **Beer–Lambert Law** for atmospheric attenuation.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Patterns

**Worked Example 1 — Solar Declination**
Find $\delta$ on **22 March (n = 81)** for Kerala.

$$
\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{360}{365}(284+81)\right]
$$

$$
= 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{360}{365}(365)\right]
$$

$$
= 23.45^\circ \sin(360^\circ) = 0^\circ
$$

(Verifies: March 22 is the Spring Equinox.)

**Worked Example 2 — Day Length at $\phi = 20^\circ$ N on Summer Solstice ($\delta = +23.45^\circ$)**

$$
\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan 20^\circ \tan 23.45^\circ)
$$

$$
\tan 20^\circ = 0.364, \quad \tan 23.45^\circ = 0.433
$$

$$
-\tan\phi\tan\delta = -(0.364)(0.433) = -0.1577
$$

$$
\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-0.1577) = 99.07^\circ
$$

$$
\text{Day Length} = \frac{2 \times 99.07^\circ}{15^\circ} = 13.21 \text{ h}
$$

**Worked Example 3 — Beam Radiation with Beer's Law**
Given $I_{on} = 1400$ W/m², $k = 0.18$, $\theta_z = 60^\circ$, find $I_{bn}$.

$$
AM = \frac{1}{\cos 60^\circ} = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2
$$

$$
I_{bn} = 1400 \times e^{-0.18 \times 2} = 1400 \times e^{-0.36} = 1400 \times 0.6977 = 976.7 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Solar Geometry Block Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Earth Sun System"] --> B["Earth Axis Tilt 23.45 deg"]
    A --> C["Earth Orbital Eccentricity 0.0167"]
    A --> D["Earth Rotation 15 deg per hour"]

    B --> E["Solar Declination delta"]
    C --> F["Eccentricity Factor E0"]
    D --> G["Hour Angle omega"]

    E --> H["Zenith Angle thetaz"]
    F --> I["Extraterrestrial Radiation Ion"]
    G --> H

    H --> J["Altitude Angle alpha"]
    H --> K["Air Mass AM"]
    H --> L["Azimuth Angle gammas"]

    J --> M["Solar Tracker Logic"]
    K --> N["Beers Law Attenuation"]
    L --> M

    I --> N
    N --> O["Beam Radiation Ibn"]
    O --> P["Global Radiation IG"]
    Q["Diffuse Radiation Id"] --> P
    R["Reflected Radiation Ir"] --> P
```

### 4.2 Atmospheric Attenuation Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Extraterrestrial Radiation 1367 W/m2"] --> B["Atmospheric Layer"]
    B --> C1["Absorption 20 percent"]
    B --> C2["Scattering 7 percent"]
    B --> C3["Reflection by clouds 25 percent"]

    C1 --> D["Residual Beam Ibn"]
    C2 --> E["Diffuse Component Id"]
    C3 --> F["Lost to Space"]

    D --> G["Surface Global Radiation IG"]
    E --> G
    H["Ground Reflected Ir"] --> G

    G --> I["PV Panel / Solar Collector"]
    G --> J["Clearness Index KT metric"]

    subgraph Atmospheric Absorbers
        X1["Ozone UV"]
        X2["Water Vapor Near IR"]
        X3["Carbon Dioxide Far IR"]
    end

    subgraph Scattering Types
        Y1["Rayleigh Molecules"]
        Y2["Mie Aerosols"]
        Y3["Non Selective Droplets"]
    end
```

### 4.3 Sun-Path Schematic (Annual Variation)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Summer_Solstice
        S1["Sun Path High in Sky"]
        S1 --> S1a["declination = +23.45 deg"]
        S1 --> S1b["Long Day Length"]
    end
    subgraph Equinoxes
        S2["Sun Path Mid Sky"]
        S2 --> S2a["declination = 0 deg"]
        S2 --> S2b["Day equals Night"]
    end
    subgraph Winter_Solstice
        S3["Sun Path Low in Sky"]
        S3 --> S3a["declination = -23.45 deg"]
        S3 --> S3b["Short Day Length"]
    end
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S2
    S2 --> S1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Question Bank]**

**Q1. Define the solar declination angle. What is its value on the day of the summer solstice?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The solar declination angle ($\delta$) is the angle between the line joining the centres of the Sun and Earth and the projection of this line on the Earth's equatorial plane. It represents the seasonal north–south position of the sun relative to the Equator.
It is given by Cooper's equation:
$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right]$$
On the **Summer Solstice (June 21, n = 172)**:
$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{360}{365}(284+172)\right] = 23.45^\circ \sin(360^\circ) \rightarrow \delta \approx +23.45^\circ$$
Hence on the summer solstice, $\delta$ is maximum positive, equal to Earth's axial tilt. (3 marks: definition + equation + numerical)

---

**Q2. What is meant by "Air Mass" in solar radiation measurement? State the AM 1.5 standard condition.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Air Mass (AM) is the ratio of the actual path length of solar radiation through the Earth's atmosphere to the path length when the sun is directly overhead (i.e., at zenith).
$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z}$$
The **AM 1.5 standard** corresponds to a solar zenith angle of $\theta_z = 48.19^\circ$, where $\sec\theta_z = 1.5$. It represents the average atmospheric path at mid-latitudes and is the international benchmark for rating photovoltaic modules (with solar irradiance normalized to 1000 W/m²). (3 marks: definition + formula + AM 1.5 condition)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Question Bank]**

**Q(a). Derive the expression for the solar zenith angle and altitude angle. Calculate the zenith angle for an observer at latitude $\phi = 13^\circ$N on 21st June at 2:00 PM solar time. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The solar zenith angle $\theta_z$ is derived using the spherical law of cosines applied to the celestial triangle formed by the zenith, the celestial pole, and the sun:
$$\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$$

The altitude angle is the complement:
$$\alpha = 90^\circ - \theta_z$$

**Numerical Computation:**

- Date: June 21, so $n = 172$.
- Latitude: $\phi = 13^\circ$N.
- Solar declination:
$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{360}{365}(284+172)\right] = 23.45^\circ \sin(360^\circ)$$

A more precise computation: $\frac{360}{365} \times 456 = 449.75^\circ$, then reduce by $360^\circ$ to get $89.75^\circ$.
$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin(89.75^\circ) \approx 23.45^\circ \times 0.99999 \approx +23.45^\circ$$

- Hour angle at 2:00 PM solar time:
$$\omega = 15^\circ \times (14 - 12) = 30^\circ$$

- Zenith angle:
$$\cos\theta_z = \sin(13^\circ)\sin(23.45^\circ) + \cos(13^\circ)\cos(23.45^\circ)\cos(30^\circ)$$

$$\sin(13^\circ) = 0.2250, \quad \cos(13^\circ) = 0.9744$$
$$\sin(23.45^\circ) = 0.3979, \quad \cos(23.45^\circ) = 0.9174$$
$$\cos(30^\circ) = 0.8660$$

$$\cos\theta_z = (0.2250)(0.3979) + (0.9744)(0.9174)(0.8660)$$
$$= 0.0895 + 0.7741 = 0.8636$$

$$\theta_z = \cos^{-1}(0.8636) \approx 30.32^\circ$$

- Altitude angle:
$$\alpha = 90^\circ - 30.32^\circ = 59.68^\circ$$

**Conclusion:** At 2:00 PM on June 21, the sun's altitude is approximately **59.7°** (very high in the sky, as expected for Kerala in summer).

---

**Q(b). Explain with neat sketches the role of the Earth's atmosphere on solar radiation. Discuss the mechanisms of absorption, scattering, and reflection. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Earth's atmosphere modifies the extraterrestrial solar radiation ($G_{sc} = 1367$ W/m²) through three principal mechanisms:

**1. Absorption (≈ 20% loss):**
Specific atmospheric gases absorb photons of particular wavelengths and convert the radiant energy into internal (thermal) energy of the gas:
- **Ozone (O₃)** — absorbs **ultraviolet** radiation ($\lambda < 0.38\,\mu$m).
- **Water Vapor (H₂O)** — absorbs in the **near-infrared** region (0.7–2 $\mu$m).
- **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)** — absorbs **far-infrared** radiation ($\lambda > 2\,\mu$m).

Absorption bands leave "windows" in the visible spectrum (0.38–0.78 $\mu$m), through which most terrestrial solar energy reaches the ground.

**2. Scattering (≈ 7% loss but dominant effect on diffuse radiation):**
- **Rayleigh Scattering** — caused by molecules much smaller than the wavelength ($\lambda \gg d$). Scattering coefficient $\propto 1/\lambda^4$, which is why the sky is **blue** (blue light scatters most) and sunsets are **red** (only red light reaches the observer through a long air mass).
- **Mie Scattering** — caused by aerosols and dust particles of size comparable to $\lambda$. Produces hazy white halos around the sun.
- **Non-selective Scattering** — caused by water droplets much larger than $\lambda$. Produces white clouds (all wavelengths scattered equally).

**3. Reflection (≈ 25% loss, mainly by clouds):**
Clouds have a high albedo ($\approx 0.6$); part of the incoming solar radiation is reflected back to space without ever entering the lower atmosphere. Snow-covered ground and high-albedo surfaces also contribute to local reflection.

**Net Effect on Surface Radiation:**
The radiation reaching the ground consists of three components:
$$I_G = I_b + I_d + I_r$$
where $I_b$ = beam (direct), $I_d$ = diffuse, $I_r$ = ground-reflected.

The clearness index $K_T = I_G / I_{on}$ quantifies atmospheric transparency and is crucial in sizing solar energy systems.

**Engineering Significance:** The atmosphere acts as a **selective filter**, removing harmful UV and IR while letting through the photosynthetically active and PV-active visible spectrum. For PV design, AM 1.5 standard spectrum is used; for clear-sky modeling, the Hottel–Whillier–Bliss equation is applied.

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Question Bank]**

**Q(a). With the help of a block diagram, explain the various components of solar geometry. Derive the equation for the extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface for the nth day of the year. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Components of Solar Geometry (Block Diagram):**

```
Solar Geometry
├── Input Parameters
│   ├── Latitude (φ)
│   ├── Day of Year (n)
│   └── Solar Time (T_sol)
├── Intermediate Angles
│   ├── Declination (δ)
│   └── Hour Angle (ω)
├── Derived Angles
│   ├── Zenith Angle (θ_z)
│   ├── Altitude Angle (α)
│   └── Azimuth Angle (γ_s)
└── Outputs
    ├── Air Mass (AM)
    ├── Beam Radiation (I_bn)
    └── Global Radiation (I_G)
```

The Earth's elliptical orbit means the actual Earth–Sun distance $r$ varies throughout the year, causing the extraterrestrial radiation $I_{on}$ to vary between approximately 1325 W/m² (aphelion, July) and 1425 W/m² (perihelion, January).

**Derivation of Extraterrestrial Radiation:**

The energy flux at any instant follows the inverse-square law:
$$\frac{I_{sc}}{I_{on}} = \left(\frac{r_0}{r}\right)^2$$

Let $E_0 = \left(\frac{r_0}{r}\right)^2$ be the eccentricity correction factor.

For an elliptical orbit with eccentricity $e$ and mean anomaly $M_a$:
$$E_0 = 1 + e\cos M_a + e^2 \cos^2 M_a + \dots$$

A commonly used engineering approximation (suitable for KTU problems) is:
$$E_0 = 1 + 0.033\cos\!\left(\frac{360n}{365}\right)$$

Therefore:
$$I_{on} = G_{sc} \times E_0 = 1367 \left[1 + 0.033\cos\!\left(\frac{360n}{365}\right)\right] \;\; \text{W/m}^2$$

On a surface normal to the sun's rays, this is the maximum extraterrestrial intensity available. For engineering design of PV and solar thermal systems, the monthly average daily extraterrestrial radiation $\bar{H}_0$ is computed by integrating $I_{on}$ over a day:
$$\bar{H}_0 = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi}\, G_{sc}\,E_0 \left(\cos\phi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s + \frac{\pi\omega_s}{180^\circ} \sin\phi \sin\delta\right) \;\; \text{J/m}^2/\text{day}$$

This expression combines solar geometry with the eccentricity correction.

---

**Q(b). A solar collector is located at latitude 28°N on 1st March. Calculate: (i) Solar declination, (ii) Hour angle at 10:00 AM solar time, (iii) Zenith angle, (iv) Altitude angle, (v) Air Mass. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $\phi = 28^\circ$N, Date = March 1, Time = 10:00 AM solar time.

**(i) Solar Declination:**
For March 1, $n = 60$.
$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{360}{365}(284+60)\right]$$
$$= 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{360 \times 344}{365}\right] = 23.45^\circ \sin(339.29^\circ)$$
$$\sin(339.29^\circ) = -\sin(20.71^\circ) = -0.3537$$
$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \times (-0.3537) \approx -8.29^\circ$$

**(ii) Hour Angle:**
$$\omega = 15^\circ \times (10 - 12) = -30^\circ$$
(Negative → sun is east of meridian, i.e., morning.)

**(iii) Zenith Angle:**
$$\cos\theta_z = \sin(28^\circ)\sin(-8.29^\circ) + \cos(28^\circ)\cos(-8.29^\circ)\cos(-30^\circ)$$
$$\sin(28^\circ) = 0.4695, \quad \cos(28^\circ) = 0.8829$$
$$\sin(-8.29^\circ) = -0.1442, \quad \cos(-8.29^\circ) = 0.9895$$
$$\cos(-30^\circ) = 0.8660$$
$$\cos\theta_z = (0.4695)(-0.1442) + (0.8829)(0.9895)(0.8660)$$
$$= -0.0677 + 0.7567 = 0.6890$$
$$\theta_z = \cos^{-1}(0.6890) \approx 46.46^\circ$$

**(iv) Altitude Angle:**
$$\alpha = 90^\circ - 46.46^\circ = 43.54^\circ$$

**(v) Air Mass:**
$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z} = \frac{1}{0.6890} \approx 1.451 \approx 1.45$$

**Conclusion:** The collector sees the sun at a moderately high altitude of 43.5° with Air Mass 1.45, typical for a spring morning at a sub-tropical latitude.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign confusion in $\omega$** — Morning is *negative*, afternoon is *positive*. Mixing this up flips the azimuth angle direction.
> 2. **Declination sign error** — Northern latitudes have *positive* $\delta$ between Spring and Autumn equinoxes. Using the wrong sign gives negative cosines for an obviously high sun.
> 3. **Unit mismatch** — Always convert **minutes** to **fractional hours** when computing $\omega$ (e.g., 2:30 PM = 14.5 h, not 14.30).
> 4. **Forgetting the $E_0$ factor** — KTU numericals often require the eccentricity correction; students who write $I_{on} = 1367$ (constant) lose marks.
> 5. **AM confusion** — Air Mass is dimensionless but *never less than 1* on Earth (1 only at the zenith). For $\theta_z = 0$, AM = 1, not 0.
> 6. **Cooper's equation domain** — It is valid for the standard Gregorian calendar; for leap years, the formula uses 366 and shifts by one day.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

#### 🔑 Quick-Recall Bullet Points

- **The Earth is tilted at $23.45^\circ$** → this single fact causes all seasons and declination variation.
- **Solar declination ($\delta$)** = seasonal tilt of the sun, ranging from $-23.45^\circ$ to $+23.45^\circ$. Use **Cooper's equation**.
- **Hour angle ($\omega$)** = Earth's rotation, $\omega = 15° \times (\text{Solar Time} - 12)$. Morning = negative.
- **Latitude ($\phi$)** is **fixed** for a given location (e.g., Trivandrum ≈ 8.5°N, Calicut ≈ 11.25°N).
- **Zenith angle ($\theta_z$)** = angle from vertical to sun; **Altitude angle ($\alpha$)** = angle above horizon. $\alpha + \theta_z = 90^\circ$.
- **The fundamental solar geometry equation**: $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$. **MEMORIZE THIS.**
- **Sunrise/Sunset hour angle** = $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)$.
- **Solar Constant** = **1367 W/m²**; modified by the **eccentricity correction factor** $E_0 = 1 + 0.033\cos(360n/365)$.
- **Air Mass** = $\sec\theta_z$; **AM 1.5** is the PV industry benchmark.
- **Beer's Law**: $I_{bn} = I_{on} e^{-k\cdot AM}$ — atmospheric attenuation is **exponential** with path length.
- **Three atmospheric mechanisms**: **A**bsorption (≈20%), **S**cattering (≈7%), **R**eflection (≈25%).
- **Absorbers** = O₃ (UV), H₂O (Near-IR), CO₂ (Far-IR).
- **Scatterers** = Rayleigh (molecules), Mie (aerosols), Non-selective (droplets).
- **Global radiation** = Beam + Diffuse + Reflected: $I_G = I_b + I_d + I_r$.
- **Clearness index** $K_T = I_G/I_{on}$ — higher means clearer sky.
- **Solar panel tilt** ≈ latitude ($\phi$) for maximum annual yield.
- **Sun-tracking** systems follow $(\alpha, \gamma_s)$ in real-time for maximum instantaneous gain.

#### 🎯 Common KTU Pitfalls to Avoid
1. Don't confuse **altitude** with **azimuth** — altitude is vertical, azimuth is horizontal.
2. The declination equation assumes the **day of the year** $n$, not the date directly.
3. The extinction coefficient $k$ is **not** the absorption coefficient alone; it includes scattering.
4. AM 1.5 corresponds to $\theta_z = 48.19°$, **not** $\theta_z = 45°$ (a common approximation error).
5. The atmosphere attenuates by approximately **30%** of incoming solar energy in clear-sky conditions; clouds can reduce this to near zero.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Mass
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET617 - SOLAR ENERGY CONSERVATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Air Mass

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air Mass (AM)** is a dimensionless quantity that quantifies the relative path length that direct solar radiation (beam radiation) travels through the Earth's atmosphere before striking a surface on the ground. It is mathematically defined as the ratio of the actual path length ($L$) of sunlight through the atmosphere to the **shortest possible path length** ($L_0$), which occurs when the Sun is directly overhead at the zenith.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$\text{AM} = \frac{L}{L_0}$$

By geometric convention, the shortest path (zenith position) is assigned a value of **AM = 1** when the Sun is directly overhead at sea level. When the Sun is at any other angular position, the path length increases, and so does the Air Mass number.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - OEMET617 Module 1):**
> Air Mass is a **foundational parameter** that influences:
> - The **spectral distribution** of solar radiation reaching the Earth's surface.
> - The **total irradiance** (power per unit area, expressed in **W/m²**) available for conversion.
> - The **standardization of solar cell and module testing** across the global photovoltaic industry.
> - The estimation of **atmospheric attenuation** effects such as scattering and absorption.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing at the bottom of a **swimming pool** on a sunny day. When you look straight up at the Sun, the sunlight passes through the **least amount of water** — the path is short and direct. Now, if you look at the Sun at a slanted angle (say, late in the afternoon), the light has to travel through a **much longer diagonal path** through the water, getting dimmer and more diffused along the way.

The **Earth's atmosphere** behaves exactly like that swimming pool. The thicker (longer) the atmospheric path the sunlight must travel through, the more it gets **scattered, absorbed, and weakened** by air molecules, dust, water vapor, and pollutants. **Air Mass** is simply a number that tells us *how thick* that atmospheric "swim" is.

- **AM1** → Sun directly overhead (noon at the equator) → shortest path.
- **AM1.5** → Sun at a moderate angle → path is 1.5 times longer.
- **AM38** → Sunrise/Sunset (Sun at the horizon) → path is enormously long.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Reference Values

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metrics for Solar Energy Engineering:**
>
> | Symbol/Quantity | Value | Description |
> |---|---|---|
> | $G_{sc}$ | **1361 W/m²** | Solar Constant (current accepted value, AM0 spectrum) |
> | $G_{sc}$ (legacy) | **1367 W/m²** | Older NASA standard value (AM0) |
> | $G_{STC}$ | **1000 W/m²** | Standard Test Condition irradiance |
> | Standard Spectrum | **AM1.5G** | Global standard for PV cell testing |
> | $\alpha$ | 41.81° | Solar altitude angle for AM1.5 |
> | $\theta_z$ | 48.19° | Zenith angle for AM1.5 |
> | Yearly average | **AM1.5G** | Used for terrestrial PV performance rating |

## 1.4 Why Air Mass Matters in Engineering

In solar energy systems (photovoltaic and solar thermal), the **performance of devices depends strongly on the spectrum of incident light**. Different Air Mass values correspond to different spectral distributions:

- **Low AM (AM0, AM1)**: Rich in short-wavelength (UV/blue) light.
- **High AM (AM2 and above)**: Red-shifted, attenuated short-wavelength components due to increased scattering.

This is why **photovoltaic cells are rated at AM1.5G** — it represents a realistic, average terrestrial solar spectrum that allows for **fair, reproducible performance comparison** across manufacturers worldwide.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Geometric Foundation of Air Mass

The atmosphere surrounding the Earth can be approximated as a series of **concentric spherical shells** of progressively increasing density as we descend toward the surface. For most engineering calculations, a **simplified flat-Earth (plane-parallel) atmosphere model** is used, which yields accurate results for solar zenith angles up to approximately **70°**.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Geometric Logic

**Step 1 — The Reference Path (Zenith Position):**
When the Sun is directly overhead, the solar radiation travels perpendicular to the Earth's surface. The path length is the **minimum**, and by definition:

$$L_0 = \text{vertical thickness of atmosphere}$$

**Step 2 — Tilted Path at Zenith Angle $\theta_z$:**
When the Sun moves away from the zenith by an angle $\theta_z$, the radiation must traverse the atmosphere diagonally. From elementary trigonometry, the path length becomes:

$$L = \frac{L_0}{\cos(\theta_z)}$$

**Step 3 — Forming the Air Mass Ratio:**

$$\text{AM} = \frac{L}{L_0} = \frac{L_0 / \cos(\theta_z)}{L_0} = \frac{1}{\cos(\theta_z)} = \sec(\theta_z)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The $\sec(\theta_z)$ relationship tells us that as the Sun gets closer to the horizon ($\theta_z \to 90°$), the Air Mass increases **non-linearly (tends to infinity)**. Even a few degrees of tilt can significantly increase the path length, especially at low Sun angles.

### 2.1.2 Relation to Solar Altitude Angle

The **solar altitude angle** ($\alpha$) is the complement of the zenith angle, measured from the **horizontal plane**:

$$\alpha + \theta_z = 90°$$

Therefore, the Air Mass can also be expressed as:

$$\text{AM} = \frac{1}{\cos(\theta_z)} = \frac{1}{\sin(\alpha)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** Students often confuse $\alpha$ (altitude) and $\theta_z$ (zenith). Remember: $\alpha$ is from the **horizon up**, $\theta_z$ is from the **overhead down**.

## 2.2 Special Air Mass Conditions

| Air Mass | Solar Position | Atmospheric Path | Irradiance (W/m²) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AM0** | Outside atmosphere (space) | None (0 km) | **1361** | Satellite PV, space-based solar cells |
| **AM1** | Sun at zenith ($\theta_z = 0°$) | Shortest possible | ~**1050** | Equatorial noon at sea level |
| **AM1.5G** | $\theta_z = 48.19°$ | 1.5× vertical | **1000** | **Standard PV testing (terrestrial)** |
| **AM1.5D** | $\theta_z = 48.19°$ | 1.5× vertical | **900** | Direct beam only (concentrator PV) |
| **AM2** | $\theta_z = 60°$ | 2× vertical | ~**800** | Mid-latitude morning/afternoon |
| **AM38** | Sun at horizon | ~38× vertical | ~**0–5** | Sunrise/Sunset |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AM1.5G vs AM1.5D:**
> - **G (Global):** Includes direct beam + diffuse sky radiation. Total = **1000 W/m²**.
> - **D (Direct):** Direct beam only. Total = **900 W/m²**. Used for concentrator systems.
> - The **200 W/m²** difference is the diffuse component at standard conditions.

## 2.3 The Kasten-Young Empirical Correction

For low Sun angles (high AM, near sunrise/sunset), the simple $\sec(\theta_z)$ model **overestimates** the Air Mass because the Earth's curvature becomes significant. The **Kasten-Young (1989)** empirical formula provides better accuracy:

$$\text{AM} = \frac{1}{\cos(\theta_z) + a \cdot (90° - \theta_z + b)^{-c}}$$

where the empirical constants are:
- $a = 0.50572$
- $b = 6.07995°$
- $c = 1.6364$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where Air Mass is Used Professionally:**
> 1. **PV Module Manufacturing:** All commercial solar panels are rated under **STC (Standard Test Conditions) = 1000 W/m² at AM1.5G spectrum at 25°C cell temperature**.
> 2. **Solar Resource Mapping:** Software (PVsyst, SAM, PVSOL) requires AM data for site-specific energy yield estimation.
> 3. **Concentrator Photovoltaics (CPV):** High-efficiency multijunction cells require **AM1.5D direct spectrum** (not global).
> 4. **Solar Thermal Collectors:** Performance depends on the **direct beam** component, which is strongly AM-dependent.
> 5. **Building-Integrated PV (BIPV):** Facade-integrated panels often operate at **AM3 to AM5** due to steep tilt angles.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CHEAT SHEET — Master These Equations:**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{AM} = 1/\cos(\theta_z)$ | Air Mass from zenith angle | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\text{AM} = 1/\sin(\alpha)$ | Air Mass from altitude angle | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{AM} = L/L_0$ | Path length ratio | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $G_{0} = G_{sc} \cdot (1 + 0.033 \cdot \cos(360n/365))$ | Extraterrestrial irradiance on day $n$ | W/m² |
| 5 | $G_{b} = G_{0} \cdot \tau_b$ | Beam irradiance (with transmittance $\tau_b$) | W/m² |
| 6 | $G_{0n} = G_{0} \cdot \cos(\theta_z)$ | Direct normal incidence (AM1) | W/m² |
| 7 | $\alpha = 90° - \theta_z$ | Altitude-zenith complement | Degrees |
| 8 | $m_a = 1/\left[\cos(\theta_z) + 0.50572 \cdot (90° - \theta_z + 6.07995)^{-1.6364}\right]$ | Kasten-Young (high $\theta_z$ correction) | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $G_{\text{STC}} = 1000$ W/m² at **AM1.5G** | PV standard test condition | W/m² |

**Where:**
- $G_{sc}$ = Solar constant = **1361 W/m²**
- $n$ = Day of the year (1 = Jan 1, 365 = Dec 31)
- $\tau_b$ = Atmospheric transmittance for beam radiation
- $\theta_z$ = Solar zenith angle
- $\alpha$ = Solar altitude angle
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Geometric Basis of Air Mass

### 3.1.1 The Setup

Consider the Earth as a sphere with a **plane-parallel atmosphere** of uniform vertical thickness $H$. Let the Sun's rays strike the atmosphere at a zenith angle $\theta_z$ (measured from the local vertical at the observer's location).

**Given:**
- $L_0 = H$ (vertical path length when Sun is at zenith)
- $\theta_z$ = solar zenith angle
- $L$ = diagonal path length through the atmosphere

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Draw the geometry.**
A ray of sunlight entering the atmosphere at the top and reaching the observer on the ground forms a right-angled triangle, where:
- The **vertical side** is the atmospheric thickness $L_0 = H$.
- The **hypotenuse** is the actual ray path $L$.
- The **angle between the hypotenuse and the vertical** is $\theta_z$.

**Step 2 — Apply trigonometry to the right triangle.**
By definition of cosine in a right triangle:

$$\cos(\theta_z) = \frac{\text{adjacent}}{\text{hypotenuse}} = \frac{L_0}{L}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $L$.**
Rearranging for the hypotenuse $L$:

$$L = \frac{L_0}{\cos(\theta_z)}$$

**Step 4 — Form the Air Mass ratio.**
By definition, $\text{AM} = L / L_0$. Substituting:

$$\text{AM} = \frac{L_0 / \cos(\theta_z)}{L_0} = \frac{1}{\cos(\theta_z)}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify using the secant function:**

$$\boxed{\text{AM} = \sec(\theta_z) = \frac{1}{\cos(\theta_z)}}$$

**Step 6 — Express in terms of altitude angle.**
Since $\theta_z = 90° - \alpha$:

$$\cos(\theta_z) = \cos(90° - \alpha) = \sin(\alpha)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{AM} = \frac{1}{\sin(\alpha)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation Note:** This derivation assumes a **flat (plane-parallel)** atmosphere, which is valid for $\theta_z \leq 70°$. Beyond this, the Earth's curvature must be considered, leading to the Kasten-Young correction.

## 3.2 Analytical Calculation: Air Mass at Standard Test Conditions

**Problem:** Verify that $\theta_z = 48.19°$ corresponds to AM1.5.

**Solution:**

$$\text{AM} = \frac{1}{\cos(\theta_z)} = \frac{1}{\cos(48.19°)}$$

$$\cos(48.19°) = 0.6667$$

$$\text{AM} = \frac{1}{0.6667} = 1.500$$

$$\therefore \text{AM} = 1.5 \;\; \checkmark$$

## 3.3 Analytical Calculation: Air Mass at a Typical Solar Noon in Kerala

**Problem:** On March 21 (vernal equinox), at solar noon in Trivandrum (latitude $\phi = 8.5°$ N), find the Air Mass.

**Step 1 — Determine the solar declination on March 21.**
On the equinox, the Sun is directly over the equator:

$$\delta = 0°$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the zenith angle at solar noon.**
At solar noon, the Sun is due south (in the Northern Hemisphere), and the zenith angle is:

$$\theta_z = \vert \phi - \delta \vert = \vert 8.5° - 0° \vert = 8.5°$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Air Mass.**

$$\text{AM} = \frac{1}{\cos(8.5°)} = \frac{1}{0.9890} = 1.0111$$

$$\boxed{\text{AM} \approx 1.01 \;\; \text{(essentially AM1)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Trivandrum, being very close to the equator, experiences an **AM value very close to 1** at solar noon on the equinox — meaning the Sun is nearly directly overhead and the atmosphere is at its thinnest possible optical thickness.

## 3.4 Analytical Calculation: Air Mass at Sunset

**Problem:** Determine the Air Mass when the Sun is exactly at the horizon ($\theta_z = 90°$).

**Step 1 — Apply the simple formula:**

$$\text{AM} = \frac{1}{\cos(90°)} = \frac{1}{0} = \infty$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Kasten-Young correction** (more realistic):

$$\text{AM} = \frac{1}{\cos(90°) + 0.50572 \cdot (90° - 90° + 6.07995)^{-1.6364}}$$

$$\text{AM} = \frac{1}{0 + 0.50572 \cdot (6.07995)^{-1.6364}}$$

$$\text{AM} = \frac{1}{0.50572 \cdot (0.16448)^{-1}}$$

$$\text{AM} = \frac{1}{0.50572 \cdot 6.07995^{1.6364}}$$

$$6.07995^{1.6364} \approx 17.965$$

$$\text{AM} = \frac{1}{0.50572 \cdot 17.965} = \frac{1}{9.086} \approx 37.9 \approx \textbf{AM38}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why AM38 (not infinity)?** At the apparent horizon, atmospheric refraction **bends sunlight** so the Sun is actually slightly below the geometric horizon. The Kasten-Young model gives a finite value of approximately **AM38** at the visual horizon.

## 3.5 Analytical Calculation: Extraterrestrial Irradiance on a Given Day

**Problem:** Calculate the extraterrestrial normal irradiance on day $n = 80$ (March 21) using $G_{sc} = 1361$ W/m².

**Formula:**

$$G_{0} = G_{sc} \cdot \left(1 + 0.033 \cdot \cos\left(\frac{360 \cdot n}{365}\right)\right)$$

**Step 1 — Compute the angle term:**

$$\frac{360 \cdot 80}{365} = \frac{28800}{365} = 78.904°$$

**Step 2 — Compute the cosine:**

$$\cos(78.904°) = 0.1932$$

**Step 3 — Apply the formula:**

$$G_{0} = 1361 \cdot (1 + 0.033 \cdot 0.1932)$$

$$G_{0} = 1361 \cdot (1 + 0.006376)$$

$$G_{0} = 1361 \cdot 1.006376 = 1369.7 \;\; \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Direct normal incidence at AM1.5 zenith angle:**

$$G_{0n} = G_0 \cdot \cos(\theta_z) = 1369.7 \cdot \cos(48.19°) = 1369.7 \cdot 0.6667 = 913.1 \;\; \text{W/m}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Connection to STC:** Notice that the extraterrestrial normal irradiance (~913 W/m² at AM1.5) is close to the **AM1.5D standard of 900 W/m²**. The slight difference is due to atmospheric attenuation being baked into the 900 W/m² standard.

## 3.6 Summary of Analytical Results

| Scenario | $\theta_z$ | AM (Plane-Parallel) | AM (Kasten-Young) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sun at zenith | 0° | **AM1** | **AM1** |
| Standard PV testing | 48.19° | **AM1.5** | **AM1.5** |
| Kerala equinox noon | 8.5° | **AM1.01** | **AM1.01** |
| Sun at 70° | 70° | **AM2.92** | **AM2.86** |
| Sun at 85° | 85° | **AM11.47** | **AM8.95** |
| Sun at horizon | 90° | **$\infty$** | **AM38** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Geometric Model of Air Mass

The following block diagram illustrates the conceptual architecture of the Air Mass geometric model, showing how the zenith angle and altitude angle relate to the atmospheric path length.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Extraterrestrial Solar Radiation] --> B[Top of Atmosphere]
    B --> C{Atmospheric Path Geometry}
    C -->|Zenith Sun| D[Vertical Path L0]
    C -->|Tilted Sun| E[Diagonal Path L]
    D --> F[AM equals 1]
    E --> G[AM equals 1 divided by cos of zenith angle]
    F --> H[Ground Level Irradiance]
    G --> H
    H --> I{Solar Altitude Alpha}
    I -->|Alpha equals 90 degrees| J[AM1 Spectrum]
    I -->|Alpha equals 41.81 degrees| K[AM1.5G Standard]
    I -->|Alpha equals 0 degrees| L[AM38 Horizon]
    K --> M[PV Module Performance Rating]
    J --> N[Space Applications]
    L --> O[Sunrise and Sunset Transition]
```

## 4.2 Air Mass Spectral Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Extraterrestrial
        A0[AM0 - 1361 W per square meter]
    end
    subgraph Terrestrial High Sun
        A1[AM1 - Zenith Position]
        A15[AM1.5G - Standard Test]
        A2[AM2 - Morning and Afternoon]
    end
    subgraph Terrestrial Low Sun
        A5[AM5 - Mid Morning and Late Afternoon]
        A10[AM10 - Dawn and Dusk]
        A38[AM38 - Horizon]
    end
    A0 --> A1
    A1 --> A15
    A15 --> A2
    A2 --> A5
    A5 --> A10
    A10 --> A38
    A0 -.->|Used in space| S1[Satellite Solar Cells]
    A15 -.->|Used in terrestrial| S2[PV Module STC Rating]
    A15 -.->|Direct beam only| S3[Concentrator CPV Systems]
```

## 4.3 Air Mass Effect on Solar Spectrum — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[Incoming Sunlight at TOA] --> ATMOS[Atmospheric Attenuation]
    ATMOS --> SCATTER[Rayleigh Scattering]
    ATMOS --> ABSORB[Absorption by Ozone Water Vapor and CO2]
    SCATTER --> DIFFUSE[Diffuse Component]
    DIFFUSE --> GLOBAL[Global Horizontal Irradiance GHI]
    ABSORB --> DIRECT[Direct Beam Component]
    DIRECT --> GLOBAL
    GLOBAL --> AMCHECK{Air Mass Value}
    AMCHECK -->|Low AM 1.0 to 1.5| HIGHBLUE[Rich in UV and Blue]
    AMCHECK -->|Mid AM 2.0 to 5.0| BALANCED[Balanced Visible Spectrum]
    AMCHECK -->|High AM above 5| REDSHIFT[Red Shifted Attenuated Blue]
    HIGHBLUE --> PVCELL[PV Cell Response]
    BALANCED --> PVCELL
    REDSHIFT --> PVCELL
    PVCELL --> OUTPUT[Electrical Power Output W]
```

## 4.4 Air Mass Calculation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start: Given Solar Geometry] --> Q1{Zenith Angle Known?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| CALC1[Compute AM equals 1 divided by cos of theta z]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Latitude Phi and Declination Delta Known?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| CALC2[Compute cos of theta z equals sin phi sin delta plus cos phi cos delta cos omega]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Time of Day and Day of Year Known?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| CALC3[Use solar position equations to find theta z]
    Q3 -->|No| STOP1[Insufficient Data - Cannot Compute]
    CALC2 --> CALC1
    CALC3 --> CALC1
    CALC1 --> VALIDATE{theta z less than 70 degrees?}
    VALIDATE -->|Yes| RESULT[Final AM from sec formula]
    VALIDATE -->|No| KASTEN[Apply Kasten-Young Correction]
    KASTEN --> RESULT
    RESULT --> APP[Application: PV Sizing or Solar Resource Assessment]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading Tip:** Each Mermaid block shows the *functional flow* of how air mass is determined, classified, and applied in solar engineering practice — a useful mental model for KTU exam derivations.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Air Mass. Explain the significance of AM0, AM1.5G, and AM1.5D in solar energy systems.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Air Mass (AM) is defined as the ratio of the optical path length of solar radiation through the Earth's atmosphere to the shortest possible path length (Sun at zenith). Mathematically:

$$\text{AM} = \frac{L}{L_0} = \frac{1}{\cos(\theta_z)} = \sec(\theta_z)$$

**Significance:**

| Air Mass | Condition | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **AM0** | Zero atmosphere (extraterrestrial) | Satellite and space-based PV cells; reference solar constant = **1361 W/m²** |
| **AM1.5G** | Sun at $\theta_z = 48.19°$ at sea level | **Standard Test Condition (STC)** for terrestrial PV module rating at **1000 W/m²** total irradiance |
| **AM1.5D** | Same geometry, direct beam only | Reference for **Concentrator Photovoltaics (CPV)** at **900 W/m²** direct beam |

**Key Point:** AM1.5G is the universal benchmark for comparing PV modules because it represents a **realistic mid-latitude average terrestrial spectrum**, including both direct and diffuse components. The 'G' stands for **Global** (direct + diffuse), while 'D' represents only the **Direct** beam component.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**With the help of a suitable diagram, derive the relationship $\text{AM} = 1/\cos(\theta_z)$.**

**Model Answer:**

**Setup:** Consider the atmosphere as a plane-parallel layer of uniform vertical thickness $L_0$. Let the Sun be at a zenith angle $\theta_z$ from the local vertical.

**Diagram (description):**
- A vertical line represents the atmospheric column of height $L_0$.
- A slanted line from the top of the atmosphere to the observer at the ground represents the solar ray path $L$.
- The angle between the slanted ray and the vertical is $\theta_z$.

**Derivation:**

From the geometry of the right triangle formed:

$$\cos(\theta_z) = \frac{\text{adjacent}}{\text{hypotenuse}} = \frac{L_0}{L}$$

Rearranging:

$$L = \frac{L_0}{\cos(\theta_z)}$$

Since $\text{AM} = L / L_0$:

$$\text{AM} = \frac{L_0 / \cos(\theta_z)}{L_0} = \frac{1}{\cos(\theta_z)}$$

$$\boxed{\therefore \text{AM} = \sec(\theta_z) = \frac{1}{\cos(\theta_z)}}$$

**Limitations:** This formula assumes a **flat (plane-parallel) atmosphere** and is accurate for $\theta_z \leq 70°$. For higher zenith angles (Sun near the horizon), the **Kasten-Young correction** must be applied.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question B (Choice A)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks)

**Explain in detail the concept of Air Mass in solar energy engineering. Derive the geometric relationship between Air Mass and solar zenith angle. Discuss the significance of AM1.5G as the standard spectrum for photovoltaic testing, with a comparison of AM0, AM1.5G, and AM1.5D. Discuss why the simple secant formula fails near sunrise and sunset, and how the Kasten-Young correction addresses this.**

#### **Part (a): Air Mass Definition, Geometric Derivation, and Standard Air Mass Values (7 Marks)**

**1. Definition of Air Mass (1 Mark):**
Air Mass is the ratio of the actual path length of direct solar radiation through the Earth's atmosphere to the path length when the Sun is at the zenith (overhead). It is a dimensionless number indicating the thickness of atmosphere the solar radiation must traverse.

**2. Geometric Derivation (3 Marks):**

Consider a flat-Earth atmosphere of vertical thickness $H = L_0$. The solar ray strikes at zenith angle $\theta_z$.

Step 1 — From the right triangle:

$$\cos(\theta_z) = \frac{L_0}{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad L = \frac{L_0}{\cos(\theta_z)}$$

Step 2 — Applying the Air Mass definition:

$$\text{AM} = \frac{L}{L_0} = \frac{L_0 / \cos(\theta_z)}{L_0} = \frac{1}{\cos(\theta_z)} = \sec(\theta_z)$$

Step 3 — Using $\alpha + \theta_z = 90°$:

$$\text{AM} = \frac{1}{\sin(\alpha)}$$

**3. Standard Air Mass Values (3 Marks):**

| Air Mass | Condition | Irradiance | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| AM0 | Space (no atmosphere) | **1361 W/m²** | Satellite PV |
| AM1.5G | $\theta_z = 48.19°$, global | **1000 W/m²** | PV module STC rating |
| AM1.5D | $\theta_z = 48.19°$, direct only | **900 W/m²** | Concentrator CPV |

---

#### **Part (b): Significance of AM1.5G, Failure of Simple Formula, Kasten-Young Correction (7 Marks)**

**1. Significance of AM1.5G as PV Standard (3 Marks):**

- The **AM1.5G spectrum** is the **terrestrial reference spectrum** defined by the American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM G-173).
- It represents a **realistic mid-latitude average** (e.g., 48.2° zenith) with **typical atmospheric conditions**: water vapor, aerosol, and ozone.
- It contains both **direct + diffuse** components, totaling **1000 W/m²** — making it a representative, not theoretical, condition.
- All commercial PV modules are flash-tested at this standard to ensure **fair global comparison** of performance.

**2. Failure of the Secant Formula at Low Sun Angles (2 Marks):**

As $\theta_z \to 90°$ (Sun at the horizon):

$$\text{AM} = \frac{1}{\cos(90°)} = \frac{1}{0} = \infty \quad \text{(Physically Unrealistic!)}$$

The plane-parallel model **ignores Earth's curvature** and assumes uniform density atmosphere. In reality, the path through the lower (denser) atmosphere dominates, but rays can also "cut through" the upper atmosphere more efficiently due to curvature. This makes the simple formula **overestimate** Air Mass at high $\theta_z$.

**3. Kasten-Young Correction (2 Marks):**

$$\text{AM}_{KY} = \frac{1}{\cos(\theta_z) + 0.50572 \cdot (90° - \theta_z + 6.07995)^{-1.6364}}$$

At $\theta_z = 90°$:

$$\text{AM}_{KY} = \frac{1}{0 + 0.50572 \cdot (6.07995)^{-1.6364}} \approx \frac{1}{0.0264} \approx 37.9 \approx \textbf{AM38}$$

This finite, physically meaningful value (AM38) is consistent with **atmospheric refraction bending** sunlight over the horizon, confirming the formula's validity.

---

### **Question B (Choice B)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**Define Air Mass. With appropriate equations, show how Air Mass is related to the solar zenith angle and altitude angle. Calculate the Air Mass and extraterrestrial irradiance on 21st March at solar noon for a location at latitude 10°N. Given: Solar constant $G_{sc} = 1361$ W/m², declination on equinox = 0°.**

#### **Part (a): Air Mass Definition and Angular Relationships (7 Marks)**

**1. Definition (2 Marks):**
Air Mass (AM) is the ratio of the optical path length of direct solar radiation through the Earth's atmosphere to the vertical (zenith) path length. It indicates how many "atmospheric thicknesses" the solar ray has to traverse.

$$\text{AM} = \frac{L}{L_0}$$

**2. Relation to Zenith Angle $\theta_z$ (3 Marks):**

From the plane-parallel atmosphere model:

$$\boxed{\text{AM} = \frac{1}{\cos(\theta_z)} = \sec(\theta_z)}$$

**3. Relation to Altitude Angle $\alpha$ (2 Marks):**

Since $\theta_z = 90° - \alpha$:

$$\cos(90° - \alpha) = \sin(\alpha)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{AM} = \frac{1}{\sin(\alpha)}}$$

**Significance:** The Air Mass value tells us the spectral character and intensity of solar radiation. Low AM → intense, blue-rich spectrum; high AM → weakened, red-shifted spectrum.

---

#### **Part (b): Calculation of Air Mass and Extraterrestrial Irradiance for the Given Location (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- Latitude: $\phi = 10°$ N
- Day: March 21 (equinox) → Declination $\delta = 0°$
- Solar constant: $G_{sc} = 1361$ W/m²
- Day number: $n = 80$ (March 21)

**Step 1 — Find the solar zenith angle at solar noon (1 Mark):**
At solar noon, the hour angle $\omega = 0°$, and the zenith angle is:

$$\cos(\theta_z) = \sin(\phi) \sin(\delta) + \cos(\phi) \cos(\delta) \cos(\omega)$$

$$\cos(\theta_z) = \sin(10°) \sin(0°) + \cos(10°) \cos(0°) \cos(0°)$$

$$\cos(\theta_z) = 0 + \cos(10°) \cdot 1 \cdot 1 = 0.9848$$

$$\theta_z = \arccos(0.9848) = 10.0°$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Air Mass (1 Mark):**

$$\text{AM} = \frac{1}{\cos(\theta_z)} = \frac{1}{0.9848} = 1.0155$$

$$\boxed{\text{AM} \approx 1.02}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the extraterrestrial irradiance $G_{0}$ (2 Marks):**

$$G_{0} = G_{sc} \cdot \left(1 + 0.033 \cdot \cos\left(\frac{360 \cdot n}{365}\right)\right)$$

$$G_{0} = 1361 \cdot \left(1 + 0.033 \cdot \cos\left(\frac{360 \cdot 80}{365}\right)\right)$$

$$G_{0} = 1361 \cdot (1 + 0.033 \cdot \cos(78.90°))$$

$$G_{0} = 1361 \cdot (1 + 0.033 \cdot 0.1932)$$

$$G_{0} = 1361 \cdot (1 + 0.006376) = 1361 \cdot 1.006376$$

$$\boxed{G_{0} \approx 1369.7 \;\; \text{W/m}^2}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the direct normal irradiance on a horizontal surface (1 Mark):**

$$G_{0n} = G_{0} \cdot \cos(\theta_z) = 1369.7 \cdot 0.9848$$

$$\boxed{G_{0n} \approx 1349.1 \;\; \text{W/m}^2}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (2 Marks):**

- The Air Mass is **AM ≈ 1.02**, essentially at the **AM1** benchmark, meaning the Sun is nearly directly overhead at this equatorial location on the equinox.
- The extraterrestrial irradiance $G_0 \approx 1369.7$ W/m² is slightly above the solar constant because the Earth-Sun distance is near its **perihelion** in early January and the eccentricity correction still adds a small positive factor.
- At ground level, atmospheric attenuation would reduce this to approximately **1000–1050 W/m²** total at AM1.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $\alpha$ with $\theta_z$:** Students often write $\text{AM} = \cos(\alpha)$ instead of $1/\sin(\alpha)$. Remember: altitude is from horizon, zenith is from overhead. They sum to 90°.
> 2. **Forgetting the declination:** When computing $\theta_z$ for a specific day and location, students forget that declination $\delta$ is **not zero** except on equinoxes. The formula $\cos(\theta_z) = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$ must be used.
> 3. **Using 1367 instead of 1361:** The current accepted solar constant is **1361 W/m²** (post-2011 NASA re-evaluation). Using 1367 (legacy) may cost a mark if examiner is strict.
> 4. **Unit errors in $G_0$:** Students sometimes write the unit as "kW/m²" or forget to multiply by the eccentricity factor. Always show: $G_{sc} = 1361$ W/m², then apply the correction.
> 5. **Not mentioning AM1.5G = 1000 W/m²** as a separate value: Many students confuse 1000 W/m² with $G_{sc}$. They are different — $G_{sc}$ is **extraterrestrial (AM0)**, while 1000 W/m² is the **terrestrial standard (AM1.5G)**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — Air Mass**
>
> - **Definition:** $\text{AM} = L / L_0$ — ratio of actual path to vertical path.
> - **Core Formula:** $\text{AM} = \sec(\theta_z) = 1/\cos(\theta_z) = 1/\sin(\alpha)$.
> - **Angle Complement:** $\alpha + \theta_z = 90°$ (altitude + zenith = 90°).
> - **AM0:** Space spectrum, $G_{sc} = 1361$ W/m², used for satellite PV.
> - **AM1.5G:** $\theta_z = 48.19°$, **1000 W/m²** total — PV standard.
> - **AM1.5D:** Same geometry, **900 W/m²** direct only — CPV standard.
> - **AM1.5G - AM1.5D = 200 W/m²** = diffuse sky component at STC.
> - **Secant limit:** Valid only for $\theta_z \leq 70°$.
> - **Kasten-Young:** Use for $\theta_z > 70°$; gives AM ≈ **38** at horizon.
> - **Eccentricity correction:** $G_0 = G_{sc} \cdot (1 + 0.033 \cos(360n/365))$.
> - **Physical meaning:** Higher AM → more atmospheric attenuation → red-shifted, dimmer radiation.
> - **Practical impact:** PV modules lose ~0.5% efficiency per unit increase in AM beyond AM1.5.
> - **Kerala context:** Latitude 8–12°N gives AM ≈ 1.0 to 1.05 at noon on equinoxes.
> - **Key examiner points:** (i) Always state the standard value clearly. (ii) Draw geometry before deriving. (iii) Specify limitation of secant formula. (iv) Know the difference between G and D spectra.
> - **Common mistake to avoid:** Writing $\text{AM} = \cos(\theta_z)$ instead of $\sec(\theta_z)$.
> - **Real-world link:** All your household rooftop solar panels are rated at AM1.5G, 1000 W/m², 25°C.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
