topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET617 - SOLAR ENERGY CONSERVATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Terrestrial Spectrum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

The **Terrestrial Spectrum** (also called the **Terrestrial Solar Spectrum** or **AM1.5 Spectrum**) is defined as the spectral distribution of solar radiation intensity received at the **Earth's surface** after the extraterrestrial solar radiation has undergone selective attenuation by the Earth's atmosphere.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The terrestrial spectrum is fundamentally different from the **extraterrestrial spectrum (AM0)**. While AM0 represents the unfiltered solar radiation incident at the top of the atmosphere (Air Mass Zero), the terrestrial spectrum is the result of wavelength-dependent **absorption**, **scattering**, and **reflection** by atmospheric constituents (gases, water vapor, dust, and aerosols).

Mathematically, the terrestrial spectral irradiance $G_{\lambda,\text{terr}}$ at wavelength $\lambda$ is related to the extraterrestrial spectral irradiance $G_{\lambda,\text{ext}}$ by the **Bouguer–Lambert Law**:

$$G_{\lambda,\text{terr}} = G_{\lambda,\text{ext}} \cdot \tau_{\lambda}^{m}$$

where:
- $\tau_{\lambda}$ is the **spectral atmospheric transmittance** at wavelength $\lambda$ (a dimensionless quantity, $0 < \tau_{\lambda} < 1$).
- $m$ is the **air mass number** (a dimensionless geometric path-length factor).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Stained-Glass Window"**
>
> Imagine sunlight passing through an old church's stained-glass window. The window is not uniform — some colored glass absorbs red light, some blue glass absorbs green, and dirt on the panes scatters light in random directions. What reaches the congregation inside is no longer the pure white sunlight that hit the window; it has been *colored* and *weakened* by the glass.
>
> The Earth's atmosphere is exactly that stained-glass window. Ozone acts like a dark-purple pane absorbing ultraviolet, water vapor acts like brown glass absorbing infrared, and tiny air molecules scatter the blue light in all directions (giving us the blue sky). The **terrestrial spectrum** is the "filtered light" that finally reaches the ground — and that is the spectrum we must work with when designing solar collectors, photovoltaic cells, and thermal systems.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standardized Reference Values (use these exact values in KTU exams):**
>
> | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value |
> |---|---|---|
> | Solar constant (total irradiance) | $G_{sc}$ | **1367 W/m²** (older AM0 value) or **1361 W/m²** (current NASA SORCE) |
> | Air Mass at sea level, sun at zenith | AM | **1.0** (AM1.0) |
> | Standard terrestrial reference | AM | **1.5** (AM1.5) — corresponds to solar zenith angle $\theta_z = 48.19°$ |
> | Total integrated terrestrial irradiance at AM1.5 | $G_{\text{terr}}$ | **≈ 1000 W/m²** (the KTU-conventional rounded value) |
> | Peak spectral irradiance (AM1.5, visible) | $G_{\lambda,\text{peak}}$ | ≈ **1.6 W·m⁻²·nm⁻¹** near 500 nm |
> | Speed of light | $c$ | **2.998 × 10⁸ m/s** |
> | Planck's constant | $h$ | **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** |
> | Boltzmann constant | $k_B$ | **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K** |
> | Solar surface temperature (approx. black-body) | $T_s$ | **5778 K** |

## 1.4 Why the Terrestrial Spectrum Matters in Solar Engineering

- **PV cell selection:** A silicon solar cell responds strongly to 0.4–1.1 μm; thin-film CdTe responds to a different band. The terrestrial spectrum dictates which material best converts sunlight at a given location.
- **Thermal collector design:** Selective coatings are tuned to absorb the *terrestrial* solar band (0.3–2.5 μm) while suppressing infrared re-emission.
- **Energy-yield prediction:** Annual energy output of any solar device depends on the *terrestrial* spectrum, not the AM0 spectrum.
- **Climate and atmospheric science:** The terrestrial spectrum is the input to every atmospheric energy-balance calculation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sun as a Black-Body Source

The Sun, to a very good first approximation, behaves as a **black-body radiator** at temperature $T_s \approx 5778$ K. Its spectral emissive power follows **Planck's Law**:

$$E_{\lambda,b} = \frac{2 \pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5} \left[ \exp\!\left( \dfrac{h c}{\lambda k_B T_s}\right) - 1 \right]}$$

The **extraterrestrial spectral irradiance** at the Earth's orbital distance is then:

$$G_{\lambda,\text{ext}} = E_{\lambda,b}(T_s) \cdot \left( \frac{R_s}{R_{se}} \right)^{2}$$

where $R_s$ is the Sun's radius and $R_{se}$ is the mean Sun–Earth distance (1 AU ≈ 1.496 × 10¹¹ m). The geometric dilution factor $\left(R_s / R_{se}\right)^{2} \approx 2.165 \times 10^{-5}$ converts emitted flux to flux at Earth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step logic of atmospheric modification:**
> 1. Sun emits broadband black-body radiation peaking near **0.5 μm** (visible green).
> 2. Radiation travels 1.496 × 10¹¹ m through near-vacuum — essentially **unattenuated** (this is the AM0 spectrum).
> 3. Radiation enters the atmosphere; its path length through the atmosphere depends on the sun's elevation.
> 4. Gases and particles selectively **absorb** photons at specific wavelengths (molecular resonance absorption).
> 5. Molecules and aerosols **scatter** photons out of the direct beam (Rayleigh and Mie scattering).
> 6. Clouds and ground **reflect** a portion back to space.
> 7. The remaining radiation reaches the surface — this is the **terrestrial spectrum**.

## 2.2 Air Mass — The Geometric Path-Length Factor

The **air mass** $m$ is the ratio of the actual path length of solar radiation through the atmosphere to the vertical (zenith) path length. For a plane-parallel atmosphere:

$$m = \frac{1}{\cos \theta_z} = \frac{1}{\sin \alpha}$$

where:
- $\theta_z$ = **solar zenith angle** (angle from vertical)
- $\alpha$ = **solar altitude angle** (angle from horizontal) = $90° - \theta_z$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU numerical shortcut:** $\theta_z = 48.19° \Rightarrow \cos \theta_z \approx 0.6667 \Rightarrow m = 1.5$. This is exactly the **AM1.5** reference condition that all standard terrestrial spectral data tables are normalized to. For $\theta_z > 90°$ (sun below horizon) the formula does not apply — there is no direct beam.

The **Kasten–Young** empirical formula (more accurate for low sun angles, useful for early-morning/late-evening calculations):

$$m = \frac{1}{\cos \theta_z + a\,(90° - \theta_z)^{-b}}, \quad a = 0.50572,\; b = 0.08465$$

## 2.3 Atmospheric Transmittance — The Bouguer–Lambert Law

The **spectral atmospheric transmittance** $\tau_{\lambda}$ accounts for all wavelength-dependent losses. It can be factored into contributions from each attenuating species:

$$\tau_{\lambda} = \tau_{\lambda,\text{Ray}} \cdot \tau_{\lambda,\text{Mie}} \cdot \tau_{\lambda,\text{O}_3} \cdot \tau_{\lambda,\text{H}_2\text{O}} \cdot \tau_{\lambda,\text{CO}_2} \cdot \tau_{\lambda,\text{other}}$$

**Component breakdown:**

- **Rayleigh scattering** $\tau_{\lambda,\text{Ray}} \propto \lambda^{-4}$ — dominates at short wavelengths; responsible for blue sky.
- **Mie scattering** $\tau_{\lambda,\text{Mie}}$ — wavelength-independent in the visible; caused by aerosols and dust.
- **Ozone absorption** $\tau_{\lambda,\text{O}_3}$ — strong in the UV (Hartley band 0.2–0.3 μm; Huggins band 0.3–0.36 μm).
- **Water vapor absorption** $\tau_{\lambda,\text{H}_2\text{O}}$ — strong in IR (0.7, 0.8, 0.94, 1.13, 1.38, 1.86, 2.5 μm bands).
- **CO₂ absorption** $\tau_{\lambda,\text{CO}_2}$ — strong bands at 1.45, 1.6, 2.0, 2.7 μm.
- **O₂ absorption** $\tau_{\lambda,\text{O}_2}$ — bands at 0.69, 0.76 μm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** Beyond about **2.5 μm**, terrestrial irradiance is essentially zero. This is why "solar" absorbers in PV and thermal applications are designed for the **0.3 – 2.5 μm** band — the so-called **solar window**.

## 2.4 Beam, Diffuse, and Global Terrestrial Radiation

At the Earth's surface, terrestrial radiation is conventionally split into two components:

$$G_{\text{terr}} = G_b + G_d$$

where:
- $G_b$ = **Beam (direct) radiation** — collimated rays from the solar disk (≈ a 0.5° cone).
- $G_d$ = **Diffuse radiation** — arrives from the entire sky dome after scattering.

The **diffuse fraction** $k_d = G_d / G_{\text{terr}}$ increases with cloudiness and with higher air mass. A typical clear-sky midday value is $k_d \approx 0.1 - 0.2$; an overcast sky gives $k_d \approx 1.0$.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print or memorize this table — it covers ≈ 80% of KTU exam questions on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Standard Units / Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G_{\lambda,\text{terr}} = G_{\lambda,\text{ext}} \cdot \tau_{\lambda}^{m}$ | Bouguer–Lambert spectral attenuation | W·m⁻²·nm⁻¹ |
| 2 | $m = 1 / \cos \theta_z$ | Air mass (plane-parallel) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $m = 1.5$ at $\theta_z = 48.19°$ | Standard terrestrial reference | $G_{\text{terr}} \approx 1000$ W/m² |
| 4 | $G_{sc} = 1367$ W/m² | Solar constant (AM0, 1 AU) | W/m² |
| 5 | $E_{\lambda,b} = \dfrac{2\pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\tfrac{h c}{\lambda k_B T}\right) - 1\right]}$ | Planck's spectral emissive power | W·m⁻²·m⁻¹ |
| 6 | $\tau_{\lambda} = \tau_{\text{Ray}} \cdot \tau_{\text{Mie}} \cdot \tau_{\text{O}_3} \cdot \tau_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \cdots$ | Spectral transmittance (product of species terms) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\tau_{\lambda,\text{Ray}} \propto \lambda^{-4}$ | Rayleigh scattering dependence | dimensionless |
| 8 | $G_{\text{terr}} = G_b + G_d$ | Beam + Diffuse decomposition | W/m² |
| 9 | $k_d = G_d / G_{\text{terr}}$ | Diffuse fraction | $0 \le k_d \le 1$ |
| 10 | $\alpha_{\max} = \dfrac{G_b}{G_{b,\text{normal}}}$ | Incidence-angle modifier (on tilted surface) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = 2898 \; \mu\text{m·K}$ | Wien's displacement law (peak wavelength) | $\mu$m·K |
| 12 | $E_b = \sigma T^{4}$ | Stefan–Boltzmann total emissive power | W/m²; $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴ |

**Real-world utility:** The Bouguer–Lambert relation (Formula 1) is the **starting equation for every solar-radiation model** (Hottel–Whillier–Bliss, Liu–Jordan, ASHRAE Clear-Sky). The $m = 1.5$ reference (Formula 3) is **the universal benchmark** for rating PV modules and for compiling standard solar spectra (ASTM G173).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Air-Mass Geometry for a Spherical Earth

We want to rigorously derive the air-mass formula for a *spherical* Earth, then show the plane-parallel approximation as a limiting case. This is a frequent **14-mark KTU question**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Context:** The simple formula $m = 1 / \cos \theta_z$ assumes the atmosphere is a *flat slab*. For sun elevations less than about 20°, the Earth's curvature must be considered; the spherical-atmosphere correction becomes important.

Consider a sun ray entering the atmosphere at zenith angle $\theta_z$. Let $R_e$ be Earth's radius and $H$ the effective atmospheric scale height (≈ 8.5 km for an isothermal atmosphere, well below Earth's radius $R_e \approx 6371$ km). The geometric path length $L$ through the atmosphere is obtained by applying the law of cosines to the triangle formed by the Earth's centre, the observer, and the top-of-atmosphere entry point.

### Step 1 — Set up the geometry

$$(R_e + H)^{2} = R_e^{2} + L^{2} - 2 R_e L \cos(180° - \theta_z)$$

The angle at the observer, between the local vertical and the incoming ray, is $180° - \theta_z$ (the supplement of the zenith angle, because the geometry is "outside" the observer's reference).

### Step 2 — Substitute and simplify

Because $\cos(180° - \theta_z) = -\cos \theta_z$:

$$(R_e + H)^{2} = R_e^{2} + L^{2} + 2 R_e L \cos \theta_z$$

$$L^{2} + 2 R_e L \cos \theta_z + R_e^{2} - (R_e + H)^{2} = 0$$

$$L^{2} + 2 R_e \cos \theta_z \cdot L - (2 R_e H + H^{2}) = 0$$

### Step 3 — Solve the quadratic for L

$$L = -R_e \cos \theta_z + \sqrt{R_e^{2} \cos^{2}\theta_z + 2 R_e H + H^{2}}$$

(We discard the negative root since $L > 0$.)

### Step 4 — Define air mass

The **air mass** is the ratio of the actual slanted path to the vertical (zenith) path $L_0$:

$$L_0 = \sqrt{2 R_e H + H^{2}} \quad (\theta_z = 0°)$$

$$\boxed{\,m = \frac{L}{L_0} = \frac{\sqrt{R_e^{2} \cos^{2}\theta_z + 2 R_e H + H^{2}} - R_e \cos \theta_z}{\sqrt{2 R_e H + H^{2}}}\,}$$

### Step 5 — Plane-parallel limit (verify textbook formula)

When $H \ll R_e$ (typical atmospheric thickness vs. Earth radius — 8.5 km vs. 6371 km), we can expand:

$$(R_e + H)^{2} \approx R_e^{2} + 2 R_e H \Rightarrow L \approx \frac{H}{\cos \theta_z}$$

Dividing by $L_0 \approx H$:

$$m \approx \frac{1}{\cos \theta_z} \quad \checkmark$$

This confirms that $m = 1/\cos \theta_z$ is the **plane-parallel** approximation, valid for $\theta_z \lesssim 75°$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU examiner's note:** Always quote $m = 1/\cos \theta_z$ as the **plane-parallel approximation** and explicitly state its range of validity. This shows conceptual depth and earns full marks.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Integrated Terrestrial Irradiance from Planck's Law

**Problem:** Starting from Planck's spectral distribution at the Sun's surface, derive the total solar irradiance at 1 AU and verify the solar constant.

### Step 1 — Planck spectral emissive power at Sun's surface

$$E_{\lambda,b}(T_s) = \frac{2 \pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5} \left[\exp\!\left(\tfrac{h c}{\lambda k_B T_s}\right) - 1\right]}$$

### Step 2 — Total emitted flux from the Sun's surface (Stefan–Boltzmann)

$$E_b(T_s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} E_{\lambda,b}\, d\lambda = \sigma T_s^{4}$$

Numerically: $E_b = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (5778)^{4} \approx 6.33 \times 10^{7}$ W/m².

### Step 3 — Flux dilution at Earth (inverse-square law)

The Sun's total radiated power is conserved through spherical shells. The flux at distance $R_{se} = 1.496 \times 10^{11}$ m is:

$$G_{sc} = E_b(T_s) \cdot \left( \frac{R_s}{R_{se}} \right)^{2}$$

With $R_s = 6.96 \times 10^{8}$ m:

$$\left(\frac{R_s}{R_{se}}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{6.96 \times 10^{8}}{1.496 \times 10^{11}}\right)^{2} = (4.652 \times 10^{-3})^{2} = 2.165 \times 10^{-5}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the solar constant

$$G_{sc} = 6.33 \times 10^{7} \times 2.165 \times 10^{-5} = 1370.5 \;\text{W/m}^{2}$$

This rounds to the standard accepted value $G_{sc} \approx \mathbf{1367 \text{ W/m}^{2}}$ ✓

## 3.3 Derivation 3: From AM0 to AM1.5 — Wide-Band Integrated Transmittance

**Problem:** The standard AM1.5 direct + circumsolar spectrum integrates to $G_{\text{direct, AM1.5}} \approx 900$ W/m², and the global tilted spectrum (direct + diffuse) at $37°$ tilt, facing sun, integrates to $G_{\text{global, AM1.5}} \approx 1000$ W/m². Derive the **effective broadband transmittance** of the atmosphere at AM1.5 for the global spectrum.

### Step 1 — Identify the relation

By definition of integrated transmittance:

$$G_{\text{terr}} = G_{sc} \cdot \bar{\tau}^{m}$$

Solving for the effective broadband transmittance $\bar{\tau}$:

$$\bar{\tau} = \left( \frac{G_{\text{terr}}}{G_{sc}} \right)^{1/m}$$

### Step 2 — Insert the values

With $G_{sc} = 1367$ W/m², $G_{\text{terr}} = 1000$ W/m², $m = 1.5$:

$$\bar{\tau} = \left( \frac{1000}{1367} \right)^{1/1.5} = (0.7315)^{0.6667}$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate

$$(0.7315)^{0.6667} = e^{0.6667 \cdot \ln(0.7315)} = e^{0.6667 \times (-0.3128)} = e^{-0.2085}$$

$$\bar{\tau} = e^{-0.2085} \approx 0.812$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering interpretation:** The atmosphere, integrated over all solar wavelengths and at AM1.5 path length, transmits roughly **81%** of the extraterrestrial radiation. The remaining 19% is lost to absorption, scattering, and reflection. Of the 1000 W/m² reaching the ground, roughly **900 W/m² is direct beam** and **100 W/m² is diffuse** for a clear sky.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

> [!TIP]
> **KTU students frequently confuse which formula applies. Use this matrix to decide instantly.**

| Given Quantity | Goal | Use This Formula | Common Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solar altitude $\alpha$ | Air mass $m$ | $m = 1/\sin\alpha$ | Confusing altitude and zenith |
| Wavelength-dependent transmittance | Spectral irradiance | $G_{\lambda,\text{terr}} = G_{\lambda,\text{ext}} \cdot \tau_{\lambda}^{m}$ | Forgetting to raise to the $m$ power |
| Total transmittance $\bar{\tau}$ and $G_{sc}$ | Global terrestrial | $G_{\text{terr}} = G_{sc} \cdot \bar{\tau}^{m}$ | Using $m = 0$ instead of $m = 1.5$ |
| Sun radius and temperature | Solar constant | $G_{sc} = \sigma T_s^{4} (R_s/R_{se})^{2}$ | Forgetting the dilution factor |
| Peak emission $\lambda_{\max}$ | Sun temperature | $T = 2898/\lambda_{\max}$ (μm·K) | Mixing nm and μm |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Convention (2024 Scheme):** Part A is compulsory, answers should be 4–6 sentences. Each question tests one Course Outcome (CO) at Bloom's *Remember/Understand* level.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]

**Differentiate clearly between the Extraterrestrial Spectrum (AM0) and the Terrestrial Spectrum (AM1.5). Mention the standard integrated-irradiance values for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Extraterrestrial Spectrum (AM0)** is the spectral distribution of solar radiation incident on the top of the Earth's atmosphere, before any atmospheric interaction. It corresponds to a solar constant of **$G_{sc} = 1367$ W/m²** integrated over all wavelengths, and represents the Sun as a 5778 K black body diluted by the $1/r^{2}$ geometric factor.

The **Terrestrial Spectrum (AM1.5)** is the radiation that actually reaches the Earth's surface after passing through 1.5 atmospheres of air mass (corresponding to a sun elevation of about 41.8°). Its broadband integrated value is standardised at **≈ 1000 W/m²**, with roughly 900 W/m² in the direct beam and 100 W/m² as diffuse radiation. Key differences are: (i) reduced overall intensity (≈ 73% of AM0), (ii) strong absorption dips in the UV (ozone) and IR (water vapour, CO₂), and (iii) the presence of a substantial diffuse component due to scattering. **[3 marks]**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]

**Define Air Mass. Show that for a plane-parallel atmosphere, AM = 1.5 corresponds to a solar zenith angle of 48.19°.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Air Mass (AM)** is defined as the ratio of the actual path length traversed by direct solar radiation through the atmosphere to the vertical (zenith) path length. For a plane-parallel atmosphere:

$$m = \frac{1}{\cos \theta_z}$$

Setting $m = 1.5$:

$$\cos \theta_z = \frac{1}{1.5} = 0.6667 \Rightarrow \theta_z = \cos^{-1}(0.6667) = 48.19°$$

This is the standard reference geometry adopted by ASTM G173 and used universally for rating photovoltaic modules. **[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Convention (2024 Scheme):** Each Part-B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Two **alternative questions** are provided so the student can answer their choice.

---

### QUESTION A — **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

#### (a) Derive the relationship between extraterrestrial spectral irradiance and terrestrial spectral irradiance. Explain each term in the Bouguer–Lambert law with physical significance. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the law (1 mark):**

When monochromatic radiation of spectral irradiance $G_{\lambda,\text{ext}}$ enters an absorbing and scattering medium, the attenuated value after traversing optical path $m$ is:

$$G_{\lambda,\text{terr}} = G_{\lambda,\text{ext}} \cdot \tau_{\lambda}^{m}$$

**Step 2 — Define each term (3 marks):**

- $G_{\lambda,\text{ext}}$ — extraterrestrial spectral irradiance at wavelength $\lambda$ (W·m⁻²·nm⁻¹). Represents the solar flux at the top of the atmosphere.
- $G_{\lambda,\text{terr}}$ — terrestrial spectral irradiance at the Earth's surface.
- $\tau_{\lambda}$ — spectral transmittance of the atmosphere at $\lambda$, with $0 < \tau_{\lambda} \le 1$. Value 1 ⇒ no attenuation; 0 ⇒ complete attenuation.
- $m$ — air mass, the ratio of slanted atmospheric path to the vertical path. For sun at zenith, $m = 1$; for AM1.5 reference, $m = 1.5$.

**Step 3 — Physical interpretation (2 marks):**

The exponent $m$ reflects the **Beer–Lambert exponential decay**: doubling the path length (e.g., from AM1 to AM2) does not halve the transmittance but squares it. This is because attenuation occurs continuously along the path, not in discrete steps.

**Step 4 — Engineering implication (1 mark):**

The Bouguer–Lambert law is the foundation of all clear-sky solar models (ASHRAE, Hottel). For example, at low sun elevations (morning, evening, winter), high $m$ values cause strong attenuation, especially at short wavelengths (Rayleigh $\lambda^{-4}$ dependence), which is why sunsets appear red.

#### (b) The peak spectral irradiance of the AM0 solar spectrum occurs at 0.48 μm. Using Wien's displacement law, estimate the Sun's effective surface temperature, and verify using the Stefan–Boltzmann law that the solar constant at 1 AU equals 1367 W/m² given $R_s = 6.96 \times 10^{8}$ m, $R_{se} = 1.496 \times 10^{11}$ m, and $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Wien's displacement law (2 marks):**

$$\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = 2898 \;\mu\text{m·K}$$

$$T = \frac{2898}{0.48} = 6037.5 \;\text{K}$$

(Note: the value 0.48 μm gives a slightly higher estimate than 5778 K; the latter comes from the more carefully weighted peak of 0.502 μm. The discrepancy is within 5%, consistent with the Sun not being a perfect black body.)

**Step 2 — Total emitted flux from the Sun (1 mark):**

$$E_b = \sigma T^{4} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (5778)^{4}$$

$$(5778)^{4} = 1.115 \times 10^{15}$$

$$E_b = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.115 \times 10^{15} = 6.32 \times 10^{7} \;\text{W/m}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Geometric dilution (2 marks):**

$$\left(\frac{R_s}{R_{se}}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{6.96 \times 10^{8}}{1.496 \times 10^{11}}\right)^{2} = 2.165 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 4 — Solar constant (2 marks):**

$$G_{sc} = E_b \times 2.165 \times 10^{-5} = 6.32 \times 10^{7} \times 2.165 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$G_{sc} \approx 1368 \;\text{W/m}^{2} \approx 1367 \;\text{W/m}^{2} \; \checkmark$$

This confirms the standard accepted value of the solar constant. **[7 marks]**

---

### QUESTION B — **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

#### (a) Explain Rayleigh and Mie scattering. State their wavelength dependencies and indicate which regions of the terrestrial spectrum they most strongly affect. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Rayleigh scattering (3 marks):**

Rayleigh scattering occurs when solar radiation interacts with air molecules and particles much smaller than the wavelength of light (diameter $d \ll \lambda$). The scattering cross-section has the well-known **$\lambda^{-4}$ dependence**:

$$\sigma_{\text{Ray}} \propto \frac{d^{6}}{\lambda^{4}}$$

Because the dependence is so steep on wavelength, **short wavelengths (blue, violet) are scattered roughly 10× more than red light**. This is why the sky appears blue (we see scattered light from directions other than the sun) and why sunsets appear red (the blue component has been removed by long atmospheric paths at low sun angles). Rayleigh scattering affects the **UV and visible** portions of the terrestrial spectrum strongly and becomes negligible beyond about 1 μm.

**Step 2 — Mie scattering (3 marks):**

Mie scattering is caused by particles of size comparable to the wavelength of light (aerosols, dust, water droplets with $d \approx \lambda$). Its wavelength dependence is **much weaker**, approximately $\lambda^{-1.3}$ to $\lambda^{0}$:

$$\sigma_{\text{Mie}} \propto \lambda^{-p}, \quad 0 \le p \le 1.3$$

This produces the **white glare** around the sun on hazy days, and the white appearance of clouds (water droplets in clouds have $d \approx 10\;\mu$m, much larger than visible wavelengths, so scattering is wavelength-independent and produces white light). Mie scattering affects the **whole solar window** but is most noticeable in the visible and near-IR.

**Step 3 — Distinguishing features in a table (1 mark):**

| Feature | Rayleigh | Mie |
|---|---|---|
| Particle size | $d \ll \lambda$ | $d \approx \lambda$ |
| Wavelength dependence | $\lambda^{-4}$ (strong) | $\lambda^{-0}$ to $\lambda^{-1.3}$ (weak) |
| Colour of scattered light | Blue (sky) | White (haze, clouds) |
| Dominant region | UV, Visible | Whole solar band |

#### (b) A horizontal surface receives a global terrestrial irradiance of 800 W/m² at AM1.5, of which 25% is diffuse. A solar panel is tilted at 30° from horizontal and faces due south. The ground albedo is 0.2. Estimate the total irradiance on the tilted panel using the isotropic-sky model. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Decompose global into beam and diffuse (1 mark):**

$$G_b = (1 - 0.25) \times 800 = 600 \;\text{W/m}^{2}\;\text{(horizontal)}$$

$$G_d = 0.25 \times 800 = 200 \;\text{W/m}^{2}\;\text{(horizontal)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the beam component on the tilted plane (2 marks):**

For AM1.5, $\theta_z = 48.19°$. The tilt is $\beta = 30°$ and we assume the panel is aligned so the incidence angle on the tilted surface equals the zenith angle minus the tilt (south-facing, sun in the south at solar noon on the equinox):

$$\cos \theta = \cos \theta_z \cos \beta + \sin \theta_z \sin \beta \cos(\gamma_s - \gamma)$$

For a south-facing panel and due-south sun at noon, $\gamma_s = \gamma$ and the azimuthal term is zero. At the equinox at solar noon, $\delta = 0$ and at low latitude $\theta_z = 90° - \phi + \delta = 90° - \phi$. To keep the calculation generic, we use the **standard AM1.5 incidence angle** of $\theta \approx 48.19° - 30° = 18.19°$ (assumes panel tilt reduces the incidence angle):

$$G_{b,\text{tilt}} = G_b \cdot \frac{\cos \theta}{\cos \theta_z} = 600 \times \frac{\cos 18.19°}{\cos 48.19°} = 600 \times \frac{0.9499}{0.6667} \approx 855 \;\text{W/m}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute diffuse using the isotropic-sky model (2 marks):**

$$G_{d,\text{tilt}} = G_d \cdot \frac{1 + \cos \beta}{2} = 200 \times \frac{1 + \cos 30°}{2} = 200 \times \frac{1 + 0.866}{2} = 200 \times 0.933 = 186.6 \;\text{W/m}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Ground-reflected component (1 mark):**

$$G_{r,\text{tilt}} = G \cdot \rho \cdot \frac{1 - \cos \beta}{2} = 800 \times 0.2 \times \frac{1 - 0.866}{2} = 800 \times 0.2 \times 0.067 = 10.7 \;\text{W/m}^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Total on tilted plane (1 mark):**

$$G_{\text{tilt}} = G_{b,\text{tilt}} + G_{d,\text{tilt}} + G_{r,\text{tilt}} = 855 + 186.6 + 10.7 \approx 1052 \;\text{W/m}^{2}$$

This is greater than the horizontal 800 W/m², illustrating why tilted surfaces collect more energy at high latitude locations. **[7 marks]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the exponent in Bouguer–Lambert.** Many students write $G_{\lambda,\text{terr}} = G_{\lambda,\text{ext}} \cdot \tau_{\lambda}$ instead of $\tau_{\lambda}^{m}$. This single mistake can cost **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing zenith and altitude angles.** Always check: at sunrise, $\alpha = 0°$, $\theta_z = 90°$, $m \to \infty$. The formula $m = 1/\cos \theta_z$ requires $\theta_z < 90°$.
> 3. **Using 1361 vs 1367 W/m² for $G_{sc}$** — both are accepted, but be consistent. The older KTU textbooks use **1367 W/m²**.
> 4. **In the tilted-plane problem, mixing horizontal diffuse with tilted beam**. Always convert diffuse to the tilted geometry using the $\frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}$ view factor.
> 5. **Forgetting the ground-reflected term** in the isotropic-sky model. Even though it is small, omitting it loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print this page before the exam.**

- **Terrestrial Spectrum** = solar spectrum *after* atmospheric attenuation, **AM1.5** standard, ≈ **1000 W/m²** total.
- **Extraterrestrial Spectrum** = AM0, **1367 W/m²**, peaks near **0.48–0.50 μm**.
- **Air mass** $m = 1/\cos \theta_z$; for AM1.5, $\theta_z = 48.19°$.
- **Spherical Earth correction** uses the exact geometric formula (Kasten–Young or the law-of-cosines derivation); plane-parallel formula is valid only for $\theta_z < 75°$.
- **Bouguer–Lambert Law**: $G_{\lambda,\text{terr}} = G_{\lambda,\text{ext}} \cdot \tau_{\lambda}^{m}$ — note the **exponent m**.
- **Rayleigh scattering** $\propto \lambda^{-4}$ — gives blue sky, red sunset; affects UV and visible.
- **Mie scattering** $\propto \lambda^{0}$ to $\lambda^{-1.3}$ — gives white haze; affects whole solar band.
- **Ozone absorbs** UV below 0.38 μm; **H₂O and CO₂ absorb** IR above 0.7 μm; **solar window** is **0.3–2.5 μm**.
- **Beam + Diffuse = Global**: $G = G_b + G_d$; clear-sky diffuse fraction $k_d \approx 0.1$–$0.2$.
- **Tilted-plane decomposition**: $G_{\text{tilt}} = G_{b,\text{tilt}} + G_{d,\text{tilt}} + G_{r,\text{tilt}}$.
- **Wien's law**: $\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = 2898 \;\mu\text{m·K}$ — Sun: $T \approx 5778$ K, $\lambda_{\max} \approx 0.5 \;\mu\text{m}$.
- **Stefan–Boltzmann**: $E_b = \sigma T^{4}$; **Solar constant**: $G_{sc} = \sigma T^{4} (R_s/R_{se})^{2}$.
- **Atmospheric broadband transmittance** at AM1.5 is approximately **$\bar{\tau} \approx 0.81$**.
- **ASTM G173** is the international standard reference terrestrial spectrum (use it in design calculations).
- **For KTU numericals:** Always state the plane-parallel assumption explicitly when using $m = 1/\cos\theta_z$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prediction of Solar Radiation. Sign Conventions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET617 - SOLAR ENERGY CONSERVATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Prediction of Solar Radiation. Sign Conventions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Prediction of Solar Radiation — Formal Definition

In the context of **Solar Energy Conversion Systems**, *Prediction of Solar Radiation* refers to the engineering estimation of the **amount, intensity, direction, and temporal distribution** of electromagnetic energy emitted by the Sun that becomes available at a specific geographical location, on a specific day, and at a specific hour of the year. This prediction is foundational to:

- **Sizing** of photovoltaic (PV) modules and solar thermal collectors.
- **Performance estimation** of flat-plate, concentrating, and evacuated-tube collectors.
- **Energy-yield forecasting** for grid-tied and off-grid solar plants.
- **Storage and hybridization** design (battery, pump, hydrogen).

The prediction is carried out in **three hierarchical tiers**:

| Tier | Quantity Predicted | Symbol |
|------|--------------------|--------|
| **Tier 1** | Extraterrestrial Radiation (outside atmosphere) | $G_{on}$, $H_o$ |
| **Tier 2** | Terrestrial (Global) Radiation on Horizontal Surface | $G$, $H$ |
| **Tier 3** | Radiation Incident on a Tilted / Tracking Surface | $G_T$, $H_T$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must be proficient in **Tier-1** prediction using solar geometry (declination, hour angle, zenith, azimuth, sunset hour angle) and the **Angstrom–Prescott empirical correlation** for Tier-2 prediction. Sign conventions for these angles are mandatory for board examination answers.

### 1.2 Sign Conventions — Why They Matter

A **Sign Convention** is a universally accepted rule that fixes the **algebraic sign (positive/negative)** and **reference direction** of physical quantities so that engineers worldwide obtain consistent, comparable, and unambiguous results.

For solar geometry, the **KTU-syllabus-mandated sign conventions** are:

- **Solar Azimuth Angle ($\gamma_s$):** Measured on the horizontal plane from the **true South** in the Northern Hemisphere (and from the true North in the Southern Hemisphere). It is **negative in the morning** (East) and **positive in the afternoon** (West).

- **Hour Angle ($\omega$):** Measured at the **celestial pole** from the local meridian. It is **negative before solar noon** and **positive after solar noon**. It varies at a rate of **15° per hour** (or 360° in 24 h).

- **Zenith Angle ($\theta_z$):** Measured from the **vertical (zenith direction)** downward to the Sun. Always positive, ranging from **0°** (Sun overhead) to **90°** (Sun at horizon).

- **Altitude / Elevation Angle ($\alpha$):** Measured from the **horizontal** upward to the Sun. Always positive during daytime. Note: $\alpha + \theta_z = 90°$.

- **Surface Tilt Angle ($\beta$):** Measured from the **horizontal** upward to the surface. **0°** = horizontal; **90°** = vertical.

- **Surface Azimuth Angle ($\gamma$):** Measured from the **true South** on the horizontal projection of the surface normal. Positive toward West.

- **Solar Declination ($\delta$):** Measured from the **equatorial plane** to the Earth–Sun line. Positive in Northern Hemisphere summer (June solstice ≈ +23.45°); negative in Northern Hemisphere winter (December solstice ≈ −23.45°).

### 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The Student-Friendly Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The School Assembly Compass":**
> Imagine the Earth as a giant *school assembly ground* and the Sun as the *Principal standing on the stage*. The ground (Earth) is rotating. Every student (solar collector) standing on the ground must constantly figure out three things:
>
> 1. **Where is the Principal right now?** → *Solar Zenith Angle* $\theta_z$ (how far up/down from directly overhead).
> 2. **What time is it on the Principal's wristwatch?** → *Hour Angle* $\omega$ (negative before noon, positive after).
> 3. **In which season did the Principal climb the podium at a tilt?** → *Declination Angle* $\delta$ (tilt of Earth's axis, ±23.45°).
>
> Once these three are known, students can predict *how bright and how warm the spotlight on them will be* — that brightness is the **predicted solar radiation**.

### 1.4 Standard Constants & Metrics

The following physical constants are **must-know** for every KTU board answer:

- **Solar Constant (Mean):** $G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2 = 1.361 \text{ kW/m}^2$ (WMO 1981 standard).
- **Variation band:** 1361–1368 W/m² depending on Sun–Earth distance.
- **Earth axial tilt (Obliquity):** $\varepsilon = 23.45°$.
- **Angular speed of Earth's rotation:** 15°/hour = 7.27 × 10⁻⁵ rad/s.
- **Mean Earth–Sun distance:** $1.496 \times 10^8$ km = 1 AU.
- **Eccentricity of Earth's orbit:** $e \approx 0.0167$.
- **1 Langleys/day = 1 cal/cm²/day = 0.041868 MJ/m²/day** (older unit).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic Chain of Solar Radiation Prediction

The prediction of solar radiation proceeds in a **strict, sequential logic chain** as laid out in KTU Module 1. Mastering this chain is non-negotiable for full marks.

1. **Determine the day of the year** $n$ (1 = Jan 1, 365 = Dec 31).
2. **Compute the Earth–Sun distance correction factor** $E_o$ using the day angle $B$:
   $$E_o = \left(\frac{r_o}{r}\right)^2 = 1 + 0.033 \cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{365}\right)$$
3. **Compute the solar declination angle** $\delta$ (apparent tilt of the Earth):
   $$\delta = 23.45° \times \sin\left[\frac{360°}{365}(284 + n)\right]$$
4. **Compute the hour angle** $\omega$ for the time of observation $t_{solar}$ (solar time in hours):
   $$\omega = 15° \times (t_{solar} - 12)$$
5. **Compute the zenith angle** $\theta_z$ for latitude $\phi$:
   $$\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$$
6. **Compute the sunrise/sunset hour angle** $\omega_s$ (when $\theta_z = 90°$):
   $$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta)$$
7. **Compute the extraterrestrial radiation** $G_{on}$ (instantaneous, on a surface normal to the Sun's rays):
   $$G_{on} = G_{sc} \cdot E_o$$
8. **Compute the extraterrestrial horizontal radiation** $G_{o}$:
   $$G_o = G_{on} \cos\theta_z$$
9. **Integrate over the day** to obtain the **daily extraterrestrial radiation** $H_o$:
   $$H_o = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi} \, G_{sc}\, E_o\, \left[\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \frac{2\pi\,\omega_s}{360°}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right]$$
10. **Apply an empirical correlation** (Angstrom–Prescott) to obtain the **terrestrial radiation** $H$ on a horizontal surface:
    $$\frac{H}{H_o} = a + b \cdot \frac{S}{S_o}$$
    where $S/S_o$ is the relative sunshine duration fraction.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Step 1–2:** The Earth's orbit is elliptical, so the solar flux at the top of the atmosphere varies by **±3.3%** over a year. This correction is **NOT optional** in KTU numericals.
- **Step 3:** The Earth's axis is tilted at 23.45°; this is why we have seasons. Without $\delta$, summer and winter would be indistinguishable.
- **Step 4:** Hour angle converts *clock-time* to *Sun-time*. A solar clock "rotates" 15° every hour.
- **Step 5–6:** The **fundamental spherical trigonometry** identity — all solar geometry equations stem from the law of cosines on the celestial sphere.
- **Step 9:** The daily integral averages out hour-to-hour variation. The factor $24 \times 3600/\pi$ converts the angular integral to **MJ/m²/day**.
- **Step 10:** The **Angstrom–Prescott correlation** (1924) is the simplest empirical bridge between *clear-sky theory* and *cloudy reality*. Constants $a$ and $b$ are location-specific and tabulated for Indian cities.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving KTU board questions on solar radiation prediction.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Range |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Solar constant | $G_{sc} = 1367$ | W/m² |
| 2 | Eccentricity correction | $E_o = 1 + 0.033\cos(2\pi n/365)$ | dimensionless, $0.967 \le E_o \le 1.033$ |
| 3 | Declination (Spencer, 1971) | $\delta = 23.45°\sin[360°(284+n)/365]$ | degrees, $-23.45° \le \delta \le +23.45°$ |
| 4 | Hour angle | $\omega = 15°(t_{solar} - 12)$ | degrees, $\pm 180°$ |
| 5 | Zenith angle | $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$ | degrees, $0° \le \theta_z \le 90°$ |
| 6 | Altitude angle | $\alpha = 90° - \theta_z$ | degrees |
| 7 | Sunrise/sunset hour angle | $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)$ | degrees |
| 8 | Day length | $N = (2/15°)\omega_s$ | hours |
| 9 | Extraterrestrial normal radiation | $G_{on} = G_{sc} E_o$ | W/m² |
| 10 | Extraterrestrial horizontal (instantaneous) | $G_o = G_{on}\cos\theta_z$ | W/m² |
| 11 | Daily extraterrestrial radiation | $H_o = \frac{86400}{\pi}G_{sc}E_o\left[\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \frac{\pi\omega_s}{180°}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right]$ | J/m²/day |
| 12 | Monthly mean extraterrestrial radiation | $H_{o,mean}$ using **average day** $n_{avg}$ per month | MJ/m²/day |
| 13 | Angstrom–Prescott equation | $\overline{H}/\overline{H_o} = a + b(\overline{n}/\overline{N})$ | dimensionless |
| 14 | Hottel's clear-day model | $G_{clear} = 0.9 \, G_{sc} \, \tau_a \, \tau_r$ | W/m² |

> **Engineering Utility:** Engineers use these predictions to:
> - Determine the **optimum tilt angle** of a fixed-tilt PV array.
> - Forecast the **annual energy yield** of a solar farm (often in kWh/kWp/year).
> - Specify the **capacity of a battery bank** in off-grid systems.
> - Plan **thermal-storage tanks** for solar water heaters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Solar Declination Angle

The Earth's axis is tilted at an angle $\varepsilon = 23.45°$ to the plane of the ecliptic. The **solar declination** $\delta$ is the angle between the **Earth–Sun line** and the **equatorial plane**.

**Step 1 — Day angle $B$:** Define the angular position of the Earth in its orbit, measured from January 1 (perihelion passage is on Jan 3):
$$B = \frac{360°(n - 1)}{365}$$

**Step 2 — Geometric projection:** The declination equals the obliquity times the sine of the day angle shifted by 80 days (this 80-day offset places the equinoxes on March 21 and Sept 23):
$$\delta = \varepsilon \, \sin\left[\frac{360°}{365}(n - 80)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Spencer's compact form:** Rewrite by shifting the argument by +284 (= 365 − 80 − 1, used to keep $n = 1$ as reference):
$$\delta = 23.45° \times \sin\left[\frac{360°}{365}(n + 284)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Validation:** 
- $n = 172$ (June 21, summer solstice in NH): $\delta = 23.45°\sin(360°×456/365) = 23.45°\sin(449.4°)$ → after reduction, $\delta \approx +23.45°$. ✅
- $n = 355$ (Dec 21): $\delta = 23.45°\sin(360°×639/365) = 23.45°\sin(630.2°) \approx -23.45°$. ✅

### 3.2 Derivation: Hour Angle from Local Solar Time

The Earth rotates **360° in 24 hours**, i.e., **15° per hour**.

**Step 1 — Definition:** The hour angle is zero at **solar noon** (Sun on local meridian), negative before noon, positive after noon.

**Step 2 — Linear relation:** Letting $t_{solar}$ be the solar time in hours (0 to 24):
$$\omega = 15° \times (t_{solar} - 12)$$

**Step 3 — Examples:**
- $t_{solar} = 9$ h (morning): $\omega = -45°$. ✅
- $t_{solar} = 12$ h (noon): $\omega = 0°$. ✅
- $t_{solar} = 15$ h (afternoon): $\omega = +45°$. ✅

### 3.3 Derivation: Zenith Angle (Spherical Cosine Law)

Consider a spherical triangle on the celestial sphere with vertices:
- **Zenith** (Z), the observer's overhead point.
- **Pole** (P), the celestial pole.
- **Sun** (S), the position of the Sun.

The three sides of this triangle are:
- Co-latitude: $90° - \phi$ (between Z and P).
- Co-declination: $90° - \delta$ (between P and S).
- Zenith distance: $\theta_z$ (between Z and S).

The **interior angle** at P is the hour angle $\omega$.

**Step 1 — Apply the law of cosines** to this spherical triangle:
$$\cos\theta_z = \cos(90° - \phi)\cos(90° - \delta) + \sin(90° - \phi)\sin(90° - \delta)\cos\omega$$

**Step 2 — Simplify using** $\cos(90° - x) = \sin x$ and $\sin(90° - x) = \cos x$:
$$\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$$

**Step 3 — Boundary check:**
- At sunrise/sunset, $\theta_z = 90°$ → $\cos\theta_z = 0$ → $\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega_s = 0$.
- Solve for $\omega_s$:
$$\cos\omega_s = -\tan\phi \tan\delta$$
$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)$$

### 3.4 Derivation: Daily Extraterrestrial Radiation on a Horizontal Surface

The instantaneous extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface is:
$$G_o = G_{on}\cos\theta_z = G_{sc} E_o \,[\sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega]$$

**Step 1 — Integrate over the day** from sunrise ($\omega = -\omega_s$) to sunset ($\omega = +\omega_s$). Each hour of hour angle = 3600 s of real time, and $d\omega = (360°/24)\,\text{h}^{-1} \cdot dt = 15°\,\text{h}^{-1} \, dt$, so $dt = (24/360°)\,d\omega$ (in hours) and $\text{seconds} = 3600 \times (24/360°)\,d\omega$:
$$H_o = \int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} G_{sc} E_o [\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega] \, \frac{86400}{2\pi}\, d\omega$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the integral:**
$$H_o = \frac{86400}{2\pi} G_{sc} E_o \left[\sin\phi\sin\delta \cdot 2\omega_s + \cos\phi\cos\delta \cdot 2\sin\omega_s\right]$$

$$\boxed{H_o = \frac{86400}{\pi} G_{sc} E_o \left[\cos\phi\cos\delta \sin\omega_s + \frac{\pi\,\omega_s}{180°}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right]}$$

**Step 3 — Unit conversion:** With $G_{sc}$ in W/m² and $\omega_s$ in degrees, the result is in **J/m²/day**. To convert to **MJ/m²/day**, divide by $10^6$.

### 3.5 Derivation: Angstrom–Prescott Correlation

This is an **empirical** (not derived from first principles) correlation developed by Anders Ångström (1924) and refined by Prescott (1940).

**Step 1 — Hypothesis:** The fraction of extraterrestrial radiation reaching the ground is a **linear function** of the relative sunshine fraction $S/S_o$:
$$\frac{H}{H_o} = a + b \cdot \frac{S}{S_o}$$

**Step 2 — Constants:** 
- $a$ = fraction of $H_o$ on a **completely overcast** day (cloudy sky still has diffuse radiation).
- $a + b$ = fraction of $H_o$ on a **clear day** (when $S = S_o$).
- Typical Indian values: $a \approx 0.25$–$0.36$, $b \approx 0.45$–$0.62$.

**Step 3 — Limitations:** It is monthly-average, location-specific, and not applicable for **hourly** predictions or complex terrain (where anisotropic models like Hay, Davies, Klucher, or Reindl are preferred).

### 3.6 Worked Example (Board-Style Numerical)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam Pattern]:** A solar collector is located at latitude $\phi = 13°$ N (Trivandrum). Calculate for $n = 80$ (March equinox), at solar noon ($t = 12$ h), the zenith angle, extraterrestrial normal and horizontal radiation.

**Solution:**

**Step A — Declination:**
$$\delta = 23.45° \sin\left[\frac{360°}{365}(80 + 284)\right] = 23.45°\sin(359.0°)$$
$$\delta = 23.45° \times (-0.01745) = -0.41° \approx 0°$$

**Step B — Hour angle at noon:**
$$\omega = 15°(12 - 12) = 0°$$

**Step C — Zenith angle:**
$$\cos\theta_z = \sin 13° \sin(-0.41°) + \cos 13° \cos(-0.41°) \cos 0°$$
$$\cos\theta_z = (0.225)(-0.00715) + (0.974)(0.9999)(1)$$
$$\cos\theta_z = -0.00161 + 0.9739 = 0.9723$$
$$\theta_z = 13.42°$$

**Step D — Eccentricity correction:**
$$E_o = 1 + 0.033\cos(2\pi \times 80/365) = 1 + 0.033\cos(1.376) = 1 + 0.033(0.192) = 1.0063$$

**Step E — Extraterrestrial normal:**
$$G_{on} = 1367 \times 1.0063 = 1375.6 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step F — Extraterrestrial horizontal:**
$$G_o = G_{on}\cos\theta_z = 1375.6 \times 0.9723 = 1337.5 \text{ W/m}^2$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Solar Geometry — Angular Relationships (Mermaid Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["OBSERVER ON EARTH SURFACE"] --> B["LATITUDE phi NORTH OR SOUTH"]
    A --> C["ZENITH Z OVERHEAD"]
    C --> D["ZENITH ANGLE theta z"]
    D --> E["ALTITUDE ANGLE alpha = 90 minus theta z"]
    A --> F["TRUE SOUTH REFERENCE LINE"]
    F --> G["SOLAR AZIMUTH gamma s"]
    G --> H["NEGATIVE BEFORE NOON EAST"]
    G --> I["POSITIVE AFTER NOON WEST"]
    J["SUN POSITION"] --> K["HOUR ANGLE omega"]
    K --> L["NEGATIVE MORNING"]
    K --> M["ZERO AT SOLAR NOON"]
    K --> N["POSITIVE AFTERNOON"]
    J --> O["DECLINATION delta"]
    O --> P["POSITIVE APRIL TO SEPTEMBER NH"]
    O --> Q["NEGATIVE OCTOBER TO MARCH NH"]
    R["EQUATORIAL PLANE"] --> O
    S["EARTH AXIS TILTED 23.45 DEG"] --> O
```

### 4.2 Three-Tier Prediction Methodology (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1["TIER 1 EXTRATERRESTRIAL"] --> T1A["Gsc = 1367 W per sqm"]
    T1 --> T1B["Eccentricity Eo"]
    T1 --> T1C["Declination delta"]
    T1 --> T1D["Hour Angle omega"]
    T1 --> T1E["Zenith theta z"]
    T1 --> T1F["OUTPUT Gon and Ho"]
    T1F --> T2
    T2["TIER 2 TERRESTRIAL HORIZONTAL"] --> T2A["Angstrom Prescott a and b"]
    T2 --> T2B["Sunshine Fraction S over So"]
    T2 --> T2C["Clearness Index KT"]
    T2 --> T2D["OUTPUT H monthly mean global"]
    T2D --> T3
    T3["TIER 3 TILTED OR TRACKING SURFACE"] --> T3A["Tilt Angle beta"]
    T3 --> T3B["Surface Azimuth gamma"]
    T3 --> T3C["Beam Component GbT"]
    T3 --> T3D["Diffuse Component GdT"]
    T3 --> T3E["Reflected Component GrT"]
    T3 --> T3F["OUTPUT GT total on tilted"]
```

### 4.3 Sign Convention Reference Card (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AM["ANGULAR SIGN CONVENTIONS"]
        direction TB
        AM1["OMEGA hour angle NEG before noon POS after noon"]
        AM2["GAMMA solar azimuth NEG east of south POS west of south"]
        AM3["DELTA declination POS NH summer NEG NH winter"]
        AM4["BETA tilt angle POS upward from horizontal 0 to 180"]
        AM5["GAMMA surface azimuth 0 due south POS west NEG east"]
        AM6["ALPHA altitude angle ALWAYS POS during day 0 to 90"]
        AM7["THETA Z zenith angle ALWAYS POS 0 to 90"]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]**
*Define the solar declination angle. Write its governing equation and explain its physical significance.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **solar declination angle ($\delta$)** is defined as the angle between the **Earth–Sun line** and the **equatorial plane**. It varies throughout the year due to the tilt of Earth's rotational axis (23.45° relative to the orbital plane).

$$\delta = 23.45° \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360°}{365}(284 + n)\right]$$

where $n$ is the day of the year. It varies from **−23.45°** (December solstice) to **+23.45°** (June solstice), and is **zero** at the equinoxes.

**Physical significance:** Declination is the **fundamental cause of seasons**. It determines the altitude of the Sun at solar noon, the day length, and the total daily insolation received at any latitude.

*(Distribution: Definition — 1 mark; Equation — 1 mark; Significance — 1 mark.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**
*State the Angstrom–Prescott correlation. What are the typical ranges of the empirical constants $a$ and $b$ for Indian stations?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Angstrom–Prescott correlation** linearly relates the **clearness index** $H/H_o$ to the **sunshine fraction** $S/S_o$:

$$\frac{\overline{H}}{\overline{H_o}} = a + b \cdot \frac{\overline{S}}{\overline{S_o}}$$

where:
- $\overline{H}$ = monthly average daily global radiation on horizontal surface.
- $\overline{H_o}$ = monthly average daily extraterrestrial radiation.
- $\overline{S}/\overline{S_o}$ = monthly average fractional sunshine duration.
- $a$ and $b$ are **empirical regression coefficients** specific to the location.

For most **Indian stations** (ISRO / IMD studies), $a$ ranges from **0.25 to 0.36** and $b$ ranges from **0.45 to 0.62**.

*(Distribution: Equation — 1.5 marks; Constant ranges — 1.5 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — KTU Board Past Paper Pattern, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the various angles used to define the position of the Sun relative to an observer on Earth. Clearly state the sign convention for each.

**(b) [7 marks)** A location is at latitude $\phi = 28.6°$ N (New Delhi). For the day number $n = 172$ (summer solstice), calculate:
   (i) The solar declination angle $\delta$.
   (ii) The extraterrestrial normal radiation $G_{on}$.
   (iii) The zenith angle at solar noon.
   (iv) The day length.

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

A labelled diagram should show:
1. **Latitude $\phi$** — angle between equatorial plane and observer's location.
2. **Zenith angle $\theta_z$** — measured from vertical to the Sun; **always positive**.
3. **Altitude angle $\alpha$** — from horizontal to the Sun; $\alpha + \theta_z = 90°$.
4. **Solar azimuth $\gamma_s$** — measured from **true South** in the horizontal plane; **negative in the morning, positive in the afternoon** (KTU/Northern Hemisphere convention).
5. **Hour angle $\omega$** — measured from local meridian at 15°/hour; **negative before noon, positive after noon**.
6. **Declination $\delta$** — from equatorial plane to Earth–Sun line; **positive NH summer, negative NH winter**.
7. **Surface tilt $\beta$** — from horizontal upward.
8. **Surface azimuth $\gamma$** — from South in the plane projection.

*(Sketch 3 marks; angle definitions 2 marks; sign convention table 2 marks.)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Declination:**
$$\delta = 23.45°\sin\left[\frac{360°}{365}(172 + 284)\right] = 23.45°\sin\left(\frac{360°\times 456}{365}\right)$$
$$\delta = 23.45°\sin(449.75°) = 23.45°\sin(89.75°) = 23.45° \times 0.99999 \approx +23.45°$$

**(ii) Extraterrestrial normal radiation:**
$$E_o = 1 + 0.033\cos(2\pi \times 172/365) = 1 + 0.033\cos(2.96) = 1 + 0.033(-0.983) = 1 - 0.0324 = 0.9676$$
$$G_{on} = 1367 \times 0.9676 = 1322.7 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**(iii) Zenith angle at solar noon** ($\omega = 0°$):
$$\cos\theta_z = \sin(28.6°)\sin(23.45°) + \cos(28.6°)\cos(23.45°)\cos(0°)$$
$$\cos\theta_z = (0.479)(0.398) + (0.878)(0.917)(1) = 0.1907 + 0.8052 = 0.9959$$
$$\theta_z = \cos^{-1}(0.9959) = 5.27°$$

**(iv) Day length:**
$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta) = \cos^{-1}(-\tan 28.6° \tan 23.45°)$$
$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-(0.545)(0.433)) = \cos^{-1}(-0.236) = 103.66°$$
$$N = \frac{2\omega_s}{15°} = \frac{2 \times 103.66°}{15°} = 13.82 \text{ hours}$$

*(i — 1.5 marks, ii — 1.5 marks, iii — 2 marks, iv — 2 marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — KTU Board Past Paper Pattern, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive an expression for the **daily extraterrestrial radiation** $H_o$ on a horizontal surface, integrating the instantaneous extraterrestrial radiation from sunrise to sunset. List the standard assumptions.

**(b) [7 marks]** Using the Angstrom–Prescott correlation with $a = 0.30$ and $b = 0.55$ for a station where $\overline{S}/\overline{S_o} = 0.7$, and monthly mean $\overline{H_o} = 32.0$ MJ/m²/day, determine:
   (i) The monthly average daily global radiation $\overline{H}$ on a horizontal surface.
   (ii) The monthly clearness index $K_T$.
   (iii) Comment on the sky conditions.

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

*Assumptions:*
1. The Earth's atmosphere is non-absorbing at the top.
2. The Earth–Sun line is the only determinant of incident flux.
3. $G_{sc}$ varies only with the inverse-square law (eccentricity correction).
4. The Sun is a point source.
5. Spherical Earth geometry.

*Derivation:*

Instantaneous extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface:
$$G_o = G_{sc}E_o[\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega]$$

Day length in radians: from $\omega = -\omega_s$ to $\omega = +\omega_s$. Total time $= (2\omega_s/360°)\times 24$ hours, so $dt = (24/360°)\,d\omega$ in hours, i.e., $dt = (86400/360°)\,d\omega$ in seconds $= (86400/2\pi)\,d\omega$ (with $\omega$ in radians).

$$H_o = \int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} G_{sc}E_o[\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega]\,\frac{86400}{2\pi}\,d\omega$$

Integrate:
$$H_o = \frac{86400}{2\pi}G_{sc}E_o\left[\sin\phi\sin\delta\cdot 2\omega_s + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cdot 2\sin\omega_s\right]$$

$$\boxed{H_o = \frac{86400}{\pi}G_{sc}E_o\left[\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \frac{\pi\omega_s}{180°}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right]}$$

where $\omega_s$ is in **degrees** (for the dimensional coefficient $\pi/180°$).

*(Assumptions 2 marks; Setup 2 marks; Integration 2 marks; Final form 1 mark.)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Using Angstrom–Prescott:**
$$\overline{H} = \overline{H_o}\,(a + b\,\overline{S}/\overline{S_o}) = 32.0 \times (0.30 + 0.55 \times 0.7)$$
$$\overline{H} = 32.0 \times (0.30 + 0.385) = 32.0 \times 0.685 = 21.92 \text{ MJ/m}^2\text{/day}$$

**(ii) Clearness index:**
$$K_T = \overline{H}/\overline{H_o} = 0.685$$

**(iii) Sky comment:**
A clearness index of **0.685** corresponds to a **moderately clear sky** (Liu & Jordan classification: $K_T = 0.65$ is generally partially cloudy with predominantly clear conditions). The station experiences mostly clear weather with intermittent cloud cover.

*(i — 3 marks, ii — 2 marks, iii — 2 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting $E_o$:** Many students compute $G_{on} = 1367$ W/m² directly. **Always include the eccentricity correction** unless explicitly stated otherwise.
> 2. **Wrong sign for $\omega$:** $\omega$ is **NEGATIVE in the morning**. A morning question with $\omega = +30°$ loses marks.
> 3. **Wrong $\omega_s$ formula sign:** It is $-\tan\phi\tan\delta$ **INSIDE** the cos⁻¹. Do NOT write $\tan\phi\tan\delta$ without the minus.
> 4. **Unit mix-up in $H_o$:** With $G_{sc}$ in W/m² and time in seconds, the result is in **J/m²/day**, not MJ. Divide by $10^6$ to convert.
> 5. **Solar azimuth sign:** In the **Northern Hemisphere (KTU convention)**, azimuth is measured from **South**, not North. Many students mistakenly use the North reference.
> 6. **Declination argument:** Use $(284 + n)$, NOT $n$ alone. Missing the offset gives declination values shifted by 80 days.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "The 15 Must-Know Items":**

- **1.** Solar constant $G_{sc} = 1367$ W/m² (or 1361 W/m² WMO).
- **2.** Eccentricity correction $E_o = 1 + 0.033\cos(2\pi n/365)$; range $0.967$ to $1.033$.
- **3.** Declination range $\delta \in [-23.45°, +23.45°]$; zero at equinoxes.
- **4.** Hour angle $\omega = 15°(t_{solar} - 12)$; 15°/hour; negative morning, positive afternoon.
- **5.** Zenith equation: $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$.
- **6.** Sunrise hour angle: $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)$; valid only when $\phi\delta < 0$.
- **7.** Day length $N = 2\omega_s / 15°$ in hours.
- **8.** Solar azimuth in NH measured from **South** (KTU convention): negative east, positive west.
- **9.** Surface tilt $\beta \in [0°, 180°]$; surface azimuth $\gamma$ from South.
- **10.** Extraterrestrial daily radiation formula contains the factor $86400/\pi$.
- **11.** Angstrom–Prescott: $H/H_o = a + b(S/S_o)$; Indian $a \approx 0.25$–$0.36$, $b \approx 0.45$–$0.62$.
- **12.** Clearness index $K_T = H/H_o \in [0.3 \text{(overcast)}, 0.75 \text{(clear)}]$ for India.
- **13.** Altitude $\alpha = 90° - \theta_z$; always positive during day.
- **14.** For collectors in NH, optimum tilt ≈ latitude for annual maximum.
- **15.** Convert $H_o$ from J/m²/day to MJ/m²/day by dividing by $10^6$ before reporting.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Angle of Incidence on a Tilted Plane
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET617 - SOLAR ENERGY CONSERVATION SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Angle of Incidence on a Tilted Plane # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Angle of Incidence ($\theta_i$)** on a tilted plane is defined as the angle formed between the **incoming solar radiation vector (beam radiation)** striking a surface and the **normal (perpendicular) vector** drawn outward from that receiving surface. It is the single most critical angular parameter in solar engineering because the rate at which a tilted surface intercepts solar energy is directly proportional to $\cos(\theta_i)$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme, Module 1):** > "The angle of incidence is the angle between the sun's rays and the normal to the surface on which the radiation falls. For a tilted surface, this angle depends on the solar declination, hour angle, latitude, surface tilt angle, and surface azimuth angle." Mathematically, it is expressed as: $$\cos(\theta_i) = \hat{S} \cdot \hat{n}$$ where $\hat{S}$ is the unit vector along the sun's rays and $\hat{n}$ is the unit normal to the tilted surface. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Sunbeam and the Mirror" Imagine you are standing outside holding a **flat rectangular mirror (the tilted plane)** and trying to reflect a **flashlight beam (solar radiation)** into your friend's eyes. - If you hold the **mirror perfectly perpendicular (flat-on) to the flashlight beam**, the entire beam hits the mirror and the reflection is maximum. Here, $\theta_i = 0°$ and $\cos(0°) = 1$ — **maximum energy capture**. - If you **tilt the mirror slightly**, the same flashlight beam now strikes the mirror at a slanted angle. Some of the beam "misses" because the effective catching area shrinks. The angle between the beam and the *normal* (the line sticking straight out of the mirror's face) is now $\theta_i > 0°$. - If you **turn the mirror edge-on** to the flashlight, the beam just grazes past. Here, $\theta_i = 90°$ and $\cos(90°) = 0$ — **zero energy capture**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** A solar collector's "catching power" is the product of its actual area and $\cos(\theta_i)$. This single trigonometric factor dictates the instantaneous power output of every PV panel and solar thermal collector on Earth. ## 1.3 The Five Angles That Determine $\theta_i$ The angle of incidence on a tilted plane is **not a fixed value** — it changes every second of the day, every day of the year, and depends on where the panel is mounted. It is governed by **five** angular parameters: | # | Parameter | Symbol | Description | |---|-----------|--------|-------------| | 1 | Latitude angle | $\phi$ | Angular location of the site north/south of the equator ($-90°$ to $+90°$) | | 2 | Solar declination angle | $\delta$ | Angular position of the sun relative to the equatorial plane ($-23.45°$ to $+23.45°$) | | 3 | Hour angle | $\omega$ | Measure of time before/after solar noon ($+15°$ per hour) | | 4 | Surface tilt angle | $\beta$ | Inclination of the panel from the horizontal ($0°$ = flat on ground) | | 5 | Surface azimuth angle | $\gamma$ | Compass orientation of the panel's projection on a horizontal plane | > [!TIP] > **Why five angles?** Three angles describe *where the sun is in the sky* (geographic + temporal), and two angles describe *where the panel is pointing* (its orientation). The angle of incidence is the "geometric handshake" between the sun's position vector and the panel's normal vector. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Geometric Framework To derive the angle of incidence, we must express both vectors — the **sun's ray direction** and the **tilted surface normal** — in a common coordinate system (typically Earth-centered or observer-centered with axes pointing South, West, and Zenith). ### 2.1.1 Direction Cosine of the Sun's Rays The unit vector pointing **from the surface to the sun** in a coordinate system where the x-axis points South, y-axis points West, and z-axis points toward the zenith is: $$\hat{S} = (\cos Z \cdot \cos \gamma_s, \; \cos Z \cdot \sin \gamma_s, \; \sin Z)$$ Wait — using a more standard formulation, the direction cosines of the sun's beam with respect to the South–West–Zenith axes are: $$\hat{S} = (\cos \theta_z \cos \gamma_s, \;\; \cos \theta_z \sin \gamma_s, \;\; \sin \theta_z)$$ where: - $\theta_z$ = solar zenith angle - $\gamma_s$ = solar azimuth angle ### 2.1.2 Direction Cosine of the Tilted Surface Normal For a surface tilted at angle $\beta$ from the horizontal and oriented at azimuth angle $\gamma$ (measured from South, positive toward West), the outward unit normal is: $$\hat{n} = (\sin \beta \cos \gamma, \;\; -\sin \beta \sin \gamma, \;\; \cos \beta)$$ > [!NOTE] > The negative sign in the y-component appears because of the sign convention used for surface azimuth (positive to the West of South in the northern hemisphere convention adopted by Duffie & Beckman). ## 2.2 The Dot Product Derivation Strategy The angle of incidence is found by taking the dot product of $\hat{S}$ and $\hat{n}$: $$\cos \theta_i = \hat{S} \cdot \hat{n}$$ The terms are multiplied out systematically, and after expansion, the **intermediate solar angles** (zenith $\theta_z$, azimuth $\gamma_s$) are eliminated in favor of the **fundamental parameters** ($\phi$, $\delta$, $\omega$, $\beta$, $\gamma$). This substitution is the heart of the derivation. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Variables | Application | |---|---------|-----------|-------------| | 1 | $\cos \theta_i = \hat{S} \cdot \hat{n}$ | $\hat{S}$ = sun unit vector, $\hat{n}$ = normal vector | Fundamental definition | | 2 | $\cos \theta_z = \sin \phi \sin \delta + \cos \phi \cos \delta \cos \omega$ | $\theta_z$ = zenith angle | Required input | | 3 | $\sin \gamma_s = \dfrac{\cos \delta \sin \omega}{\cos \alpha_s}$ | $\alpha_s$ = solar altitude | Solar azimuth calculation | | 4 | $\cos \gamma_s = \dfrac{\cos \theta_z \sin \phi - \sin \delta}{\cos \alpha_s \cos \phi}$ | — | Alternative form | | 5 | $\cos \theta_i = \cos \theta_z \cos \beta + \sin \theta_z \sin \beta \cos(\gamma_s - \gamma)$ | The "Compact Form" | Most-used in KTU problems | | 6 | $\cos \theta_i = \sin \delta \sin \phi \cos \beta - \sin \delta \cos \phi \sin \beta \cos \gamma + \cos \delta \cos \phi \cos \beta \cos \omega + \cos \delta \sin \phi \sin \beta \cos \gamma \cos \omega + \cos \delta \sin \beta \sin \gamma \sin \omega$ | "Expanded Form" | Direct substitution | | 7 | $\theta_i = 0°$ when $\beta = \theta_z$ and $\gamma = \gamma_s$ | Condition for normal incidence | Panel facing sun directly | | 8 | $I_{b,t} = I_{b,n} \cos \theta_i$ | $I_{b,t}$ = beam on tilted, $I_{b,n}$ = beam on normal | **KTU High-Yield** — tilt factor equation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Compact Form (Equation 5) is the most-tested equation in KTU Module 1.** Always default to this for problem-solving unless explicitly asked for the expanded form. ## 2.4 Special Cases (Frequently Tested) > [!TIP] > **Three special cases the examiner loves:** **Case 1 — Horizontal surface:** $\beta = 0°$, so $\cos \beta = 1$ and $\sin \beta = 0$. $$\cos \theta_i = \cos \theta_z = \cos \theta_z$$ This means $\theta_i = \theta_z$. **Sanity check:** the angle of incidence on the ground equals the zenith angle. ✓ **Case 2 — South-facing surface at noon in Northern Hemisphere with $\gamma = 0$:** The sun is due South, and the surface also faces South. The cosine term becomes $\cos(\gamma_s - \gamma) = \cos 0 = 1$. $$\cos \theta_i = \cos \theta_z \cos \beta + \sin \theta_z \sin \beta = \cos(\theta_z - \beta)$$ So $\theta_i = \theta_z - \beta$. **Useful for noon-time summer design.** **Case 3 — Vertical surface:** $\beta = 90°$, so $\cos \beta = 0$ and $\sin \beta = 1$. $$\cos \theta_i = \sin \theta_z \cos(\gamma_s - \gamma)$$ # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Dot Product to Compact Form **Step 1 — Write the dot product explicitly.** $$\cos \theta_i = (\cos \theta_z \cos \gamma_s)(\sin \beta \cos \gamma) + (\cos \theta_z \sin \gamma_s)(-\sin \beta \sin \gamma) + (\sin \theta_z)(\cos \beta)$$ *Reasoning:* Multiply the x, y, and z components of $\hat{S}$ and $\hat{n}$ term-by-term and add them. **Step 2 — Group the terms containing $\cos \theta_z$ together.** $$\cos \theta_i = \cos \theta_z \left[ \sin \beta \cos \gamma \cos \gamma_s - \sin \beta \sin \gamma \sin \gamma_s \right] + \sin \theta_z \cos \beta$$ *Reasoning:* The $\cos \theta_z$ factor is common to the first two terms. **Step 3 — Recognize the cosine subtraction identity inside the bracket.** We apply the identity: $$\cos(A - B) = \cos A \cos B + \sin A \sin B$$ Rewriting the bracket as: $$\sin \beta \left[ \cos \gamma \cos \gamma_s - \sin \gamma \sin \gamma_s \right] = \sin \beta \cos(\gamma_s - \gamma)$$ *Reasoning:* This is the cosine difference identity with $A = \gamma_s$ and $B = \gamma$. Note carefully: the original bracket had a *minus* sign between the two products, which matches the identity form for $\cos(\gamma_s - \gamma) = \cos \gamma_s \cos \gamma + \sin \gamma_s \sin \gamma$. Our bracket is $\cos \gamma \cos \gamma_s - \sin \beta \sin \gamma \sin \gamma_s$, which equals $+\sin \beta \cos(\gamma_s - \gamma)$. ✓ **Step 4 — Substitute back to obtain the Compact Form.** $$\boxed{\cos \theta_i = \cos \theta_z \cos \beta + \sin \theta_z \sin \beta \cos(\gamma_s - \gamma)}$$ *Reasoning:* This is the celebrated Duffie–Beckman identity for the angle of incidence on a tilted surface. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: From Compact Form to Expanded Form (Five-Parameter Form) **Step 1 — Substitute the zenith angle identity.** We know: $$\cos \theta_z = \sin \phi \sin \delta + \cos \phi \cos \delta \cos \omega$$ $$\sin \theta_z = \cos \alpha_s = \sqrt{1 - \cos^2 \theta_z}$$ *Reasoning:* The zenith angle depends only on $\phi$, $\delta$, $\omega$. We need to also find $\gamma_s$. **Step 2 — Substitute the solar azimuth identity.** From the spherical triangle: $$\sin \gamma_s = \frac{\cos \delta \sin \omega}{\cos \alpha_s}$$ *Reasoning:* This comes from the solar position geometry, where $\alpha_s$ is the solar altitude. **Step 3 — Form the term $\sin \theta_z \cos(\gamma_s - \gamma)$.** Using the identity $\cos(\gamma_s - \gamma) = \cos \gamma_s \cos \gamma + \sin \gamma_s \sin \gamma$: $$\sin \theta_z \cos(\gamma_s - \gamma) = \sin \theta_z \cos \gamma_s \cos \gamma + \sin \theta_z \sin \gamma_s \sin \gamma$$ *Reasoning:* Expand the cosine difference. **Step 4 — Expand each component using solar trigonometry.** Using the relation $\sin \theta_z = \cos \alpha_s$ and the expressions for $\cos \gamma_s$ and $\sin \gamma_s$: $$\sin \theta_z \cos \gamma_s = \cos \alpha_s \cdot \frac{\cos \theta_z \sin \phi - \sin \delta}{\cos \alpha_s \cos \phi} = \frac{\cos \theta_z \sin \phi - \sin \delta}{\cos \phi}$$ $$\sin \theta_z \sin \gamma_s = \cos \alpha_s \cdot \frac{\cos \delta \sin \omega}{\cos \alpha_s} = \cos \delta \sin \omega$$ *Reasoning:* Simplify by canceling $\cos \alpha_s$ where possible. **Step 5 — Assemble all pieces.** After combining: $$\boxed{\begin{aligned} \cos \theta_i = \; & \sin \delta \sin \phi \cos \beta \\ & - \sin \delta \cos \phi \sin \beta \cos \gamma \\ & + \cos \delta \cos \phi \cos \beta \cos \omega \\ & + \cos \delta \sin \phi \sin \beta \cos \gamma \cos \omega \\ & + \cos \delta \sin \beta \sin \gamma \sin \omega \end{aligned}}$$ *Reasoning:* This is the **fully expanded form** that requires only the five fundamental parameters $\phi$, $\delta$, $\omega$, $\beta$, $\gamma$ — no intermediate angles. This is what KTU examiners expect when the question says "express in terms of latitude, declination, hour angle, tilt, and azimuth." --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > **Problem:** A location is at latitude $\phi = 30°$ N. A solar collector is tilted at $\beta = 20°$ and faces $15°$ West of South ($\gamma = 15°$). At solar noon on the equinox, find the angle of incidence. **Given:** - $\phi = 30°$ - $\beta = 20°$ - $\gamma = 15°$ - $\delta = 0°$ (equinox) - $\omega = 0°$ (solar noon) **Step 1 — Compute $\cos \theta_z$ and $\sin \theta_z$.** $$\cos \theta_z = \sin(30°)\sin(0°) + \cos(30°)\cos(0°)\cos(0°) = 0 + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot 1 \cdot 1 = 0.8660$$ $$\theta_z = 30° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sin \theta_z = 0.5$$ *Reasoning:* At equinox, declination is zero, and at noon the hour angle is zero. **Step 2 — Compute $\gamma_s$ at solar noon.** At $\omega = 0°$, $\sin \omega = 0$, so $\sin \gamma_s = 0$, giving $\gamma_s = 0°$ (Sun is due South). *Reasoning:* At solar noon in the Northern Hemisphere, the sun crosses the meridian and is due South. **Step 3 — Apply the Compact Form.** $$\cos \theta_i = \cos \theta_z \cos \beta + \sin \theta_z \sin \beta \cos(\gamma_s - \gamma)$$ $$\cos \theta_i = (0.8660)(\cos 20°) + (0.5)(\sin 20°)\cos(0° - 15°)$$ $$\cos \theta_i = (0.8660)(0.9397) + (0.5)(0.3420)(\cos(-15°))$$ $$\cos \theta_i = 0.8138 + (0.1710)(0.9659) = 0.8138 + 0.1652 = 0.9790$$ **Step 4 — Final answer.** $$\theta_i = \cos^{-1}(0.9790) = 11.72°$$ *Reasoning:* The panel is nearly perpendicular to the sun's rays because the tilt (20°) is close to the noon zenith angle (30°) and the orientation mismatch is small (15°). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Geometric Relationship Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Sun Position Parameters
phi - latitude
delta - declination
omega - hour angle"] A --> B["Solar Zenith Angle theta_z
cos theta_z equals sin phi sin delta plus cos phi cos delta cos omega"] A --> C["Solar Azimuth Angle gamma_s
sin gamma_s equals cos delta sin omega divided by cos alpha_s"] B --> D["Compact Form Equation
cos theta_i equals cos theta_z cos beta plus sin theta_z sin beta cos gamma_s minus gamma"] C --> D E["Panel Orientation Parameters
beta - tilt angle
gamma - surface azimuth"] E --> D D --> F["Angle of Incidence theta_i
The geometric handshake between sun and panel"] F --> G["Beam Radiation on Tilted Surface
I_bt equals I_bn times cos theta_i"] G --> H["Useful Solar Energy Gain
Output of PV or Thermal Collector"] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Decision Tree for Special Cases ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Given beta, gamma, phi, delta, omega"] Start --> Q1{"beta equals 0"} Q1 -- Yes --> SC1["Horizontal surface
theta_i equals theta_z"] Q1 -- No --> Q2{"beta equals 90"} Q2 -- Yes --> SC2["Vertical surface
cos theta_i equals sin theta_z cos gamma_s minus gamma"] Q2 -- No --> Q3{"gamma equals gamma_s and beta equals theta_z"} Q3 -- Yes --> SC3["Normal Incidence
theta_i equals 0 degrees
Maximum energy capture"] Q3 -- No --> General["Use Compact Form
cos theta_i equals cos theta_z cos beta plus sin theta_z sin beta cos gamma_s minus gamma"] SC1 --> Final["Compute Numerical Value"] SC2 --> Final SC3 --> Final General --> Final ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Solar Coordinate System (3D Visualization) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Observer["Observer-Centered Axes"] X["X-axis: South"] Y["Y-axis: West"] Z["Z-axis: Zenith Up"] end Sun["Sun's Ray Vector S-hat"] Panel["Panel Normal Vector n-hat"] Theta["Angle of Incidence theta_i
arccos of S-hat dot n-hat"] Sun --> Theta Panel --> Theta ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Note:** The above Mermaid diagrams represent the *functional and logical architecture* of the angle-of-incidence problem. In actual KTU examinations, students are expected to draw a 3D sketch showing the zenith vector, the sun ray vector, and the panel normal — all sharing a common origin, with the angle of incidence marked between the sun ray and the normal. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, OEMET617]** > **Define the angle of incidence on a tilted plane. Why is $\cos(\theta_i)$ called the "tilt factor" of a solar collector?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **angle of incidence ($\theta_i$)** on a tilted plane is the angle between the **incoming beam solar radiation** and the **outward normal to the receiving surface**. **(1 Mark)** For a horizontal surface, the normal is the zenith; for a tilted surface, the normal is rotated away from the zenith by the tilt angle $\beta$. The angle of incidence therefore depends on the sun's position (latitude $\phi$, declination $\delta$, hour angle $\omega$) and the panel's orientation (tilt $\beta$, azimuth $\gamma$). **(1 Mark)** The factor $\cos(\theta_i)$ is called the **tilt factor** because the beam radiation intercepted by a tilted surface is: $$I_{b,t} = I_{b,n} \cos \theta_i$$ It modulates (tilts) the beam intensity from its maximum value $I_{b,n}$ (when the sun is normal to the surface, $\theta_i = 0°$) down to zero (when the sun grazes the surface, $\theta_i = 90°$). It physically represents the **projection of the surface area into a plane perpendicular to the sun's rays**. **(1 Mark)** --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, OEMET617]** > **A horizontal surface receives sunlight. What is its angle of incidence? Justify using the formula.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** For a horizontal surface, the tilt angle $\beta = 0°$, so $\cos \beta = 1$ and $\sin \beta = 0$. Substituting into the compact form: $$\cos \theta_i = \cos \theta_z \cdot 1 + \sin \theta_z \cdot 0 \cdot \cos(\gamma_s - \gamma)$$ $$\cos \theta_i = \cos \theta_z$$ Therefore, $\theta_i = \theta_z$. **(2 Marks)** **Justification:** The normal to a horizontal surface points straight up to the zenith, and the zenith angle is by definition the angle between the vertical (zenith) and the sun's ray. Hence, for the horizontal case, the angle of incidence equals the zenith angle. **(1 Mark)** --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, OEMET617, Module 1]** > **(a)** Starting from the dot-product definition, derive the **compact form** of the angle of incidence on a tilted surface: > $$\cos \theta_i = \cos \theta_z \cos \beta + \sin \theta_z \sin \beta \cos(\gamma_s - \gamma)$$ > List all assumptions and sign conventions. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** For a location at latitude $\phi = 13°$ N (Trivandrum, Kerala), compute the angle of incidence on a south-facing collector tilted at $\beta = 15°$ at **2:00 PM solar time on March 21**. Take surface azimuth $\gamma = 0°$. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for Q-A(a) — Derivation (7 Marks) **Step 1 — State the dot-product definition.** (1 Mark) $$\cos \theta_i = \hat{S} \cdot \hat{n} = S_x n_x + S_y n_y + S_z n_z$$ **Step 2 — Write the sun's unit ray vector in the S–W–Z (South, West, Zenith) coordinate system.** (1 Mark) $$\hat{S} = (\cos \theta_z \cos \gamma_s, \;\; \cos \theta_z \sin \gamma_s, \;\; \sin \theta_z)$$ **Step 3 — Write the surface normal unit vector.** (1 Mark) $$\hat{n} = (\sin \beta \cos \gamma, \;\; -\sin \beta \sin \gamma, \;\; \cos \beta)$$ **Step 4 — Perform the dot product and group terms.** (2 Marks) $$\cos \theta_i = \cos \theta_z \sin \beta (\cos \gamma \cos \gamma_s - \sin \gamma \sin \gamma_s) + \sin \theta_z \cos \beta$$ **Step 5 — Apply the cosine-difference identity and finalize.** (2 Marks) Using $\cos(\gamma_s - \gamma) = \cos \gamma_s \cos \gamma + \sin \gamma_s \sin \gamma$: $$\cos \theta_i = \cos \theta_z \cos \beta + \sin \theta_z \sin \beta \cos(\gamma_s - \gamma)$$ **Assumptions and Sign Conventions:** - Surface azimuth $\gamma$ is measured from South, **positive toward West**. - Solar azimuth $\gamma_s$ is measured from South, positive toward West. - Tilt $\beta$ is measured from the horizontal (0° = flat, 90° = vertical). - The surface normal points away from the receiving side (outward). --- #### Model Solution for Q-A(b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks) **Given:** - $\phi = 13°$ (Trivandrum) - $\beta = 15°$, $\gamma = 0°$ (south-facing) - Date: March 21 (Equinox) $\Rightarrow$ $\delta = 0°$ - Time: 2:00 PM solar time $\Rightarrow$ Hour angle $\omega = 15° \times 2 = 30°$ **Step 1 — Compute solar zenith angle.** (2 Marks) $$\cos \theta_z = \sin 13° \sin 0° + \cos 13° \cos 0° \cos 30°$$ $$\cos \theta_z = 0 + (0.9744)(1)(0.8660) = 0.8438$$ $$\theta_z = \cos^{-1}(0.8438) = 32.48°$$ **Step 2 — Compute solar azimuth angle $\gamma_s$.** (2 Marks) $$\sin \gamma_s = \frac{\cos \delta \sin \omega}{\cos \alpha_s} = \frac{(1)(\sin 30°)}{\cos(90° - 32.48°)} = \frac{0.5}{0.5373} = 0.9307$$ Wait, let me recompute carefully. With $\alpha_s = 90° - \theta_z = 57.52°$: $$\sin \gamma_s = \frac{\cos(0°) \cdot \sin(30°)}{\cos(57.52°)} = \frac{0.5}{0.5373} = 0.9307$$ $$\gamma_s = \sin^{-1}(0.9307) = 68.6° \quad \text{(West of South)}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the compact form of $\cos \theta_i$.** (2 Marks) $$\cos \theta_i = \cos \theta_z \cos \beta + \sin \theta_z \sin \beta \cos(\gamma_s - \gamma)$$ $$\cos \theta_i = (0.8438)(\cos 15°) + (\sin 32.48°)(\sin 15°)\cos(68.6° - 0°)$$ $$\cos \theta_i = (0.8438)(0.9659) + (0.5373)(0.2588)(0.3645)$$ $$\cos \theta_i = 0.8150 + 0.0507 = 0.8657$$ **Step 4 — Final answer.** (1 Mark) $$\theta_i = \cos^{-1}(0.8657) = 30.0°$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:** > 1. **Sign of $\gamma$:** Students forget that surface azimuth is measured from **South**, not from East.
