Mixing up the convention yields a wrong answer with no partial credit. > 2. **Hour angle conversion:** 2:00 PM solar time = 2 hours *after* solar noon = $2 \times 15° = 30°$ **positive**. A.M. is negative. Confusing this gives a wrong sign on $\omega$. > 3. **Units in calculator mode:** Many students compute $\cos^{-1}$ in **radian mode** instead of **degree mode**, getting bizarre angles like 1.27 "radians." Always verify your calculator is in DEG. > 4. **Declination sign for Southern Hemisphere stations:** Kerala is just 8° north of the equator, so always use $\delta$ as given in the Cooper equation $\delta = 23.45° \sin\left(\dfrac{360°}{365}(284 + n)\right)$ for the day number $n$. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, OEMET617, Module 1]** > **(a)** Starting from the dot-product definition, derive the **fully expanded form** of $\cos \theta_i$ in terms of the five fundamental parameters $\phi$, $\delta$, $\omega$, $\beta$, $\gamma$. Show all algebraic steps. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** A solar collector is installed at $\phi = 10°$ N, tilted at $\beta = 10°$, and oriented with $\gamma = 30°$ (West of South). On June 21 (declination $\delta = +23.45°$), find the angle of incidence at **9:00 AM solar time** ($\omega = -45°$). **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for Q-B(a) — Expanded Form Derivation (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Start from the compact form.** (1 Mark) $$\cos \theta_i = \cos \theta_z \cos \beta + \sin \theta_z \sin \beta \cos(\gamma_s - \gamma)$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the zenith angle expression.** (1 Mark) Using $\cos \theta_z = \sin \phi \sin \delta + \cos \phi \cos \delta \cos \omega$: The first term becomes: $$\cos \theta_z \cos \beta = (\sin \phi \sin \delta + \cos \phi \cos \delta \cos \omega)\cos \beta$$ **Step 3 — Substitute solar altitude and azimuth relations.** (2 Marks) The second term $\sin \theta_z \cos(\gamma_s - \gamma)$ expands using: $$\sin \theta_z \cos(\gamma_s - \gamma) = \sin \theta_z \cos \gamma_s \cos \gamma + \sin \theta_z \sin \gamma_s \sin \gamma$$ With $\sin \theta_z = \cos \alpha_s$, $\cos \gamma_s = \dfrac{\cos \theta_z \sin \phi - \sin \delta}{\cos \alpha_s \cos \phi}$, and $\sin \gamma_s = \dfrac{\cos \delta \sin \omega}{\cos \alpha_s}$: $$\sin \theta_z \cos \gamma_s = \frac{\cos \theta_z \sin \phi - \sin \delta}{\cos \phi}, \quad \sin \theta_z \sin \gamma_s = \cos \delta \sin \omega$$ **Step 4 — Multiply by $\sin \beta$ and combine all terms.** (2 Marks) $$\sin \theta_z \sin \beta \cos(\gamma_s - \gamma) = \sin \beta \cos \gamma \left[\frac{\cos \theta_z \sin \phi - \sin \delta}{\cos \phi}\right] + \sin \beta \sin \gamma \cos \delta \sin \omega$$ **Step 5 — Final expanded form.** (1 Mark) After combining and substituting $\cos \theta_z$ everywhere: $$\boxed{\begin{aligned} \cos \theta_i = \; & \sin \delta \sin \phi \cos \beta \\ & - \sin \delta \cos \phi \sin \beta \cos \gamma \\ & + \cos \delta \cos \phi \cos \beta \cos \omega \\ & + \cos \delta \sin \phi \sin \beta \cos \gamma \cos \omega \\ & + \cos \delta \sin \beta \sin \gamma \sin \omega \end{aligned}}$$ --- #### Model Solution for Q-B(b) — Numerical (7 Marks) **Given:** $\phi = 10°$, $\beta = 10°$, $\gamma = 30°$, $\delta = 23.45°$, $\omega = -45°$. **Step 1 — Compute the five terms of the expanded formula.** (3 Marks) | Term | Expression | Numerical Value | |------|-----------|-----------------| | $T_1$ | $\sin 23.45° \sin 10° \cos 10°$ | $(0.3979)(0.1736)(0.9848) = 0.0680$ | | $T_2$ | $- \sin 23.45° \cos 10° \sin 10° \cos 30°$ | $- (0.3979)(0.9848)(0.1736)(0.8660) = -0.0590$ | | $T_3$ | $\cos 23.45° \cos 10° \cos 10° \cos(-45°)$ | $(0.9174)(0.9848)(0.9848)(0.7071) = 0.6295$ | | $T_4$ | $\cos 23.45° \sin 10° \sin 10° \cos 30° \cos(-45°)$ | $(0.9174)(0.1736)(0.1736)(0.8660)(0.7071) = 0.0179$ | | $T_5$ | $\cos 23.45° \sin 10° \sin 30° \sin(-45°)$ | $(0.9174)(0.1736)(0.5)(-0.7071) = -0.0563$ | **Step 2 — Sum the terms.** (2 Marks) $$\cos \theta_i = 0.0680 - 0.0590 + 0.6295 + 0.0179 - 0.0563 = 0.6001$$ **Step 3 — Final answer.** (2 Marks) $$\theta_i = \cos^{-1}(0.6001) = 53.13°$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Q-B Specific:** > 1. **Hour angle sign for A.M.:** 9:00 AM is *before* solar noon, so $\omega = -45°$ (negative). Marking it as $+45°$ is a 1-mark deduction. > 2. **Rounding errors cascade:** Each term is small, and a 0.01 error in $\sin 23.45°$ propagates to a 5° error in the final answer. Show at least 4 decimal places. > 3. **Azimuth convention confusion:** $\gamma = 30°$ means 30° West of South. The formula uses $\cos \gamma$ and $\sin \gamma$ directly — do **not** flip signs based on East/West. > 4. **Missing the negative sign on $T_2$:** The expanded form has alternating signs in the first two terms. A common slip is making both positive. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **The 12 Things You Must Remember Before Walking Into the KTU Exam:** 1. **The Compact Form is your default equation** for the angle of incidence: $$\cos \theta_i = \cos \theta_z \cos \beta + \sin \theta_z \sin \beta \cos(\gamma_s - \gamma)$$ 2. **The Expanded Form** uses the five fundamental parameters directly — no intermediate angles needed: $$\cos \theta_i = \sin \delta \sin \phi \cos \beta - \sin \delta \cos \phi \sin \beta \cos \gamma + \cos \delta \cos \phi \cos \beta \cos \omega + \cos \delta \sin \phi \sin \beta \cos \gamma \cos \omega + \cos \delta \sin \beta \sin \gamma \sin \omega$$ 3. **Sign convention for azimuth:** Both $\gamma_s$ and $\gamma$ are measured from **South**, **positive toward West**. Do not use the North-referenced compass convention. 4. **Hour angle sign rule:** $\omega = 0°$ at solar noon, $\omega = -15°$ per hour before noon, $\omega = +15°$ per hour after noon. 5. **Special case — horizontal surface:** $\beta = 0° \Rightarrow \theta_i = \theta_z$. 6. **Special case — vertical surface:** $\beta = 90° \Rightarrow \cos \theta_i = \sin \theta_z \cos(\gamma_s - \gamma)$. 7. **Special case — normal incidence (maximum capture):** $\theta_i = 0°$ requires $\beta = \theta_z$ and $\gamma = \gamma_s$ simultaneously. 8. **The "tilt factor" relationship:** $I_{b,t} = I_{b,n} \cos \theta_i$ — this is why $\cos \theta_i$ is so important in solar collector design. 9. **Range of validity:** $\cos \theta_i$ must lie in $[-1, +1]$. If your calculation gives a value outside this range, you have a sign or input error. 10. **Negative $\cos \theta_i$** physically means the sun is *behind* the tilted surface — the surface receives no direct beam radiation. Set $I_{b,t} = 0$ in such cases. 11. **The dot-product foundation:** $\cos \theta_i = \hat{S} \cdot \hat{n}$ is the starting point of every derivation. Memorize the unit vectors $\hat{S}$ and $\hat{n}$ in the S–W–Z coordinate system. 12. **Always state units and conventions in derivations.** KTU examiners award 1 mark for clearly stating the coordinate system and sign conventions used. > **End of Module 1 Notes — Angle of Incidence on a Tilted Plane**

topics.title: Shading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET617 - SOLAR ENERGY CONSERVATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Shading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shading** in the context of Solar Photovoltaic (PV) systems is defined as the *partial or complete obstruction of incident solar irradiance* falling on one or more cells of a PV module caused by stationary or moving objects such as nearby buildings, trees, utility poles, adjacent module rows, clouds, bird droppings, dust accumulation, or self-shadowing of the module frame.

Shading is broadly classified into the following categories recognized by the KTU 2024 OEMET617 syllabus:

1. **Hard Shading** — Solid, well-defined shadows cast by opaque objects (e.g., a tree trunk, a chimney, a neighbouring building).
2. **Soft Shading** — Diffuse, gradual reduction in irradiance caused by clouds, haze, smog, or dust deposition.
3. **Row-to-Row (Inter-Row) Shading** — Mutual shadowing of adjacent tilted module rows in a utility-scale solar farm.
4. **Self-Shading** — Shading of one part of the same module by another part (e.g., during steep sun angles).
5. **Partial Shading** — A non-uniform irradiance distribution across the module surface, where some cells receive the design irradiance while others receive significantly less.

> [!NOTE]
> The standard metric used by MNRE (Ministry of New and Renewable Energy, India) and IEC 61215 is the **Shading Factor (SF)**, defined as the ratio of the shaded active area to the total active area of the module.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

Imagine you are a gardener watering a long row of connected drinking straws (each straw represents a PV cell) using a single tap. The tap's flow rate is fixed — equivalent to the current generated by the **weakest, most-shaded cell** in the string. Now, even if 59 of the 60 straws are wide open, that *one* crushed straw restricts water (current) flow through the entire pipe.

In an electrical PV module, all cells are wired in **series** to add up their voltages. When one cell is shaded, it cannot supply the same current as the illuminated cells. To keep the current flowing, the shaded cell is forced into **reverse bias** (it now behaves like a *resistor* instead of a *source*). The power generated by the remaining 59 cells is wasted heating up that single shaded cell — a phenomenon called the **hot-spot effect**. This is why shading is not merely a "loss of area" — it is a destructive phenomenon that can permanently damage the module if not engineered against.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Standard Test Condition (STC)** irradiance reference is **G_STC = 1000 W/m²** at AM 1.5 and cell temperature **T_c = 25 °C (298.15 K)**. Any shading analysis is benchmarked against these values.

### 1.3 Real-World Significance

A study by Sandia National Laboratories showed that even **1% shading** of a string can cause up to **70% reduction in power output** due to the "Christmas-light effect" of series-connected cells. Kerala, with its high population density and lush vegetation, frequently suffers from **vegetative and structural shading**, making this topic of paramount importance for KTU students specializing in renewable energy.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physics of Shading on a PV Cell

The photo-generated current of a PV cell is directly proportional to the incident irradiance:

$$I_{ph} = I_{ph,\text{STC}} \cdot \frac{G}{G_{\text{STC}}}$$

When a cell receives a reduced irradiance $G_s < G$, its photo-current drops to:

$$I_{ph,s} = I_{ph,\text{STC}} \cdot \frac{G_s}{G_{\text{STC}}}$$

### 2.2 The Hot-Spot Effect (Step-by-Step Logic)

- **Step 1 — Current Bottleneck:** In a series string, the current is forced to be identical through every cell. The shaded cell demands a current it cannot supply.
- **Step 2 — Reverse Biasing:** The I-V curve of the shaded cell intersects the operating curve of the string at a **negative voltage** point. The shaded cell becomes a *load* rather than a *source*.
- **Step 3 — Power Dissipation:** The illuminated cells pump current *into* the shaded cell. Power dissipated as heat: $P_{\text{hot-spot}} = I_{string} \cdot \vert V_{shaded} \vert$.
- **Step 4 — Thermal Runaway:** If cell temperature exceeds roughly **85 °C to 100 °C**, irreversible damage to the encapsulant (EVA) and solder bonds occurs.

### 2.3 Role of Bypass Diodes (Engineering Countermeasure)

> [!NOTE]
> A **bypass diode** is an anti-parallel Schottky diode connected across a group of 20–24 cells in a 60/72-cell module. When the substring voltage reverses (due to shading), the diode becomes forward-biased and offers a low-resistance (~0.4 V drop) alternate path, **protecting the cells from hot-spot damage** at the cost of losing that substring's contribution to module voltage.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $I_{ph} = I_{ph,\text{STC}} \cdot \dfrac{G}{G_{\text{STC}}}$ | Photo-current proportional to irradiance | A |
| 2 | $\text{SF} = \dfrac{A_{\text{shaded}}}{A_{\text{total}}}$ | Shading Factor definition | dimensionless |
| 3 | $G_{\text{eff}} = G \cdot (1 - \text{SF})$ | Effective irradiance under uniform partial shading | W/m² |
| 4 | $P_{\text{loss}} = P_{\text{rated}} \cdot \text{SF}_{\text{weighted}}$ | Weighted shading power loss | W |
| 5 | $V_{\text{module,shaded}} = V_{\text{oc}} - V_{\text{diode}}$ | Module voltage when one substring bypassed (≈ 0.4–0.7 V drop) | V |
| 6 | $P_{\text{loss,sub}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{module}}}{N_{\text{sub}}}$ | Loss per bypassed substring | W |
| 7 | $D_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{L \cdot \sin\beta}{\tan\alpha_{\text{ws}}}$ | Minimum inter-row spacing to avoid shading at winter solstice | m |
| 8 | $\alpha_{\text{ws}} = 90^{\circ} - \phi + 23.45^{\circ}$ | Solar altitude at winter solstice noon (latitude $\phi$) | degrees |
| 9 | $D_{\text{ratio}} = \dfrac{D}{H}$ | Ground Coverage Ratio (GCR) inverse | dimensionless |
| 10 | $I_{\text{bypass}} = I_{\text{string}} - I_{\text{shaded,reverse-limit}}$ | Current diverted by bypass diode | A |
| 11 | $T_{\text{cell}} = T_{\text{amb}} + \dfrac{\text{NOCT} - 20}{0.8 \cdot kW/m^2} \cdot G$ | Cell temperature from NOCT model | °C |
| 12 | $\eta_{\text{shade}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{shaded}}}{P_{\text{unshaded}}} \times 100\%$ | Shading efficiency ratio | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Examiner Tip: Always clearly state the **assumptions** before applying the formulas. Common assumptions are (a) STC conditions, (b) uniform cell temperature, (c) bypass diode drop of **0.4 V** (Schottky) or **0.7 V** (standard Si), and (d) negligible wiring losses.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rooftop Designers:** Use shading analysis tools (PVsyst, Helioscope, SAM) to lay out modules avoiding chimney/AC unit shadows.
- **Utility-Scale Farms:** Inter-row spacing optimization using the $D_{\text{min}}$ formula saves crores of rupees in shadow losses.
- **Smart Inverter MPPT:** Modern MPPT algorithms (Perturb & Observe, Incremental Conductance) handle partial shading by finding the **Global Maximum Power Point (GMPP)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Shading Factor and Effective Power Loss

**Problem Setup:**
Consider a 60-cell crystalline silicon module rated at $P_{\text{max,STC}} = 300$ W at STC. Out of 60 cells, $n_s = 6$ cells in one substring are uniformly shaded. The shaded cells receive $G_s = 200$ W/m² while the remaining cells receive $G_u = 1000$ W/m². The module has 3 substrings (each of 20 cells) with three bypass diodes. Determine the shading factor, effective irradiance, and the power loss.

**Step 1 — Compute the Shading Factor (SF).**

The shading factor is defined as the ratio of shaded active area to total active area. Assuming all cells have equal area $A_c$:

$$\text{SF} = \frac{n_s \cdot A_c}{N_{\text{total}} \cdot A_c} = \frac{n_s}{N_{\text{total}}}$$

Substituting $n_s = 6$ and $N_{\text{total}} = 60$:

$$\text{SF} = \frac{6}{60} = 0.10 \quad (10\%)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Effective Irradiance (Area-Weighted).**

$$G_{\text{eff}} = G_u \cdot (1 - \text{SF}) + G_s \cdot \text{SF}$$

$$G_{\text{eff}} = 1000 \cdot 0.90 + 200 \cdot 0.10$$

$$G_{\text{eff}} = 900 + 20 = 920 \; W/m^2$$

**Step 3 — Naive (Linear) Power Estimate.**

If we naively assume linear scaling (which is INCORRECT for series strings):

$$P_{\text{naive}} = P_{\text{max,STC}} \cdot \frac{G_{\text{eff}}}{G_{\text{STC}}} = 300 \cdot \frac{920}{1000} = 276 \; W$$

**Step 4 — Actual Power Accounting for Bypass Diode Action.**

Since 6 of the 20 cells in substring 1 are shaded and the current of the entire string is forced to the lower value dictated by the shaded cells, the bypass diode across substring 1 turns ON. The voltage contribution of substring 1 is replaced by the diode drop $V_D \approx 0.7$ V.

The module output voltage becomes:

$$V_{\text{module}} = 2 \cdot V_{\text{substring}} + V_D$$

where $V_{\text{substring}} \approx \frac{V_{\text{oc}}}{3}$. For $V_{\text{oc}} = 45$ V (typical), $V_{\text{substring}} = 15$ V. Thus:

$$V_{\text{module}} = 2 \cdot 15 + 0.7 = 30.7 \; V$$

The current is now limited by the shaded cells:

$$I_{\text{module}} = I_{\text{ph,STC}} \cdot \frac{G_s}{G_{\text{STC}}} = I_{\text{ph,STC}} \cdot 0.2$$

If the rated MPP current is $I_{\text{mpp}} = 8.33$ A (so that $300 = 36 \times 8.33$):

$$I_{\text{module}} = 8.33 \cdot 0.2 = 1.666 \; A$$

**Step 5 — Realistic Power Output.**

$$P_{\text{actual}} = V_{\text{module}} \cdot I_{\text{module}} = 30.7 \cdot 1.666 \approx 51.1 \; W$$

**Step 6 — Power Loss and Efficiency Ratio.**

$$P_{\text{loss}} = 300 - 51.1 = 248.9 \; W$$

$$\eta_{\text{shade}} = \frac{51.1}{300} \times 100\% \approx 17.0\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This calculation shows that **only 10% cell-area shading** can cause a power drop to **~17%** of rated value. This is the famous "non-linear shading loss" and is heavily tested in KTU papers.

### 3.2 Derivation B — Minimum Inter-Row Spacing (No-Shadow Geometry)

**Problem Setup:**
A solar farm is being installed at a location in Kerala with latitude $\phi = 10^{\circ}$ N. Each module has length $L = 1.65$ m and is tilted at $\beta = 15^{\circ}$ from horizontal. Determine the minimum row-to-row spacing $D_{\text{min}}$ such that no row-to-row shading occurs at solar noon on the **winter solstice** (worst-case design day).

**Step 1 — Identify the Critical Solar Altitude.**

The solar declination on the winter solstice is $\delta = -23.45^{\circ}$. At solar noon, the solar altitude is:

$$\alpha_{\text{ws}} = 90^{\circ} - \phi + \delta = 90^{\circ} - 10^{\circ} + (-23.45^{\circ})$$

$$\alpha_{\text{ws}} = 56.55^{\circ}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Even at latitude 10° N (Kerala), the sun is well above the horizon at noon in winter, so panels facing south (true south, with $\gamma = 0^{\circ}$) still receive direct beam radiation.

**Step 2 — Compute Vertical Height of the Tilted Module.**

The top edge of the module sits at a vertical height above the bottom edge of:

$$H = L \cdot \sin\beta = 1.65 \cdot \sin(15^{\circ})$$

$$H = 1.65 \cdot 0.2588 = 0.4270 \; m$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Shadow Length on the Ground.**

When the sun is at altitude $\alpha_{\text{ws}}$, an object of height $H$ casts a shadow of length:

$$S = \frac{H}{\tan\alpha_{\text{ws}}} = \frac{0.4270}{\tan(56.55^{\circ})}$$

$$S = \frac{0.4270}{1.5141} = 0.2820 \; m$$

**Step 4 — Set Minimum Row Spacing.**

To ensure the shadow of row $n$ does not fall on row $n+1$ at winter noon:

$$D_{\text{min}} = S = 0.2820 \; m$$

**Step 5 — Verify with the General Formula.**

Using the consolidated formula:

$$D_{\text{min}} = \frac{L \cdot \sin\beta}{\tan\alpha_{\text{ws}}} = \frac{1.65 \cdot \sin(15^{\circ})}{\tan(56.55^{\circ})} = 0.282 \; m$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In practice, designers use $D = 1.5 \cdot S$ to $D = 2.0 \cdot S$ to allow for **diffuse shadow penumbra** and **early-morning/late-afternoon shading**. So the practical $D \approx 0.42$ m to $0.56$ m, often rounded up.

### 3.3 Derivation C — Bypass Diode Current Equation

**Problem Setup:**
A series string of $N = 20$ cells is delivering a current $I_{\text{string}}$ to the load. One cell is shaded. The shaded cell can sustain a maximum reverse current of $I_{\text{rv,max}} = -2$ A (negative sign denotes reverse). The bypass diode has a forward drop $V_D = 0.4$ V (Schottky). At what string current does the bypass diode activate?

**Step 1 — Write the KVL Around the Substring Loop.**

When the bypass diode is OFF, the current $I$ flows through all 20 cells. The voltage across the shaded cell $V_{sh}$ is given by the cell's I-V curve evaluated at current $I$:

$$V_{sh} = V_{oc} \cdot \left[ 1 - \frac{I_{ph,sh}}{I_{ph,un}} \right] - I \cdot R_s$$

For shading ratio $k = G_s/G_u = 0.2$, the shaded cell's photo-current drops to 20% of the unshaded value. Setting the unshaded cell photo-current to $I_{ph,un}$:

$$V_{sh} \approx V_{oc} \cdot (1 - 0.2) - I \cdot R_s = 0.8 V_{oc} - I R_s$$

**Step 2 — Activation Condition.**

The bypass diode turns ON when the voltage across the substring containing the shaded cell becomes negative, i.e.:

$$V_{sh} \leq -V_D \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.8 V_{oc} - I R_s \leq -0.4$$

Solving for the activation current:

$$I_{\text{activate}} = \frac{0.8 V_{oc} + 0.4}{R_s}$$

For $V_{oc} = 0.65$ V (single Si cell) and $R_s = 0.01 \; \Omega$:

$$I_{\text{activate}} = \frac{0.8 \cdot 0.65 + 0.4}{0.01} = \frac{0.92}{0.01} = 92 \; A$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is well above the operating current, confirming that the bypass diode activates in practice for typical $I_{\text{mpp}} \approx 8$ A. The shaded cell's voltage simply clamps to a small negative value, and the diode begins to conduct almost any amount of reverse current safely.

**Step 3 — Diode Current.**

Once activated, the bypass diode current is:

$$I_{D} = I_{\text{string}} - I_{\text{shaded,eq}}$$

The current diverted is what would have been dissipated as heat in the shaded cell, protecting it from the hot-spot phenomenon.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — PV Module Internal Architecture with Bypass Diodes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Solar Cell 1]:::cell --> nodeB[Solar Cell 2]:::cell
    nodeB --> nodeC[Solar Cell 3]:::cell
    nodeC --> nodeD[Series Substring A 20 cells]:::substring
    nodeD -.-> nodeBP1[Bypass Diode 1 Schottky]:::bypass
    nodeBP1 --> nodeJ[Substring B 20 cells]:::substring
    nodeJ -.-> nodeBP2[Bypass Diode 2 Schottky]:::bypass
    nodeBP2 --> nodeK[Substring C 20 cells]:::substring
    nodeK -.-> nodeBP3[Bypass Diode 3 Schottky]:::bypass
    nodeBP3 --> nodeL[Output Terminal Positive]:::term
    nodeA2[Negative Terminal]:::term --- nodeD
    nodeS[Shaded Object Tree or Pole]:::shade -.Shadow.-> nodeC

    classDef cell fill:#ffe680,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef substring fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bypass fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef term fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef shade fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Shading Effects Pathway (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    nodeB --> nodeC[Series String Forced Equal Current]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Any Cell Shaded?}
    nodeD -- No --> nodeE[Normal Operation at MPP]
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeF[Shaded Cell Goes into Reverse Bias]
    nodeF --> nodeG{Vsub less than minus VD}
    nodeG -- No --> nodeH[Minor Mismatch Power Loss]
    nodeG -- Yes --> nodeI[Bypass Diode Activates]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Substring Voltage Lost approx 0.4 to 0.7 V]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Multiple Local Maxima on P-V Curve]
    nodeK --> nodeL[MPPT Searches for Global Maximum]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Reduced Array Power Output]

    nodeN --> nodeO[Cell Temperature above 100C]
    nodeO --> nodeP[Encapsulant Delamination and Burn Marks]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define shading in a solar PV system. Differentiate between hard shading and soft shading with two examples each.*

**Model Answer:**

> Shading in a solar PV system refers to the partial or complete obstruction of solar irradiance reaching the active surface of a PV module, leading to a reduction in the photo-generated current of the affected cells.

**Hard shading** is caused by well-defined opaque objects and produces sharp-edged shadows.
- *Examples*: (1) Shadow of a neighbouring building, (2) Shadow of a tree trunk or utility pole.

**Soft shading** is caused by diffuse atmospheric or surface phenomena and produces gradual irradiance reduction.
- *Examples*: (1) Cloud cover and haze, (2) Uniform dust deposition on the module glass.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is the hot-spot effect? Explain how a bypass diode prevents it.*

**Model Answer:**

> The hot-spot effect is the localized overheating of a shaded PV cell in a series string. When one cell is shaded, the string current (forced equal) exceeds the shaded cell's photo-current, driving it into reverse bias. The illuminated cells then dissipate their generated power as heat inside the shaded cell, raising its temperature above 100 °C and causing permanent damage such as encapsulant delamination and solder-bond failure.

> A bypass diode is connected in anti-parallel across a group of 20–24 cells. When the substring voltage reverses (becomes negative), the diode becomes forward-biased at ~0.4 V (Schottky) and diverts the string current around the shaded substring. This protects the cell from overheating, at the cost of losing that substring's contribution to module voltage (about 1/3 of $V_{oc}$ for a 3-diode 60-cell module).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Internal Choice Instruction:** *Answer either Question A OR Question B.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the I-V characteristic curve of a PV module under uniform irradiance and under partial shading conditions. Discuss the role of bypass diodes.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 60-cell crystalline silicon module is rated 320 W at STC ($V_{oc} = 45.6$ V, $I_{sc} = 9.6$ A, $V_{mpp} = 36$ V, $I_{mpp} = 8.89$ A). When 4 cells in one substring are uniformly shaded to 250 W/m² while the rest of the module receives 1000 W/m², calculate: (i) the shading factor, (ii) the effective irradiance, and (iii) the approximate power output. Assume the bypass diode drop is 0.7 V.

**Model Solution to (a):**

The I-V curve under **uniform irradiance** is a smooth curve from $I_{sc}$ (on the current axis) to $V_{oc}$ (on the voltage axis) with a single "knee" indicating the Maximum Power Point (MPP).

Under **partial shading**, the I-V curve develops **multiple steps** (staircase shape). Each step corresponds to a substring being progressively bypassed. The P-V curve correspondingly develops **multiple local maxima**, with only one being the **Global MPP (GMPP)**. The job of the MPPT controller is to detect the GMPP and operate at that point.

**Bypass diodes** are anti-parallel diodes (one per 20–24 cells) that turn ON when the substring voltage reverses. They eliminate the hot-spot risk at the cost of a small step in the I-V curve. **Three bypass diodes** in a 60-cell module yield a 3-step I-V curve under partial shading.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**(i) Shading Factor:**

$$\text{SF} = \frac{n_s}{N_{\text{total}}} = \frac{4}{60} = 0.0667 \; (6.67\%)$$

**(ii) Effective Irradiance (Area-Weighted):**

$$G_{\text{eff}} = G_u (1 - \text{SF}) + G_s \cdot \text{SF}$$

$$G_{\text{eff}} = 1000 \cdot 0.9333 + 250 \cdot 0.0667 = 933.3 + 16.7 = 950 \; W/m^2$$

**(iii) Approximate Power Output (with Bypass Diode Action):**

Since 4 cells in one substring are shaded, the bypass diode for that substring activates. The current is now limited by the shaded cells:

$$I_{\text{shaded}} = I_{\text{sc}} \cdot \frac{G_s}{G_{\text{STC}}} = 9.6 \cdot \frac{250}{1000} = 2.4 \; A$$

The module voltage drops by one substring's contribution plus the diode drop:

$$V_{\text{module}} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot V_{\text{mpp}} + V_D = \frac{2}{3} \cdot 36 + 0.7 = 24 + 0.7 = 24.7 \; V$$

**Power Output:**

$$P_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{module}} \cdot I_{\text{shaded}} = 24.7 \cdot 2.4 = 59.28 \; W$$

> This is only **18.5%** of the rated 320 W — demonstrating the disproportionate impact of even small shading.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for minimum inter-row spacing $D_{\text{min}}$ between two adjacent rows of tilted PV modules to avoid row-to-row shading at solar noon on the winter solstice. State clearly the assumptions used.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 1 MW solar farm is proposed at a site in Tamil Nadu (latitude 11° N). Each module is 1.7 m long, 1.0 m wide, tilted at 12° facing true south. Calculate: (i) the minimum row-to-row spacing to avoid shading at winter solstice noon, and (ii) the Ground Coverage Ratio (GCR).

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Assumptions:**

1. The sun's path on the winter solstice is the worst-case (lowest noon altitude).
2. Modules are oriented due south (azimuth $\gamma = 0^{\circ}$).
3. Module rows are infinitely long in the east–west direction.
4. The shadow geometry is two-dimensional (no end effects).

**Derivation:**

Let $L$ = module length (along the slope), $\beta$ = tilt angle, $H$ = vertical height of the top edge of the module above ground:

$$H = L \cdot \sin\beta$$

The solar altitude at winter solstice noon at latitude $\phi$ is:

$$\alpha_{\text{ws}} = 90^{\circ} - \phi + \delta_{\text{ws}}$$

where $\delta_{\text{ws}} = -23.45^{\circ}$, giving:

$$\alpha_{\text{ws}} = 66.55^{\circ} - \phi$$

An object of height $H$ casts a shadow of length $S$ on level ground:

$$S = \frac{H}{\tan\alpha_{\text{ws}}}$$

To prevent shadow of row $n$ from falling on row $n+1$, the inter-row spacing must be at least equal to $S$:

$$\boxed{D_{\text{min}} = \frac{L \cdot \sin\beta}{\tan\alpha_{\text{ws}}}}$$

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $L = 1.7$ m, $\beta = 12^{\circ}$, $\phi = 11^{\circ}$ N.

**(i) Minimum Row Spacing:**

Solar altitude at winter solstice noon:

$$\alpha_{\text{ws}} = 90^{\circ} - 11^{\circ} + (-23.45^{\circ}) = 55.55^{\circ}$$

Height of module top:

$$H = L \cdot \sin\beta = 1.7 \cdot \sin(12^{\circ}) = 1.7 \cdot 0.2079 = 0.3534 \; m$$

Minimum spacing:

$$D_{\text{min}} = \frac{H}{\tan\alpha_{\text{ws}}} = \frac{0.3534}{\tan(55.55^{\circ})} = \frac{0.3534}{1.4592} = 0.2422 \; m$$

**Recommended practical spacing** (with safety factor of 1.5 for diffuse penumbra):

$$D_{\text{practical}} = 1.5 \cdot 0.2422 = 0.3633 \; m$$

**(ii) Ground Coverage Ratio (GCR):**

The GCR is defined as the ratio of module area to ground area occupied:

$$\text{GCR} = \frac{L}{D_{\text{min}}} = \frac{1.7}{0.2422} = 7.02$$

> This GCR > 1 is non-physical, indicating that at latitude 11° N with a 12° tilt, the rows can theoretically be very close without shading at noon. In practice, **GCR is capped at ~0.6–0.7** to allow for diffuse shading, O&M access roads, and morning/evening shadows.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Pitfalls (KTU Examiner Observations):**
> 1. **Sign Error in Solar Declination** — Always use $\delta = -23.45^{\circ}$ for the winter solstice, not $+23.45^{\circ}$.
> 2. **Confusing Module Length and Width** — Length is along the slope (north-south for south-facing arrays), width is the lateral dimension.
> 3. **Forgetting Bypass Diode Drop** — Always subtract 0.4–0.7 V per activated bypass diode from the module voltage.
> 4. **Naive Linear Power Scaling** — Never simply multiply $P_{\text{rated}}$ by $\text{GCR}$ ratio; always account for the substring-bypass mechanism.
> 5. **Latitude Confusion in Kerala** — Kerala is at 8°–12° N, so the sun is to the *north* of the modules at noon during parts of the year — a frequent source of mark loss.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Shading = any obstruction of solar irradiance on a PV cell.
- **Two Major Types:** Hard (sharp-edged, structural) vs Soft (diffuse, atmospheric).
- **The Killer Effect:** Hot-spot — caused by reverse-biased shaded cells dissipating power as heat.
- **Bypass Diodes:** Standard protection in modern modules; one per 20–24 cells.
- **Trade-off:** Bypass diodes save the cell but cost a substring of voltage (≈ 1/3 of $V_{oc}$ per bypassed substring).
- **I-V Curve Signature:** Partial shading produces a **staircase I-V curve** and a **multi-peak P-V curve** — the GMPPT challenge.
- **Shading Factor Formula:** $\text{SF} = A_{\text{shaded}} / A_{\text{total}}$.
- **Effective Irradiance (Linear):** $G_{\text{eff}} = G_u (1 - \text{SF}) + G_s \cdot \text{SF}$.
- **Real Power Loss:** Disproportionate to SF — even 1% area shading can drop power by 30–70%.
- **Inter-Row Spacing Formula:** $D_{\text{min}} = L \sin\beta / \tan\alpha_{\text{ws}}$, with $\alpha_{\text{ws}} = 90^{\circ} - \phi - 23.45^{\circ}$ (note sign).
- **Mitigation Hierarchy:** Site design → Module design → Power electronics (MLPE, optimizers, microinverters).
- **Kerala-Specific Note:** Latitude < 23.45° means the sun crosses the zenith twice a year; shading analysis must consider the full annual sun-path, not just winter solstice.
- **KTU Magic Numbers to Memorize:** $G_{\text{STC}} = 1000$ W/m², $T_c = 25$ °C, $\delta_{\text{ws}} = -23.45^{\circ}$, Schottky $V_D \approx 0.4$ V, Si $V_D \approx 0.7$ V.
- **Engineering Best Practice:** Always include a **safety factor of 1.5×–2×** for inter-row spacing to account for diffuse shadows and O&M access.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sun-Path Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET617 - SOLAR ENERGY CONSERVATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sun-Path Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sun-Path Diagram (KTU Syllabus Definition)**
> A **Sun-Path Diagram** is a two-dimensional graphical representation (typically constructed using a **stereographic projection** of the celestial hemisphere onto a flat plane) that traces the apparent trajectory of the Sun across the sky throughout a typical day and across different seasons of the year, for a specific geographical location defined by its **latitude (φ)**.

The diagram simultaneously plots the **Solar Altitude Angle (α)** along concentric circular contours and the **Solar Azimuth Angle (γ)** along radial angular lines, measured from true North. It serves as the fundamental design tool in solar energy engineering for determining the optimal orientation, tilt, and shading requirements of solar collectors, photovoltaic (PV) modules, and passive solar building architectures.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Invisible Dome Clock"**
> Imagine you are standing in the middle of a perfectly transparent **glass dome** (the celestial hemisphere). The Sun is a small light bulb attached to the inside surface of this dome, and the dome itself slowly rotates overhead throughout the day. Now, if you could **flatten the dome onto the floor** like a paper craft, the curved path the light bulb traces on the glass becomes a series of **arched curves** on the flat paper. This flattened paper is exactly what a **Sun-Path Diagram** is!

- During **summer** (in the Northern Hemisphere), the bulb is high overhead → small, tight curves near the center.
- During **winter**, the bulb stays close to the horizon → large, sweeping curves near the outer edge.
- The curves on the flattened paper (sun-path diagram) allow an engineer to **read the Sun's position at any hour of any day** at a glance, much like reading a clock face.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Standard Values to Memorize for KTU Exams**
> - **Solar Constant (G\_sc)**: $\mathbf{1367 \text{ W/m}^2}$ (also accepted: **1361 W/m²** as per newer WMO standards)
> - **Axial Tilt of Earth (Obliquity, ε)**: $\mathbf{23.45^\circ}$
> - **Earth's Rotation Rate**: $\mathbf{15^\circ \text{ per hour}}$ (this converts hour angle directly)
> - **Mean Earth-Sun Distance**: $\mathbf{1.496 \times 10^{11} \text{ m}}$ (1 Astronomical Unit)
> - **Total Atmospheric Path at Zenith (Air Mass Zero baseline)**: $\mathbf{1.013 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}}$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Foundational Coordinate Parameters

To fully specify the Sun's position in the local sky, three primary angular coordinates must be determined:

#### 2.1.1 Solar Declination Angle (δ)
- **Definition**: The angle between the line joining the centres of the Sun and Earth, and the projection of this line on the equatorial plane.
- **Range**: $-\mathbf{23.45^\circ} \le \delta \le +\mathbf{23.45^\circ}$
- **Why it exists**: Because the Earth's rotational axis is tilted at $\mathbf{23.45^\circ}$ relative to the orbital plane (ecliptic).
- **Key markers**:
  * $\delta = +23.45^\circ$ → **Summer Solstice (June 21)** in Northern Hemisphere
  * $\delta = 0^\circ$ → **Equinoxes** (March 21 & September 23)
  * $\delta = -23.45^\circ$ → **Winter Solstice (December 22)**

#### 2.1.2 Hour Angle (ω)
- **Definition**: The angular displacement of the Sun from the local meridian due to Earth's rotation.
- **Convention**: Negative **before** solar noon, zero **at** solar noon, positive **after** solar noon.
- **Linear in time**: $\omega = 15^\circ \times (t_{solar} - 12)$

#### 2.1.3 Latitude (φ)
- **Definition**: The angular position of the observer North (+) or South (−) of the Equator.
- **Range**: $-90^\circ \le \phi \le +90^\circ$

### 2.2 Derived Sky Coordinates

#### 2.2.1 Solar Altitude Angle (α)
- **Definition**: The angle of the Sun **above** the local horizon plane.
- **Range**: $0^\circ \le \alpha \le 90^\circ$

#### 2.2.2 Solar Zenith Angle (θz)
- **Definition**: The angle between the vertical (zenith) and the Sun's position.
- **Simple complement**: $\theta_z = 90^\circ - \alpha$

#### 2.2.3 Solar Azimuth Angle (γ)
- **Definition**: The compass bearing of the Sun's projection on the horizon, measured from **true North** (or sometimes true South, depending on convention — KTU follows **North-referenced convention**).
- **Range**: $0^\circ \le \gamma \le 360^\circ$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Master Table — Memorize These for KTU ESE**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Range / Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Declination Angle (Cooper's Equation) | $\delta = 23.45 \sin\left[\dfrac{360}{365}(n + 284)\right]$ | $-23.45^\circ \le \delta \le +23.45^\circ$ | $n$ = day of year (1 = Jan 1) |
| 2 | Hour Angle | $\omega = 15^\circ (t_{solar} - 12)$ | $-180^\circ \le \omega \le +180^\circ$ | $t_{solar}$ in decimal hours |
| 3 | Altitude Angle | $\sin \alpha = \sin \phi \sin \delta + \cos \phi \cos \delta \cos \omega$ | $0^\circ \le \alpha \le 90^\circ$ | Valid for any location |
| 4 | Azimuth Angle | $\cos \gamma = \dfrac{\sin \alpha \sin \phi - \sin \delta}{\cos \alpha \cos \phi}$ | $0^\circ \le \gamma \le 360^\circ$ | N-referenced, measured clockwise |
| 5 | Zenith Angle | $\theta_z = 90^\circ - \alpha$ | $0^\circ \le \theta_z \le 90^\circ$ | Direct complement |
| 6 | Air Mass (Kasten-Young) | $AM = \dfrac{1}{\cos \theta_z + 0.50572(96.07995 - \theta_z)^{-1.6364}}$ | $AM \ge 1$ | $\theta_z$ in degrees |
| 7 | Equation of Time (Spencer) | $E_o = 229.18 [0.000075 + 0.001868\cos B - 0.032077\sin B - 0.014615\cos 2B - 0.04089\sin 2B]$ | minutes | $B = \dfrac{360}{365}(n - 81)$ in degrees |
| 8 | Local Solar Time | $LST = LMT + E_o + 4(L_{st} - L_{loc})$ | decimal hours | $L_{st}$, $L_{loc}$ in degrees longitude |
| 9 | Sunrise/Sunset Hour Angle | $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan \phi \tan \delta)$ | $-180^\circ \le \omega_s \le +180^\circ$ | Day length: $N = \dfrac{2}{15} \omega_s$ hours |
| 10 | Direction Cosine (Zenith) | $\cos \theta_z = \sin \phi \sin \delta + \cos \phi \cos \delta \cos \omega$ | dimensionless | Identical to $\sin \alpha$ |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in Real-World Solar Design

> [!NOTE]
> **Where These Formulas Are Used Professionally**
> - **PV System Design**: Determining optimal tilt angle = $\phi \pm 15^\circ$ (seasonal optimization) and azimuth = true South (Northern Hemisphere) for maximum annual yield.
> - **Concentrated Solar Power (CSP)**: Aligning heliostats toward the sun-path's annual centroid.
> - **Building Energy Modeling**: Passive solar window sizing requires hourly $\alpha$ and $\gamma$ to compute incident radiation on vertical façades.
> - **Solar Tracker Control Algorithms**: Real-time dual-axis trackers compute $(\alpha, \gamma)$ every minute to position panels.
> - **Shading Analysis**: Architects overlay the sun-path diagram on site plans to identify seasonal shadows from neighboring buildings or trees.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Altitude Angle Equation

> [!NOTE]
> **The Spherical Trigonometry Behind the Sky Coordinates**

Consider the **celestial sphere** centered at the observer. Three great circles intersect to form a spherical triangle:

1. **The Horizon Circle** (passing through zenith, nadir, North, South, East, West).
2. **The Hour Circle** (passing through the celestial pole and the Sun).
3. **The Equator Projection** (passing through the celestial poles and East-West).

By applying the **cosine rule of spherical trigonometry** to the spherical triangle formed by the **zenith**, the **celestial pole**, and the **Sun's position**:

$$\cos(90^\circ - \alpha) = \cos(90^\circ - \phi)\cos(90^\circ - \delta) + \sin(90^\circ - \phi)\sin(90^\circ - \delta)\cos \omega$$

Since $\cos(90^\circ - x) = \sin(x)$ and $\sin(90^\circ - x) = \cos(x)$, we simplify term by term:

**Step 1**: Replace the LHS cosine term.
$$\sin \alpha = \cos(90^\circ - \phi)\cos(90^\circ - \delta) + \sin(90^\circ - \phi)\sin(90^\circ - \delta)\cos \omega$$

**Step 2**: Convert each $90^\circ - x$ using the identity.
$$\sin \alpha = \sin \phi \sin \delta + \cos \phi \cos \delta \cos \omega$$

**Final Compact Form**:
$$\boxed{\sin \alpha = \sin \phi \sin \delta + \cos \phi \cos \delta \cos \omega}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Azimuth Angle Equation

We begin with the same spherical triangle, but now apply the **sine rule** combined with the cosine rule along a different edge.

**Step 1**: Apply the cosine rule to the edge connecting the pole to the Sun.

$$\cos(90^\circ - \delta) = \cos(90^\circ - \phi)\cos(90^\circ - \alpha) + \sin(90^\circ - \phi)\sin(90^\circ - \alpha)\cos \gamma$$

**Step 2**: Apply the trigonometric identities.
$$\sin \delta = \sin \phi \sin \alpha + \cos \phi \cos \alpha \cos \gamma$$

**Step 3**: Isolate the $\cos \gamma$ term.
$$\cos \phi \cos \alpha \cos \gamma = \sin \delta - \sin \phi \sin \alpha$$

**Step 4**: Solve explicitly for $\cos \gamma$.
$$\boxed{\cos \gamma = \frac{\sin \delta - \sin \phi \sin \alpha}{\cos \phi \cos \alpha}}$$

This can also be rearranged using $\sin \alpha$ substitution to obtain an **azimuth formula independent of altitude**:
$$\boxed{\sin \gamma = \frac{-\cos \delta \sin \omega}{\cos \alpha}}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (Modeled on KTU Past Exam Style)**
> Calculate the **Solar Declination (δ)**, **Hour Angle (ω)**, **Solar Altitude (α)**, and **Solar Azimuth (γ)** for a location at latitude $\phi = 13^\circ \text{ N}$ (Trivandrum, Kerala) on **March 15** at **2:30 PM Indian Standard Time (IST)**. The standard meridian for IST is $82.5^\circ \text{ E}$, and Trivandrum's longitude is $76.95^\circ \text{ E}$.

#### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step A: Determine the Day Number (n)**
March 15 corresponds to $n = 31 (\text{Jan}) + 28 (\text{Feb}) + 15 = 74$.

**Step B: Compute Declination Angle using Cooper's Equation**

$$\delta = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(n + 284)\right]$$

Substituting $n = 74$:
$$\delta = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(74 + 284)\right] = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(358)\right]$$

$$\delta = 23.45 \sin\left[353.09^\circ\right]$$

$$\delta = 23.45 \times (-0.1219) = -2.86^\circ$$

The negative value confirms we are slightly past the **Vernal Equinox** — the Sun is moving northward but still slightly south of the equator.

**Step C: Compute the Equation of Time (EoT)**

First, compute the auxiliary angle $B$:
$$B = \frac{360}{365}(n - 81) = \frac{360}{365}(74 - 81) = \frac{360 \times (-7)}{365} = -6.904^\circ$$

Apply Spencer's Equation of Time formula:
$$E_o = 229.18 [0.000075 + 0.001868\cos(-6.904^\circ) - 0.032077\sin(-6.904^\circ) - 0.014615\cos(2 \times -6.904^\circ) - 0.04089\sin(2 \times -6.904^\circ)]$$

Evaluating each term:

$$E_o = 229.18 [0.000075 + 0.001854 - 0.003855 - 0.014579 + 0.009815]$$

$$E_o = 229.18 \times (-0.00669) = -9.21 \text{ minutes}$$

The negative EoT indicates the **mean Sun is ahead of the true Sun** — i.e., the true solar noon occurs later than mean solar noon on this date.

**Step D: Compute Local Solar Time (LST)**

The longitude correction is:
$$4 \times (L_{st} - L_{loc}) = 4 \times (82.5 - 76.95) = 4 \times 5.55 = +22.2 \text{ minutes}$$

Local Mean Time (LMT) at 2:30 PM IST = 14:30 hours.
$$LST = 14:30 + E_o + 22.2 \text{ min} = 14:30 - 9.21 + 22.2 = 14:42.99 \approx 14:43 \text{ hours}$$

In decimal hours: $t_{solar} = 14 + 43/60 = 14.717 \text{ hours}$.

**Step E: Compute Hour Angle (ω)**

$$\omega = 15^\circ \times (t_{solar} - 12) = 15^\circ \times (14.717 - 12) = 15^\circ \times 2.717 = 40.76^\circ$$

The positive value confirms it is **afternoon** (Sun past meridian).

**Step F: Compute Solar Altitude (α)**

$$\sin \alpha = \sin(13^\circ)\sin(-2.86^\circ) + \cos(13^\circ)\cos(-2.86^\circ)\cos(40.76^\circ)$$

$$\sin \alpha = (0.2250)(-0.0499) + (0.9744)(0.9988)(0.7576)$$

$$\sin \alpha = -0.01123 + 0.7373 = 0.7261$$

$$\alpha = \sin^{-1}(0.7261) = 46.59^\circ$$

**Step G: Compute Solar Azimuth (γ)**

$$\cos \gamma = \frac{\sin \delta - \sin \phi \sin \alpha}{\cos \phi \cos \alpha}$$

$$\cos \gamma = \frac{\sin(-2.86^\circ) - \sin(13^\circ)\sin(46.59^\circ)}{\cos(13^\circ)\cos(46.59^\circ)}$$

$$\cos \gamma = \frac{-0.0499 - (0.2250)(0.7261)}{(0.9744)(0.6874)}$$

$$\cos \gamma = \frac{-0.0499 - 0.1634}{0.6699} = \frac{-0.2133}{0.6699} = -0.3184$$

$$\gamma = \cos^{-1}(-0.3184) = 108.56^\circ \text{ from North (clockwise)}$$

Since it is afternoon (ω > 0), the Sun is in the **western half** of the sky. Verifying: $180^\circ < \gamma < 360^\circ$ corresponds to West side, so we use $360^\circ - 108.56^\circ = 251.44^\circ$ when measured from North with West = 270° reference. Actually, the convention resolves this as:

For KTU North-referenced clockwise convention with afternoon:
$$\gamma = 360^\circ - 108.56^\circ = 251.44^\circ$$

**Final Consolidated Results Table**:

| Parameter | Symbol | Computed Value |
|-----------|--------|----------------|
| Day Number | $n$ | 74 |
| Declination | $\delta$ | $-2.86^\circ$ |
| Equation of Time | $E_o$ | $-9.21 \text{ min}$ |
| Local Solar Time | $LST$ | $14:43 \text{ hours}$ |
| Hour Angle | $\omega$ | $40.76^\circ$ |
| Solar Altitude | $\alpha$ | $46.59^\circ$ |
| Solar Azimuth | $\gamma$ | $251.44^\circ$ (from N, CW) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Sun-Path Diagram Construction

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide**: Since a physical stereographic projection plot is a 2D spatial object, the Mermaid diagram below represents the **functional pipeline** an engineer follows to construct and interpret a sun-path diagram. This maps every interaction step rather than attempting a literal physical drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Site Latitude phi] --> B[Compute Declination Angle delta for Key Days]
    B --> B1[Summer Solstice n equals 172]
    B --> B2[Equinoxes n equals 81 and n equals 264]
    B --> B3[Winter Solstice n equals 355]
    B1 --> C[Compute Hour Angle omega Sweep from -180 to +180 in 15 deg Steps]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[For Each Day Calculate Solar Altitude alpha]
    D --> E[For Each Day Calculate Solar Azimuth gamma]
    E --> F[Apply Stereographic Projection Transform]
    F --> F1[Radial Distance r equals 90 minus alpha]
    F --> F2[Polar Angle equals gamma measured clockwise from North]
    F1 --> G[Plot Concentric Altitude Circles 0 30 60 90 deg]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Draw Cardinal Directions N E S W as Cross]
    H --> I[Plot Hour Lines at 7 AM 9 AM 12 PM 3 PM 5 PM]
    I --> J[Connect Sun Position Points to Form Day Curves]
    J --> K[Annotate Final Sun-Path Diagram]
    K --> K1[Ready for Collector Orientation Analysis]
    K --> K2[Ready for Shading Mask Calculation]
    K --> K3[Ready for Building Daylighting Design]
```

### 4.2 Hierarchical Concept Map of Solar Geometry Variables

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Solar Geometry Master Variables] --> A[Independent Variables]
    ROOT --> B[Dependent Sky Angles]
    ROOT --> C[Time Conversion Variables]
    A --> A1[Latitude phi]
    A --> A2[Day Number n]
    A --> A3[Declination delta]
    A --> A4[Hour Angle omega]
    B --> B1[Solar Altitude alpha]
    B --> B2[Solar Azimuth gamma]
    B --> B3[Zenith Angle theta z]
    B --> B4[Air Mass AM]
    C --> C1[Equation of Time Eo]
    C --> C2[Local Solar Time LST]
    C --> C3[Local Mean Time LMT]
    A1 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    A4 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    A2 --> A3
    C2 --> A4
    C1 --> C2
    C3 --> C2
```

### 4.3 Sun-Path Curve Interpretation Matrix

| Curve on Diagram | Day of Year | Declination δ | Shape Characteristic | Engineering Implication |
|------------------|-------------|---------------|----------------------|-------------------------|
| **Outer Curve** | Winter Solstice (Dec 22) | $-23.45^\circ$ | Low, wide arc hugging horizon | Worst-case for shadow length & winter heating load |
| **Middle Curves** | Equinoxes (Mar 21 & Sep 23) | $0^\circ$ | Symmetric arc passing through East and West | Reference for designing year-round balanced solar systems |
| **Inner Curve** | Summer Solstice (Jun 21) | $+23.45^\circ$ | High, tight arc near zenith | Maximum solar gain; deep overhangs may fully shade south windows |
| **Hour Lines** | All days | Varies | Radial lines at $15^\circ$ spacing | Track instantaneous Sun position for tracker programming |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define the Solar Hour Angle. How is it related to Solar Time?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
The **Solar Hour Angle (ω)** is defined as the angular displacement of the Sun from the local meridian of the observer, caused by the Earth's rotation about its axis at a rate of **15° per hour**. Mathematically,

$$\omega = 15^\circ \times (t_{solar} - 12)$$

where $t_{solar}$ is the local solar time expressed in decimal hours. **The convention** adopted by KTU is: $\omega = 0^\circ$ at **solar noon** (Sun on meridian), negative values in the **morning** (before noon), and positive values in the **afternoon** (after noon). The total range during a single day spans from $-180^\circ$ at midnight to $+180^\circ$ at the next midnight.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Insight (1 Mark Breakdown)**: Definition → 1 Mark | Formula → 1 Mark | Convention → 1 Mark

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**What is a Sun-Path Diagram? List its two principal applications in solar energy engineering.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
A **Sun-Path Diagram** is a graphical plot constructed using a **stereographic projection** of the celestial hemisphere onto a flat horizontal plane, showing the trajectory of the Sun across the sky at a given latitude throughout the day and across seasons.

Two principal applications:
1. **Solar Collector Orientation & Tilt Design**: Engineers use the diagram to identify the optimal tilt angle (typically $\phi - 15^\circ$ for summer, $\phi + 15^\circ$ for winter, or $\phi$ for annual optimization) and azimuthal orientation (true South in Northern Hemisphere) of PV modules and solar thermal collectors to maximize incident radiation.
2. **Shading & Building Daylight Analysis**: Architects overlay site context (buildings, trees) on the diagram to evaluate seasonal shadow patterns and design passive solar fenestration, overhangs, and light shelves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Insight (1 Mark Breakdown)**: Definition → 1 Mark | Each application → 1 Mark each (total 3)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | OEM Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive the equation for the **Solar Altitude Angle (α)** in terms of latitude (φ), declination (δ), and hour angle (ω). Clearly state all assumptions and the trigonometric identities used. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A solar collector is to be installed at Kochi (latitude $\phi = 10^\circ \text{ N}$, longitude $76.3^\circ \text{ E}$) on **August 15**. Calculate the **declination angle**, **hour angle**, and **solar altitude** at exactly **11:00 AM Indian Standard Time (IST)**. Use the standard meridian $82.5^\circ \text{ E}$ and assume EoT = $-4.5$ minutes for that date. **[7 Marks]**

---

##### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]**:

**Step 1**: Construct the spherical triangle on the celestial sphere.
Three great circles intersect: the **horizon**, the **hour circle** of the Sun, and the **celestial equator**. The vertices are the **zenith (Z)**, the **celestial pole (P)**, and the **Sun's position (S)**. **[1 Mark]**

**Step 2**: Identify the side lengths of the spherical triangle.
- Arc from Z to P = $90^\circ - \phi$ (co-latitude)
- Arc from P to S = $90^\circ - \delta$ (co-declination)
- Arc from Z to S = $90^\circ - \alpha$ (co-altitude, i.e., zenith angle $\theta_z$)
- Angle at vertex P = $\omega$ (hour angle) **[1 Mark]**

**Step 3**: Apply the **cosine rule for spherical trigonometry**.

$$\cos(\text{arc ZS}) = \cos(\text{arc ZP})\cos(\text{arc PS}) + \sin(\text{arc ZP})\sin(\text{arc PS})\cos(\omega)$$

**[1 Mark]**

**Step 4**: Substitute the side lengths.

$$\cos(90^\circ - \alpha) = \cos(90^\circ - \phi)\cos(90^\circ - \delta) + \sin(90^\circ - \phi)\sin(90^\circ - \delta)\cos \omega$$

**[1 Mark]**

**Step 5**: Apply the complementary angle identities:
- $\cos(90^\circ - x) = \sin x$
- $\sin(90^\circ - x) = \cos x$

**[1 Mark]**

**Step 6**: Simplify to final form.

$$\boxed{\sin \alpha = \sin \phi \sin \delta + \cos \phi \cos \delta \cos \omega}$$

**[2 Marks]**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 Marks]**:

**Step 1**: Determine the day number $n$ for August 15.
$n = 31 + 28 + 31 + 30 + 31 + 30 + 31 + 15 = 227$. **[1 Mark]**

**Step 2**: Calculate the declination angle using Cooper's equation.

$$\delta = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(227 + 284)\right] = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(511)\right]$$

$$\delta = 23.45 \sin\left[503.84^\circ\right] = 23.45 \sin\left[503.84^\circ - 360^\circ\right] = 23.45 \sin\left[143.84^\circ\right]$$

$$\delta = 23.45 \times 0.5931 = +13.91^\circ$$

**[2 Marks]**

**Step 3**: Convert local clock time to local solar time.
Longitude correction = $4 \times (82.5 - 76.3) = 24.8 \text{ minutes}$.

$$LST = 11{:}00 + 24.8 + (-4.5) = 11{:}20.3 \text{ hours} \approx 11.338 \text{ hours}$$

**[1 Mark]**

**Step 4**: Calculate the hour angle.

$$\omega = 15^\circ \times (11.338 - 12) = 15^\circ \times (-0.662) = -9.93^\circ$$

**[1 Mark]**

**Step 5**: Compute the solar altitude.

$$\sin \alpha = \sin(10^\circ)\sin(13.91^\circ) + \cos(10^\circ)\cos(13.91^\circ)\cos(-9.93^\circ)$$

$$\sin \alpha = (0.1736)(0.2404) + (0.9848)(0.9707)(0.9851)$$

$$\sin \alpha = 0.04173 + 0.9416 = 0.9833$$

$$\alpha = \sin^{-1}(0.9833) = 79.49^\circ$$

**[2 Marks]**

**Final Answers**:
| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Declination $\delta$ | $+13.91^\circ$ |
| Hour Angle $\omega$ | $-9.93^\circ$ |
| Solar Altitude $\alpha$ | $79.49^\circ$ |

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | OEM Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches how a **Sun-Path Diagram** is constructed using the **stereographic projection** method. Discuss its **limitations** and **advantages**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For a location at latitude $\phi = 28.6^\circ \text{ N}$ (Delhi), calculate the **day length**, **sunrise hour angle**, and **solar noon altitude** on the **summer solstice** (June 21, $n = 172$). **[7 Marks]**

---

##### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Sun-Path Diagram Construction & Analysis [7 Marks]**:

**Construction Procedure**:
1. **Draw Concentric Circles** representing solar altitude from 0° (outer horizon) to 90° (zenith at center) at every 15° or 30°. **[1 Mark]**
2. **Mark Cardinal Directions**: North at top, East at right (90°), South at bottom (180°), and West at left (270°). **[1 Mark]**
3. **Compute declination δ** for the key days of interest using Cooper's equation. **[1 Mark]**
4. **For each selected day**, calculate hourly altitude (α) and azimuth (γ) values using the fundamental equations. **[1 Mark]**
5. **Convert to plot coordinates** using stereographic projection:
   - Radial distance from center: $r = (90^\circ - \alpha)$
   - Polar angle: equal to azimuth γ. **[1 Mark]**
6. **Connect plotted points** to form smooth day curves. Add hour ticks at 7 AM, 9 AM, 12 PM, 3 PM, 5 PM. **[1 Mark]**

**Advantages**: (any 2, 1/2 Mark each)
- Single visual reference for entire year-round solar geometry.
- Direct visual identification of seasonal shading & sun-exposure windows.
- Foundation for solar collector design and overhang depth calculations.

**Limitations**: (any 2, 1/2 Mark each)
- Latitude-specific — a new diagram needed for every location.
- Cannot represent cloud cover, atmospheric refraction, or horizon obstructions.
- Lacks quantitative radiation intensity data (only angular geometry).

**[1 Mark Total for Advantages + Limitations]**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 Marks]**:

**Step 1**: Day number for June 21.
$n = 31 + 28 + 31 + 30 + 21 = 141$. (Note: Some references use $n = 172$ — KTU accepts $n = 172$ if specified directly.)

**Step 2**: Calculate declination angle for June 21.
At summer solstice, $\delta = +23.45^\circ$ (this is by definition, no calculation needed). **[1 Mark]**

**Step 3**: Compute the sunrise hour angle.

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan \phi \tan \delta) = \cos^{-1}(-\tan 28.6^\circ \times \tan 23.45^\circ)$$

$$\tan(28.6^\circ) = 0.5446, \quad \tan(23.45^\circ) = 0.4335$$

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-0.5446 \times 0.4335) = \cos^{-1}(-0.2361) = 103.66^\circ$$

**[2 Marks]**

**Step 4**: Calculate day length.

$$N = \frac{2}{15} \times \omega_s = \frac{2 \times 103.66}{15} = 13.82 \text{ hours} \approx 13 \text{ hours } 49 \text{ minutes}$$

**[2 Marks]**

**Step 5**: Calculate solar noon altitude ($\omega = 0$).

$$\sin \alpha_{noon} = \sin \phi \sin \delta + \cos \phi \cos \delta \cos(0)$$

$$\sin \alpha_{noon} = \sin \phi \sin \delta + \cos \phi \cos \delta = \cos(\phi - \delta)$$

This is a useful simplification when $\omega = 0$:

$$\alpha_{noon} = \cos^{-1}[\cos(\phi - \delta)] = |\phi - \delta| = |28.6^\circ - 23.45^\circ| = 5.15^\circ \text{ from zenith}$$

Wait — this gives zenith angle. Altitude is:

$$\alpha_{noon} = 90^\circ - 5.15^\circ = 84.85^\circ$$

Alternatively using direct formula:

$$\sin \alpha_{noon} = (0.4787)(0.3979) + (0.8780)(0.9174)(1) = 0.1905 + 0.8055 = 0.9960$$

$$\alpha_{noon} = \sin^{-1}(0.9960) = 85.05^\circ$$

(Slight difference due to rounding in $\delta$ value.)

**[2 Marks]**

**Final Answers**:
| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Declination $\delta$ | $+23.45^\circ$ |
| Sunrise Hour Angle $\omega_s$ | $103.66^\circ$ |
| Day Length $N$ | $\mathbf{13.82 \text{ hours}}$ |
| Solar Noon Altitude $\alpha_{noon}$ | $\mathbf{85.05^\circ}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Sign Convention Error on Hour Angle**: Students often forget that $\omega$ is **negative in the morning** and **positive in the afternoon**. Carrying the wrong sign will flip your azimuth to the wrong half of the sky. Lose **2 marks** if your afternoon azimuth appears in the eastern quadrant.
> 2. **Forgetting to Convert Local Time to Solar Time**: Many students plug the **clock time directly** into the hour angle formula instead of first applying the EoT and longitude corrections. This introduces errors of up to 30 minutes in $\omega$, cascading into wrong $\alpha$ and $\gamma$ values.
> 3. **Declination Formula Off-By-One Day**: Cooper's equation $(n + 284)$ — students frequently write $(n - 81)$ which is actually the EoT auxiliary angle $B$. Mixing these up is a **3-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Declination on Solstice Without Justification**: If the problem says "summer solstice", you may **state $\delta = +23.45°$ directly** without invoking the formula. But if you do compute it, ensure your $n$ value matches the convention.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Know Before KTU Exam**

- ✅ **Solar Constant** = $\mathbf{1367 \text{ W/m}^2}$. Always quote this figure when extraterrestrial radiation questions appear.
- ✅ **Cooper's Equation** for declination: $\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin[360(n+284)/365]$. **Always** convert degrees in trigonometric functions.
- ✅ **Hour Angle**: $\omega = 15^\circ \times (t_{solar} - 12)$. Each hour past solar noon = **+15°**.
- ✅ **Altitude Equation**: $\sin \alpha = \sin \phi \sin \delta + \cos \phi \cos \delta \cos \omega$. This is the **workhorse** — memorize verbatim.
- ✅ **Azimuth Equation**: $\cos \gamma = (\sin \delta - \sin \phi \sin \alpha)/(\cos \phi \cos \alpha)$. North-referenced, clockwise positive.
- ✅ **Day Length Formula**: $N = 2\omega_s / 15$, where $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan \phi \tan \delta)$.
- ✅ **Sun-Path Diagram** uses **stereographic projection**: $r = 90 - \alpha$, plotted against $\gamma$. Inner curves = summer; outer curves = winter.
- ✅ **Local Solar Time Correction**: $LST = LMT + E_o + 4(L_{st} - L_{loc})$. Never skip the EoT term.
- ✅ **Earth's Axial Tilt**: $\mathbf{23.45^\circ}$ — the maximum range of declination, occurring at solstices.
- ✅ **Stereographic projection** maps the celestial hemisphere onto a flat disk, preserving circular symmetry about the zenith.
- ✅ **Solar Noon Altitude** simplifies to $\alpha_{noon} = 90^\circ - \mid\phi - \delta\mid$ for locations where the Sun transits the meridian on the same side as the observer's zenith.
- ✅ For **Kerala** (latitude ~10° N), the Sun is nearly overhead twice a year (April 15 & August 28), giving $\alpha > 80°$ routinely.
- ✅ **Direction conventions**: KTU uses **true North = 0°**, measured **clockwise**. East = 90°, South = 180°, West = 270°.
- ✅ **Atmospheric Air Mass** at sea level = $\sec(\theta_z)$ for $\theta_z < 60°$; use Kasten-Young formula for higher zenith angles.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overhangs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET617 - SOLAR ENERGY CONSERVATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overhangs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

An **overhang** is a fixed, opaque architectural projection (horizontal, vertical, or combined) extending outward from the external face of a building envelope, deliberately engineered to intercept direct-beam solar radiation before it strikes the glazing (window) or wall surface beneath it. In passive solar building design, overhangs function as a **passive shading device** that exploits the seasonal variation of solar altitude and azimuth angles to control solar heat gain — admitting low-angle winter sun for space heating and rejecting high-angle summer sun for space cooling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 — Passive Design):**
> Overhangs belong to the category of *fixed (stationary) external shading devices* and are the single most cost-effective method of reducing the **cooling load** of glazed façades in tropical and subtropical climates. They consume **zero operational energy**.

**Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**
- **Solar Constant (Iₛc)** = **1367 W/m²**
- **Latitude (φ)** — location dependent, measured in degrees
- **Solar declination (δ)** varies between **+23.45°** (Summer Solstice) and **−23.45°** (Winter Solstice)
- **Wall Solar Azimuth (γw)** = angle between the projection of sun's rays on a horizontal plane and the normal to the wall

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine standing in the afternoon sun wearing a **baseball cap (visor)**.

- In the **morning and evening**, when the sun is low on the horizon, the cap does NOT block your face — sunlight easily enters under the brim.
- At **noon**, when the sun is directly overhead, the cap casts a deep shadow over your entire face, protecting your eyes.

A building overhang behaves **identically**:

- **In summer (May–August)**, the sun rides high in the sky → the overhang casts a long shadow that covers the window → room stays cool, AC load drops.
- **In winter (November–February)**, the sun rides low → the overhang's shadow is short and falls above the window → warm sunlight enters the room → free heating.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Sun Visor" Principle:** The overhang is to a building exactly what the visor of a cap is to the human face — an elegantly simple, gravity-driven, zero-energy seasonal thermostat. The trick is sizing the projection correctly, which is purely a geometry + solar-astronomy problem.

---

### 1.3 Classification of Overhangs (KTU Expected)

| Type | Geometry | Best Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Horizontal Overhang** | Projects outward above a window | South-facing windows (N-hemisphere) |
| **Vertical Fin / Louver** | Projects sideways beside a window | East/West-facing windows |
| **Egg-Crate Overhang** | Combined horizontal + vertical grid | Glare control + uniform shading |
| **Recessed (Inset) Window** | Wall itself forms the shade | Deep walls (≥ 1 m) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Why and How

The design of an overhang rests on **three pillars of solar geometry**:

1. **Solar Altitude Angle (α)** — How *high* the sun is above the horizon at a given instant.
2. **Wall Solar Azimuth Angle (γw)** — How *perpendicular* the sun's rays are to the wall plane.
3. **Profile (Horizontal Shadow) Angle (Ω)** — The angle the sun's rays make with a plane perpendicular to the wall.

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**

- **Step 1 — Determine Design Day & Hour:** Identify the "worst-case cooling" day (typically **21st June** — Summer Solstice) and the critical hour (usually **solar noon ± 2 hours**, when the cooling load is peak).
- **Step 2 — Compute Solar Geometry:** For that date and hour at the given latitude, calculate α and γw.
- **Step 3 — Compute Profile Angle:** Convert (α, γw) into the **profile angle Ω**, which is the *effective* angle that an overhang actually "sees".
- **Step 4 — Sizing Rule:** A horizontal overhang of projection **P** mounted at height **H** above the window sill will cast a shadow of length **L = H / tan(Ω)** on the wall. To fully shade the window: **P ≥ L**.
- **Step 5 — Winter Check:** Verify that on the **Winter Solstice (21st December)**, the same overhang still allows sun penetration at noon — guaranteeing free winter heating.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MANDATORY READING FOR BOARD EXAM** — Memorize these seven equations. They cover ~85% of numerical questions on passive shading.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Solar Declination | $\delta = 23.45 \sin \left( \dfrac{360(284+n)}{365} \right)$ | n = day of year; δ in degrees |
| 2 | Hour Angle | $\omega = 15(t_{solar} - 12)$ | t in hours; ω in degrees |
| 3 | Solar Altitude | $\sin \alpha = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$ | α in degrees |
| 4 | Wall Solar Azimuth | $\cos\gamma_w = \dfrac{\sin\alpha \sin\phi - \sin\delta}{\cos\alpha \cos\phi}$ | γw in degrees |
| 5 | Profile Angle (Ω) | $\tan \Omega = \dfrac{\tan \alpha}{\cos \gamma_w}$ | Ω in degrees |
| 6 | Shadow Length on Wall | $L = \dfrac{H}{\tan \Omega}$ | L in metres |
| 7 | Required Overhang Projection | $P = H \times \tan \Omega$ | P in metres |

**Vertical Shadow Angle (for vertical fins):**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 8 | Vertical Shadow Angle (ν) | $\tan \nu = \tan \gamma_w \cdot \cos \alpha$ | ν in degrees |

**Symbols used throughout (KTU standard):**
- φ = Latitude of location
- δ = Solar declination
- ω = Hour angle (0° at solar noon, −ve before noon, +ve after)
- α = Solar altitude angle
- γw = Wall solar azimuth angle
- Ω = Profile angle (horizontal shadow angle)
- ν = Vertical shadow angle
- H = Height of window (or distance below overhang)
- P = Projection of overhang
- L = Shadow length cast on wall

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HVAC Load Reduction:** Properly designed overhangs reduce the **Solar Heat Gain Coefficient (SHGC)** through south-facing windows by **40–60%** in summer, translating to 10–25% reduction in annual cooling energy.
- **Glare Control:** Eliminates the need for internal blinds, preserving daylighting.
- **Zero Maintenance:** Unlike automated louvers, fixed overhangs have no motors, no failure modes, and a lifespan matching the building.
- **Standards Reference:** **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code, India 2017)** and **ASHRAE 90.1** both prescribe overhangs as a prescriptive compliance path for glazed façades.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Profile Angle (Ω) from First Principles

**Setup:** Consider a sunbeam striking the wall at altitude α and azimuth γw (measured from the wall's normal in horizontal projection). We want the angle this beam makes with the *normal to the wall*, projected onto a vertical plane perpendicular to the wall — this is the **profile angle Ω**.

**Step 1 — Decompose the sunbeam vector into horizontal and vertical components:**
The horizontal component of the beam makes angle γw with the wall normal.
The vertical component of the beam is governed by altitude α.

**Step 2 — Horizontal projection of beam vector:**
The horizontal projection of the solar beam has a vertical component (in the wall-perpendicular plane):

$$\text{Horizontal vertical-component (perpendicular to wall)} = \tan \alpha \cdot \cos \gamma_w$$

**Step 3 — Definition of profile angle:**
By definition of the profile angle, the ratio (horizontal projection length / vertical drop) equals tan(Ω):

$$\tan \Omega = \tan \alpha \cdot \cos \gamma_w$$

But wait — careful with definitions. The **ASHRAE convention** (used universally in building science) defines Ω as the angle in a plane **perpendicular to the wall** between the sun ray and the **horizontal plane in front of the wall**. For this convention:

$$\boxed{\tan \Omega = \dfrac{\tan \alpha}{\cos \gamma_w}}$$

**Step 4 — Special case: South-facing wall at solar noon.**
At solar noon, ω = 0° → γw = 0° (sun due south, perpendicular to wall).
Therefore: $\tan \Omega = \tan \alpha / 1 = \tan \alpha \Rightarrow \Omega = \alpha$.

This is the crucial simplification used in **most KTU numerical problems** (because noon is the design critical hour).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Overhang Projection Formula

**Setup:** A horizontal overhang of projection P is mounted at vertical height H above the top of a window of height h (so total height from window sill = H, assuming window bottom is at sill).

**Geometric Diagram (mental model):**
- The sunbeam makes profile angle Ω with the horizontal ground.
- The overhang casts a shadow of length L on the wall below it.
- Right-angled triangle: opposite = L (shadow on wall), adjacent = P (projection).
- Therefore: $\tan \Omega = L / P \Rightarrow L = P / \tan \Omega$.

For complete shading, **shadow length L must equal H**:
$$H = \dfrac{P}{\tan \Omega} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{P = H \cdot \tan \Omega}$$

**Step-by-step numerical application:**

1. Compute δ for the design day.
2. Compute α at the design hour.
3. Compute γw at the design hour.
4. Compute Ω from α and γw.
5. Compute P = H · tan Ω.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example (KTU Standard Pattern)

**Problem:** A south-facing window of height **1.2 m** is located in a building at latitude **13°N (Trivandrum)**. The overhang is placed **0.6 m** above the top of the window. Determine the projection P required to fully shade the window at **solar noon on 21st June**.

**Given:** φ = 13°, Day n = 172 (June 21), ω = 0° (solar noon), Wall = South, Total height H = 1.2 + 0.6 = 1.8 m.

**Solution Step 1 — Declination δ:**

$$\delta = 23.45 \sin \left( \dfrac{360 \times (284 + 172)}{365} \right) = 23.45 \sin \left( \dfrac{360 \times 456}{365} \right)$$

$$\dfrac{360 \times 456}{365} = 449.75^\circ \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sin(449.75^\circ) = \sin(89.75^\circ) \approx 0.9999$$

$$\delta \approx 23.45 \times 0.9999 \approx 23.45^\circ$$

**Solution Step 2 — Solar Altitude α (at noon, ω = 0):**

$$\sin \alpha = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos(0)$$

$$\sin \alpha = \sin 13^\circ \sin 23.45^\circ + \cos 13^\circ \cos 23.45^\circ \cdot 1$$

$$\sin \alpha = (0.225)(0.3979) + (0.9744)(0.9174)$$

$$\sin \alpha = 0.0895 + 0.8939 = 0.9834$$

$$\alpha = \sin^{-1}(0.9834) \approx 79.6^\circ$$

**Solution Step 3 — Wall Solar Azimuth γw (at noon, south-facing):**

At solar noon, the sun is due south → γw = 0° (sun perpendicular to south wall).

**Solution Step 4 — Profile Angle Ω:**

$$\tan \Omega = \dfrac{\tan \alpha}{\cos \gamma_w} = \dfrac{\tan 79.6^\circ}{\cos 0^\circ} = \dfrac{5.48}{1} = 5.48$$

$$\Omega = \tan^{-1}(5.48) \approx 79.6^\circ$$

(Since γw = 0, profile angle equals altitude angle — as expected.)

**Solution Step 5 — Required Projection P:**

$$P = H \cdot \tan \Omega = 1.8 \times 5.48 \approx 9.86 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** A ~10 m overhang is impractical for a 1.2 m window. This shows why horizontal overhangs alone are insufficient at low latitudes (close to equator) — designers must use **egg-crate overhangs**, **recessed windows**, or **external louvres** instead.

**Solution Step 6 — Winter Check (21st December, n = 355):**

$$\delta = 23.45 \sin\left(\dfrac{360 \times 639}{365}\right) = 23.45 \sin(630.0^\circ) = 23.45 \sin(270^\circ) = -23.45^\circ$$

$$\sin \alpha = \sin 13^\circ \sin(-23.45^\circ) + \cos 13^\circ \cos(-23.45^\circ)$$

$$\sin \alpha = -(0.0895) + (0.9744)(0.9174) = -0.0895 + 0.8939 = 0.8044$$

$$\alpha \approx 53.5^\circ \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Omega \approx 53.5^\circ \quad \Rightarrow \quad L_{winter} = 9.86 / 5.48 \approx 1.8 \text{ m}$$

Since the winter shadow length (1.8 m) **exactly equals H**, the winter sun grazes the bottom of the window — the geometry is *perfect*, though the projection is impractically large.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Depth of Cutoff

A useful KTU concept: the **Depth of Cutoff** is the maximum vertical extent of the window that remains shaded for a given overhang.

$$\text{Depth of Cutoff} = \dfrac{P}{\tan \Omega_{design}} \leq H$$

Equivalently, the **"shadow fraction"** of the window:

$$f_{shaded} = \dfrac{P}{H \tan \Omega_{design}}$$

- f < 1 → window partially shaded
- f = 1 → window fully shaded
- f > 1 → window shaded plus margin on wall

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Overhang Design Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Latitude phi and Wall Orientation] --> B[Step 1: Select Design Day n and Hour omega]
    B --> C[Step 2: Compute Declination delta]
    C --> D[Step 3: Compute Altitude alpha and Azimuth gamma_w]
    D --> E[Step 4: Compute Profile Angle Omega = arctan(tan alpha / cos gamma_w)]
    E --> F[Step 5: Apply Projection Formula P = H times tan Omega]
    F --> G[Step 6: Winter Solstice Verification]
    G --> H{Window fully shaded in summer AND}
    H -->|Yes| I[Sun penetrates in winter: DESIGN ACCEPTED]
    H -->|No| J[Increase P OR add vertical fins OR use egg-crate]
    J --> F
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Architecture Diagram — Shadow Geometry on a Vertical Wall

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PlaneA[Wall Cross Section]
        nodeW[Wall Surface]
        nodeO[Overhang Projection P]
        nodeH[Window Height H below overhang]
        nodeS[Shadow Line on Wall length L]
    end
    SunBeam[Sun Ray at Profile Angle Omega] -->|strikes edge of overhang| nodeO
    nodeO -->|casts shadow down the wall| nodeS
    nodeS -.->|length L equals P slash tan Omega| nodeH
    SunBeam --> nodeO
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Components of an Overhang System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input1[Solar Geometry Inputs] --> M1[Declination delta]
    Input2[Latitude phi] --> M1
    Input3[Hour Angle omega] --> M1
    M1 --> M2[Altitude alpha]
    M1 --> M3[Wall Azimuth gamma_w]
    M2 --> M4[Profile Angle Omega]
    M3 --> M4
    M4 --> M5[Required Projection P]
    Input4[Window Height H] --> M5
    M5 --> Output1[Geometric Design Output]
    Output1 --> C1[Horizontal Overhang]
    Output1 --> C2[Vertical Fin]
    Output1 --> C3[Egg Crate Grid]
    C1 --> A[Summer: Shade Achieved]
    C2 --> A
    C3 --> A
    A --> B[Winter: Free Solar Gain Preserved]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** In the first diagram, follow the vertical flow top-to-bottom — each step depends on the previous. The conditional node (diamond shape `{ }`) is the design acceptance criterion that decides whether to iterate.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the term "Profile Angle" in the context of overhang design. Why is it preferred over the simple solar altitude angle for non-south-facing walls?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **profile angle (Ω)** is the angle made by a sun ray with the horizontal plane in a vertical section that is **perpendicular to the wall surface** under consideration.

It is mathematically expressed as:

$$\tan \Omega = \dfrac{\tan \alpha}{\cos \gamma_w}$$

where α is the solar altitude and γw is the **wall solar azimuth angle**.

**Why preferred over altitude for non-south walls:**
For a south-facing wall at solar noon, γw = 0°, so Ω = α. However, for east/west walls or off-noon hours, the sun's rays strike the wall obliquely (γw ≠ 0). The shadow cast by an overhang is then foreshortened. The profile angle correctly accounts for this oblique incidence by **projecting the sun's ray onto a plane normal to the wall**, which is the plane in which the overhang actually casts its shadow. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for justification.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] List any three advantages of fixed horizontal overhangs over movable shading devices in tropical buildings.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Zero operational energy:** Fixed overhangs require no electricity, motors, or controls — they work purely by geometry and seasonal solar motion.
2. **Zero maintenance:** No moving parts means no wear-and-tear, no replacement schedule, and a lifespan equal to the building itself.
