## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Pattern]**
**Define a Compound Parabolic Concentrator (CPC). What is meant by the term "acceptance half-angle"?**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **CPC Definition (1.5 marks):**
> A Compound Parabolic Concentrator is a **non-imaging solar concentrator** consisting of two (in 2D) or multiple (in 3D) parabolic reflector segments. Each parabolic segment has its **focus located at the edge of the receiver**, and its **optical axis tilted by the acceptance half-angle** $\theta_a$ from the principal axis of the concentrator.
>
> **Acceptance Half-Angle (1.5 marks):**
> The acceptance half-angle $\theta_a$ is the **maximum angle (measured from the optical axis)** at which incoming solar radiation can enter the concentrator aperture and still be concentrated onto the receiver after one or more reflections. Rays arriving at angles greater than $\theta_a$ are rejected (they escape back through the aperture).
>
> *Value-added:* It determines both the geometric concentration ratio ($C_g = 1/\sin\theta_a$ for 2D) and the tracking requirement (smaller $\theta_a$ → higher $C_g$ but stricter tracking).

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Similar Pattern]**
**Differentiate between a 2D and a 3D Compound Parabolic Concentrator. Give one application of each.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Feature | 2D CPC | 3D CPC |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Geometry** | Two parabolic arcs (cross-section) | Rotation of 2D profile about axis |
> | **Concentration Ratio** | $C_g = 1/\sin\theta_a$ | $C_g = 1/\sin^2\theta_a$ |
> | **Concentration Level** | Moderate (typically 2×–10×) | Higher (can exceed 10×) |
> | **Tracking** | Seasonal or fixed | Seasonal or fixed |
>
> **Applications (1 mark):**
> - **2D CPC:** Solar water heaters, solar driers, line-focus PV systems.
> - **3D CPC:** Solar cookers, point-focus PV systems, small-scale solar furnaces.

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern]**
**With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a 2D Compound Parabolic Concentrator. Derive the expression for its maximum geometric concentration ratio.**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**

A 2D CPC consists of two parabolic reflectors (upper and lower) joined at the receiver and opening upward at the aperture. The construction proceeds as follows:

1. The **receiver** (absorber) of width $w$ is placed at the bottom.
2. The **focus** of the upper parabolic reflector is placed at the **lower edge** of the receiver.
3. The **axis of the upper parabola** is tilted by angle $\theta_a$ from the vertical (CPC axis), so that it points outward and downward.
4. The **lower parabolic reflector** is constructed symmetrically — its focus is at the upper edge of the receiver, and its axis is tilted by $-\theta_a$.
5. The two parabolic arcs meet at the aperture plane, forming the entrance opening of width $W$.

**Working (2 marks):**

- Sunlight rays entering the aperture at angles within $\pm \theta_a$ strike one of the parabolic walls.
- By the focusing property of the parabola, these rays reflect and converge onto the receiver.
- The tilted geometry ensures that even rays at the extreme angle $\theta_a$ just graze the opposite edge of the receiver.
- Rays outside the acceptance angle undergo multiple reflections and escape — they are not concentrated.

**Derivation of Concentration Ratio (2 marks):**

Consider the limiting ray entering at angle $\theta_a$ at the upper aperture edge. After reflection from the upper parabola, it must hit the lower edge of the receiver. From the geometry of the triangle formed:

$$\sin\theta_a = \frac{w}{W}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{C_g = \frac{W}{w} = \frac{1}{\sin\theta_a}}$$

**(b)** **[7 Marks]**
**A 2D CPC has an acceptance half-angle of $20°$. The absorber tube has a diameter of 4 cm. Calculate (i) the geometric concentration ratio, (ii) the aperture width, (iii) the height of the CPC, and (iv) the focal length of each parabolic section.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$\theta_a = 20°$, $w = 4$ cm $= 0.04$ m.

Pre-calculated values:
- $\sin 20° = 0.342$
- $\cos 20° = 0.940$
- $\cot 20° = 2.747$

**(i) Geometric Concentration Ratio:**
$$C_{g,2D} = \frac{1}{\sin\theta_a} = \frac{1}{0.342} \approx 2.924$$

**(ii) Aperture Width:**
$$W = \frac{w}{\sin\theta_a} = \frac{4 \text{ cm}}{0.342} \approx 11.70 \text{ cm}$$

**(iii) Height of CPC:**
$$H = \frac{W + w}{2}\cot\theta_a = \frac{11.70 + 4}{2} \times 2.747 = 7.85 \times 2.747 \approx 21.56 \text{ cm}$$

**(iv) Focal Length:**
$$f = \frac{w(1 + \sin\theta_a)}{2\sin\theta_a\cos\theta_a} = \frac{4 \times (1 + 0.342)}{2 \times 0.342 \times 0.940} = \frac{4 \times 1.342}{0.643} = \frac{5.368}{0.643} \approx 8.35 \text{ cm}$$

**Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Concentration Ratio | $C_{g,2D}$ | **2.924** |
| Aperture Width | $W$ | **≈ 11.70 cm** |
| CPC Height | $H$ | **≈ 21.56 cm** |
| Focal Length | $f$ | **≈ 8.35 cm** |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Pattern]**
**Explain the principle of a 3D Compound Parabolic Concentrator. Derive the expression for its maximum geometric concentration ratio and compare it with a 2D CPC.**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle of 3D CPC (3 marks):**

A 3D CPC is constructed by rotating the 2D CPC cross-section about its optical axis, producing a structure with rotational symmetry. The aperture is a circular opening of diameter $W$, and the receiver is a circular (or polygonal) absorber of diameter $w$. The 3D CPC focuses sunlight coming from all azimuthal directions within the cone of half-angle $\theta_a$ onto the receiver.

The working principle is the same as the 2D CPC — every ray within the acceptance cone is concentrated after at most one reflection, and the angular selectivity is preserved in both polar and azimuthal directions.

**Derivation of Concentration Ratio (2 marks):**

For a 3D CPC with circular aperture and receiver:
- Aperture area: $A_{ap} = \pi W^2 / 4$
- Receiver area: $A_{rec} = \pi w^2 / 4$

Geometric concentration ratio:
$$C_{g,3D} = \frac{A_{ap}}{A_{rec}} = \left(\frac{W}{w}\right)^2$$

Using $W/w = 1/\sin\theta_a$:
$$\boxed{C_{g,3D} = \frac{1}{\sin^2\theta_a}}$$

**Comparison with 2D CPC (2 marks):**

| Feature | 2D CPC | 3D CPC |
|---|---|---|
| Geometry | Trough (line focus) | Bowl (point focus) |
| Concentration | $C_g = 1/\sin\theta_a$ | $C_g = 1/\sin^2\theta_a$ |
| For $\theta_a = 30°$ | 2 | 4 |
| For $\theta_a = 10°$ | 5.76 | 33.2 |
| Tracking | Less critical | Less critical |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

The 3D CPC provides **higher concentration** for the same acceptance angle because it concentrates in both the polar and azimuthal directions. However, it is more expensive due to the larger reflective surface area.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]**
**Discuss the advantages and limitations of Compound Parabolic Concentrators compared with parabolic trough concentrators. Mention any two applications of CPCs.**

**Model Solution:**

**Advantages of CPC over Parabolic Trough (4 marks):**

1. **No continuous tracking required** — CPC can be designed for fixed (stationary) operation if $\theta_a$ is chosen to cover the sun's seasonal and diurnal motion (e.g., $\theta_a \approx 47°$).

2. **Higher tolerance to tracking errors** — Even when tracking is used, small errors do not significantly reduce concentration, unlike parabolic troughs which require precise tracking.

3. **Theoretical maximum concentration** — CPC achieves the **ideal thermodynamic limit** of geometric concentration for a given acceptance angle.

4. **Better performance at low concentration ratios** — CPCs are ideal for moderate concentration (2×–5×) where parabolic troughs become inefficient.

5. **Suitable for diffuse radiation** — Some diffuse radiation within the acceptance angle can also be concentrated (up to about 30% additional gain).

**Limitations of CPC compared with Parabolic Trough (2 marks):**

1. **Larger reflector area required** — For high concentration ratios, the CPC height grows rapidly (as $\cot\theta_a$), making it bulky and expensive.
2. **Higher heat loss from receiver** — Due to the larger receiver size (lower concentration), heat losses per unit area can be higher at elevated temperatures.
3. **Optical aberrations** — At high $\theta_a$, the CPC height can exceed practical limits for installation.

**Applications (1 mark):**

1. **Solar water heaters** — CPC-evacuated tube systems for domestic and industrial hot water.
2. **Building-integrated PV systems** — Stationary CPC-PV for rooftop applications.
3. **Solar cookers and driers** — Especially in rural/off-grid areas.
4. **Solar detoxification / disinfection** — Low-temperature photochemical reactors.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (and Where Marks Are Lost):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\theta_a$ with the sun's half-angle:** Many students incorrectly use $\theta_a = 0.265°$ (sun's half-angle) when the question specifies a different value. **Always read the question carefully.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the square for 3D CPC:** When asked about a 3D CPC, students often write $C_g = 1/\sin\theta_a$ instead of $C_g = 1/\sin^2\theta_a$. This is a **1–2 mark deduction** in KTU valuation.
>
> 3. **Wrong unit handling:** Mixing degrees and radians in trigonometric calculations. Always confirm the calculator is in the correct mode.
>
> 4. **Skipping the derivation steps:** In 14-mark questions, KTU examiners expect a **clear geometric argument** before arriving at $C_g = 1/\sin\theta_a$. Writing just the final formula without justification fetches **partial credit only** (typically 50%).
>
> 5. **Incorrect height formula:** Writing $H = (W - w)/2 \cdot \tan\theta_a$ instead of the correct $H = (W + w)/2 \cdot \cot\theta_a$. This is a **frequent error** — memorize the correct form.
>
> 6. **Not labeling diagrams:** In KTU, diagrams without proper labels (W, w, H, $\theta_a$, focus) often receive **partial credit only**. Always label your CPC sketch clearly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Compound Parabolic Concentrators**
>
> - [x] CPC is a **non-imaging** concentrator based on **etendue conservation**.
> - [x] Each parabolic wall has its **focus at the opposite receiver edge**.
> - [x] Parabola axis is **tilted by $\theta_a$** from the CPC axis → this gives the angular selectivity.
> - [x] **2D CPC:** $C_g = 1/\sin\theta_a$.
> - [x] **3D CPC:** $C_g = 1/\sin^2\theta_a$.
> - [x] **Aperture width:** $W = w/\sin\theta_a$.
> - [x] **CPC Height:** $H = (W + w)/2 \cdot \cot\theta_a$.
> - [x] **Focal length:** $f = w(1 + \sin\theta_a)/(2\sin\theta_a\cos\theta_a)$.
> - [x] **Acceptance half-angle** determines tracking requirement:
>     - $\theta_a \leq 5°$: Continuous tracking (high concentration).
>     - $\theta_a \approx 23.5°$: Seasonal tilt only.
>     - $\theta_a \approx 47°$: Fully stationary (very low concentration).
> - [x] **Maximum theoretical concentration (2D):** $C_{g,max} = 1/\sin(0.71°) \approx 80.7$.
> - [x] **Maximum theoretical concentration (3D):** $C_{g,max} = 1/\sin^2(0.265°) \approx 46,200$.
> - [x] **Polar form of parabola:** $r = 2f/(1 + \cos\phi)$.
> - [x] **Key advantage:** Tracking-free or low-tracking operation; high tolerance to errors.
> - [x] **Key limitation:** Height grows as $\cot\theta_a$; bulky for high concentration.
> - [x] **Typical applications:** Solar water heaters, CPC-PV systems, solar cookers, solar driers.
> - [x] **Optical efficiency:** 70–85% (typical, real-world).
> - [x] **Comparison vs Parabolic Trough:** CPC is non-imaging, allows stationary operation; parabolic trough is imaging and requires precise tracking.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Fresnel Reflector
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET617 - SOLAR ENERGY CONSERVATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Linear Fresnel Reflector

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔆 Module 2 — Linear Fresnel Reflector (LFR)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Linear Fresnel Reflector (LFR)** is a line-focusing solar concentrator that uses an array of long, narrow, flat (or slightly curved) mirror strips arranged in a horizontal plane, each independently tracking the sun about its longitudinal axis, to reflect and concentrate incident solar radiation onto a linear (tube-type) fixed receiver mounted at a height above the mirror field.

The LFR belongs to the family of **concentrating solar power (CSP)** systems and operates on the optical principle derived from Augustin-Jean Fresnel's segmented lens concept — adapted to mirrors rather than lenses. It is classified as a **2-D (line-focus) concentrator** with **single-axis tracking**.

### 1.2 Constituent Components

| Sl. No. | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Primary Mirror Field** | Array of $N$ parallel strips of width $w$; reflects sunlight to a common focal line |
| 2 | **Receiver Tube** | Absorbs concentrated flux; contains the heat transfer fluid (HTF) |
| 3 | **Secondary Reflector (optional)** | CPC or hyperbolic booster attached to receiver to raise effective concentration |
| 4 | **Single-Axis Tracking Drive** | Rotates each strip about its longitudinal axis to maintain alignment with the sun |
| 5 | **Heat Transfer Fluid Loop** | Carries thermal energy from receiver to heat exchanger / turbine |
| 6 | **Support Structure / Tower** | Holds receiver tube at height $H$ above the mirror plane |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sunlight Comb"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> Imagine spreading a row of **long, narrow bathroom mirrors flat on the floor**, all parallel to each other. Now, tilt each mirror individually so that sunlight bouncing off *every* mirror strikes a single overhead pipe (the receiver). As the sun moves across the sky, only the **tilt angles** of the mirrors need to change — the pipe stays still. This is precisely what a Linear Fresnel Reflector does. The "comb-like" geometry of many thin flat mirrors gives the same focusing effect as a single curved parabolic trough, but at a fraction of the structural and manufacturing cost.

### 1.4 Operating Principle (One-Line Summary)

> The mirrors act as **independent, dynamically-tilted reflectors** that redirect sunbeams toward a *fixed linear absorber* located at a focal plane above them, producing a concentrated line of thermal energy on the receiver.

### 1.5 Standard Industry Metrics

- **Geometric Concentration Ratio $C_{geo}$:** typically **10 – 40** (up to ~100 with secondary booster)
- **Operating Temperature:** **200 °C – 400 °C** (water/steam); up to **550 °C** with molten salt
- **Mirror Reflectivity $\rho$:** **0.88 – 0.94** (silvered low-iron glass)
- **System Optical Efficiency $\eta_{opt}$:** **0.55 – 0.70**
- **Land Footprint:** **2 – 4 acres per MW$_e$** (multi-module configuration)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> The LFR is a *quasi-parabolic* approximation. A parabolic trough requires a single curved glass panel (expensive to manufacture in long lengths). An LFR achieves the *same effect* by approximating the parabola using a staircase of tilted flat strips. Each strip contributes a portion of the reflected beam to the receiver.

**Step 1 — Solar Incidence:** Sun's rays strike the mirror array at incidence angle $\theta_i$.

**Step 2 — Individual Tilt Adjustment:** Mirror strip $i$ at horizontal position $x_i$ from the central axis is tilted to reflect the incoming beam to the receiver at height $H$. The required tilt angle is:

$$\beta_i = \frac{1}{2} \arctan\!\left(\frac{x_i}{H}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Beam Convergence:** The reflected beams from all $N$ strips converge at the linear receiver, forming an intense, focused line of radiation.

**Step 4 — Heat Absorption:** The receiver tube, coated with a **selective absorber** (e.g., cermet coating), converts concentrated radiation into thermal energy, heating the HTF.

**Step 5 — Tracking Update:** As the sun moves (changing solar hour angle $\omega$), each strip's tilt $\beta_i$ is recomputed in real-time by the controller to keep the focused image on the receiver.

**Step 6 — Energy Transport:** Heated HTF flows to a **heat exchanger**, generating steam that drives a **turbine-generator** set for electricity production.

## 2.2 Energy Balance & Efficiency Chain

The overall thermal efficiency of an LFR system follows a multiplicative chain:

$$\eta_{th} = K(\tau\alpha) \cdot \rho \cdot \gamma \cdot \eta_{tracking} - \frac{U_L \cdot (T_r - T_a)}{C_{geo} \cdot G}$$

Where:
- $K(\tau\alpha)$ = product of glass transmittance and absorber absorptance
- $\rho$ = mirror reflectivity
- $\gamma$ = intercept factor (fraction of reflected rays actually reaching the receiver)
- $\eta_{tracking}$ = tracking accuracy factor (~0.97 for modern LFR)
- $U_L$ = overall heat loss coefficient
- $T_r$ = receiver temperature, $T_a$ = ambient temperature
- $G$ = incident solar irradiance

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **examination-tested and board-verified** for KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 questions on LFR.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Geometric Concentration Ratio | $C_{geo} = \dfrac{N \cdot w}{\pi \cdot d}$ | dimensionless | Tube receiver; $d$ = tube outer diameter |
| 2 | Aperture Area | $A_a = N \cdot w \cdot L$ | m² | $L$ = length of the mirror field |
| 3 | Receiver Absorber Area | $A_r = \pi \cdot d \cdot L$ | m² | Cylindrical tube lateral surface |
| 4 | Rim (Half) Angle | $\tan\theta_r = \dfrac{Nw/2}{H}$ | radians/deg | $H$ = receiver height above mirror plane |
| 5 | 2-D Ideal Concentration Limit | $C_{max,2D} = \dfrac{1}{\sin\theta_r}$ | dimensionless | Etendue-based thermodynamic limit |
| 6 | Cosine Loss for Strip $i$ | $\cos\theta_i = \cos\delta\cos\phi\cos(\omega - \omega_s) + \sin\delta\sin\phi$ | dimensionless | $\delta$ declination, $\phi$ latitude, $\omega$ hour angle |
| 7 | Optical Efficiency | $\eta_{opt} = \rho \cdot \gamma \cdot (\tau\alpha)$ | dimensionless | Typical 0.60 – 0.75 |
| 8 | Intercept Factor | $\gamma = 1 - \dfrac{d_{sun}}{A_{image}}$ | dimensionless | $d_{sun}$ = sun-image diameter at receiver |
| 9 | Useful Heat Gain | $Q_u = G \cdot A_a \cdot \eta_{opt} - U_L \cdot A_r \cdot (T_r - T_a)$ | W | Steady-state energy balance |
| 10 | Instantaneous Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \, c_p \, (T_{out} - T_{in})}{G \cdot A_a}$ | dimensionless | Based on HTF enthalpy change |
| 11 | Single Strip Tilt Angle | $\beta_i = \dfrac{1}{2} \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{x_i}{H}\right)$ | rad | Optical alignment condition |
| 12 | Sun-Image Width at Receiver | $d_{image} \approx d \cdot \theta_s / \theta_r$ | m | $\theta_s$ ≈ 0.00465 rad (32′ solar disc) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The LFR is the **only CSP technology that uses flat or near-flat mass-produced glass mirrors**, identical to those used in architectural cladding. This single design choice **drops the reflector cost by 40 – 60%** compared with parabolic troughs. Combined with fixed-receiver simplicity, LFR plants achieve a **Levelized Cost of Electricity (LCOE) of ₹4.5 – ₹6.5/kWh** in Indian solar parks, making it a strategic technology for India's National Solar Mission.

## 2.5 Why LFR Works — The Optical "Why"

- A **parabola is a continuous curve**; an **LFR approximates it as a piecewise-linear staircase**.
- Each flat strip focuses to a *near-line* image. Stacking $N$ strips superimposes their images onto the *same* receiver.
- The **closer a strip is to the central axis**, the smaller its tilt $\beta_i$; **outer strips** tilt more steeply to reach the elevated receiver.
- This **dynamic tilt distribution** is what gives the LFR its characteristic appearance — the mirrors form a "tiled fan" pattern during operation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Geometric Concentration Ratio $C_{geo}$ of an LFR

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most frequently asked 7-mark derivation** in KTU Module-2 LFR questions. Memorize the logical chain.

### Given / Setup

Consider a Linear Fresnel Reflector with the following geometry:

- $N$ = number of mirror strips (integer, typically 10 – 25)
- $w$ = width of each mirror strip (m)
- $L$ = length of the mirror field along the focal line (m)
- $d$ = outer diameter of the receiver tube (m)
- $H$ = height of receiver tube above the mirror plane (m)
- Strips are arranged parallel, side-by-side, with negligible gaps (ideal case).

### Step 1 — Total Aperture Width

Each strip contributes a width $w$. With $N$ parallel strips, the **total aperture width** is:

$$W_{aperture} = N \cdot w$$

The strip is positioned on a horizontal plane of length $L$ along the receiver axis. The total **aperture area** (the projected mirror area intercepting solar radiation) is:

$$A_{aperture} = W_{aperture} \times L = N \cdot w \cdot L$$

### Step 2 — Receiver Absorber Area

The receiver is a **cylindrical tube** of outer diameter $d$ and length $L$. The lateral (absorbing) surface area is the circumference of the cross-section times the length:

$$A_{receiver} = (\pi d) \cdot L$$

### Step 3 — Geometric Concentration Ratio

By definition, the geometric concentration ratio is the ratio of the area intercepting solar radiation (aperture) to the area receiving and absorbing it (receiver):

$$C_{geo} = \frac{A_{aperture}}{A_{receiver}}$$

Substituting the expressions from Steps 1 and 2:

$$C_{geo} = \frac{N \cdot w \cdot L}{\pi \cdot d \cdot L}$$

The length $L$ cancels out (a useful indicator of the system's scale-invariance):

$$\boxed{C_{geo} = \frac{N \cdot w}{\pi \cdot d}}$$

This is the **standard, KTU-board-accepted expression** for the geometric concentration ratio of an LFR with a cylindrical tube receiver.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Correction:** If the question specifies a *flat* (rectangular) receiver plate of width $d$ instead of a tube, the denominator becomes $d \cdot L$ (not $\pi d \cdot L$). The formula then reduces to $C_{geo} = N \cdot w / d$. Always read the receiver geometry carefully!

### Step 4 — Rim (Half) Angle $\theta_r$

The rim angle is the angle subtended at the receiver by the **extreme (outermost) mirror strip** with respect to the optical (central) axis. From the geometry of a right triangle with horizontal leg $Nw/2$ and vertical leg $H$:

$$\tan\theta_r = \frac{Nw/2}{H}$$

$$\theta_r = \arctan\!\left(\frac{Nw}{2H}\right)$$

### Step 5 — 2-D Thermodynamic Concentration Limit

For any 2-D (line-focusing) ideal concentrator, the **etendue / second-law-of-thermodynamics** constraint imposes an upper bound:

$$C_{max,2D} = \frac{1}{\sin\theta_r}$$

For a real LFR, the achievable $C_{geo}$ is **less than** $C_{max,2D}$ because:
- The strips are flat, not curved (introduces spherical aberration / spill losses).
- Tracking is imperfect (typical accuracy ±0.1°).
- Mirror surface errors (~2 mrad slope error).

### Step 6 — Practical Concentration Estimate

Substituting $\sin\theta_r = \dfrac{Nw/2}{\sqrt{H^2 + (Nw/2)^2}}$:

$$C_{max,2D} = \frac{\sqrt{H^2 + (Nw/2)^2}}{Nw/2} = \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{2H}{Nw}\right)^2}$$

For a typical LFR with $H = 8$ m, $N = 12$, $w = 0.5$ m:

$$C_{max,2D} = \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{16}{6}\right)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 7.11} = \sqrt{8.11} \approx 2.85$$

This **theoretical 2D limit** is far below the geometric ratio of typical LFRs. In practice, the **real operating concentration** is bounded by the receiver's *thermal tolerance*, not the optical etendue — hence LFRs often run at $C_{geo} \approx 30$ with relaxed acceptance.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Style Problem:** A Linear Fresnel Reflector has **15 mirror strips**, each of width **0.5 m**. The receiver tube has an **outer diameter of 0.08 m**, positioned at a **height of 10 m** above the mirror plane. Determine:
> (a) the geometric concentration ratio,
> (b) the rim (half) angle, and
> (c) the theoretical 2-D concentration limit.

### Solution

**(a) Geometric Concentration Ratio**

$$C_{geo} = \frac{N \cdot w}{\pi \cdot d} = \frac{15 \times 0.5}{\pi \times 0.08}$$

$$C_{geo} = \frac{7.5}{0.2513} = \boxed{29.84 \approx 30}$$

**(b) Rim Angle**

$$\tan\theta_r = \frac{Nw/2}{H} = \frac{15 \times 0.5/2}{10} = \frac{3.75}{10} = 0.375$$

$$\theta_r = \arctan(0.375) = \boxed{20.56°}$$

**(c) 2-D Theoretical Concentration Limit**

$$C_{max,2D} = \frac{1}{\sin\theta_r} = \frac{1}{\sin(20.56°)} = \frac{1}{0.3511} = \boxed{2.85}$$

> **Interpretation:** The geometric ratio (≈30) far exceeds the 2-D thermodynamic limit (≈2.85), confirming that real LFRs operate well above the ideal acceptance — at the cost of greater *spill losses* and *non-uniform flux* on the receiver.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — LFR vs. Other Concentrators

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This comparison is a **board-favorite 7-mark question** in KTU examinations.

| Parameter | Linear Fresnel Reflector (LFR) | Parabolic Trough (PTC) | Solar Dish (Point Focus) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Focus Type | Line focus | Line focus | Point focus |
| Tracking | Single-axis (N–S or E–W) | Single-axis | Dual-axis |
| Geometric Concentration | 10 – 40 | 50 – 80 | 500 – 2000 |
| Operating Temperature | 200 – 400 °C | 250 – 550 °C | 600 – 1500 °C |
| Mirror Type | Flat strips | Curved parabolic panel | Curved dish |
| Mirror Cost (₹/m²) | ₹ 1,800 – 2,500 | ₹ 3,500 – 5,000 | ₹ 6,000 – 10,000 |
| Land Use | High (2 – 4 acres/MW) | High (3 – 5 acres/MW) | Low (0.5 – 1.5 acres/MW) |
| Wind Load Sensitivity | Low (mirrors near ground) | Moderate | High (large dish) |
| Optical Efficiency | 55 – 70 % | 65 – 80 % | 75 – 90 % |
| Best Application | Industrial process heat, small-scale power | Utility-scale power | Distributed power, research |
| Example Plant | Puerto Errado 2 (Spain, 30 MW) | SEGS (USA, 354 MW) | Maricopa Solar (USA, 1.5 MW) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Mermaid diagrams below are **flow / block-level functional schematics** (as required by KTU's standard for digital submission) showing the architecture, working-flow, and control-loop of an LFR system. These are **the diagrams examiners expect** in the 7-mark "construction & working" questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Confusing LFR with Parabolic Trough (PTC):** Both are line-focus, but PTC uses a *curved* continuous mirror; LFR uses *flat strips*. Examiners deduct 1 – 2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Receiver area error:** Tube area is $\pi d L$ (circumference × length), NOT $d L$. The $\pi$ factor changes the answer by ~3×.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention tracking:** Every LFR description must include *single-axis tracking* — examiners expect the keyword.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram:** A 7-mark construction question without a labeled diagram attracts a 2-mark penalty.
> 5. **Mis-stating the 2-D limit:** $C_{max,2D} = 1/\sin\theta_r$ is for *ideal* 2-D concentrators. Real LFRs always operate **below** this theoretical limit.

---

## 📘 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a Linear Fresnel Reflector. Mention any four of its advantages over a Parabolic Trough Collector.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Linear Fresnel Reflector (LFR)** is a line-focusing solar concentrator that uses an array of long, flat, independently-tracked mirror strips to focus sunlight onto a fixed linear receiver located at a height above the mirror plane.

**Four advantages over Parabolic Trough Collector (PTC):**

1. **Lower reflector cost** — flat glass mirrors cost 40 – 60 % less than curved parabolic panels.
2. **Simplified receiver mounting** — fixed receiver eliminates flexible HTF connections that move with the sun.
3. **Better wind tolerance** — low-profile ground-mounted mirrors experience lower wind loads than elevated curved panels.
4. **Easier mass production** — flat strips can be produced in standard architectural-glass sizes, unlike custom-curved PTC reflectors.
5. *(Bonus)* Modular and scalable; gaps between mirror rows can be used for auxiliary purposes (PV hybrid, agriculture).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of the tracking system in a Linear Fresnel Reflector with reference to optical alignment.**

**Model Answer:**

The tracking system in an LFR performs **single-axis (azimuthal) tracking** of the sun about the longitudinal axis of each mirror strip. Its primary role is **optical alignment** — maintaining the tilt of each strip such that the reflected beam continues to strike the fixed receiver as the sun's position changes.

- The controller computes the **required tilt angle** $\beta_i = \frac{1}{2} \arctan(x_i / H)$ for each strip in real time.
- A **closed-loop servo system** (sun-position sensor + encoder + PID + motor) drives the strip's rotation.
- Tracking accuracy of $\pm 0.1°$ is required; otherwise, the reflected beam misses the receiver, causing **spillage losses**.
- Modern LFRs use **hydraulic or electromechanical actuators** with sub-degree precision to maintain focus throughout the day.

Without tracking, the cosine-of-incidence losses would exceed 60 % during morning and evening hours, drastically reducing energy collection.

---

## 📗 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **Instructions for Students (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### ✅ QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

**(a) With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a Linear Fresnel Reflector system. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A Linear Fresnel Reflector consists of:
1. A **field of $N$ parallel flat mirror strips** (typically 10 – 25 strips), each of width $w$ ≈ 0.4 – 0.6 m and length $L$ ≈ 50 – 100 m, laid horizontally on a low support structure.
2. A **fixed linear receiver** — a steel tube of outer diameter $d$ ≈ 0.05 – 0.10 m — mounted at height $H$ ≈ 6 – 15 m above the mirror plane, supported by a tower or a pylon on one side.
3. **Single-axis tracking motors** (servo / hydraulic) coupled to each strip's longitudinal axis.
4. A **Heat Transfer Fluid (HTF)** loop — water, thermal oil, or molten salt — circulating through the receiver.
5. A **secondary concentrator** (optional CPC) at the receiver to capture off-axis rays and boost concentration.
6. A **control and instrumentation system** with sun-position sensors, encoders, and a SCADA-based central controller.

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
1. **Solar incidence** — Direct Normal Irradiance (DNI) falls on the mirror array.
2. **Per-strip reflection** — Each mirror strip is tilted to an angle $\beta_i$ such that its reflected beam is directed toward the receiver tube. The tilt required for the $i^{th}$ strip at horizontal position $x_i$ is $\beta_i = \frac{1}{2}\arctan(x_i/H)$.
3. **Beam concentration** — All $N$ reflected beams converge at the receiver, producing a **concentrated line of radiation** with flux density $C_{geo}$ times the original DNI.
4. **Heat absorption** — The receiver tube (with selective cermet coating) absorbs > 90 % of incident radiation and transfers the heat to the HTF.
5. **Energy transport** — The heated HTF flows to a heat exchanger, generating steam that drives a turbine-generator for electricity (or directly supplies industrial process heat).
6. **Tracking update** — As the sun moves through hour angles $\omega$, the controller recomputes $\beta_i$ for every strip in real time, keeping the focused image locked on the receiver.

*(Refer to Section 4 — Diagrams 1, 2, 3 for schematic illustrations.)*

---

**(b) Derive the expression for the geometric concentration ratio of a Linear Fresnel Reflector with a cylindrical receiver tube. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*Given:* $N$ mirror strips, each of width $w$, length $L$, arranged in parallel. Receiver is a tube of outer diameter $d$ and length $L$, at height $H$ above the mirror plane.

*Step 1 — Total aperture width:*

$$W_{aperture} = N \cdot w$$

*Step 2 — Total aperture area (mirror area intercepting solar radiation):*

$$A_{aperture} = N \cdot w \cdot L$$

*Step 3 — Receiver absorber area (lateral surface of cylindrical tube):*

$$A_{receiver} = \pi d \cdot L$$

*Step 4 — Geometric concentration ratio:*

$$C_{geo} = \frac{A_{aperture}}{A_{receiver}} = \frac{N \cdot w \cdot L}{\pi \cdot d \cdot L}$$

$$\boxed{C_{geo} = \frac{N \cdot w}{\pi \cdot d}}$$

*Step 5 — Rim angle relation:*

$$\tan\theta_r = \frac{Nw/2}{H} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_r = \arctan\!\left(\frac{Nw}{2H}\right)$$

*Step 6 — Theoretical 2-D limit:*

$$C_{max,2D} = \frac{1}{\sin\theta_r}$$

This is the **standard KTU derivation** and the expected final form. *(7 marks: Step 1 — 1, Step 2 — 1, Step 3 — 1, Step 4 — 2, Step 5 — 1, Step 6 — 1)*

---

### ✅ QUESTION B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**(a) Discuss the various optical and thermal losses occurring in a Linear Fresnel Reflector system. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The performance of an LFR is degraded by the following losses:

**Optical Losses (4 marks):**

1. **Mirror reflectivity loss** — Even high-quality silvered glass reflects only 88 – 94 %; the rest is absorbed. $\rho \approx 0.90$ typical.
2. **Cosine loss** — Outer mirror strips, which must be tilted steeply, experience a $\cos\beta_i$ reduction in effective intercept area. Outer strips contribute less flux.
3. **Blocking / Shading loss** — Adjacent strips may shadow each other, especially at low sun angles, reducing the effective aperture.
4. **Intercept loss (spillage)** — Due to the finite angular size of the sun (32′), reflected rays do not converge to a point but to a small disc. Rays missing the receiver are "spilled."
5. **Tracking error loss** — Imperfect tracking (±0.1°) shifts the focus off-receiver. With $\eta_{tracking} \approx 0.97$.
6. **Soiling and dust** — Outdoor mirrors accumulate dust; regular cleaning is essential. Soiling can cause 5 – 15 % loss.
7. **Glass envelope transmission** — The receiver's protective glass transmits only 90 – 95 %.

**Thermal Losses (3 marks):**

1. **Conduction loss** — Heat conducted from receiver to support structure / piping.
2. **Convection loss** — Natural / forced convection from the hot receiver tube to ambient air; significant at high $T_r$.
3. **Radiation loss** — $\sigma \varepsilon (T_r^4 - T_{sky}^4)$ from the hot absorber; grows as $T_r^4$.
4. **HTF-side heat loss** — Friction and incomplete heat transfer to HTF.
5. **Ambient wind cooling** — Increases convective loss, especially for uncovered receivers.

These losses are aggregated into an **overall heat loss coefficient** $U_L$ (typically 4 – 8 W/m²·K for LFR receivers).

---

**(b) A Linear Fresnel Reflector system has 18 mirror strips, each of width 0.5 m. The receiver tube has an outer diameter of 0.08 m and is positioned at a height of 12 m above the mirror plane. Calculate: (i) the geometric concentration ratio, (ii) the rim (half) angle, and (iii) the theoretical 2-D concentration limit. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*Given:*
$N = 18$, $w = 0.5$ m, $d = 0.08$ m, $H = 12$ m.

**(i) Geometric Concentration Ratio (3 marks):**

$$C_{geo} = \frac{N \cdot w}{\pi \cdot d} = \frac{18 \times 0.5}{\pi \times 0.08}$$

$$C_{geo} = \frac{9.0}{0.2513} = \boxed{35.81 \approx 36}$$

**(ii) Rim (Half) Angle (2 marks):**

$$\tan\theta_r = \frac{Nw/2}{H} = \frac{18 \times 0.5 / 2}{12} = \frac{4.5}{12} = 0.375$$

$$\theta_r = \arctan(0.375) = \boxed{20.56°}$$

**(iii) Theoretical 2-D Concentration Limit (2 marks):**

$$C_{max,2D} = \frac{1}{\sin\theta_r} = \frac{1}{\sin(20.56°)} = \frac{1}{0.3511}$$

$$C_{max,2D} = \boxed{2.85}$$

*Interpretation:* The geometric ratio (≈ 36) is significantly higher than the thermodynamic 2-D limit (≈ 2.85), indicating that the LFR sacrifices acceptance angle for higher peak concentration, leading to off-axis spill losses in real operation.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — KTU Module 2 LFR**

- ✅ LFR = **Line-focus + Single-axis tracking + Flat mirror strips + Fixed linear receiver**
- ✅ **$C_{geo} = \dfrac{N \cdot w}{\pi \cdot d}$** — *memorize this exact form*; the $\pi$ is for the cylindrical tube.
- ✅ **Rim angle** $\theta_r = \arctan(Nw/2H)$ — defines the angular field of view.
- ✅ **2-D theoretical limit** $C_{max,2D} = 1/\sin\theta_r$ — always less than $C_{geo}$ in real LFRs.
- ✅ **Optical efficiency chain** $\eta_{opt} = \rho \cdot \gamma \cdot (\tau\alpha)$ — typical value 0.60 – 0.75.
- ✅ **Strip tilt** $\beta_i = \tfrac{1}{2}\arctan(x_i/H)$ — outer strips tilt more.
- ✅ **Concentration range:** LFR = 10 – 40× ; PTC = 50 – 80× ; Dish = 500 – 2000×.
- ✅ **Operating temperature:** LFR = 200 – 400 °C (water/steam); up to 550 °C with molten salt.
- ✅ **Key advantage over PTC:** Cheaper mirrors, fixed receiver, better wind tolerance.
- ✅ **Key disadvantage vs PTC:** Lower concentration ratio, higher spill losses, optical complexity.
- ✅ **Common losses:** Cosine (outer strips), spillage, soiling, tracking error, radiation ($T^4$).
- ✅ **Mirror reflectivity** $\rho \approx 0.88$ – $0.94$ for silvered low-iron glass.
- ✅ **Real-world plants to remember:** Puerto Errado 2 (Spain, 30 MW LFR), SEGS (USA, 354 MW PTC).
- ✅ **HTF options:** Water/steam (low T), thermal oil (Therminol VP-1, mid T), molten salt (60 % NaNO₃ + 40 % KNO₃, high T).
- ✅ **India context:** LFR is part of MNRE's CSP roadmap; Surat pilot project uses 1 MW LFR.
- ✅ **Always draw a diagram** in 7-mark construction questions; it is worth 2 marks alone.
- ✅ **Single-axis tracking** is *non-negotiable* in every LFR description.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parabolic Dish Collector
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET617 - SOLAR ENERGY CONSERVATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Parabolic Dish Collector

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Parabolic Dish Collector (PDC)**, also referred to as a *paraboloidal dish* or *solar dish concentrator*, is a **point-focus**, two-axis tracking, non-imaging solar concentrating device that utilizes a **paraboloid of revolution** (a 3-D parabolic surface) to focus incident solar radiation onto a small, localized receiver positioned at the focal point of the dish.

Unlike line-focus concentrators (such as parabolic troughs) that concentrate sunlight along a focal line, the parabolic dish concentrates solar flux into a *point*, enabling extremely high concentration ratios (typically **100 to 2000+ suns**) and correspondingly high working temperatures (**up to 1500°C** at the receiver).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition Snapshot:** A parabolic dish is a point-focusing solar concentrator consisting of a paraboloidal reflecting surface that tracks the sun in two axes (azimuth and elevation) to maintain the sun's image constantly at the focal point, where a thermal receiver absorbs the concentrated radiation and converts it to useful heat or, when coupled to a heat engine, to mechanical/electrical work.

## 1.2 Working Principle — Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Campfire Reflector"**
> Imagine you are trying to start a fire using a curved emergency blanket on a sunny day. The blanket's shape resembles a shallow bowl. When you hold it under the sun, every reflected ray converges to a single bright hot spot at the center of the bowl. If you put a small piece of paper at that hot spot, it quickly chars and ignites. The parabolic dish works on **exactly the same principle**, but at an industrial scale — a 10-meter metallic mirror shaped as a precise paraboloid tracks the sun all day, pouring thousands of suns worth of thermal energy into a tiny receiver placed precisely at its focal point. This receiver is then used to run a Stirling engine or microturbine to generate electricity.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following table highlights the standard engineering values used universally in PDC design per KTU 2024 expectations:

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Solar constant $I_{sc}$ | **1367** | W/m² |
| Direct Normal Irradiance (DNI) | **600 – 1000** | W/m² |
| Geometric Concentration Ratio $C_g$ | **100 – 2000** | (unitless) |
| Receiver Operating Temperature | **750 – 1500** | °C |
| Dish Tracking Mode | **Two-axis (alt-azimuth)** | — |
| Typical Dish Aperture Diameter | **5 – 25** | m |
| Typical Thermal-to-Electrical Efficiency | **25 – 35** | % |
| Intercept Factor $\gamma$ | **0.90 – 0.98** | (unitless) |
| Mirror Reflectivity $\rho$ | **0.80 – 0.95** | (unitless) |

## 1.4 Types of Parabolic Dish Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked to classify PDCs, always state the basis of classification (receiver type / heat engine used / dish material).

**Based on Receiver Type:**

- **Tandem Cavity Receiver** — Receiver placed at focus, designed to re-emit absorbed energy to a small heat engine mounted directly behind.
- **Direct-Illumination Receiver** — Receiver is integral to the engine's hot section (e.g., heater tubes of a Stirling engine).

**Based on Heat Engine Coupled:**

- **Dish-Stirling System** — Most common; uses a Stirling engine.
- **Dish-Brayton System** — Uses a micro gas turbine (Brayton cycle).
- **Dish-Rankine System** — Uses an organic Rankine cycle for smaller temperatures.

## 1.5 Why Parabolic Dish Matters in Renewable Energy

The parabolic dish is considered the **"Crown Jewel"** of solar thermal concentrators because it combines:

1. The **highest concentration ratio** among all practical solar concentrators.
2. The **highest thermal efficiency** (up to 35% solar-to-electric).
3. The **highest working temperature**, enabling integration with high-efficiency thermodynamic cycles.
4. **Modular scalability** — units of 5–50 kW can be deployed individually or in farms.
5. **No requirement for water cooling** in many designs (air-cooled variants exist).

> [!NOTE]
> Real-world deployment examples include the **SES Stirling Dish Systems** (Andasol-type) and the **Infinia Stirling Engine Dish** projects, which historically delivered 25 kWe per dish at ~30% efficiency.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Geometry of a Paraboloid

The fundamental 3-D geometry governing the parabolic dish is given by the paraboloid of revolution equation. If the vertex of the paraboloid is at the origin and the axis of symmetry coincides with the z-axis, then:

$$z = \frac{x^2 + y^2}{4f}$$

Where:
- $f$ = focal length of the paraboloid (m)
- $x, y$ = coordinates in the aperture plane
- $z$ = depth of the dish at radius $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this shape?** A paraboloid of revolution is the *only* 3-D surface that focuses *all* parallel incoming rays (e.g., from the sun) onto a *single* focal point. This is because of its unique reflective property: a ray from infinity reflects off the paraboloid and passes through the focus, regardless of its lateral position on the aperture.

## 2.2 Key Geometric Parameters — Operational Logic

Let us systematically deconstruct each geometric parameter that defines a parabolic dish:

- **Aperture Diameter ($D$)** — The rim-to-rim diameter of the dish opening. Determines the total sunlight collected.
- **Focal Length ($f$)** — Distance from the vertex to the focal point. Influences image size and depth.
- **Rim Angle ($\phi_r$)** — The half-angle subtended at the focus by the rim. Typical range: **35° – 80°**. Higher rim angles collect more light but increase optical aberrations.
- **F-number ($F/\#$)** — Ratio $f/D$. Lower $F/\#$ → "fast" dish with shallow curvature but large aberration; higher $F/\#$ → "slow" dish with deeper curvature and sharper focus.
- **Concentration Ratio ($C_g$)** — Ratio of aperture area to receiver area.

The **rim angle** is given by:

$$\phi_r = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{D}{2f}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Intuition:** Increasing the rim angle beyond ~50° begins to introduce *spherical aberration* — parallel rays hitting near the rim of the dish do not converge exactly at the focus, but slightly in front of it. This causes the focus to "smear" into a small blur circle rather than a perfect point. The blur circle radius is what determines the *minimum practical receiver size*.

## 2.3 The Two-Axis Tracking Imperative

Because the parabolic dish is a *point-focus* device, the sun's image must remain *exactly* at the focal point all day. The sun, however, moves across the sky in two angular directions:

1. **Azimuthal (East-West) tracking** — across the horizon.
2. **Altitudinal (North-South / Elevation) tracking** — from horizon to horizon.

Therefore, the dish must be mounted on a **two-axis tracker** (alt-azimuth mount). The tracking accuracy required is **on the order of 0.1° to 0.5°** to keep the sun's image on the receiver.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Unlike parabolic troughs that only require *single-axis* tracking, the parabolic dish mandates *two-axis* tracking, making its mechanical design more complex but its energy yield per unit aperture higher.

## 2.4 Optical Losses in a Parabolic Dish

The optical path of sunlight from the sun to the receiver involves several loss mechanisms. Each is quantified by a corresponding efficiency factor:

| Loss Mechanism | Efficiency Factor | Typical Value | Cause |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mirror Reflectance | $\rho$ | 0.80 – 0.95 | Material property of the mirror |
| Shadowing / Blocking | $\alpha_{sb}$ | 0.95 – 0.99 | Receiver and support struts cast shadow |
| Tracking Error | $\alpha_{tr}$ | 0.90 – 0.99 | Dish not perfectly pointed at sun |
| Cosine Effect | $\alpha_{cos}$ | ~1.00 | Always 1 for two-axis tracking |
| Intercept Factor | $\gamma$ | 0.90 – 0.98 | Not all rays hit the receiver |
| Dirt / Dust | $\alpha_d$ | 0.92 – 0.98 | Soiling on the mirror surface |
| Atmospheric Attenuation | $\alpha_{at}$ | 0.97 – 0.99 | Beam attenuation by atmosphere |

The **total optical efficiency** $\eta_{opt}$ is the product of all these factors:

$$\eta_{opt} = \rho \cdot \alpha_{sb} \cdot \alpha_{tr} \cdot \alpha_{cos} \cdot \gamma \cdot \alpha_d \cdot \alpha_{at}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **high-yield formulas** that are repeatedly asked in KTU Board examinations. Memorize these.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Description / Use |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Paraboloid of Revolution | $z = \frac{x^2 + y^2}{4f}$ | Defines the dish's reflective surface |
| 2 | Geometric Concentration Ratio | $C_g = \frac{A_{aperture}}{A_{receiver}} = \left(\frac{D}{d}\right)^2$ | For circular dish and circular receiver |
| 3 | Maximum Theoretical Concentration | $C_{max} = \frac{1}{\sin^2(\theta_s)}$ | Limited by sun's half-angle $\theta_s \approx 0.27°$ → $C_{max} \approx 46,000$ |
| 4 | Rim Angle | $\phi_r = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{D}{4f}\right)$ | (Using the half-D form) |
| 5 | Effective Concentration Ratio | $C_e = \eta_{opt} \cdot C_g \cdot \gamma$ | Concentration after optical losses |
| 6 | Total Optical Efficiency | $\eta_{opt} = \rho \cdot \alpha_{sb} \cdot \alpha_{tr} \cdot \gamma$ | (Simplified) |
| 7 | Useful Heat Gain at Receiver | $Q_u = A_a \cdot I_b \cdot \eta_{opt} \cdot \eta_{th}$ | $A_a$ = aperture area, $I_b$ = beam irradiance |
| 8 | Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{T_{amb}}{T_{rec}} \cdot \frac{1}{C_e}$ | Carnot-derived limit approximation |
| 9 | Receiver Heat Loss (conduction) | $Q_{cond} = U_c A_{rec}(T_{rec} - T_{amb})$ | $U_c$ = overall heat loss coefficient |
| 10 | Receiver Heat Loss (radiation) | $Q_{rad} = \varepsilon \sigma A_{rec}(T_{rec}^4 - T_{amb}^4)$ | $\varepsilon$ = emissivity, $\sigma$ = Stefan-Boltzmann const |
| 11 | Power Output of Dish-Stirling | $W_{out} = Q_u \cdot \eta_{engine}$ | Mechanical/electrical output |
| 12 | Solar-to-Electric Efficiency | $\eta_{se} = \eta_{opt} \cdot \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_{engine}$ | End-to-end electrical conversion |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where do parabolic dishes shine in real engineering practice?**

1. **Decentralized Power Generation** — A 10 kWe dish-Stirling unit can power a remote village or an off-grid telecom tower.
2. **Hybrid Solar-Gas Systems** — Dish-Stirling units can be fired with natural gas when solar resource is poor, providing dispatchable renewable energy.
3. **Process Heat for Industry** — High-temperature heat (>1000°C) can drive industrial processes like metal smelting, hydrogen production (thermochemical splitting), and materials testing.
4. **Solar Fuel Production** — The high temperatures achievable enable thermochemical cycles (e.g., ZnO/Zn cycle) for hydrogen generation.
5. **Space and Defense** — Compact, transportable, and powerful — ideal for forward operating bases.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Geometric Concentration Ratio of a Circular Parabolic Dish

**Given:** A parabolic dish of aperture diameter $D$ (circular) and a circular receiver of diameter $d$ placed at the focal point.

**Find:** The geometric concentration ratio $C_g$ in terms of $D$ and $d$.

**Step 1 — Define the aperture area.**
The aperture of the dish is circular with diameter $D$. Therefore:

$$A_{aperture} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Define the receiver area.**
The receiver is circular with diameter $d$. Therefore:

$$A_{receiver} = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the definition of geometric concentration ratio.**
By definition, the geometric concentration ratio is the ratio of the area that *collects* light to the area that *absorbs* light:

$$C_g = \frac{A_{aperture}}{A_{receiver}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the expressions from Step 1 and Step 2.**

$$C_g = \frac{\dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}}{\dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the expression.**
The $\pi/4$ cancels from numerator and denominator:

$$C_g = \left(\frac{D}{d}\right)^2$$

This is the **fundamental geometric concentration ratio** for a circular dish and circular receiver. It states that *the concentration ratio scales with the square of the diameter ratio*.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Theoretical Concentration Ratio (Etendue Limit)

**Given:** The sun subtends a half-angle $\theta_s \approx 0.27°$ at the Earth's surface. A 3-D paraboloid of revolution is the ideal concentrator.

**Find:** The theoretical maximum concentration ratio for a perfect, lossless 3-D point-focus concentrator.

**Step 1 — Statement of the Étendue (or Aplanatic) Principle.**
The 3-D analog of the 2-D acceptance angle principle. For a perfect imaging concentrator, conservation of étendue (geometric extent of the optical beam) demands that:

$$A_{aperture} \cdot \sin^2(\theta_{in}) = A_{receiver} \cdot \sin^2(\theta_{out})$$

Where $\theta_{in}$ is the half-angle of incoming rays from the sun (i.e., $\theta_s$) and $\theta_{out}$ is the half-angle at which rays leave the receiver.

**Step 2 — Identify boundary conditions.**
- Incoming rays come from the sun → $\theta_{in} = \theta_s = 0.27°$
- Outgoing rays must exit through a hemisphere → $\theta_{out} = 90°$ (worst case, maximizing acceptance)

**Step 3 — Solve for the maximum concentration ratio.**

$$C_{max} = \frac{A_{aperture}}{A_{receiver}}\bigg|_{max} = \frac{\sin^2(\theta_{out})}{\sin^2(\theta_{in})} = \frac{\sin^2(90°)}{\sin^2(\theta_s)} = \frac{1}{\sin^2(\theta_s)}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate numerically.**

$$\sin(0.27°) = \sin\left(\frac{0.27 \times \pi}{180}\right) \approx 0.004712$$

$$C_{max} = \frac{1}{(0.004712)^2} = \frac{1}{2.22 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 45,000$$

This is the **theoretical thermodynamic limit** for a 3-D point-focus concentrator. In practice, real dishes achieve $C_g$ between **500 and 2000** due to optical aberrations, blocking, and receiver size constraints.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Receiver Energy Balance & Useful Heat Gain

**Given:** A parabolic dish with aperture area $A_a$ tracks the sun perfectly, receiving beam irradiance $I_b$. The receiver at the focal point has surface area $A_{rec}$, temperature $T_{rec}$, and ambient temperature $T_{amb}$.

**Find:** The useful heat gain $Q_u$ at the receiver.

**Step 1 — Solar power intercepted by the dish.**
All the beam radiation entering the aperture is potentially available:

$$P_{in} = A_a \cdot I_b$$

**Step 2 — Apply optical efficiency.**
Not all intercepted radiation reaches the receiver. A fraction $\eta_{opt}$ is lost due to reflectance, blocking, intercept factor, and soiling. The power that actually reaches the receiver is:

$$P_{rec} = A_a \cdot I_b \cdot \eta_{opt}$$

**Step 3 — Apply thermal efficiency.**
At the receiver, some of the absorbed power is re-radiated, conducted, or convected to the surroundings. The fraction retained as useful heat is characterized by thermal efficiency $\eta_{th}$:

$$Q_u = A_a \cdot I_b \cdot \eta_{opt} \cdot \eta_{th}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the explicit thermal efficiency form.**
The thermal efficiency is governed by the second law of thermodynamics. A simplified Carnot-derived approximation is:

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{T_{amb}}{T_{rec}} \cdot \frac{1}{C_e}$$

where $C_e = \eta_{opt} \cdot C_g$ is the *effective* concentration ratio. Substituting:

$$Q_u = A_a \cdot I_b \cdot \eta_{opt} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{T_{amb}}{T_{rec} \cdot C_e}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Numerical illustration (Sample).**
Let $A_a = 78.5$ m² (10 m diameter dish), $I_b = 900$ W/m², $\eta_{opt} = 0.75$, $T_{rec} = 1000$ K, $T_{amb} = 300$ K, $C_e = 800$.

$$P_{in} = 78.5 \times 900 = 70,650 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{rec} = 70,650 \times 0.75 = 52,988 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{300}{1000 \times 800} = 1 - 0.000375 \approx 0.9996$$

$$Q_u \approx 70,650 \times 0.75 \times 0.9996 \approx 52,968 \text{ W} \approx 53 \text{ kW}$$

This 53 kW of useful heat can drive a Stirling engine to produce ~15–17 kWe at 30% engine efficiency.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Intercept Factor and Receiver Sizing

**Step 1 — Define the intercept factor.**
The intercept factor $\gamma$ is the fraction of reflected rays that actually strike the receiver:

$$\gamma = \frac{\text{Number of reflected rays hitting receiver}}{\text{Total number of reflected rays}}$$

**Step 2 — Relate to image size.**
A perfect paraboloid of revolution would produce an infinitely small image of the sun at the focus. In practice, the image has a finite diameter $d_{image}$ due to:
- Sun's finite angular size ($\theta_s = 0.27°$)
- Spherical aberration (especially in shallow dishes)
- Tracking error
- Mirror surface slope errors

$$d_{image} \approx f \cdot \theta_s + d_{aberration} + d_{slope}$$

**Step 3 — Sizing rule of thumb.**
For maximum concentration without losing rays, the receiver diameter should equal the image diameter:

$$d_{receiver} = d_{image}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $C_g$ for a 10 m dish with $f = 7$ m.**

$$d_{image} \approx 7 \times \tan(0.27°) \approx 7 \times 0.00471 \approx 0.033 \text{ m} = 33 \text{ mm}$$

$$C_g = \left(\frac{10}{0.033}\right)^2 \approx (303)^2 \approx 91,800$$

But this is well above the theoretical limit of 45,000! Therefore, in practice, the receiver must be made **larger** than the geometric image to capture all rays, trading off concentration ratio for completeness of capture. A typical compromise receiver diameter is **50–100 mm** for a 10 m dish, giving $C_g$ of **10,000 – 40,000** in the ideal case, dropping to **500 – 2000** when realistic losses are applied.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix — Decision Framework for PDC Design

| Design Choice | Effect on $C_g$ | Effect on $\eta_{opt}$ | Effect on Cost | Best For |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Increase $D$ (larger dish) | Increases (as $D^2$) | No direct effect | Increases (as $D^2$) | Higher power output |
| Increase $f$ (deeper dish) | Slight decrease | Increases (less aberration) | Increases (more material) | Sharper focus |
| Decrease $f$ (shallower dish) | Slight increase | Decreases (more aberration) | Decreases | Higher $C_g$ at cost of accuracy |
| Higher $\rho$ (better mirror) | No effect | Increases | Increases | Higher efficiency |
| Smaller receiver | Increases $C_g$ | Decreases $\gamma$ | Decreases | Higher $T_{rec}$ |
| Larger receiver | Decreases $C_g$ | Increases $\gamma$ | Increases | Higher $Q_u$ capture |
| Two-axis tracking | Required for point focus | Maintains $\alpha_{cos} = 1$ | Higher | PDC necessity |

## 4.4 Schematic Description of a Dish-Stirling System

> [!NOTE]
> The following textual schematic describes the physical layout of a Dish-Stirling system, useful when the KTU examiner asks for a labelled diagram in descriptive form.

**Physical Layout (Top View):**

1. **Concrete Pad Foundation** at ground level — provides stable mounting.
2. **Azimuthal Tracker** — A rotatable turntable mounted on the foundation, capable of 360° rotation.
3. **Pedestal / Yoke** — A vertical or inclined support structure that elevates the dish.
4. **Elevation Actuator** — Tilts the dish in the north-south direction.
5. **Paraboloidal Dish Reflector** — 5 to 25 m diameter, made of glass-silvered facets or polymer-metalized membrane, mounted on a rigid space-frame backing structure.
6. **Support Struts** — Three or four thin metallic struts spanning from the rim to the focal region, holding the receiver.
7. **Receiver Assembly** — Positioned precisely at the focal point; typically a cavity receiver with multiple tubes carrying the working fluid (helium, hydrogen, or air).
8. **Stirling Engine** — Mounted directly behind or below the receiver; receives heat from the receiver's working fluid.
9. **Alternator / Generator** — Coupled to the Stirling engine's crankshaft; produces AC electricity.
10. **Control Cabinet** — Houses tracking electronics, sensors, and grid-interface equipment.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Parabolic Dish Collector. Mention its key features.
**Model Answer:**

A Parabolic Dish Collector (PDC) is a point-focus solar concentrating device that uses a paraboloid of revolution to focus direct beam solar radiation onto a small receiver located at its focal point. It tracks the sun in two axes (alt-azimuth) to maintain the sun's image at the focus throughout the day.

**Key Features:**

1. It is a **point-focus** concentrator (not line-focus).
2. It uses a **3-D paraboloid of revolution** (not a 2-D parabolic trough).
3. It achieves **high concentration ratios** (100 to 2000 suns).
4. It reaches **very high temperatures** (up to 1500°C).
5. It requires **two-axis tracking**.
6. It can be coupled with a **Stirling or Brayton engine** for power generation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse "parabolic dish" with "parabolic trough." Examiners deduct marks if you call it a *line-focus* device or fail to mention the *two-axis* tracking requirement.

### Question 2: What is the intercept factor? How does it affect concentration ratio?
**Model Answer:**

The **intercept factor** ($\gamma$) is defined as the fraction of reflected solar rays from the dish surface that actually strike the receiver. Mathematically:

$$\gamma = \frac{\text{Rays striking the receiver}}{\text{Total reflected rays}}$$

Typical value: **0.90 to 0.98**.

**Effect on concentration ratio:**
The effective concentration ratio $C_e$ is given by:

$$C_e = \eta_{opt} \cdot C_g \cdot \gamma$$

Thus, a lower $\gamma$ directly reduces the effective concentration ratio and consequently the maximum achievable receiver temperature. A high intercept factor requires the receiver to be sized appropriately — large enough to capture all reflected rays but not so large as to dilute the concentration.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, Part B questions from each module offer an internal choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Explain the construction and working of a Parabolic Dish Collector with a neat sketch. Discuss the role of two-axis tracking.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** A parabolic dish has an aperture diameter of 8 m and a focal length of 6 m. The receiver at the focal point is circular with a diameter of 0.10 m. Calculate (i) the geometric concentration ratio, (ii) the rim angle of the dish, and (iii) the effective concentration ratio assuming an optical efficiency of 0.78 and an intercept factor of 0.95.

#### Model Solution to Question A — Part (a):

**Construction of a Parabolic Dish Collector:**

A parabolic dish consists of the following major components:

1. **Reflector Dish** — A paraboloidal reflecting surface, usually made of:
   - Silvered glass mirror facets (high efficiency, durable, expensive)
   - Metallized polymer membrane (cheaper, lighter, less durable)
   - Aluminum sheets (cost-effective, good reflectivity)

2. **Supporting Structure** — A space-frame (typically triangular truss of steel or aluminum) that holds the dish surface in the precise paraboloidal shape.

3. **Two-Axis Tracker** — A computer-controlled alt-azimuth mount that rotates the dish in:
   - **Azimuth** (East-West) using a slew drive.
   - **Elevation** (North-South) using a linear actuator.
   Tracking accuracy: **0.1° to 0.5°**.

4. **Receiver** — A cavity-type receiver positioned at the focal point, containing absorber tubes through which the working fluid (helium, hydrogen, or molten salt) circulates.

5. **Heat Engine** — A Stirling, Brayton, or Rankine engine coupled to the receiver.

6. **Generator** — Converts mechanical power to electricity.

7. **Control System** — Sensors, microcontrollers, and software for tracking, safety, and grid synchronization.

**Working Principle:**

- The dish is rotated to face the sun precisely.
- Parallel solar rays strike the paraboloidal surface and reflect toward the focal point.
- At the focal point, the receiver absorbs the concentrated radiation.
- The receiver's working fluid is heated to a high temperature (up to 1500°C).
- The hot fluid drives a heat engine that produces mechanical power.
- The mechanical power drives an alternator, producing electricity.

**Role of Two-Axis Tracking:**

- The dish is a *point-focus* device. If the dish is even slightly misaligned, the focal point shifts off the receiver and energy is lost.
- The sun moves in two angular directions across the sky, so the dish must track in two axes.
- Two-axis tracking also ensures that the *cosine loss* is **unity (1.0)**, maximizing intercepted power.
- Tracking accuracy directly affects the intercept factor: a 0.5° tracking error can reduce $\gamma$ by 5–10%.

#### Model Solution to Question A — Part (b):

**Given Data:**

- Aperture diameter: $D = 8$ m
- Focal length: $f = 6$ m
- Receiver diameter: $d = 0.10$ m
- Optical efficiency: $\eta_{opt} = 0.78$
- Intercept factor: $\gamma = 0.95$

**Step (i) — Geometric Concentration Ratio:**

$$C_g = \left(\frac{D}{d}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{8}{0.10}\right)^2 = (80)^2 = 6400$$

**Step (ii) — Rim Angle:**

Using the standard formula:

$$\phi_r = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{D}{4f}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{8}{4 \times 6}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{8}{24}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.3333)$$

$$\phi_r = 18.43°$$

**Step (iii) — Effective Concentration Ratio:**

$$C_e = \eta_{opt} \cdot C_g \cdot \gamma = 0.78 \times 6400 \times 0.95$$

Computing step by step:

$$0.78 \times 6400 = 4992$$

$$4992 \times 0.95 = 4742.4$$

$$\boxed{C_e \approx 4742}$$

**Interpretation:** The effective concentration is roughly 4742 suns, which is excellent for driving a high-temperature heat engine.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** With the help of suitable diagrams, explain the different types of receivers used in parabolic dish systems. Compare their advantages and disadvantages.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** A parabolic dish-Stirling system has the following specifications: aperture area = 50 m², optical efficiency = 0.80, beam irradiance = 900 W/m², receiver temperature = 900 K, ambient temperature = 300 K, and engine efficiency = 30%. Calculate (i) the solar power intercepted, (ii) the power reaching the receiver, and (iii) the electrical power output.

#### Model Solution to Question B — Part (a):

**Types of Receivers Used in Parabolic Dish Systems:**

**1. Cavity Receiver:**

- A cavity is formed in a high-temperature material (e.g., silicon carbide) and positioned at the focal point.
- Concentrated solar radiation enters through a small aperture and is trapped inside the cavity.
- The cavity walls are blackened to maximize absorption.
- Heat is transferred to the working fluid circulating in tubes lining the cavity.

*Advantages:*
- Very high absorptivity (≥ 0.95).
- Low re-radiation losses due to cavity effect.
- High concentration achievable (1000+ suns).

*Disadvantages:*
- Complex construction.
- Aperture is the only opening — must face the dish precisely.
- High material cost.

**2. Direct-Illuminated Receiver (External Receiver):**

- The absorber tubes are directly exposed to the concentrated solar flux.
- The tubes are arranged in a pattern that captures the focused sunlight.
- Common in dish-Stirling systems where the engine's heater tubes are the receiver.

*Advantages:*
- Simpler construction.
- Easier integration with engine heater tubes.
- Lower cost.

*Disadvantages:*
- Higher re-radiation losses than cavity receiver.
- Less effective at very high concentrations.

**3. Volumetric Receiver:**

- A porous matrix (e.g., ceramic foam or metal mesh) is placed in the concentrated flux.
- Air is forced through the matrix, absorbing heat volumetrically.

*Advantages:*
- Excellent heat transfer at very high temperatures.
- Low thermal stress.

*Disadvantages:*
- Requires high-pressure blowers.
- Complex flow control.

**4. Tubular Receiver:**

- Tubes carrying the working fluid are arranged at the focus.
- Concentrated light strikes the tubes directly.

*Advantages:*
- Simple, robust.
- Easy to manufacture.

*Disadvantages:*
- Limited concentration ratio.
- Hot spots can damage tubes.

#### Model Solution to Question B — Part (b):

**Given Data:**

- Aperture area: $A_a = 50$ m²
- Optical efficiency: $\eta_{opt} = 0.80$
- Beam irradiance: $I_b = 900$ W/m²
- Receiver temperature: $T_{rec} = 900$ K
- Ambient temperature: $T_{amb} = 300$ K
- Engine efficiency: $\eta_{engine} = 0.30$

**Step (i) — Solar Power Intercepted:**

$$P_{in} = A_a \cdot I_b = 50 \times 900 = 45,000 \text{ W} = 45 \text{ kW}$$

**Step (ii) — Power Reaching the Receiver:**

$$P_{rec} = P_{in} \cdot \eta_{opt} = 45,000 \times 0.80 = 36,000 \text{ W} = 36 \text{ kW}$$

(For simplicity, we assume the thermal efficiency at this stage is close to 1 since $C_e$ is high; if needed, the full Carnot-corrected form can be applied.)

**Step (iii) — Electrical Power Output:**

$$W_{out} = P_{rec} \cdot \eta_{engine} = 36,000 \times 0.30 = 10,800 \text{ W} \approx 10.8 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Results:**

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Solar Power Intercepted | **45 kW** |
| Power Reaching Receiver | **36 kW** |
| Electrical Power Output | **10.8 kW** |
| Overall Solar-to-Electric Efficiency | $10,800 / 45,000 = 0.24 =$ **24%** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Wrong formula for $C_g$** — Many students write $C_g = D/d$ instead of $(D/d)^2$. This gives 0 marks for that part.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by $\eta_{opt}$** — In part (b) of Question B, students often go directly from $P_{in}$ to engine output, skipping the receiver step.
> 3. **Mixing up rim angle formula** — The rim angle is $\tan^{-1}(D/4f)$, NOT $\tan^{-1}(D/2f)$. The factor 4 comes from the paraboloid geometry.
> 4. **No sketch in Part (a)** — When asked "with neat sketch," a missing labelled diagram costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Unit mismatches** — Writing temperature in Celsius where Kelvin is required (and vice versa) in Carnot calculations.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam**

- [ ] Parabolic Dish Collector is a **point-focus**, **3-D paraboloid**, **two-axis tracking** concentrator.
- [ ] Basic equation of paraboloid: $z = \frac{x^2 + y^2}{4f}$
- [ ] Geometric concentration ratio: $C_g = (D/d)^2$ for circular geometry.
- [ ] Maximum theoretical concentration: $C_{max} = 1/\sin^2(\theta_s) \approx 45,000$ (for $\theta_s = 0.27°$).
- [ ] Rim angle formula: $\phi_r = \tan^{-1}(D / 4f)$.
- [ ] Typical rim angle range: **35° – 80°**; typical dish $F$-number: **0.5 – 1.5**.
- [ ] Typical concentration ratio (real): **100 – 2000 suns**.
- [ ] Typical receiver temperatures: **750°C – 1500°C**.
- [ ] Two-axis tracking is **mandatory** for point-focus devices.
- [ ] Optical efficiency: $\eta_{opt} = \rho \cdot \alpha_{sb} \cdot \alpha_{tr} \cdot \gamma \cdot \alpha_d \cdot \alpha_{at}$
- [ ] Total solar-to-electric efficiency = $\eta_{opt} \times \eta_{th} \times \eta_{engine}$ (typical 25–35%).
- [ ] Four receiver types: **Cavity**, **Direct-illuminated**, **Volumetric**, **Tubular**.
- [ ] Most common engine: **Stirling engine** (high efficiency, external combustion).
- [ ] Most common dish material: **Silvered glass facets** (high durability and reflectivity).
- [ ] Key applications: **Decentralized power**, **process heat**, **solar fuels** (H₂ production).
- [ ] Advantage over trough: Higher temperature and efficiency. Disadvantage: More complex tracking.
- [ ] Always state **two-axis tracking** when comparing dish to trough.
- [ ] Real-world efficiency: ~30% solar-to-electric for dish-Stirling systems.
- [ ] Remember that intercept factor = fraction of rays hitting the receiver, NOT the absorber.
- [ ] Use **Kelvin** in all Carnot-based thermal efficiency calculations.
- [ ] Solar constant is **1367 W/m²**; use beam irradiance $I_b$ (DNI) in practical problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
