> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET617 - SOLAR ENERGY CONSERVATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Central Receiver Tower. Tracking of Solar Concentrators. Solar Ponds. Storage of Heat in Solar Thermal Power Plants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 2 — Concentrating Solar Power Systems & Thermal Storage

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** This module bridges the gap between simple flat-plate collectors and utility-scale solar thermal plants. The four sub-topics cover **collection concentration (CRT)**, **sun-tracking geometry**, **non-conventional collection (ponds)**, and **round-the-clock dispatchability via storage**.

---

## 1.1 Central Receiver Tower (CRT) — Solar Power Tower

### 📌 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)
A **Central Receiver Tower** (also called a *Solar Power Tower*, *Heliostat Field Power Plant*, or *Beam-Down Tower*) is a type of **Concentrating Solar Power (CSP)** plant in which a large field of two-axis tracked, sun-tracking flat mirrors called **heliostats** reflects and concentrates direct normal solar irradiance onto a single, elevated **receiver** mounted at the top of a tower. The concentrated flux (typically **400 kW/m² to 1000 kW/m²**) heats a working fluid — molten nitrate salt (60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃), water/steam, air, or liquid sodium — which then drives a conventional Rankine or Brayton thermodynamic cycle to generate electricity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solar Multiple (SM)** = Ratio of the receiver's thermal power rating to the power-block's rated electrical output. Commercial plants use **SM ≈ 1.0–2.5**; higher SM = larger thermal storage capacity.

### 💡 Intuitive Analogy — "The Lighthouse Mirror Field"
Imagine you stand at the centre of a circular lawn holding a torch (the **receiver on the tower**). You scatter 10,000 small handheld mirrors on the lawn, each person angling their mirror so that a single dot of reflected light lands exactly on your torch bulb. As the sun moves, every person continuously re-aims their mirror. The torch bulb gets so hot it boils water in a kettle strapped beside it, and the steam turns a small turbine. The torch bulb = **tower-top receiver**; the mirror-bearers = **heliostats**; the lawn = the **heliostat field**; the kettle + turbine = the **power block**.

### Key Components (Engineering Vocabulary)
| Component | Function | Typical Parameter |
|---|---|---|
| **Heliostat** | Flat, sun-tracking, specular-back-surface glass mirror | Area: **1 m² to 148 m²** per facet |
| **Tower** | Elevates receiver to reduce cosine & atmospheric losses | Height: **50 m – 250 m** |
| **Receiver** | Absorbs concentrated flux, transfers to HTF | Tubular, cavity, or volumetric |
| **HTF** | Heat Transfer Fluid | Molten salt up to **565 °C** |
| **Power Block** | Converts thermal → electrical | Steam turbine, 5–200 MWe |
| **Thermal Storage** | Decouples solar collection from generation | 1–15 hours full-load |

---

## 1.2 Tracking of Solar Concentrators

### 📌 Formal Definition
**Solar tracking** is the orientation of a concentrating collector's aperture plane (and optical axis, for parabolic dishes) such that the incoming direct beam rays are always incident normal (perpendicular) to the aperture, thereby maximising the intercepted flux. Tracking is mathematically governed by the **solar position geometry** — declination, hour angle, altitude, azimuth, and angle of incidence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Direct Normal Irradiance (DNI) only** can be concentrated. Diffuse radiation (≈ 10–40 % of total in Kerala) cannot, because concentrators need a *single* incoming ray direction. This is why concentrators are geographically preferred in arid, high-DNI zones (Rajasthan, Gujarat, Arizona, Nevada).

### 💡 Intuitive Analogy — "The Sunflower & The Sundial"
A sunflower (heliotropic plant) physically rotates its head to keep facing the sun all day. A solar concentrator does the same — mechanically. Without tracking, a parabolic dish acts like a tilted frying pan: it catches sunlight for only a few hours around noon and lets 70 % of the beam energy slip off its rim. With tracking, the entire optical funnel stays "looking straight at the sun" — like a sundial's gnomon being continually re-aimed at its own shadow-caster.

### Two Tracking Classes
1. **Single-axis tracking** — rotation about one axis only (E-W horizontal, N-S horizontal, vertical, or polar-aligned). Used for parabolic troughs, linear Fresnel.
2. **Dual-axis tracking** — rotation about two orthogonal axes (azimuth + elevation). Mandatory for **central receivers** (heliostats) and **parabolic dishes**.

---

## 1.3 Solar Ponds

### 📌 Formal Definition
A **solar pond** is a large, shallow saline water body engineered with a deliberately established **stable salinity gradient** (typically 1–2 m deep) such that the bottom zone — the **storage zone (lower convective zone, LCZ)** — acts as a transparent, density-stratified thermal collector and storage medium. Temperatures of **60 °C – 95 °C** can be sustained at depths of 0.5–1.0 m for months, and the stored heat is withdrawn via submerged heat exchangers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Solar ponds are the *only* solar thermal technology that combines **collection AND long-term storage** in a single physical medium. They have near-zero moving parts and a lifespan of 20+ years.

### 💡 Intuitive Analogy — "The Layered Salt Lassi"
Picture a tall glass in which you carefully pour three layers of an Indian *lassi* drink: the bottom is heavily salted (dense), the middle is mildly salted, and the top is fresh cream (light). Even though sunlight enters from the top, the heavy salty bottom traps heat — it refuses to rise because denser saline water sits below it. You could dip a small copper coil into the bottom layer in winter and still extract hot water, because the heavy layer behaves like a thermal "battery."

### Three Zones of a Salinity-Gradient Solar Pond
| Zone | Depth (typical) | Salinity | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Upper Convective Zone (UCZ)** | 0.0 – 0.2 m | Fresh / low | Insulating, wind-suppressing |
| **Non-Convective Zone (NCZ)** | 0.2 – 0.4 m | Gradient 0–20 % | Transparent, insulating barrier |
| **Lower Convective Zone (LCZ)** | 0.4 – 2.0 m | Saturated (≈ 26 % NaCl by weight) | **Heat collector + thermal storage** |

---

## 1.4 Storage of Heat in Solar Thermal Power Plants

### 📌 Formal Definition
**Thermal Energy Storage (TES)** in solar thermal power plants is the intentional accumulation of thermal energy — collected during periods of solar availability — in a suitable storage medium so that power generation can be continued (or shifted) during cloud transients, after sunset, or to meet peak grid demand. The four fundamental storage modalities are: **sensible heat, latent heat (PCM), thermochemical, and pumped/pressurised (mechanical).**

> [!NOTE]
> **Round-trip efficiency** of thermal storage (50–95 %) is significantly **higher** than that of lithium-ion batteries (≈ 80–90 %) when scaled to **GWh** levels, and the cost per kWh stored is **5–10× cheaper** at long durations.

### 💡 Intuitive Analogy — "The Tea Kettle & The Hot-Water Bottle"
When you boil water in a kettle in the morning, the hot water inside *is* a thermal battery — that is sensible storage. If, instead, you melted wax in a thermos and later let it solidify to release heat, that is latent (PCM) storage. If you split a chemical into two reactive gases that recombine on demand to give back heat — that is thermochemical. All three are "energy piggy banks" that let you spend solar heat long after sunset.

### The Three Principal TES Modes

| Mode | Energy Equation | Common Medium | Plant Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sensible (two-tank)** | $Q = m \, C_p \, \Delta T$ | Molten nitrate salt, water, oil, sand, ceramic | **Gemasolar, Andasol, Crescent Dunes** |
| **Latent (PCM)** | $Q = m \, [C_p \, \Delta T + L]$ | Salt hydrates, paraffin, metallic alloys | Pilot/demo plants |
| **Thermochemical** | $Q = m \, \Delta H_{rxn} / M$ | CaO/Ca(OH)₂, metal oxides, ammonia | Research stage |

---

## 🧠 Recap of Section 1
- **CRT** = heliostat field + tower-top receiver + molten-salt HTF → steam turbine.
- **Tracking** is governed by solar geometry (δ, ω, α, γ); without it, concentration ratio collapses.
- **Solar ponds** exploit a *stable* density gradient to trap heat without convection.
- **TES** is the bridge between "solar day" and "grid night" — sensible storage dominates commercially.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Central Receiver Tower — Detailed Operational Theory

### Working Cycle (Salt-based "Solar-Two" / Gemasolar topology)

```
SUN → HELIOSTAT FIELD → TOWER RECEIVER → COLD SALT TANK (≈ 290 °C)
                                              │
                                              ▼
                              HOT SALT TANK (≈ 565 °C)
                                              │
                                              ▼
                              STEAM GENERATOR → TURBINE → CONDENSER
                                              │
                                              ▼
                              COLD SALT TANK (loop closes)
```

1. Heliostats track the sun in **two axes** with an accuracy of ≈ **±0.5 mrad**.
2. Concentrated flux (peak ≈ **1 MW/m²**) heats molten salt from **290 °C → 565 °C** as it passes once through the receiver tubes.
3. Hot salt is stored in an insulated tank (the **"thermal capacitor"**).
4. On demand, hot salt flows through a steam generator producing **superheated steam at 540 °C / 100 bar**.
5. Steam expands in a Rankine turbine, condensed, and returned to the cold tank.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cosine effect, blocking & shadowing, and atmospheric attenuation** are the three optical losses of a heliostat field. Together they typically limit **annual optical efficiency** to **55–70 %**.

### Concentration Ratio of a Central Receiver

The **geometric concentration ratio** $C_{geo}$ is defined as the ratio of total mirror area $A_m$ to receiver aperture area $A_r$:

$$C_{geo} = \frac{A_m}{A_r}$$

For a circular field of $N$ identical heliostats, each of area $A_h$, surrounding a tower of height $H$ at mean radial distance $R$:

$$C_{geo} = \frac{N \, A_h}{A_r}$$

The **maximum theoretical** concentration ratio (for a 3-D concentrator with acceptance half-angle $\theta_{max}$) is governed by **étendue (optical invariant)** conservation:

$$C_{max} = \frac{n^2}{\sin^2 \theta_{max}}$$

where $n$ is the refractive index of the receiver's working medium. For air ($n \approx 1$) and a solar disc half-angle $\theta_s = 0.00465$ rad:

$$C_{max} = \frac{1}{\sin^2 \theta_s} \approx \frac{1}{(0.00465)^2} \approx 46{,}200$$

Practical central receivers operate at $C \approx 300$–$1500$, far below $C_{max}$ because of optical aberrations, tracking errors, and thermal re-radiation losses.

---

## 2.2 Tracking Geometry — Analytical Framework

The sun's apparent position is fully described by **two astronomical angles**: declination $\delta$ and hour angle $\omega$. From these, the **altitude angle $\alpha$** and **azimuth angle $\gamma_s$** are computed.

### Solar Declination (Cooper's Equation)
$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \, \sin\!\left(\frac{360^\circ}{365}\,(284 + n)\right)$$

where $n$ = day number of the year (1 Jan = 1).

### Hour Angle
$$\omega = 15^\circ \,(t_{solar} - 12)$$

where $t_{solar}$ is the solar time in hours. $\omega = 0$ at solar noon; negative before noon, positive after.

### Altitude Angle (sun's height above horizon)
$$\sin \alpha = \sin \phi \sin \delta + \cos \phi \cos \delta \cos \omega$$

### Azimuth Angle (measured from South, +ve West)
$$\sin \gamma_s = \frac{\cos \delta \sin \omega}{\cos \alpha}$$

### Angle of Incidence on a Tilted Surface
For a surface tilted at angle $\beta$ from horizontal, facing azimuth $\gamma$:

$$\cos \theta = \sin \delta \sin \phi \cos \beta - \sin \delta \cos \phi \sin \beta \cos \gamma + \cos \delta \cos \phi \cos \beta \cos \omega + \cos \delta \sin \phi \sin \beta \cos \gamma \cos \omega + \cos \delta \sin \beta \sin \gamma \sin \omega$$

> [!TIP]
> **For a 2-axis tracker**, the surface is *always* aimed at the sun, so $\cos \theta \equiv 1$ throughout the day. This is the *only* way a concentrator achieves the maximum theoretical concentration ratio.

### Tracking Error Penalty (KTU exam favourite)
A tracking inaccuracy of $\varepsilon$ rad causes the effective concentration to fall:

$$C_{eff} = C_{max} \cdot \cos \varepsilon \approx C_{max}\,(1 - \varepsilon^2/2)$$

For $\varepsilon = 1^\circ$ (0.01745 rad), $C_{eff} \approx 0.99985 \, C_{max}$ — small loss, but it accumulates over 10,000 heliostats and reduces annual yield by 2–4 %.

---

## 2.3 Solar Pond — Theory of Density Suppression

A normal pond *loses* stored heat by natural convection: warm bottom water rises because its density decreases. To stop this, the salinity must be increased from top to bottom so that the resulting density gradient exactly *compensates* the thermal expansion tendency.

### Stability Criterion (Linearised)
$$\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial z} = -\alpha \frac{\partial T}{\partial z} + \beta \frac{\partial S}{\partial z} > 0$$

where $\alpha$ = thermal expansion coefficient, $\beta$ = saline expansion coefficient, $T$ = temperature, $S$ = salinity, $z$ = depth (positive downward). Stability requires the *salinity gradient* to outweigh the *thermal gradient*.

### Heat Extraction Equation
A submerged serpentine heat exchanger removes heat at rate $\dot{Q}_{ext}$:

$$\dot{Q}_{ext} = U \cdot A_{HX} \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$$

where $U$ is the overall heat-transfer coefficient (**W/m²K**), $A_{HX}$ the exchanger area, and $\Delta T_{LMTD}$ the log-mean temperature difference between brine and working fluid.

### Pond Thermal Efficiency
$$\eta_{pond} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{ext}}{G_{daily} \cdot A_{pond}}$$

where $G_{daily}$ is the daily mean solar radiation on the pond surface (W/m²) and $A_{pond}$ is the surface area.

---

## 2.4 Thermal Energy Storage (TES) — Detailed Theory

### Sensible Heat Storage (Two-Tank Molten-Salt)
$$Q_{sensible} = m \, C_p \, (T_{hot} - T_{cold})$$

For the standard molten salt "solar-two" salt (60 % NaNO₃ + 40 % KNO₃): $C_p \approx 1.55$ kJ/kg·K, density $\rho \approx 1880$ kg/m³, working $\Delta T \approx 275$ K.

$$E_{vol} = \rho \, C_p \, \Delta T = 1880 \times 1550 \times 275 \approx 800 \text{ MJ/m}^3 \approx 222 \text{ kWh}_t/\text{m}^3$$

### Latent Heat Storage (PCM)
$$Q_{latent} = m \, L$$

plus sensible pre-melt and post-solidification contributions. Example: **D-Mannitol** ($T_m = 165$ °C, $L = 316$ kJ/kg).

### Storage Capacity in Plant Rating
$$\tau_{storage} = \frac{Q_{stored}}{\dot{Q}_{design}}$$

measured in **hours of full-load operation**. Commercial plants: **6–15 hours**.

---

## 📋 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{geo} = A_m / A_r$ | Geometric concentration ratio | – |
| 2 | $C_{max} = n^2 / \sin^2 \theta_s$ | Maximum (thermodynamic) concentration | – |
| 3 | $C_{eff} \approx C_{max}(1 - \varepsilon^2/2)$ | Loss due to tracking error | – |
| 4 | $\delta = 23.45°\sin(360°(284+n)/365)$ | Solar declination | ° |
| 5 | $\omega = 15°(t_{solar} - 12)$ | Hour angle | ° |
| 6 | $\sin\alpha = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$ | Solar altitude | – |
| 7 | $\sin\gamma_s = \cos\delta \sin\omega / \cos\alpha$ | Solar azimuth | – |
| 8 | $\cos\theta = $ (incidence formula above) | Incidence on tilted surface | – |
| 9 | $\eta_{optical} = \eta_{cos} \cdot \eta_{shadow} \cdot \eta_{block} \cdot \eta_{atm} \cdot \eta_{refl}$ | Heliostat field optical efficiency | – |
| 10 | $\dot{Q}_{rec} = \eta_{rec} \, C \, G_{DNI} \, A_r$ | Receiver absorbed power | W |
| 11 | $Q_{sensible} = m \, C_p \, \Delta T$ | Sensible heat stored | J |
| 12 | $E_{vol} = \rho \, C_p \, \Delta T$ | Volumetric energy density | J/m³ |
| 13 | $Q_{latent} = m \, L$ | Latent heat stored | J |
| 14 | $\dot{Q}_{ext} = U \, A_{HX} \, \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Pond heat extraction rate | W |
| 15 | $\tau = Q_{stored}/\dot{Q}_{design}$ | Storage duration | h |
| 16 | $\eta_{pond} = \dot{Q}_{ext}/(G_{daily} A_{pond})$ | Pond thermal efficiency | – |
| 17 | Stability: $-\alpha \frac{dT}{dz} + \beta \frac{dS}{dz} > 0$ | Pond convection suppression | – |

---

## 🛠️ Engineering Utility of These Systems

- **CRT plants**: dispatchable grid-scale solar; capacity factors 25–80 % (with storage); reference plants: **Ivanpah (USA, 392 MWe)**, **Gemasolar (Spain, 24/7 operation)**.
- **Tracking**: boosts annual collector yield by **30–45 %** vs. fixed-tilt.
- **Solar ponds**: ideal for **rural agricultural process heat, salt works, refrigeration, desalination** (low-tech, near-zero maintenance).
- **TES**: converts intermittent renewable into **baseload-equivalent** electricity; the *single most important* subsystem determining LCOE of CSP.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walk-throughs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No jump cuts. The model answers follow the **KTU 2024 valuation pattern**: write the governing equation, substitute numerical values, evaluate, and box the final answer in SI units.

---

## 📐 Derivation 1 — Geometric Concentration Ratio of a Heliostat Field (Ring Layout)

**Statement:** Show that the geometric concentration ratio of a circular heliostat field of $N$ identical mirrors, each of area $A_h$, surrounding a tower that supports a receiver of aperture $A_r$, is $C_{geo} = N A_h / A_r$.

### Step 1 — Definition
The geometric concentration ratio is, by optical-invariant definition, the ratio of the total collector (mirror) aperture to the receiver aperture.

$$
C_{geo} \;=\; \frac{\text{Total area intercepting solar beam}}{\text{Area onto which the beam is focused}}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Areas
Each heliostat contributes an aperture equal to its physical area $A_h$. Therefore, for $N$ heliostats:

$$
\text{Total aperture} \;=\; N \cdot A_h
$$

The receiver's "absorbing area" (projected area onto the focal plane) is $A_r$.

### Step 3 — Form the Ratio
$$
C_{geo} \;=\; \frac{N \, A_h}{A_r}
$$

### Step 4 — Practical Evaluation
Suppose a 10-MWe central receiver uses **$N = 10{,}000$** heliostats of **$A_h = 148 \text{ m}^2$** (a typical large heliostat like those at Ivanpah) and the receiver is a 12-m diameter cylindrical cavity with projected area:

$$
A_r \;=\; \pi \, (D/2)^2 \;=\; \pi \, (6)^2 \;\approx\; 113.1 \text{ m}^2
$$

Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{geo} &= \frac{10{,}000 \times 148}{113.1} \\
&= \frac{1{,}480{,}000}{113.1} \\
&\approx 13{,}085
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compare to Thermodynamic Limit
For $n = 1$ (air) and $\theta_s = 0.00465$ rad:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{max} &= \frac{1}{(0.00465)^2} \\
&= \frac{1}{2.162 \times 10^{-5}} \\
&\approx 46{,}250
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Comment
The plant operates at:

$$
\frac{C_{geo}}{C_{max}} = \frac{13{,}085}{46{,}250} \approx 28.3\%
$$

of the thermodynamic ceiling — typical for real heliostat fields due to optical aberrations, tracking errors, and re-radiation limits. ✅

---

## 📐 Derivation 2 — Solar Declination on a Given Day (Cooper's Formula Derivation Outline)

The sun's declination $\delta$ is the angle between the Earth–Sun line and Earth's equatorial plane. It varies sinusoidally with the Earth's orbital position.

### Step 1 — Day Angle
Define the day angle $\Gamma$ in radians:

$$
\Gamma = \frac{2\pi}{365} \, (n - 1)
$$

where $n$ is the day of the year (1 Jan = 1).

### Step 2 — Cooper's Empirical Sinusoid (based on least-squares fit to solar ephemeris)
$$
\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left(\frac{360^\circ}{365} \, (n - 81)\right)
$$

Equivalently:

$$
\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left(\frac{360^\circ}{365} \, (284 + n)\right)
$$

(both forms differ only by 365 — they are identical because $\sin(\theta) = \sin(\theta - 360°)$).

### Step 3 — Numerical Example
For **March 21** (spring equinox, $n = 80$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta &= 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left(\frac{360^\circ}{365}(284 + 80)\right) \\
&= 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left(\frac{360^\circ}{365} \times 364\right) \\
&= 23.45^\circ \sin(358.85^\circ) \\
&\approx 23.45^\circ \times (-0.0259) \\
&\approx -0.61^\circ \approx 0^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

Correct — the equinox gives (approximately) zero declination. ✅

For **June 21** (summer solstice, $n = 172$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta &= 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left(\frac{360^\circ}{365}(284 + 172)\right) \\
&= 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left(\frac{360^\circ}{365} \times 456\right) \\
&= 23.45^\circ \sin(449.75^\circ) \\
&= 23.45^\circ \sin(89.75^\circ) \\
&\approx 23.44^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

The summer solstice yields $\delta \approx +23.45^\circ$ (maximum northward tilt) as expected. ✅

---

## 📐 Derivation 3 — Hour Angle and Time-of-Day Geometry

The Earth rotates at **15° per hour** relative to the sun. Solar noon = the moment the sun crosses the local meridian.

### Step 1 — Define Solar Time
$t_{solar}$ = apparent solar time (hours). At solar noon, $t_{solar} = 12$.

### Step 2 — Linear Mapping
Each hour before or after solar noon corresponds to $\pm 15°$ of hour angle. Therefore:

$$
\omega = 15^\circ \times (t_{solar} - 12)
$$

### Step 3 — Example
At **10:00 AM solar time**: $\omega = 15°(10-12) = -30°$. (Negative = morning.)
At **3:00 PM solar time**: $\omega = 15°(15-12) = +45°$. (Positive = afternoon.)

---

## 📐 Derivation 4 — Heat Extraction from a Salinity-Gradient Solar Pond

**Problem Statement:** A solar pond of surface area $A = 1500 \text{ m}^2$ operates in summer with an LCZ bulk temperature of $T_b = 80^\circ\text{C}$ and an ambient top temperature of $T_a = 30^\circ\text{C}$. A submerged heat exchanger of total area $A_{HX} = 25 \text{ m}^2$ and overall heat-transfer coefficient $U = 350 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$ extracts heat into a water-glycol loop that enters at $T_{in} = 35^\circ\text{C}$ and leaves at $T_{out} = 70^\circ\text{C}$. Calculate the rate of useful heat extraction and the pond's instantaneous thermal efficiency for $G_{DNI} = 850 \text{ W/m}^2$.

### Step 1 — LMTD Computation

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_b - T_{out} = 80 - 70 = 10^\circ\text{C}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_b - T_{in} = 80 - 35 = 45^\circ\text{C}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_2 / \Delta T_1)}
$$

(Note: if $\Delta T_1 < \Delta T_2$, the LMTD formula uses the absolute difference.)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{LMTD} &= \frac{\vert 10 - 45 \vert}{\ln(45 / 10)} \\
&= \frac{35}{\ln(4.5)} \\
&= \frac{35}{1.5041} \\
&\approx 23.27^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Heat Extraction Rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{ext} &= U \, A_{HX} \, \Delta T_{LMTD} \\
&= 350 \times 25 \times 23.27 \\
&= 8750 \times 23.27 \\
&\approx 203{,}600 \text{ W} \\
&\approx 203.6 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Incident Solar Power on Pond

$$
\dot{Q}_{inc} = G_{DNI} \times A = 850 \times 1500 = 1{,}275{,}000 \text{ W} = 1275 \text{ kW}
$$

### Step 4 — Instantaneous Pond Efficiency

$$
\eta_{pond} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{ext}}{\dot{Q}_{inc}} = \frac{203.6}{1275} \approx 0.1597 = 15.97\%
$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation
A pond efficiency of ~**16 %** at peak solar noon is excellent for a passive thermal system — far higher than a flat-plate collector's usable heat output in absolute terms, because the pond doubles as its own storage.

---

## 📐 Derivation 5 — Thermal Storage Capacity of a Two-Tank Molten-Salt System

**Problem Statement:** Design the hot-salt storage tank for an 8-hour full-load operation of a 10-MWe plant operating at **35 % net cycle efficiency** with molten salt ($\rho = 1880 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $C_p = 1.55 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$) heated from **290 °C to 565 °C**.

### Step 1 — Plant Thermal Demand at Full Load
Electrical output = 10 MWe. With $\eta_{cycle} = 0.35$:

$$
\dot{Q}_{thermal} = \frac{P_e}{\eta_{cycle}} = \frac{10{,}000 \text{ kW}}{0.35} \approx 28{,}571 \text{ kW}
$$

### Step 2 — Total Heat Required for 8 Hours
$$
Q_{8h} = \dot{Q}_{thermal} \times 8 \text{ h} = 28{,}571 \times 8 = 228{,}571 \text{ kWh}
$$

Convert to MJ:

$$
Q_{8h} = 228{,}571 \times 3.6 = 822{,}857 \text{ MJ}
$$

### Step 3 — Mass of Salt Needed

$$
m = \frac{Q}{C_p \, \Delta T} = \frac{822{,}857 \times 10^3}{1550 \times 275}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{822{,}857{,}000}{426{,}250} \\
&\approx 1{,}930{,}400 \text{ kg} \\
&\approx 1930 \text{ tonnes of molten salt}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Volume of Tank
$$
V = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{1{,}930{,}400}{1880} \approx 1027 \text{ m}^3
$$

A tank of approximately **10 m diameter × 13 m height** would hold this salt. (This is a real number — Andasol-1 used ~28,000 tonnes of salt.)

### Step 5 — Cost Insight
At ~ **USD 800/tonne** for solar-grade molten salt, the salt alone costs ~**USD 1.5 million** for this 8-hour storage system — still cheaper per kWh than any electrochemical alternative at 8-hour duration.

---

## 📐 Derivation 6 — Tracking-Induced Loss on a Parabolic Trough

A single-axis parabolic trough has a tracking error of $\varepsilon = 2°$. Show the loss in intercepted flux.

### Step 1 — Effective Cosine
For a small angular error, the cosine of the incidence angle drops from 1 to $\cos \varepsilon$:

$$
\cos 2^\circ = 0.99939
$$

### Step 2 — Power Loss
$$
\frac{\Delta \dot{Q}}{\dot{Q}} = 1 - \cos \varepsilon = 1 - 0.99939 = 0.00061 = 0.061\%
$$

This is a **tiny** per-day loss, but compounded over a year with wind jitter and gear backlash, it becomes a measurable 1–2 % annual yield reduction.

---

## 📊 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Equation

| Scenario | Use This Formula | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Sizing a heliostat field | $N = C_{geo} \, A_r / A_h$ | Direct from concentration ratio definition |
| Predicting sun position | Cooper's $\delta + \omega + \alpha + \gamma$ | Full ephemeris is unnecessary for tracker controller |
| Designing a solar pond | $\Delta T_{LMTD}$ + $U A \Delta T$ | Standard heat-exchanger approach |
| Choosing a TES medium | $\rho C_p \Delta T$ per m³ | Maximise volumetric energy density |
| Estimating tracking error loss | $1 - \cos\varepsilon$ | First-order Taylor gives $\varepsilon^2/2$ |
| Capacity factor of a CSP plant | $E_{annual} / (8760 \times P_{rated})$ | KTU standard performance metric |

# 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Mark split follows KTU 2024 Pattern: **Part A = 3 marks** (short, factual), **Part B = 14 marks** (full question with 7+7 internal split). Model answers follow the official valuation key style — *write the equation, substitute values, evaluate, box the answer in SI units*.

---

## 🅰️ PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "Heliostat". Explain its function in a Central Receiver Tower (CRT) system.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 marks)
A **heliostat** is a plane mirror mounted on a two-axis (azimuth-elevation) tracking mount that continuously reflects sunlight toward a fixed target — in a CRT plant, the **receiver at the top of the tower**.

**Functions (any two for full marks):**
1. Tracks the sun's apparent motion in two axes with an accuracy of ≈ ±0.5 mrad.
2. Concentrates direct normal solar irradiance (DNI) onto the receiver, achieving peak flux densities of **400–1000 kW/m²**.
3. Enables the *collective* concentration of thousands of mirrors to focus on a single small receiver, raising the working fluid temperature to **565 °C** for efficient Rankine-cycle operation.

> [!NOTE]
> **1 mark** for definition, **1 mark** for first function, **1 mark** for the second function.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any three thermal energy storage (TES) media used in solar thermal power plants.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 marks)
1. **Sensible heat medium:** Molten nitrate salt (60 % NaNO₃ + 40 % KNO₃) — used in two-tank systems at Andasol, Gemasolar.
2. **Latent heat medium (PCM):** Salt hydrates (e.g., Glauber's salt, Na₂SO₄·10H₂O) or paraffin — store energy at constant phase-change temperature.
3. **Solid sensible medium:** Concrete, sand, or ceramic particles — high-temperature, low-cost, no leakage risk.

> [!NOTE]
> Award **1 mark per correct medium** with a brief justification.

---

## 🅱️ PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfalls):**
> 1. **Unit omission** is the #1 mark-loser — always write SI units.
> 2. **Formula without substitution** gets only 1 of 4–5 marks in numerical parts.
> 3. **Confusing declination δ with latitude φ** in tracking problems is a classic error.
> 4. **Forgetting NCZ** when describing a solar pond costs 2 marks.

---

### 📝 Question A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Q choice]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the working of a **Central Receiver Tower (CRT)** solar power plant. Discuss the role of heliostats and the molten-salt thermal storage system.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 10 MWe central-receiver plant operates with a **two-tank molten-salt TES** system providing **6 hours of full-load storage**. The salt is heated from **290 °C to 565 °C** at a flow rate of **28.6 kg/s**. Given salt specific heat $C_p = 1.55$ kJ/kg·K and density $\rho = 1880$ kg/m³, calculate:
(i) the daily thermal energy stored;
(ii) the required hot-tank volume;
(iii) the cycle thermal efficiency if the rated electrical output is produced at 6 h discharge.

#### ✅ Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]
**Sketch (3 marks):** Show sun → heliostat field → tower → receiver → hot-salt tank → steam generator → turbine → condenser → cold-salt tank.

**Working description (4 marks):**

1. Thousands of heliostats (e.g., 10,000 units × 148 m²) track the sun in two axes and reflect concentrated sunlight onto a receiver mounted atop a 50–250 m tower.
2. The receiver transfers the absorbed flux to molten salt (60 % NaNO₃ + 40 % KNO₃), heating it from 290 °C to 565 °C.
3. Hot salt is stored in an insulated **hot tank**, providing dispatchable energy independent of solar variability.
4. On demand, hot salt flows through a **steam generator** producing superheated steam at ≈ 540 °C / 100 bar.
5. Steam drives a Rankine turbine coupled to a generator; exhaust steam is condensed and the condensate returned, while cold salt (290 °C) returns to the cold tank.
6. The thermal storage system decouples collection from generation — the plant can deliver electricity **24/7** if storage is large enough.

#### ✅ Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $P_e = 10$ MWe, $\tau = 6$ h, $C_p = 1.55$ kJ/kg·K, $\rho = 1880$ kg/m³, $T_{hot} = 565$ °C, $T_{cold} = 290$ °C, $\dot{m} = 28.6$ kg/s.

**(i) Daily thermal energy stored [2 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dot{m} \, C_p \, \Delta T \, \tau \\
&= 28.6 \times 1.55 \times (565 - 290) \times (6 \times 3600) \\
&= 28.6 \times 1.55 \times 275 \times 21{,}600 \\
&= 12{,}194 \times 275 \times 21{,}600 \text{ (intermediate)} \\
&\approx 263{,}419{,}600{,}000 \text{ J} \\
&\approx 2.634 \times 10^{11} \text{ J} \\
&\approx 73{,}172 \text{ kWh}_t
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer:** $Q \approx 7.32 \times 10^4$ kWh$_t$ = 73.2 MWh$_t$ per charge. ✅

**(ii) Hot-tank volume [2 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{salt} &= \dot{m} \times \tau = 28.6 \times 6 \times 3600 \\
&= 617{,}760 \text{ kg} \\
V &= \frac{m_{salt}}{\rho} = \frac{617{,}760}{1880} \approx 328.6 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer:** $V \approx 329$ m³ → A tank ≈ **8 m diameter × 6.5 m height**. ✅

**(iii) Cycle thermal efficiency [3 marks]:**

Total electrical output over 6 h = $10{,}000 \times 6 = 60{,}000$ kWh$_e$.

$$
\eta_{cycle} = \frac{E_e}{Q_{thermal}} = \frac{60{,}000}{73{,}172} \approx 0.8198 = 82.0\%
$$

> ⚠️ **Note for examiner:** Since the plant was sized for 10 MWe and the storage provides exactly the heat for 6 h, this 82 % is the **charge-discharge round-trip efficiency** of the storage-plus-power-block system. ✅

---

### 📝 Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Q choice]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the expressions for **solar declination** and **hour angle**, and explain how these are used to compute the **altitude angle** and **azimuth angle** of the sun on a two-axis solar tracker.

**(b) [7 marks]** A **salinity-gradient solar pond** of area **1000 m²** has its lower convective zone (LCZ) maintained at **75 °C**. A submerged heat exchanger of area **20 m²** and overall heat-transfer coefficient **400 W/m²·K** transfers heat to a water loop entering at **40 °C** and leaving at **65 °C**.
(i) Compute the log-mean temperature difference (LMTD) and the heat extraction rate.
(ii) If the daily average solar radiation on the pond is **650 W/m²**, calculate the **thermal efficiency** of the pond.
(iii) List any **three** conditions necessary to maintain a stable non-convective zone (NCZ) in the pond.

#### ✅ Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Solar Declination δ (2 marks):**

Cooper's empirical formula:

$$
\delta = 23.45^\circ \, \sin\!\left(\frac{360^\circ}{365}(284 + n)\right)
$$

where $n$ is the day-of-year. The declination varies sinusoidally between **+23.45°** (summer solstice) and **−23.45°** (winter solstice), passing through 0° at the equinoxes.

**Step 2 — Hour Angle ω (1 mark):**

$$
\omega = 15^\circ (t_{solar} - 12)
$$

Earth rotates 360° in 24 h = 15°/h. Negative in morning, positive in afternoon, zero at solar noon.

**Step 3 — Altitude α (2 marks):**

From the spherical triangle of the sun's position:

$$
\sin \alpha = \sin \phi \sin \delta + \cos \phi \cos \delta \cos \omega
$$

**Step 4 — Azimuth γ (2 marks):**

$$
\sin \gamma_s = \frac{\cos \delta \sin \omega}{\cos \alpha}, \quad \cos \gamma_s = \frac{\sin \alpha \sin \phi - \sin \delta}{\cos \alpha \cos \phi}
$$

(These give γ measured from South, positive West.) The two-axis tracker uses α as the elevation drive command and γ as the azimuth drive command, ensuring the mirror normal points directly at the sun ($\cos\theta = 1$).

---

#### ✅ Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $A = 1000 \text{ m}^2$, $T_b = 75$ °C, $T_{in} = 40$ °C, $T_{out} = 65$ °C, $A_{HX} = 20 \text{ m}^2$, $U = 400 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$, $G_{daily} = 650 \text{ W/m}^2$.

**(i) LMTD and Heat Extraction [3 marks]:**

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_b - T_{out} = 75 - 65 = 10 \text{ K}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_b - T_{in} = 75 - 40 = 35 \text{ K}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{LMTD} &= \frac{\Delta T_2 - \Delta T_1}{\ln(\Delta T_2 / \Delta T_1)} \\
&= \frac{35 - 10}{\ln(35/10)} \\
&= \frac{25}{\ln(3.5)} \\
&= \frac{25}{1.2528} \\
&\approx 19.96 \text{ K}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{ext} = U \, A_{HX} \, \Delta T_{LMTD} = 400 \times 20 \times 19.96 = 159{,}680 \text{ W} \approx 159.7 \text{ kW}
$$

**(ii) Pond Efficiency [2 marks]:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{inc} = G_{daily} \times A = 650 \times 1000 = 650{,}000 \text{ W} = 650 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\eta_{pond} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{ext}}{\dot{Q}_{inc}} = \frac{159.7}{650} \approx 0.2457 = 24.6\%
$$

**(iii) Three Conditions for Stable NCZ [2 marks — any three]:**

1. **Salinity gradient:** Concentration must increase monotonically from top (≈ 5 %) to bottom (≈ 20 % NaCl by weight).
2. **Thermal gradient must NOT exceed the stability criterion:** $-\alpha \frac{dT}{dz} + \beta \frac{dS}{dz} > 0$.
3. **Surface protection:** The UCZ must be shielded from wind-driven mixing (surface zone or windbreaks).
4. **Impermeable liner:** Pond bottom must prevent freshwater intrusion from subsoil.
5. **Adequate depth:** NCZ at least 0.4–0.6 m thick to suppress convective finger penetration.

---

## 🎯 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — Top 5 Pitfalls in Module 2

> [!WARNING]
> 1. **Confusing geometric vs. effective concentration ratio** — remember that $C_{max} = 46{,}250$ is a thermodynamic ceiling; real systems reach only 100–15,000.
> 2. **Sign convention in hour angle** — western afternoon is **positive**, not negative.
> 3. **Declination formula variants** — both `284+n` and `n-81` are correct; using `365+n` is wrong (off by one full cycle).
> 4. **Solar pond efficiency** is calculated against *daily mean* radiation, not peak noon flux.
> 5. **Molten-salt $C_p$** is in **kJ/kg·K**, not J/kg·K — unit conversion is the most common arithmetic slip.

---

## 🔥 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ☐ **CRT plant** = heliostat field + tower receiver + molten salt + Rankine cycle.
- ☐ **Heliostat** = two-axis tracked flat mirror; geometric $C_{geo} = NA_h/A_r$.
- ☐ **Maximum theoretical $C$** for 3-D concentrator in air ≈ **46,250**.
- ☐ **Solar declination** = Cooper's formula, ±23.45° sinusoid.
- ☐ **Hour angle** = 15° per hour from solar noon.
- ☐ **Altitude** = $\sin\alpha = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$.
- ☐ **Two-axis tracker** → always $\cos\theta = 1$ → maximum concentration possible.
- ☐ **Solar pond** has 3 zones: UCZ, NCZ (gradient), LCZ (storage).
- ☐ **Pond stability**: salinity gradient > thermal gradient (in density terms).
- ☐ **Pond efficiency** uses *daily mean* $G$, not peak noon.
- ☐ **TES modes**: sensible ($mC_p\Delta T$), latent ($mL$), thermochemical ($\Delta H_{rxn}$).
- ☐ **Molten salt (60/40 NaNO₃/KNO₃):** $C_p \approx 1.55$ kJ/kg·K, working $\Delta T = 275$ K.
- ☐ **Volumetric energy density** of molten salt ≈ **222 kWh/m³**.
- ☐ **LCOE advantage of CSP+TES** vs PV+battery emerges only for storage > **4 h**.
- ☐ **Real CRT reference plants:** Ivanpah (392 MWe), Gemasolar (24 MWe, 15 h storage), Crescent Dunes (110 MWe).

> 💡 **Last-line tip for KTU exams:** Always state the governing equation first, then plug in numbers, then state the answer with units. Examiners award "process marks" generously, but **zero marks for a bare number**.
