topics.title: Storage Media and Heat Transfer Fluids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET617 - SOLAR ENERGY CONSERVATION SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Storage Media and Heat Transfer Fluids # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Thermal Energy Storage (TES) in Solar Systems? **Formal KTU Definition:** *Thermal Energy Storage (TES) is the temporary holding of thermal energy in a storage medium for later use, compensating for the temporal mismatch between solar energy availability (peaking at noon) and energy demand (often peaking in mornings, evenings, or nighttime). In solar conservation systems, TES ensures a continuous, reliable supply of thermal energy independent of solar irradiance fluctuations.* **Classification of Storage Modes:** | Storage Mode | Physical Basis | Energy Equation | Typical Energy Density | |---|---|---|---| | **Sensible** | Temperature change | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Low–Moderate (50–500 kJ/kg) | | **Latent (PCM)** | Phase change at constant T | $Q = m \cdot h_{sl}$ | High (150–600 kJ/kg) | | **Thermochemical** | Reversible chemical reaction | $Q = m \cdot \Delta H_{rxn} \cdot \alpha$ | Very High (500–3000 kJ/kg) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 specifically focuses on **Storage Media** (sensible solids, sensible liquids, PCMs) and **Heat Transfer Fluids (HTFs)** that transport thermal energy between the collector, storage, and load. Both media and fluids are the *physical substances* that carry or hold the sun's heat. --- ## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy: A "Thermal Battery" Think of your solar thermal system as a **kitchen water heater**, but with the sun as the burner: - **The Heat Transfer Fluid (HTF)** is the *water* that actually circulates and gets heated. - **The Storage Medium** is the *insulated tank* that holds the hot fluid/material, releasing it later like a thermal battery. - **Without storage**, heat is delivered *only* when the sun shines — like a stove that stops cooking when unplugged. - **With storage**, heat is delivered *on demand* — like a battery that charges at noon and discharges at night. If the HTF is the **delivery boy** carrying heat packets, the storage medium is the **warehouse** holding them safely until needed. The smarter the warehouse design (PCM, molten salt, packed-bed), the longer the system works after sunset. --- ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Standard Properties to Memorize for KTU Exams:** > - **Density of water** at 25 °C: $\rho_w = 997 \,\text{kg/m}^3$ > - **Specific heat of water**: $c_{p,w} = 4.186 \,\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$ — among the **highest** of common liquids > - **Volumetric heat capacity of water**: $\rho c_p = 4176 \,\text{kJ/(m}^3\text{·K)}$ > - **Latent heat of fusion of ice/water**: $h_{sl} = 333.4 \,\text{kJ/kg}$ at **0 °C** > - **Paraffin wax (typical PCM) latent heat**: $h_{sl} = 200$–$250 \,\text{kJ/kg}$ > - **Molten salt (Hitec)**: $c_p \approx 1.5 \,\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$, usable up to **538 °C** > - **Therminol VP-1 oil**: usable range **12 °C to 400 °C** > - **Stefan-Boltzmann constant**: $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \,\text{W/(m}^2\text{·K}^4$ > - **Solar constant** (AM1.5): $G_{sc} = 1367 \,\text{W/m}^2$ --- ## 1.4 Why Storage Media and HTFs Are Critical > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Reality:** The choice of *storage medium* and *HTF* alone can decide whether a 1 MW solar plant is technically and economically viable. For example, a **parabolic trough** plant cannot operate above 400 °C with synthetic oil but can with molten salt — directly impacting annual energy yield by **20–30%**. A solar energy conservation system performs four thermal functions, and storage + HTF are central to three of them: 1. **Collection** (absorber → HTF) 2. **Transport** (HTF in pipelines) 3. **Storage** (HTF → storage medium, or direct storage in HTF) 4. **Delivery** (storage → load) # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Sensible Heat Storage — Concept and Mechanics Sensible heat storage exploits the **temperature rise (or fall)** of a material. The quantity of heat stored is directly proportional to mass, specific heat, and temperature swing. **Why "sensible"?** Because the temperature change is *sensed* (felt) — unlike latent heat, where temperature stays constant during phase change. ### 2.1.1 Solid Sensible Storage Media | Material | $\rho$ (kg/m³) | $c_p$ (kJ/kg·K) | $k$ (W/m·K) | $\rho c_p$ (kJ/m³·K) | Max T (°C) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Concrete (heavy)** | 2200 | 0.88 | 1.0 | 1936 | ~600 | | **Cast iron** | 7200 | 0.46 | 50 | 3312 | ~1000 | | **Granite/rocks** | 2700 | 0.84 | 2.5 | 2270 | ~800 | | **Sand (dry)** | 1500 | 0.84 | 0.4 | 1260 | ~800 | | **Fire bricks** | 2000 | 1.0 | 1.2 | 2000 | ~1200 | | **Aluminium** | 2700 | 0.90 | 230 | 2430 | ~600 | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Although **cast iron** has the highest $\rho c_p$ ($\approx 3312 \,\text{kJ/m}^3\text{·K}$), it is rarely used in domestic solar systems due to weight and cost. **Pebble-bed** (small rocks) is the most common low-cost storage. ### 2.1.2 Liquid Sensible Storage Media | Material | $\rho$ (kg/m³) | $c_p$ (kJ/kg·K) | $k$ (W/m·K) | Operating T (°C) | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Water** | 1000 | 4.186 | 0.6 | 0–100 | Cheapest, highest $c_p$ | | **Thermal oil** | ~870 | ~2.1 | 0.11 | 12–400 | Used in troughs | | **Molten salt (Hitec)** | 1900 | 1.55 | 0.55 | 220–538 | Used in tower plants | | **Sodium (liquid)** | 850 | 1.3 | 70 | 98–883 | High-temp systems | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Water** has an unbeatable combination: highest $c_p$ of any common liquid (4.186 kJ/kg·K), low cost, non-toxic, non-flammable — making it the **#1 storage/HTF below 100 °C**. --- ## 2.2 Latent Heat Storage — Phase Change Materials (PCMs) PCMs store energy during a **phase transition** (typically solid↔liquid) at a near-constant temperature. They offer: - **High energy density** (often 5–10× sensible storage per unit mass) - **Near-isothermal operation** — ideal for matching a constant load temperature ### 2.2.1 PCM Classification | Type | Examples | $h_{sl}$ (kJ/kg) | T_range (°C) | Pros/Cons | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Organic (paraffin)** | n-Eicosane, n-Docosane | 200–250 | 30–70 | Stable, non-corrosive, low $k$ (~0.2 W/m·K) | | **Organic (non-paraffin)** | Fatty acids (stearic) | 150–200 | 50–70 | Subcooling issues | | **Inorganic (salt hydrates)** | Glauber's salt (Na₂SO₄·10H₂O) | 250 | 32 | Cheap, high $h_{sl}$, but **subcooling** & **segregation** | | **Inorganic (salts)** | NaNO₃, KNO₃ | 170–250 | 220–330 | Used in CSP | | **Eutectics** | Salt-salt, salt-water mixtures | 200–400 | 20–500 | Custom melting point | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget that PCMs suffer from: > 1. **Subcooling** — PCM solidifies below its nominal melting point (defeats isothermal benefit) > 2. **Low thermal conductivity** — PCMs are mostly **poor conductors** (0.2–0.5 W/m·K) — needs **fins/nanoparticle doping** > 3. **Cycle degradation** — phase segregation after repeated melt/solidify ### 2.2.2 Total Heat Stored in a PCM System (with sensible + latent components) $$Q_{total} = m \left[ c_{p,s} (T_m - T_i) + h_{sl} + c_{p,l} (T_f - T_m) \right]$$ Where: - $T_i$ = initial temperature (cold, solid) - $T_m$ = melting temperature - $T_f$ = final temperature (hot, liquid) - $c_{p,s}, c_{p,l}$ = specific heats of solid and liquid phases - $h_{sl}$ = latent heat of fusion --- ## 2.3 Heat Transfer Fluids (HTFs) — Deep Dive An HTF is the **circulating medium** that transports heat from the solar collector to the storage tank/load. Unlike storage media (which *hold* heat), HTFs *carry* heat continuously. ### 2.3.1 Required Properties of an Ideal HTF > [!IMPORTANT] > **Selection Criteria for HTF (must remember for KTU):** > 1. **High thermal conductivity** $k$ → faster heat transfer > 2. **High specific heat** $c_p$ → more heat carried per kg > 3. **Low viscosity** $\mu$ → lower pumping power > 4. **Wide liquid range** (low freezing point, high boiling point) > 5. **Thermal stability** (no decomposition at max T) > 6. **Non-toxic, non-flammable, low cost** ### 2.3.2 Common HTFs and Their Characteristics | HTF | Type | $T_{max}$ (°C) | $c_p$ (kJ/kg·K) | Viscosity | Application | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Water** | Liquid | 100 (250 with pressure) | 4.186 | Low | Domestic, low-temp | | **Therminol VP-1** (biphenyl-diphenyl oxide) | Synthetic oil | 400 | 1.6 | Medium | Parabolic troughs | | **Therminol 66** | Synthetic oil | 345 | 2.1 | Medium | Industrial | | **Dowtherm A** | Synthetic oil | 400 | 1.6 | Medium | Process heat | | **Hitec (60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃)** | Molten salt | 538 | 1.55 | Higher at low T | CSP tower storage | | **Hitec XL** | Molten salt | 500 | 1.4 | Lower at low T | CSP, freezing ~120°C | | **Liquid Sodium** | Metal | 880 | 1.3 | Very low | Advanced nuclear/space | | **Air** | Gas | Very high | 1.005 | Very low | Solar towers, dry systems | | **CO₂ (supercritical)** | Gas | > 31 °C, > 7.4 MPa | High effective | Low | Advanced CSP | > [!NOTE] > **Practical Rule of Thumb:** Below **100 °C** → use **water**; 100–400 °C → use **thermal oil**; above 400 °C → use **molten salt** or **air**. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **These formulas are the KTU exam "weapon" — memorize, derive, and practice.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q = m \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | Sensible heat stored | J or kJ | | 2 | $Q = V \, \rho \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | Sensible heat (per unit volume) | J or kJ | | 3 | $Q = m \, h_{sl}$ | Latent heat stored | J or kJ | | 4 | $Q_{PCM} = m [c_{p,s}\Delta T_s + h_{sl} + c_{p,l}\Delta T_l]$ | Total PCM heat | J or kJ | | 5 | $\rho c_p$ | Volumetric heat capacity | kJ/(m³·K) | | 6 | $E_d = Q/m$ | Specific energy density | kJ/kg | | 7 | $E_d^V = Q/V$ | Volumetric energy density | kJ/m³ | | 8 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{c_p \, \Delta T}$ | HTF mass flow rate | kg/s | | 9 | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{\rho \, c_p \, \Delta T}$ | HTF volumetric flow rate | m³/s | | 10 | $P_{pump} = \dfrac{\dot{V} \, \Delta P}{\eta_{pump}}$ | Pumping power | W | | 11 | $Nu = \dfrac{h D}{k}$ | Nusselt number (convection) | — | | 12 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number | — | | 13 | $Pr = \dfrac{\mu c_p}{k}$ | Prandtl number | — | | 14 | $h_{loss} = U \, A \, \Delta T$ | Storage tank loss | W | | 15 | $\tau_s = \dfrac{m \, c_p}{U \, A}$ | Storage time constant | s | | 16 | $\eta_{storage} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{loss}}{Q_{stored}}$ | Storage efficiency | — | --- ## 2.5 Energy Density Comparison — Engineering Significance | Storage Type | Energy Density (kJ/kg) | Energy Density (kJ/L) | Equivalent Heating of Water | |---|---|---|---| | Water (ΔT = 30 K) | 125 | 125 | 1× | | Rocks (ΔT = 30 K) | 25 | 67 | 0.5× | | Concrete (ΔT = 30 K) | 26 | 57 | 0.5× | | **Paraffin PCM** | 250 | 215 | 2× | | **Salt hydrate PCM** | 250 | 350 | 2.8× | | **Molten salt (ΔT = 300 K)** | 465 | 880 | 7× | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility:** In a 1 MWh residential storage tank: > - **Water** would need $\sim 8$ m³ volume > - **PCM** would need $\sim 4$ m³ — half the size, half the tank cost # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Energy Stored in a Combined Sensible + Latent System **Problem Context:** A salt-hydrate PCM melts from 30 °C to 45 °C as the storage charges. We want the **total** energy stored per kg. **Given (KTU-style data):** - Initial temperature: $T_i = 25\,°\text{C}$ - Melting temperature: $T_m = 32\,°\text{C}$ - Final temperature: $T_f = 45\,°\text{C}$ - $c_{p,solid} = 1.7\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$ - $c_{p,liquid} = 2.4\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$ - $h_{sl} = 250\,\text{kJ/kg}$ ### Step-by-Step Exhaustive Derivation **Step 1 — Identify the three energy contributions.** The storage process has three distinct stages: 1. Sensible heating of **solid** PCM from $T_i$ to $T_m$ 2. **Latent heat** absorbed at $T_m$ during solid → liquid phase change 3. Sensible heating of **liquid** PCM from $T_m$ to $T_f$ **Step 2 — Apply $Q = m c_p \Delta T$ for each sensible stage, and $Q = m h_{sl}$ for the phase change.** For a unit mass ($m = 1$ kg): $$Q_{s1} = 1 \cdot c_{p,s} \cdot (T_m - T_i) = 1.7 \times (32 - 25) = 1.7 \times 7 = 11.9\,\text{kJ}$$ $$Q_{lat} = 1 \cdot h_{sl} = 250\,\text{kJ}$$ $$Q_{s2} = 1 \cdot c_{p,l} \cdot (T_f - T_m) = 2.4 \times (45 - 32) = 2.4 \times 13 = 31.2\,\text{kJ}$$ **Step 3 — Sum all three contributions to get the total energy stored.** $$Q_{total} = Q_{s1} + Q_{lat} + Q_{s2}$$ $$Q_{total} = 11.9 + 250 + 31.2 = 293.1\,\text{kJ/kg}$$ **Step 4 — Compute volumetric energy density (engineering metric).** Assume $\rho_{PCM} = 1400$ kg/m³ (typical salt hydrate): $$E_d^V = Q_{total} \cdot \rho_{PCM} = 293.1 \times 1400 = 410{,}340\,\text{kJ/m}^3 \approx 410\,\text{MJ/m}^3$$ **Step 5 — Compare with water storage at $\Delta T = 30$ K (volume benchmark).** $$E_{d,water}^V = \rho_w \, c_{p,w} \, \Delta T = 1000 \times 4.186 \times 30 = 125{,}580\,\text{kJ/m}^3$$ $$\text{Enhancement Factor} = \frac{410{,}340}{125{,}580} \approx 3.27 \times$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** The PCM stores **3.27× more energy per unit volume** than water in the same temperature window — a critical argument for using PCM in compact residential systems. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: HTF Mass Flow Rate for a Given Solar Collector Output **Problem Context:** A flat-plate solar collector delivers $\dot{Q}_u = 2000$ W useful heat. The water HTF enters at 40 °C and leaves at 75 °C. Find the mass and volumetric flow rates. **Given:** - $\dot{Q}_u = 2000$ W - $T_{in} = 40$ °C, $T_{out} = 75$ °C - $c_{p,w} = 4186$ J/(kg·K) - $\rho_w = 985$ kg/m³ (at ~60 °C average) ### Step-by-Step Exhaustive Derivation **Step 1 — Energy balance on the collector (steady state).** $$\dot{Q}_u = \dot{m} \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$ **Step 2 — Solve for $\dot{m}$.** $$\dot{m} = \frac{\dot{Q}_u}{c_{p,w} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute numerical values (note unit conversion: $c_p$ in J/(kg·K)).** $$\dot{m} = \frac{2000}{4186 \times (75 - 40)} = \frac{2000}{4186 \times 35}$$ $$= \frac{2000}{146{,}510} = 0.01365\,\text{kg/s}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to kg/hr and L/hr (engineering-friendly units).** $$\dot{m} = 0.01365 \times 3600 = 49.14\,\text{kg/hr}$$ **Step 5 — Compute volumetric flow rate.** $$\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho_w} = \frac{0.01365}{985} = 1.386 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$\dot{V} = 1.386 \times 10^{-5} \times 3600 \times 1000 = 49.9\,\text{L/hr}$$ **Step 6 — Sanity check using total daily heat (8 hr sunshine) and tank sizing.** Daily useful heat: $Q_{daily} = 2000 \times 8 \times 3600 = 57.6\,\text{MJ} = 16\,\text{kWh}$ Required hot water for 16 kWh at $\Delta T = 35$ K: $$V_{tank} = \frac{Q_{daily}}{\rho c_p \Delta T} = \frac{57.6 \times 10^6}{1000 \times 4186 \times 35} = 393\,\text{L}$$ So a **~400 L tank** is needed. This is exactly the size of a typical 2-person household solar water heater. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Storage Tank Heat Loss & Time Constant **Problem Context:** A cylindrical water storage tank of diameter $D = 0.6$ m, height $H = 1.5$ m$, is insulated with $U A = 2.5$ W/K overall loss coefficient. If the water is initially at 80 °C and ambient is 25 °C, determine: 1. Initial rate of heat loss 2. Time constant of storage 3. Temperature after 12 hours (assuming no solar input) **Given:** - $D = 0.6$ m, $H = 1.5$ m - $U A = 2.5$ W/K - $T_0 = 80$ °C, $T_\infty = 25$ °C - $m_w = \rho V = 1000 \times \pi (0.3)^2 \times 1.5 = 424$ kg ### Step-by-Step Exhaustive Derivation **Step 1 — Apply Newton's law of cooling for initial heat loss.** $$\dot{Q}_{loss}(0) = U A \cdot (T_0 - T_\infty) = 2.5 \times (80 - 25) = 2.5 \times 55 = 137.5\,\text{W}$$ **Step 2 — Set up the energy balance ODE for the tank.** Energy in − Energy out = Energy stored rate: $$-U A (T - T_\infty) = m \, c_p \, \frac{dT}{dt}$$ **Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate.** $$m \, c_p \, \frac{dT}{T - T_\infty} = -U A \, dt$$ Integrating from $T_0$ to $T(t)$: $$\ln\left(\frac{T(t) - T_\infty}{T_0 - T_\infty}\right) = -\frac{U A}{m c_p} t$$ **Step 4 — Identify the time constant $\tau_s$.** $$\tau_s = \frac{m \, c_p}{U A} = \frac{424 \times 4186}{2.5} = \frac{1{,}774{,}864}{2.5} = 709{,}945.6\,\text{s}$$ Convert to hours: $$\tau_s = \frac{709{,}945.6}{3600} \approx 197.2\,\text{hr} \approx 8.2\,\text{days}$$ **Step 5 — Compute temperature after $t = 12$ hr.** $$T(12) = T_\infty + (T_0 - T_\infty) \exp\left(-\frac{t}{\tau_s}\right)$$ $$T(12) = 25 + (80 - 25) \exp\left(-\frac{12 \times 3600}{709{,}945.6}\right)$$ $$= 25 + 55 \times \exp(-0.0609)$$ $$= 25 + 55 \times 0.9409 = 25 + 51.75 = 76.75\,°\text{C}$$ **Step 6 — Engineering interpretation.** > [!NOTE] > **Result:** A well-insulated tank loses only **~3.25 °C in 12 hours** overnight — sufficient for morning use. This is why high-quality insulation (polyurethane foam) is **non-negotiable** in solar storage design. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection Guide for KTU Exam | Operating T Range | Recommended Storage | Recommended HTF | Example Application | |---|---|---|---| | **< 100 °C** | Water (sensible) | Water | Domestic water heating, pool heating | | **30–80 °C** | Paraffin or salt-hydrate PCM | Water | Compact DHW, building thermal mass | | **100–250 °C** | Thermal oil in tank | Therminol VP-1 / water (pressurized) | Low-temp process heat, drying | | **250–400 °C** | Molten salt (Hitec) | Therminol VP-1 | Parabolic trough CSP | | **> 400 °C** | Solid particles (sand, ceramic) | Air / molten salt / liquid Na | Solar tower (central receiver) | | **> 800 °C** | Refractory bricks | Air | Advanced high-temp thermochemical | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Storage & HTF in a Solar Thermal System ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Solar Collector
Flat Plate or Concentrator] --> B[Heat Exchanger 1
Primary Loop] B --> C[Heat Transfer Fluid HTF
Water or Thermal Oil] C --> D[Pump P1
Circulates HTF] D --> E[Heat Exchanger 2
Charges Storage] E --> F[Thermal Storage Tank
Water PCM or Molten Salt] F --> G[Sensed Sensible Q1
mcT] F --> H[Latent Q2 mhsl
PCM Only] F --> I[Sensed Sensible Q3
mcT] F --> J[Insulation Layer
U x A] J --> K[Heat Loss to Ambient Qloss] F --> L[Heat Exchanger 3
Discharges to Load] L --> M[End User
DHW Space Heat Process] M --> N[Pump P2
Secondary Loop] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 style C fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#8B4513,color:#FFF style F fill:#4682B4,stroke:#1C3D5A,color:#FFF style M fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,color:#000 style K fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF ``` > [!NOTE] > **Block-Level Explanation:** > - **Top loop (orange):** HTF carries solar heat from collector to storage > - **Middle block (blue):** Storage tank holds energy in 3 modes (sensible cold, latent, sensible hot) > - **Right loop (green):** Discharges energy to the actual consumer > - **Red arrow:** Heat losses through tank walls (the enemy of storage efficiency) --- ## 4.2 Classification Flowchart of Storage Media and HTFs ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Thermal Energy Storage] --> B[Sensible] A --> C[Latent PCM] A --> D[Thermochemical] B --> B1[Solid] B --> B2[Liquid] B --> B3[Gas] B1 --> B1a[Concrete] B1 --> B1b[Rocks Pebbles] B1 --> B1c[Cast Iron] B1 --> B1d[Bricks Sand] B2 --> B2a[Water] B2 --> B2b[Thermal Oil] B2 --> B3c[Molten Salt] B2 --> B3d[Liquid Sodium] B3 --> B3a[Air] B3 --> B3b[Steam] B3 --> B3c[Supercritical CO2] C --> C1[Organic] C --> C2[Inorganic] C --> C3[Eutectic] C1 --> C1a[Paraffin] C1 --> C1b[Fatty Acids] C2 --> C2a[Salt Hydrates] C2 --> C2b[Salts] D --> D1[Metal Hydrides] D --> D2[Carbonate Loops] D --> D3[Ammonia Synthesis] style A fill:#4B0082,stroke:#000,color:#FFF style B fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 style C fill:#00CED1,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000 style D fill:#DC143C,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF ``` --- ## 4.3 HTF Selection Decision Tree (Temperature-Based) ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Choose HTF] --> Q1{Operating
Temperature?} Q1 -->|Below 100C| Q2[Use Water] Q1 -->|100 to 250C| Q3[Use Pressurized Water or Low Temp Oil] Q1 -->|250 to 400C| Q4[Use Synthetic Oil Therminol VP1] Q1 -->|Above 400C| Q5{Plant Type?} Q5 -->|Central Receiver Tower| Q6[Use Molten Salt Hitec or Air with Particles] Q5 -->|Dish Stirling| Q7[Use Hydrogen Helium or Liquid Sodium] Q5 -->|Advanced sCO2| Q8[Use Supercritical CO2] Q2 --> End[Design Complete] Q3 --> End Q4 --> End Q6 --> End Q7 --> End Q8 --> End style Start fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#000,color:#FFF style Q2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 style Q4 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 style Q6 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF style End fill:#32CD32,stroke:#006400,color:#FFF ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, OEMET617]** *List any **three desirable properties** of a heat transfer fluid used in solar thermal systems. Name the most commonly used HTF for temperatures below 100 °C.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Three desirable properties of an HTF: 1. **High thermal conductivity** — ensures rapid heat transport from collector to storage 2. **High specific heat capacity** — allows the fluid to carry more energy per unit mass, reducing flow rate 3. **Wide liquid range (low freezing point, high boiling point)** — prevents solidification at night and vaporization at peak summer For temperatures below 100 °C, the most commonly used HTF is **water** because of its exceptionally high $c_p$ = 4.186 kJ/(kg·K), low cost, non-toxicity, and ready availability. *(1 mark for each property, 1 mark for naming water)* --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, OEMET617]** *Differentiate between **sensible heat storage** and **latent heat storage** materials. Give one example of each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Sensible Heat Storage | Latent Heat Storage | |---|---|---| | Physical basis | Temperature change in a single phase | Phase change at nearly constant T | | Energy equation | $Q = m c_p \Delta T$ | $Q = m h_{sl}$ | | Energy density | Low–moderate (50–200 kJ/kg) | High (150–400 kJ/kg) | | Temperature during storage | Continuously varying | Nearly constant | | Example | Water (heating from 30 to 80 °C) | Paraffin wax (melting at 60 °C) | *(2 marks for differentiation table, 1 mark for examples)* --- ## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, OEMET617]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of thermal energy storage systems** with neat diagrams. Compare their energy densities. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A flat-plate solar collector heats water from 35 °C to 80 °C at a useful heat gain of 1800 W. Calculate: - (i) The mass flow rate of water (HTF) - (ii) The daily hot water production (assume 8 hours of equivalent sunshine) - (iii) The required storage tank volume for full day storage. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A: **(a) Classification of TES Systems (7 marks):** Thermal energy storage is classified into three main categories: **1. Sensible Heat Storage:** Stores energy by raising the temperature of a material. The quantity stored is $Q = m c_p \Delta T$. - **Solid media:** rocks, concrete, sand, cast iron, firebricks — used in packed-bed storage - **Liquid media:** water (most common below 100 °C), thermal oil, molten salts (Hitec) - **Gaseous media:** air (used in solar towers) **2. Latent Heat Storage (PCM):** Stores energy during phase transition (mostly solid↔liquid) at nearly constant temperature. - **Organic:** paraffin waxes, fatty acids — $h_{sl}$ ≈ 200–250 kJ/kg - **Inorganic:** salt hydrates, molten salts — $h_{sl}$ ≈ 200–400 kJ/kg - **Eutectic:** salt-salt or salt-water mixtures with custom melting points **3. Thermochemical Storage:** Stores energy through reversible chemical reactions. - Examples: metal hydride decomposition (e.g., MgH₂), ammonia synthesis - Highest energy density (up to 3000 kJ/kg), but technologically complex **Comparison of Energy Densities:** | Storage Type | Energy Density (kJ/kg) | Typical Application | |---|---|---| | Water (ΔT = 50 K) | ~210 | Domestic | | Rocks (ΔT = 50 K) | ~42 | Packed-bed | | Paraffin PCM | ~250 | Latent storage | | Salt-hydrate PCM | ~300 | Building thermal mass | | Molten salt (ΔT = 300 K) | ~465 | CSP plants | | Thermochemical | 500–3000 | Future systems | *(Diagrams: 2 marks; classification: 3 marks; comparison: 2 marks = 7 marks total)* --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** - $T_{in} = 35$ °C, $T_{out} = 80$ °C - $\dot{Q}_u = 1800$ W - $c_{p,w} = 4186$ J/(kg·K) - $\rho_w = 985$ kg/m³ - $t = 8$ hr **Step 1 — Calculate mass flow rate.** $$\dot{m} = \frac{\dot{Q}_u}{c_p (T_{out} - T_{in})} = \frac{1800}{4186 \times (80 - 35)} = \frac{1800}{188{,}370} = 9.55 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{kg/s}$$ $$\dot{m} = 0.00955 \times 3600 = 34.4\,\text{kg/hr}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate daily hot water production.** Total daily useful heat: $Q_{day} = \dot{Q}_u \times t = 1800 \times 8 \times 3600 = 5.184 \times 10^7\,\text{J} = 51.84\,\text{MJ}$ Daily hot water mass: $m_{day} = \dot{m} \times t = 0.00955 \times (8 \times 3600) = 275\,\text{kg}$ Daily hot water volume: $V_{day} = \dfrac{m_{day}}{\rho_w} = \dfrac{275}{985} = 0.279\,\text{m}^3 = 279\,\text{L/day}$ **Step 3 — Calculate required storage tank volume.** Assuming the tank stores water from 35 °C (cold) to 80 °C (hot), and is fully drained per day, the tank volume equals the daily production: $$V_{tank} = 0.279\,\text{m}^3 \approx 280\,\text{L}$$ *(i) 2 marks, (ii) 2.5 marks, (iii) 2.5 marks = 7 marks total)* --- ### Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, OEMET617]** **(a)** Discuss **Phase Change Materials (PCMs)** as latent heat storage media in solar systems. What are their advantages and limitations? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A cylindrical water storage tank of diameter 0.5 m and height 1.2 m is used in a solar water heating system. The overall heat loss coefficient is $UA = 3.0$ W/K. If the water temperature is initially 75 °C and ambient is 28 °C, find: - (i) The initial rate of heat loss - (ii) The storage time constant - (iii) The temperature after 10 hours **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B: **(a) PCMs in Solar Systems (7 marks):** **Definition:** Phase Change Materials (PCMs) are substances that absorb/release large quantities of **latent heat** during a phase transition (typically solid ↔ liquid) at a near-constant temperature called the *phase change temperature* or *melting point*. **Classification:** - **Organic PCMs:** Paraffin waxes (n-eicosane, n-docosane), fatty acids — chemically stable, non-corrosive, but low thermal conductivity - **Inorganic PCMs:** Salt hydrates (Glauber's salt Na₂SO₄·10H₂O), molten salts (NaNO₃, KNO₃) — higher latent heat, lower cost - **Eutectic mixtures:** Customized melting points for specific applications **Advantages of PCMs:** 1. **High energy density** (5–10× sensible storage per unit mass) — compact storage 2. **Near-isothermal operation** — ideal for constant-temperature loads 3. **Wide selection of melting points** — match storage T to demand T 4. **Reusable over thousands of cycles** with proper encapsulation 5. **Reduces storage volume and hence capital cost** **Limitations:** 1. **Low thermal conductivity** (0.2–0.5 W/m·K) — requires **fins, microencapsulation, or nano-doping** to improve 2. **Subcooling** — PCM may not solidify at nominal T, causing incomplete discharge 3. **Phase segregation** in salt hydrates (heavy ions settle, change composition) 4. **Container compatibility issues** — some PCMs are corrosive to metals 5. **Cycle degradation** over time, reducing effective latent heat **Applications in Solar Systems:** Domestic hot water tanks (paraffin @ 55–65 °C), building thermal mass walls (salt hydrates @ 25–30 °C), CSP latent storage (molten salts @ 220–300 °C). *(Definition: 1 mark, classification: 2 marks, advantages: 2 marks, limitations: 2 marks = 7 marks total)* --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** - $D = 0.5$ m, $H = 1.2$ m - $UA = 3.0$ W/K - $T_0 = 75$ °C, $T_\infty = 28$ °C - $t = 10$ hr - $c_{p,w} = 4186$ J/(kg·K) **Step 1 — Calculate tank volume and water mass.** $$V = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} H = \frac{\pi (0.5)^2}{4} \times 1.2 = 0.2356\,\text{m}^3$$ $$m = \rho V = 1000 \times 0.2356 = 235.6\,\text{kg}$$ **Step 2 — Initial rate of heat loss.** $$\dot{Q}_{loss}(0) = UA (T_0 - T_\infty) = 3.0 \times (75 - 28) = 3.0 \times 47 = 141\,\text{W}$$ **Step 3 — Storage time constant.** $$\tau_s = \frac{m \, c_p}{UA} = \frac{235.6 \times 4186}{3.0} = \frac{986{,}222}{3.0} = 328{,}740\,\text{s}$$ $$\tau_s = \frac{328{,}740}{3600} = 91.3\,\text{hr} \approx 3.8\,\text{days}$$ **Step 4 — Temperature after 10 hours.** $$T(t) = T_\infty + (T_0 - T_\infty) \exp\left(-\frac{t}{\tau_s}\right)$$ $$\frac{t}{\tau_s} = \frac{10}{91.3} = 0.1095$$ $$T(10) = 28 + 47 \times e^{-0.1095} = 28 + 47 \times 0.8963 = 28 + 42.13 = 70.13\,°\text{C}$$ **Result:** The tank cools from 75 °C to **~70 °C** in 10 hours — only **4.87 °C drop** overnight, validating the high storage efficiency. *(i) 2 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 3 marks = 7 marks total)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing kJ and J within the same derivation will cost you 1–2 marks instantly. Convert all values to SI (J) before substitution. > 2. **Forgetting density:** Many students write $Q = m c_p \Delta T$ but fail to multiply by $\rho$ when computing volumetric capacity — this is a frequent 1-mark loss. > 3. **Ignoring sensible contributions in PCM:** The total PCM energy equation is $Q = m[c_{p,s}\Delta T_s + h_{sl} + c_{p,l}\Delta T_l]$ — *not* just $m h_{sl}$. Writing only the latent term loses 2 marks. > 4. **Confusing $c_p$ (J/kg·K) vs $\rho c_p$ (J/m³·K):** KTU questions often ask "energy stored per unit volume" — students who report per-mass values lose 1 mark. > 5. **Wrong sign in cooling equation:** Use $(T_0 - T_\infty)$ not $(T_\infty - T_0)$ for initial losses — careful with the direction of heat flow. > 6. **Omitting classification diagrams in Part B:** For 7-mark theory questions, KTU examiners **expect at least one labeled diagram or table** — its absence typically results in 1 mark deduction. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Storage Media & Heat Transfer Fluids (KTU Module 2):** - ☐ **Three storage modes:** Sensible ($mc_p\Delta T$), Latent ($mh_{sl}$), Thermochemical ($\Delta H_{rxn}$) - ☐ **Water's unbeatable $c_p$:** 4.186 kJ/(kg·K) — the highest among common liquids - ☐ **PCM = compact storage:** Energy density 3–5× that of water for the same temperature range - ☐ **Subcooling & segregation:** Two main PCM failure modes to mention in theory - ☐ **HTF selection rule:** Water < 100 °C; Thermal oil 100–400 °C; Molten salt > 400 °C - ☐ **Therminol VP-1** is the **standard oil HTF** for parabolic trough CSP up to 400 °C - ☐ **Hitec salt** (60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃): used in solar tower storage up to 538 °C - ☐ **Storage time constant** $\tau_s = m c_p / (UA)$ — larger $m$ or smaller $UA$ means better retention - ☐ **Energy density enhancement factor** of PCM over water in same ΔT ≈ 3–4× — quantify it in numerical problems - ☐ **Volumetric heat capacity** $\rho c_p$ is the key figure of merit for storage media comparison - ☐ **Pump power** depends on $\dot V$ and pressure drop $\Delta P$, *not* directly on heat transported - ☐ **Storage efficiency** = 1 − (Q_loss / Q_stored); improved by **insulation** and **larger $m$** - ☐ **Molten salts** are *also* used as both storage medium and HTF in two-tank CSP systems - ☐ **Supercritical CO₂** is the emerging HTF for next-generation CSP (Brayton cycle) - ☐ **Formula Table 2.4** in Section 2.4 contains the **16 must-know equations** for KTU problems > **Final Note:** Master the **3 storage equations** (sensible, latent, combined PCM), the **HTF mass flow equation**, and the **cooling ODE** for time-constant problems — these five items alone account for **~70% of the marks** in Module 2's examination.

topics.title: Principles and Performance Analysis, Photovoltaic Materials, Modules, Array, Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) etc
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET617 - SOLAR ENERGY CONSERVATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Principles and Performance Analysis, Photovoltaic Materials, Modules, Array, Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) etc

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Photovoltaic (PV) Effect — Formal Definition

The **Photovoltaic Effect** is a physical and chemical phenomenon (specifically a photoelectric effect) in which a semiconductor material exposed to incident photons of energy **greater than or equal to its band-gap energy ($E_g$)** generates a voltage and a corresponding electric current, thereby directly converting solar radiant energy into usable DC electrical energy at the atomic level.

The foundational quantum relationship governing this conversion is expressed by **Planck's Energy Relation**:

$$E_{photon} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

Where:
- $h$ = **Planck's constant = $6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}$**
- $\nu$ = frequency of the incident photon (Hz)
- $c$ = **speed of light in vacuum = $2.998 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$**
- $\lambda$ = wavelength of incident light (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A photon must possess energy **$\geq E_g$** to liberate an electron. If $E_{photon} < E_g$, the photon passes through or is transmitted as heat — this is the core reason why different semiconductor materials (Si, GaAs, CdTe, CIGS) respond to different spectral bands.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Elevator & The Staircase"

Imagine an electron inside a silicon crystal as a person trapped on the **ground floor of a building (Valence Band)**. The **roof of the building is the Conduction Band**, and the floor-to-roof distance is the **Band-gap ($E_g$)**.

- If you push the person with **too little energy (a weak photon, like a red-light photon)**, they stumble on the stairs and fall back to the ground floor — *no electricity produced*.
- If you push them with **exactly the right amount of energy ($E \geq E_g$, like a blue-light photon)**, they reach the roof and can now freely move (conduct electricity) — *this is the PV effect*.
- If you push them with **way too much energy**, they fly past the roof — the **excess energy is wasted as heat** (thermalization loss). This is why there is an *optimal band-gap* (~1.34 eV) for the solar spectrum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** A PV cell is essentially a "one-way escalator" for electrons — built using a **p-n semiconductor junction** that separates the freed electrons (n-side) from the holes (p-side), creating a DC voltage.

## 1.3 PV Materials — The Engineering Taxonomy

Photovoltaic materials are classified based on their **crystallographic structure, generation, and conversion efficiency**:

| Generation | Material Family | Examples | KTU-relevant Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1st Gen** | Crystalline Silicon (c-Si) | Mono-c-Si, Multi-c-Si | ~90% of global market; robust; $\eta \approx 15\text{–}22\%$ |
| **2nd Gen** | Thin-Film Amorphous / Poly-crystalline | a-Si, CdTe, CIGS, GaAs | Lower cost, flexible, lower $\eta$ (~10–13%) |
| **3rd Gen** | Emerging / Multi-junction | Perovskite, DSSC, Organic PV, Quantum Dots | Lab efficiencies exceed 30%; R&D focus |
| **4th Gen** | Hybrid / Nano-composite | Tandem cells, Nano-wire arrays | Future scope |

## 1.4 PV Module, Array, and System Hierarchy

- **Cell** → A single p-n junction (typically 0.5 V to 0.7 V open-circuit; ~3 A short-circuit for 6-inch wafer).
- **Module** → A sealed, weatherproof assembly of typically **36 or 72 cells** connected in series (gives ~18 V or ~36 V $V_{oc}$).
- **Array** → A mechanically integrated and electrically interconnected group of modules (series = "string"; parallel = "array").
- **System** → Array + **MPPT charge controller / inverter** + BOS (Balance of System: mounting, wiring, protection).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vocabulary for KTU Board Exams:** A "module" is what you buy (the panel on the roof). An "array" is the entire DC power-generating field of many modules.

## 1.5 Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) — Definition

**MPPT** is a real-time, dynamic electronic control algorithm implemented in a **DC-DC converter (or DC-AC inverter)** that continuously perturbs and observes the operating voltage/current of the PV array to force it to operate at the **knee point of its I-V curve** where the product $P = V \times I$ is maximized for the given instantaneous irradiance ($G$) and cell temperature ($T_c$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why MPPT is mandatory:** Without MPPT, a PV array operates at a fixed voltage determined by the battery or grid, and can lose **20% to 35%** of available energy. With MPPT, harvest efficiency is optimized continuously.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Photovoltaic Conversion — Step-by-Step Physics

1. **Photon Absorption:** An incident solar photon with $E_{photon} \geq E_g$ strikes the semiconductor and is absorbed, transferring its energy to a valence electron.
2. **Electron-Hole Pair (EHP) Generation:** The electron is excited from the **Valence Band (VB)** to the **Conduction Band (CB)**, leaving behind a positively charged "hole" in the VB.
3. **Charge Separation at the Junction:** The built-in electric field of the **p-n junction** (formed via doping — p-type: Boron; n-type: Phosphorus for silicon) sweeps electrons toward the n-side and holes toward the p-side.
4. **Collection at Contacts:** Metallic front (grid fingers) and back (full plate) contacts collect the charge carriers, which flow through an external load, doing useful electrical work.
5. **Recombination Loss:** Some EHPs recombine (radiatively or non-radiatively) before being collected — a key loss mechanism captured by the **series resistance ($R_s$) and shunt resistance ($R_{sh}$)** in the equivalent circuit.

## 2.2 Equivalent Circuit of a Practical PV Cell

The **Single-Diode Model** of a real PV cell consists of:
- A **photo-generated current source** ($I_L$ or $I_{ph}$) — proportional to irradiance.
- A **diode** representing the p-n junction diffusion current ($I_D$).
- A **series resistance** ($R_s$) — ohmic losses in contacts and bulk.
- A **shunt (parallel) resistance** ($R_{sh}$) — leakage across the junction.

Applying **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at the output node:

$$I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{a V_t}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

Where:
- $I$ = terminal output current (A)
- $V$ = terminal output voltage (V)
- $I_{ph}$ = photo-generated current (A)
- $I_0$ = reverse saturation current of the diode (A)
- $a$ = diode ideality factor (typically $1 \leq a \leq 2$)
- $V_t = \dfrac{kT}{q}$ = thermal voltage $\approx 25.85\ \text{mV}$ at $T = 300\ \text{K}$
- $k$ = **Boltzmann constant = $1.381 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$**
- $q$ = **electron charge = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$**

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Photo-current $I_{ph}$** | $I_{ph} = I_{ph,STC} \cdot \dfrac{G}{G_{STC}} \cdot [1 + \alpha_I (T_c - T_{STC})]$ | A | Effect of irradiance $G$ and temperature $T_c$ |
| 2 | **Reverse saturation current $I_0$** | $I_0 = I_{0,STC} \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_c}{T_{STC}}\right)^3 \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_g}{a k}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{STC}} - \dfrac{1}{T_c}\right)\right]$ | A | Temperature-dependent leakage |
| 3 | **Open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$** | $V_{oc} \approx a V_t \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | V | Critical for array sizing |
| 4 | **Short-circuit current $I_{sc}$** | $I_{sc} \approx I_{ph}$ (when $V = 0$) | A | Proportional to $G$ |
| 5 | **Fill Factor (FF)** | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Quality indicator of a cell |
| 6 | **Cell / Module efficiency $\eta$** | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{G \cdot A_c} = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{G \cdot A_c}$ | % | Primary performance metric |
| 7 | **Maximum power** | $P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$ | W (or kW) | The "harvested" power |
| 8 | **Module $V_{oc}$ (series)** | $V_{oc,array} = N_s \cdot V_{oc,cell} - N_s \cdot \vert \Delta V_T \vert$ | V | Design rule for strings |
| 9 | **Array $I_{sc}$ (parallel)** | $I_{sc,array} = N_p \cdot I_{sc,module}$ | A | Design rule for parallel branches |
| 10 | **Temperature coefficient of $P_{max}$** | $\dfrac{\partial P_{max}}{\partial T_c} \approx -0.4\ \%/^\circ\text{C}$ (c-Si) | %/°C | Why hot panels lose power |
| 11 | **Photon flux (for $E \geq E_g$)** | $\Phi = \int_{0}^{\lambda_g} \dfrac{G(\lambda) \lambda}{hc} d\lambda$ | photons/m²/s | Theoretical upper limit |
| 12 | **MPPT converter duty cycle (boost)** | $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{1 - D}$ | V | MPPT hardware interface |

> [!NOTE]
> **STC = Standard Test Conditions:** $G_{STC} = 1000\ \text{W/m}^2$, $T_{STC} = 25\ ^\circ\text{C}$, AM 1.5 spectrum. Every PV datasheet is rated at STC.

## 2.4 Effect of Irradiance and Temperature on I-V Curve

- **Irradiance (G) ↑** → $I_{sc}$ rises **proportionally**; $V_{oc}$ rises **logarithmically**. Result: $P_{max} \uparrow$ dramatically.
- **Cell Temperature (Tc) ↑** → $I_{sc}$ rises slightly (band-gap narrowing), but $V_{oc}$ **falls sharply** (~2 mV/°C per cell). Result: $P_{max} \downarrow$ (~0.4–0.5%/°C loss for c-Si).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world insight:** A module in sunny Kerala at 45°C cell temperature may produce **15–20% LESS power** than its STC rating, even with the same 1000 W/m² sun. This is why thermal management and MPPT are critical.

## 2.5 Module-to-Array Configurations — Engineering Utility

| Configuration | Voltage | Current | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **$N_s$ in series** (string) | $\uparrow N_s \cdot V$ | unchanged | High DC bus voltage (e.g., 600 V / 1000 V) |
| **$N_p$ in parallel** | unchanged | $\uparrow N_p \cdot I$ | Higher current capacity |
| **Series-Parallel (SP)** | tuned | tuned | Standard utility-scale arrays |
| **Total- Cross-Tied (TCT)** | same as SP | redistributes mismatch current | Better partial-shade tolerance |

> [!WARNING]
> **Mismatch losses** occur when modules in a string have differing $I-V$ characteristics (e.g., due to partial shading). TCT and **bypass diodes** (typically 2–3 per module) are engineered solutions.

## 2.6 MPPT — The "Why" and "How"

A PV array's $P$-$V$ curve is **non-linear** and depends on $G$ and $T_c$. The **Maximum Power Point (MPP)** is the unique operating point where $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$.

Since $P = V \cdot I$:

$$\frac{dP}{dV} = I + V \frac{dI}{dV} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{dI}{dV} = -\frac{I}{V}$$

This is the **fundamental condition** that MPPT algorithms must satisfy in real-time.

### Popular MPPT Algorithms (KTU 2024 Module-3 Syllabus)

| Algorithm | Principle | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P&O (Perturb & Observe)** | Perturb $V$, observe $\Delta P$; keep direction if $\Delta P > 0$ | Simple, low-cost | Oscillates around MPP; poor under rapid $G$ changes |
| **Incremental Conductance (IncCond)** | Compares $\Delta I / \Delta V$ to $-I/V$ | Fast, accurate tracking | Slightly more complex; sensor noise sensitive |
| **Fractional $V_{oc}$ / $I_{sc}$** | $V_{mpp} \approx k_1 \cdot V_{oc}$; $I_{mpp} \approx k_2 \cdot I_{sc}$ | Very simple | Not truly "maximum" — approximation only |
| **Fuzzy Logic / Neural Net** | AI-based adaptation | Excellent under partial shade | Computational overhead |
| **Constant Voltage** | Hold $V \approx 0.78 \cdot V_{oc}$ | Cheapest | Not adaptive; weather-dependent errors |

## 2.7 Hardware Implementation of MPPT

The MPPT controller is inserted **between the PV array and the load/battery**, typically using a **DC-DC converter**:

- **Buck Converter** → steps voltage *down* (e.g., 36 V module → 12 V battery)
- **Boost Converter** → steps voltage *up* (e.g., 18 V module → 48 V bus)
- **Buck-Boost / Cuk / SEPIC** → bidirectional flexibility

The **PWM duty cycle ($D$)** of the converter's MOSFET switch is varied by the MPPT algorithm, dynamically adjusting the **input impedance seen by the PV array** so it always presents the optimal $V_{mpp}$ to the source.

$$R_{in,converter} = R_{load} (1 - D)^2 \quad \text{(for a buck converter)}$$

By matching $R_{in}$ to the array's **dynamic impedance at MPP** ($R_{mpp} = V_{mpp} / I_{mpp}$), **Maximum Power Transfer Theorem** ensures optimum harvest.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** MPPT is what makes rooftop solar in Kerala cost-effective — without it, even premium modules would underperform by ~30% during cloudy spells.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Photon Energy to Band-gap Cut-off Wavelength

A photon of wavelength $\lambda$ carries energy $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$. For the photon to be absorbed and generate an EHP, the **cut-off (absorption edge) wavelength** $\lambda_g$ is defined by the condition $E_{photon} = E_g$:

$$\frac{hc}{\lambda_g} = E_g$$

Solving for the cut-off wavelength:

$$\lambda_g = \frac{hc}{E_g}$$

Substituting standard constants with $E_g$ in **electron-volts (eV)** (where $1\ \text{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J}$):

$$\lambda_g\,(\mu\text{m}) = \frac{1.24}{E_g\,(\text{eV})}$$

**Numerical Example (Silicon, $E_g = 1.12\ \text{eV}$):**

$$\lambda_g = \frac{1.24}{1.12} \approx 1.107\ \mu\text{m} = 1107\ \text{nm}$$

**Interpretation:** Silicon absorbs photons with $\lambda \leq 1107$ nm (visible + near-IR). Photons of longer wavelength (far-IR) pass through and are not absorbed. This sets the **theoretical upper spectral limit** of any Si-based solar cell.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Open-Circuit Voltage from the Diode Equation

At open circuit, $I = 0$. The current equation becomes:

$$0 = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{a V_t}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{V_{oc}}{R_{sh}}$$

Assuming an *ideal* cell with $R_{sh} \to \infty$:

$$I_{ph} = I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{a V_t}\right) - 1\right]$$

Since $I_{ph} \gg I_0$ (typically by 8–10 orders of magnitude), $\exp$ term dominates:

$$\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{a V_t}\right) \approx \frac{I_{ph}}{I_0}$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\frac{V_{oc}}{a V_t} = \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_0}\right)$$

$$\boxed{V_{oc} = a V_t \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right) \approx a V_t \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_0}\right)}$$

**KTU Numerical Illustration:**

Given: $I_{ph} = 3.2\ \text{A}$, $I_0 = 1 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{A}$, $a = 1.3$, $T = 300\ \text{K}$, so $V_t = 25.85\ \text{mV}$.

$$V_{oc} = 1.3 \times 0.02585 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{3.2}{10^{-9}}\right)$$

$$V_{oc} = 0.03361 \times \ln(3.2 \times 10^{9})$$

$$V_{oc} = 0.03361 \times 21.886 = 0.7356\ \text{V}$$

This matches a typical silicon cell's open-circuit voltage of ~0.72 V at STC. ✓

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Power Point Condition

We begin with the simplified ideal diode equation (neglecting $R_s$ and $R_{sh}$ for the MPP analysis):

$$I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V}{a V_t}\right) - 1\right]$$

The power delivered to the load is:

$$P = V \cdot I = V \cdot \left\{I_{ph} - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V}{a V_t}\right) - 1\right]\right\}$$

At MPP, $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$. Differentiating with respect to $V$:

$$\frac{dP}{dV} = I + V \frac{dI}{dV} = 0$$

Compute $\dfrac{dI}{dV}$:

$$\frac{dI}{dV} = -\frac{I_0}{a V_t} \exp\!\left(\frac{V}{a V_t}\right)$$

Substituting:

$$I - \frac{V \cdot I_0}{a V_t} \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{mpp}}{a V_t}\right) = 0$$

$$I_{ph} - I_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{mpp}}{a V_t}\right) - \frac{I_0 V_{mpp}}{a V_t} \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{mpp}}{a V_t}\right) = 0$$

Using the implicit definition $I_{mpp} = I_{ph} - I_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{mpp}}{a V_t}\right) + I_0$:

$$I_{mpp} = I_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{mpp}}{a V_t}\right) \left(1 + \frac{V_{mpp}}{a V_t}\right) - I_0$$

**Result (transcendental equation for $V_{mpp}$):**

$$\boxed{V_{mpp} = a V_t \ln\!\left[\frac{a V_t (I_{ph} + I_0) - I_{mpp}}{a V_t \cdot I_0}\right]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This transcendental equation has no closed-form solution, which is precisely why **iterative MPPT algorithms** (P&O, IncCond) are needed in practice.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Fill Factor (FF) Empirical Approximation (Green's Equation)

For an *ideal* solar cell (no $R_s$, infinite $R_{sh}$), an empirical fit by **M. A. Green (1981)** gives FF as a function of $V_{oc}$ (in normalized form $v_{oc} = V_{oc} / (a V_t)$):

$$FF_0 = \frac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}$$

**Numerical check:** For $V_{oc} = 0.62\ \text{V}$ and $a V_t = 0.02585\ \text{V}$, $v_{oc} = 0.62 / 0.02585 = 23.98$.

$$FF_0 = \frac{23.98 - \ln(24.70)}{24.98} = \frac{23.98 - 3.207}{24.98} = \frac{20.77}{24.98} = 0.831$$

So an *ideal* cell FF ≈ 0.83; a *real* c-Si module typically has $FF \approx 0.70\text{–}0.78$ due to $R_s$, $R_{sh}$, and recombination.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — P&O Algorithm Working Logic (Pseudocode-free Analytical Flow)

**State variables:** $V_k$, $I_k$ at iteration $k$; computed $P_k = V_k I_k$.

**Step 1: Perturb**
Apply a small voltage perturbation $\Delta V$ (positive or negative). New operating point: $V_{k+1} = V_k + \Delta V$.

**Step 2: Measure**
Sample new $I_{k+1}$, compute $P_{k+1} = V_{k+1} I_{k+1}$, evaluate $\Delta P = P_{k+1} - P_k$.

**Step 3: Decision Logic**

| Case | $\Delta V$ | $\Delta P$ | Action | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | $> 0$ | $> 0$ | Continue perturbing in same direction | Approaching MPP from left |
| B | $> 0$ | $< 0$ | Reverse perturbation direction | Passed the MPP |
| C | $< 0$ | $> 0$ | Continue perturbing in same direction | Approaching MPP from right |
| D | $< 0$ | $< 0$ | Reverse perturbation direction | Passed the MPP |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> P&O is essentially a "hill-climbing" algorithm — it always moves in the direction of increasing $P$, oscillating around the MPP. The amplitude of oscillation is set by $\Delta V$ (called the *perturbation step size*).

**Drawback:** Under rapidly changing irradiance (e.g., moving cloud), P&O can become confused and move *away* from the true MPP. This is a classic KTU question.

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Incremental Conductance (IncCond) Algorithm

The MPP condition from Section 3.3:

$$\frac{dI}{dV} = -\frac{I}{V}$$

Define the **Instantaneous Conductance** $G_I = \dfrac{I}{V}$ and the **Incremental Conductance** $\Delta G = \dfrac{\Delta I}{\Delta V}$. Then:

| Operating Region | Condition | MPPT Action |
|---|---|---|
| Left of MPP (rising $P$ region) | $\dfrac{\Delta I}{\Delta V} > -\dfrac{I}{V}$ | Increase $V$ |
| At MPP (peak) | $\dfrac{\Delta I}{\Delta V} = -\dfrac{I}{V}$ | Hold $V$ constant |
| Right of MPP (falling $P$ region) | $\dfrac{\Delta I}{\Delta V} < -\dfrac{I}{V}$ | Decrease $V$ |

**Advantage over P&O:** IncCond can halt at the *exact* MPP and is more robust under varying irradiance.

## 3.7 Numerical Worked Example — Module Performance under Non-STC

**Given:**
- Module rated: $P_{max,STC} = 320\ \text{W}$ at $G = 1000\ \text{W/m}^2$, $T_c = 25\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- Temperature coefficient of $P_{max}$: $\gamma_P = -0.40\ \%/^\circ\text{C}$
- Operating conditions: $G = 800\ \text{W/m}^2$, $T_c = 45\ ^\circ\text{C}$ (typical Kerala afternoon)
- Module area $A_c = 1.65\ \text{m}^2$ (approx.)

**Step 1: Irradiance scaling (linear, since $I_{sc} \propto G$):**

$$P_{max,\,G=800} \approx 320 \times \frac{800}{1000} = 256\ \text{W}$$

**Step 2: Temperature derating:**

$$\Delta T = 45 - 25 = 20\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

$$\Delta P_T = 256 \times (-0.0040) \times 20 = 256 \times (-0.08) = -20.48\ \text{W}$$

**Step 3: Effective output power:**

$$P_{max,\,real} = 256 - 20.48 = 235.52\ \text{W}$$

**Step 4: Module efficiency at real conditions:**

$$\eta_{real} = \frac{235.52}{800 \times 1.65} = \frac{235.52}{1320} = 17.84\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Many students forget the temperature derating and quote $P = 256$ W as the output. Always apply BOTH irradiance AND temperature corrections.

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Series vs. Parallel Array Sizing

| Parameter | Series String ($N_s$ modules) | Parallel Branches ($N_p$ strings) |
|---|---|---|
| $V_{oc,array}$ | $N_s \cdot V_{oc,mod}$ | $V_{oc,mod}$ (unchanged) |
| $I_{sc,array}$ | $I_{sc,mod}$ (unchanged) | $N_p \cdot I_{sc,mod}$ |
| $P_{max,array}$ | $N_s \cdot P_{max,mod}$ | $N_p \cdot P_{max,mod}$ |
| Mismatch Sensitivity | Voltage mismatch (1 bad module = 1 bad string) | Current mismatch (partial loss only) |
| Hot-spot Risk | High (mitigated by bypass diodes) | Low |

## 4.1 PV Cell — Single-Diode Equivalent Circuit (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    LightSrc["Solar Irradiance G"] --> CellP["PV Cell Active Region"]
    CellP --> PhotoCurr["Photo-Current Source Iph"]
    PhotoCurr --> NodeA["Node A Top Contact"]
    NodeA --> SeriesR["Series Resistance Rs"]
    SeriesR --> NodeB["Node B Load Terminal Positive"]
    NodeB --> Diode["Diode p-n Junction"]
    Diode --> NodeC["Node C Load Terminal Negative"]
    NodeC --> ShuntR["Shunt Resistance Rsh"]
    ShuntR --> NodeA
    NodeB --> Load["External Load R L"]
    Load --> NodeC
```

## 4.2 PV System Hierarchy — Cell to Grid Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Sun["Sun Irradiance G"] --> Cell["PV Cell 0.5 to 0.7 V 3 to 9 A"]
    Cell --> Module["PV Module 36 or 72 cells in series 18 V or 36 V Voc"]
    Module --> String["String Ns modules in series"]
    String --> Array["Array Np strings in parallel"]
    Array --> MPPTCtrl["MPPT DC-DC Controller Boost or Buck"]
    MPPTCtrl --> DCBus["DC Bus 48 V to 1000 V"]
    DCBus --> Inverter["DC to AC Inverter MPPT Stage 2"]
    Inverter --> ACLoad["AC Load or Grid 230 V 50 Hz"]
    MPPTAlgo["MPPT Algorithm P and O or IncCond"] -.->|PWM Duty D| MPPTCtrl
    Array -->|Sensors V I T G| MPPTAlgo
```

## 4.3 MPPT Control Loop — Functional Flow Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    PVArray["PV Array Vpv Ipv"] --> Sense["Voltage and Current Sensors"]
    Sense --> ADC["ADC Sampling"]
    ADC --> Ctrl["Microcontroller or DSP"]
    Ctrl --> AlgoP["P and O Algorithm or IncCond"]
    AlgoP --> DutyOut["PWM Duty Cycle D"]
    DutyOut --> GateDrv["MOSFET Gate Driver"]
    GateDrv --> Conv["DC-DC Converter"]
    Conv --> LoadBus["DC Bus or Battery"]
    LoadBus --> MPPReturn["Sensed V I Feedback"]
    MPPReturn --> Sense
```

## 4.4 P&O Algorithm — Decision State Machine

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start["Read V_k I_k"] --> CalcP["Compute P_k = V_k I_k"]
    CalcP --> Perturb["Perturb V to V k+1 = V k plus or minus delta V"]
    Perturb --> ReadNew["Read V k+1 I k+1"]
    ReadNew --> CalcP2["Compute P k+1"]
    CalcP2 --> TestDP{"delta P greater than 0"}
    TestDP -- "Yes and delta V greater than 0" --> Same["Keep Direction Increase V"]
    TestDP -- "Yes and delta V less than 0" --> Same2["Keep Direction Decrease V"]
    TestDP -- "No and delta V greater than 0" --> Reverse1["Reverse Direction Decrease V"]
    TestDP -- "No and delta V less than 0" --> Reverse2["Reverse Direction Increase V"]
    Same --> Loop["Loop back to Perturb"]
    Same2 --> Loop
    Reverse1 --> Loop
    Reverse2 --> Loop
```

## 4.5 MPPT Hardware — Boost Converter Topology

```mermaid

    Vin["PV Array Vpv"] --> L["Inductor L"]
    L --> SwNode["Switch Node"]
    SwNode --> MOSFET["MOSFET Switch Q"]
    SwNode --> DiodeOut["Diode D Cathode"]
    MOSFET --> Gnd1["Ground"]
    DiodeOut --> Co["Output Capacitor Co"]
    Co --> RLoad["Load R Load"]
    RLoad --> Gnd2["Ground"]
    DiodeOut --> Vout["Vout Boosted DC Bus"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

**Define the Photovoltaic Effect. State Planck's energy relation and explain its significance in solar cell operation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Photovoltaic Effect** is the direct conversion of light (photon) energy into electrical energy at a p-n semiconductor junction, where incident photons of energy **$\geq E_g$** (band-gap) generate electron-hole pairs that are separated by the junction's built-in electric field.

**Planck's energy relation** states that the energy of a photon is:

$$E_{photon} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

where $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}$ and $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$.

**Significance:**
- A photon must satisfy $E_{photon} \geq E_g$ to be absorbed. This sets a **cut-off wavelength** $\lambda_g = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}\,\mu\text{m}$.
- For silicon ($E_g = 1.12$ eV), $\lambda_g \approx 1107$ nm; longer-wavelength photons pass through unabsorbed.
- Photons with $E \gg E_g$ lose excess energy as **heat** (thermalization), reducing efficiency.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**List any three generations of PV materials with one example each and state their typical conversion efficiencies.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Generation | Example | Typical Efficiency |
|---|---|---|
| **1st Gen** — Crystalline Si | Mono-c-Si, Multi-c-Si | 15–22% |
| **2nd Gen** — Thin-Film | CdTe, CIGS, a-Si | 10–13% |
| **3rd Gen** — Emerging | Perovskite, DSSC, Tandem | Lab: 25–33% (commercial: lower) |

Crystalline silicon dominates ~90% of the global PV market due to its abundance, stability, and well-established manufacturing base.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]

**A. (a)** Derive the **I-V characteristic equation of a solar cell** from its equivalent circuit. Clearly state the meaning of each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A silicon PV cell has the following parameters at 25 °C:
$I_{ph} = 3.0\ \text{A}$, $I_0 = 1.5 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{A}$, ideality factor $a = 1.2$, $R_s = 0.01\ \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 300\ \Omega$.
**Compute:** (i) the open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$, and (ii) the short-circuit current $I_{sc}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

The single-diode model of a practical PV cell consists of a photo-current source $I_{ph}$ in parallel with a diode (representing the p-n junction), a shunt resistance $R_{sh}$ (leakage path), and a series resistance $R_s$ (ohmic losses). The current $I$ flows out of the cell terminals to the external load, and $V$ is the terminal voltage.

Applying **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at the top node:

$$I_{ph} = I_D + I_{sh} + I$$

Where:
- $I_D$ = diode diffusion current $= I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V + I R_s}{a V_t}\right) - 1\right]$
- $I_{sh} = \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$ (Ohm's law across $R_{sh}$)
- $V_t = \dfrac{kT}{q}$ = thermal voltage

Substituting back:

$$\boxed{I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V + I R_s}{a V_t}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}}$$

**Meaning of each term:**
- $I$ — terminal output current (load current)
- $V$ — terminal voltage across the load
- $I_{ph}$ — current generated by absorbed photons (proportional to irradiance)
- $I_0$ — reverse saturation current (depends on temperature)
- $a$ — diode ideality factor (1 = ideal; 2 = recombination-dominated)
- $V_t$ — thermal voltage ($\approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K)
- $R_s$ — series resistance (metal contacts, emitter, base)
- $R_{sh}$ — shunt resistance (manufacturing defects, edge leakage)

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $I_{ph} = 3.0\ \text{A}$, $I_0 = 1.5 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{A}$, $a = 1.2$, $T = 25\ ^\circ\text{C} = 298\ \text{K}$.

**Step 1: Compute thermal voltage:**

$$V_t = \frac{kT}{q} = \frac{1.381 \times 10^{-23} \times 298}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.02569\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2: Compute $V_{oc}$** (use $I = 0$, ideal approximation $R_{sh} \to \infty$):

$$V_{oc} = a V_t \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right)$$

$$V_{oc} = 1.2 \times 0.02569 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{3.0}{1.5 \times 10^{-9}} + 1\right)$$

$$V_{oc} = 0.03083 \times \ln(2.0 \times 10^{9} + 1)$$

$$V_{oc} = 0.03083 \times \ln(2.0 \times 10^{9})$$

$$V_{oc} = 0.03083 \times (21.416)$$

$$\boxed{V_{oc} \approx 0.660\ \text{V}}$$

**Step 3: Compute $I_{sc}$** (set $V = 0$ in the I-V equation):

$$I_{sc} = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{I_{sc} R_s}{a V_t}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{I_{sc} R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

Since $R_s$ is very small, the term $\dfrac{I_{sc} R_s}{a V_t} \approx 0$, so $\exp(\cdot) \approx 1$, and the diode current is negligible. Also, $\dfrac{I_{sc} R_s}{R_{sh}} \approx \dfrac{3 \times 0.01}{300} = 10^{-4}$ A (negligible).

$$\boxed{I_{sc} \approx I_{ph} = 3.0\ \text{A}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - (2 marks lost commonly) Students forget the thermal voltage calculation and use $V_t = 25.85$ mV without recomputing at the specified temperature.
> - (2 marks lost) Students use $V_{oc} = \dfrac{a k T}{q} \ln(I_{ph}/I_0)$ but **omit the $+1$** inside the logarithm or **drop the ideality factor $a$**.
> - (1 mark lost) Sign convention reversed in KCL equation. Always write $I = I_{ph} - I_D - I_{sh}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model — Alternative Choice]

**B. (a)** Explain the **principle of MPPT**. Describe the **Perturb and Observe (P&O) algorithm** with a flowchart. State its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 320 W mono-c-Si PV module has $V_{oc} = 45.5\ \text{V}$, $I_{sc} = 9.50\ \text{A}$, and a temperature coefficient of power of $-0.40\ \%/^\circ\text{C}$. Estimate the **module output power and efficiency** when operating at $G = 750\ \text{W/m}^2$ and cell temperature $T_c = 50\ ^\circ\text{C}$. Module area $A_c = 1.95\ \text{m}^2$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — MPPT & P&O Algorithm (7 marks):**

**MPPT Principle:** A PV array's I-V curve is non-linear and varies with irradiance and temperature. There exists a **unique operating point** called the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)** where the product $P = V \times I$ is maximum. The condition is $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$, i.e., $\dfrac{dI}{dV} = -\dfrac{I}{V}$.

A **DC-DC converter** (boost/buck) sits between the PV array and the load. By varying its **PWM duty cycle $D$**, the input impedance presented to the array changes, allowing the array to be forced to operate at $V_{mpp}$ for the prevailing atmospheric conditions.

**P&O Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Measure** the present $V_k$ and $I_k$, compute $P_k = V_k I_k$.
2. **Perturb** the voltage by a small $\Delta V$: $V_{k+1} = V_k + \Delta V$.
3. **Measure** the new $I_{k+1}$ and compute $P_{k+1}$.
4. **Compare:** $\Delta P = P_{k+1} - P_k$
   - If $\Delta P > 0$: **continue** perturbing in the same direction.
   - If $\Delta P < 0$: **reverse** the perturbation direction.
5. **Repeat** until oscillation around the MPP settles.

**Flowchart:** (Draw a 4-box decision diagram with cases $\Delta V > 0 / \Delta P > 0$, $\Delta V > 0 / \Delta P < 0$, etc.)

**Advantages:** Simple, low computational cost, no prior knowledge of module parameters required.

**Limitations:** Oscillates around MPP; under rapidly changing irradiance, can be misled; slow convergence.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1: STC efficiency of the module:**

$$\eta_{STC} = \frac{P_{STC}}{G_{STC} \cdot A_c} = \frac{320}{1000 \times 1.95} = \frac{320}{1950} = 16.41\%$$

**Step 2: Irradiance correction** (assuming $I_{sc} \propto G$ and $V_{oc}$ weakly affected):

$$P_{G} = 320 \times \frac{750}{1000} = 320 \times 0.75 = 240.0\ \text{W}$$

**Step 3: Temperature correction** (with $\gamma_P = -0.40\ \%/^\circ\text{C}$ and $\Delta T = 50 - 25 = 25\ ^\circ\text{C}$):

$$P_T = 240.0 \times [1 + (-0.0040) \times 25]$$

$$P_T = 240.0 \times [1 - 0.10] = 240.0 \times 0.90$$

$$\boxed{P_{max} = 216.0\ \text{W}}$$

**Step 4: Efficiency at real operating conditions:**

$$\eta_{real} = \frac{216.0}{750 \times 1.95} = \frac{216.0}{1462.5}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{real} \approx 14.77\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - (1 mark lost) Forgetting to apply the temperature derating — quoting $P = 240$ W directly.
> - (1 mark lost) Using the **STC** area-times-irradiance in the efficiency denominator but with the real power. Always use the **real** irradiance ($G = 750$ W/m²) in the denominator.
> - (1 mark lost) Confusing the sign of $\gamma_P$; it is **negative** (more heat = less power).
> - (1 mark lost) Not specifying STC conditions explicitly — examiners reward "STC: $G=1000\ \text{W/m}^2$, $T_c = 25\ ^\circ\text{C}$, AM 1.5" in the opening line.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 — High-Density Summary**

- **Photovoltaic Effect** = Photon-induced EHP generation + separation at p-n junction.
- **Photon energy relation:** $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$; for absorption, $E \geq E_g$, giving $\lambda_g = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g (\text{eV})}\ \mu\text{m}$.
- **Planck's constant** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s; **speed of light** $c = 2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s; **Boltzmann's $k$** $= 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K; **electron charge** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C.
- **PV I-V equation (single-diode):** $I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V + I R_s}{a V_t}\right) - 1\right] - \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$.
- **Thermal voltage:** $V_t = kT/q \approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K.
- **Open-circuit voltage:** $V_{oc} \approx a V_t \ln(I_{ph}/I_0)$.
- **Short-circuit current:** $I_{sc} \approx I_{ph} \propto G$.
- **Fill Factor:** $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} I_{mp}}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}$; Green's approximation $FF_0 = \dfrac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}$.
- **Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc} I_{sc} FF}{G A_c}$.
- **STC conditions:** $G = 1000\ \text{W/m}^2$, $T_c = 25\ ^\circ\text{C}$, AM 1.5.
- **PV materials:** 1st Gen c-Si (dominant); 2nd Gen thin-film (CdTe, CIGS, a-Si); 3rd Gen emerging (perovskite, DSSC, tandem).
- **Module** = series string of 36/72 cells; **Array** = series-parallel combination of modules.
- **Temperature effect on $P_{max}$:** $\gamma_P \approx -0.4\ \%/^\circ\text{C}$ for c-Si.
- **MPPT** = DC-DC converter control to force operation at $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$.
- **Algorithms:** P&O (hill-climbing, simple, oscillates); Incremental Conductance (uses $\Delta I/\Delta V$ vs. $-I/V$, faster, exact).
- **Boost converter:** $V_{out} = V_{in}/(1 - D)$; **Buck:** $V_{out} = D \cdot V_{in}$.
- **Mismatch losses** in arrays are mitigated using **bypass diodes** (per 20–24 cells) and **blocking diodes** (in series with each parallel string).
- **Total-Cross-Tied (TCT)** array topology offers **better partial-shade tolerance** than simple SP.
- **Quick mental check:** Hot panel + cloudy day ≈ 25–35% power loss vs. STC rating.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET617 - SOLAR ENERGY CONSERVATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Standalone PV System: Components and Design of Standalone System, Fundamentals of Battery System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ Standalone PV Systems — Core Foundations & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3 Focus):**
> A **Standalone Photovoltaic (PV) System** (also called an *off-grid* or *autonomous* system) is an independent electrical power generation assembly that operates without connection to the utility grid. It relies on **photovoltaic arrays** as the prime energy source, supported by **battery banks** for energy storage, **charge controllers** for battery protection, **power conditioning units (inverters)** for AC load compatibility, and **balance-of-system (BOS)** components for safe and reliable operation.

---

## 🔆 What Exactly Is a Standalone PV System?

A standalone PV system is essentially a self-contained "solar power plant in a box." It harvests sunlight via solar panels, stores the electrical energy in batteries (because the sun doesn't shine 24/7), and then converts that stored DC power into usable AC or DC power for end-user appliances — completely independent of the Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) grid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Board Favorite Question):**
> - **Standalone (Off-Grid) System** → Has battery storage, no grid connection. Powers local loads only.
> - **Grid-Tied System** → No batteries, synchronized to utility grid, exports excess power.
> - **Hybrid System** → Has batteries AND grid connection. Can operate in both modes.

---

## 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — The "Savings Account" Model

Think of a standalone PV system like your **personal bank account**:

| Component | Real-World Analogy | Engineering Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Solar PV Array** | Your monthly salary (income source) | Generates DC electricity from sunlight |
| **Battery Bank** | Your savings account (storage) | Stores excess energy for night/cloudy days |
| **Charge Controller** | Bank's spending limit rules | Prevents overcharge/deep discharge of batteries |
| **Inverter** | Currency exchange counter | Converts DC (rupees) to AC (dollars) |
| **Loads (Appliances)** | Your daily expenses (lights, fan) | Consume the electrical energy |
| **Mounting Structure** | The office building housing your work | Holds panels at optimal tilt angle |
| **Wiring & Protection** | Internet banking security | Safe current transport and fault isolation |

> 💡 **Intuition Hook:** Just as you can't spend your salary *before* you earn it, and you can't withdraw from an empty account — a PV system needs to carefully balance **generation**, **storage**, and **consumption** every single day.

---

## 🧩 Complete List of Standalone PV System Components (KTU Mandate)

A standalone PV system consists of **eight mandatory subsystems** (the KTU examiner loves asking you to "list and explain" these):

1. **PV Array (Module + String + Array configuration)**
2. **Battery Bank (Lead-acid / Li-ion storage)**
3. **Charge Controller (PWM or MPPT type)**
4. **Power Conditioning Unit — Inverter (DC-AC)**
5. **Mounting Structures & Tracking Systems**
6. **Cables, Connectors & Junction Boxes**
7. **Protection Devices (Fuses, MCBs, SPDs, Earthing)**
8. **Load Management / Monitoring System (Energy Meter, Sensors)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric:** The **Average Daily Peak Sun Hours (PSH)** for Kerala is approximately **4.5–5.0 kWh/m²/day**, which is essential for all standalone sizing calculations (we'll use this constant repeatedly in Section 3 derivations).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis — Components, Working & KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

---

## ⚙️ Component-Wise Working Logic

### 1. 🔋 **PV Array — The Prime Mover**

A **solar cell** is essentially a large-area **p-n junction diode** made of doped silicon. When photons (with energy $E = h\nu \geq E_g$) strike the cell, they generate electron-hole pairs. The built-in electric field at the junction sweeps electrons to the n-side and holes to the p-side, producing a **photocurrent**.

**Why multiple cells?** A single silicon cell produces only ~0.5–0.6 V (open-circuit). To power real loads, cells are **series-connected** (to raise voltage) into **modules**, modules into **strings**, and strings into **arrays** (parallel connection raises current).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Vocab:** A **Module** = encapsulated series-connected cells (typically 36 or 72 cells). A **String** = series-connected modules. An **Array** = parallel-connected strings.

---

### 2. 🔌 **Charge Controller — The Battery Guardian**

The charge controller sits **between the PV array and the battery bank**. Its job is to:

- **Prevent Overcharge:** Disconnect PV array when battery voltage exceeds safe limit (e.g., 14.4 V for 12 V lead-acid).
- **Prevent Deep Discharge:** Disconnect loads when battery voltage drops below cutoff (e.g., 10.5 V for 12 V lead-acid).
- **Regulate Charging Current:** Maintains optimal charging profile (Bulk → Absorption → Float stages).

**Two Technologies (KTU Exam Favorite):**

| Feature | PWM Controller | MPPT Controller |
|---|---|---|
| Full Form | Pulse Width Modulation | Maximum Power Point Tracking |
| Efficiency | ~65–75% | ~92–98% |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Operation | Matches array voltage to battery voltage | Decouples array from battery; extracts max power via DC-DC conversion |
| Best For | Small systems, warm climates | Large systems, cold climates, high power |

---

### 3. 🔋 **Battery Bank — The Energy Reservoir**

Batteries store DC electrical energy via reversible electrochemical reactions. For KTU exams, you must understand:

- **Capacity Rating:** Expressed in **Ampere-hours (Ah)** at a specific **C-rate** and discharge time.
- **Depth of Discharge (DoD):** The percentage of battery capacity that has been discharged. For lead-acid, DoD is typically limited to **50–80%** to maximize cycle life.
- **State of Charge (SoC):** Remaining capacity as a percentage of total.
- **Cycle Life:** Number of complete charge-discharge cycles before capacity drops to 80% of rated.

**Lead-Acid vs. Lithium-Ion (KTU Comparison Table):**

| Parameter | Lead-Acid (VRLA/AGM) | Lithium-Ion (LiFePO₄) |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Density (Wh/kg) | 30–50 | 90–160 |
| Cycle Life | 500–1200 | 2000–5000 |
| DoD Limit | 50% | 80–90% |
| Cost (₹/kWh) | Low (~₹8,000) | High (~₹18,000) |
| Maintenance | Periodic water top-up (flooded) | Sealed, no maintenance |
| Operating Temp | -20°C to 50°C | -10°C to 45°C |

---

### 4. 🔄 **Inverter — DC to AC Conversion**

Standalone inverters convert battery DC (12/24/48 V) to AC (230 V, 50 Hz in India). They also provide:
- **Voltage regulation** (±5%)
- **Frequency stability** (50 Hz ± 0.5 Hz)
- **Overload and short-circuit protection**
- **Pure sine wave output** (essential for sensitive electronics)

---

### 5. 🏗️ **Balance of System (BOS)**

Includes cables (sized for <2% voltage drop), fuses, MCBs, surge protection devices (SPDs), earthing (resistance < 5 Ω), lightning arrestors, and mounting structures (fixed tilt ≈ latitude ± 10° in Kerala ≈ 8°–12° N, so optimal tilt ≈ 10°–15° facing south).

---

## 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master This Table!)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TABLE — Memorize for KTU ESE & Internal Exams**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Daily Energy Demand | $E_{load} = \sum (P_i \times t_i)$ | Wh/day | Sum of all (power × hours used) |
| 2 | PV Array Size | $P_{PV} = \dfrac{E_{load}}{\eta_{sys} \times PSH}$ | Wp (peak watt) | $\eta_{sys}$ = 0.75 to 0.85 |
| 3 | Battery Capacity | $C_{bat} = \dfrac{E_{load} \times N_{aut}}{V_{bat} \times DoD \times \eta_{bat}}$ | Ah | $N_{aut}$ = autonomy days |
| 4 | Charge Controller Size | $I_{cc} = 1.25 \times I_{sc,array}$ | A | 25% safety margin |
| 5 | Inverter Size | $P_{inv} = 1.25 \times P_{peak,load}$ | W or VA | 25% safety margin |
| 6 | Cable Size (Area) | $A = \dfrac{2 \times \rho \times L \times I}{V_{drop} \times V_{sys}}$ | mm² | For round-trip, factor 2 |
| 7 | Energy In Array | $E_{PV} = P_{PV} \times PSH \times \eta_{sys}$ | Wh/day | Actual delivered energy |
| 8 | SoC of Battery | $SoC = \dfrac{E_{stored}}{C_{bat} \times V_{bat}} \times 100$ | % | At any instant |
| 9 | Days of Autonomy | $N_{aut} = \dfrac{C_{bat} \times V_{bat} \times DoD \times \eta_{bat}}{E_{load}}$ | days | Reverse of formula 3 |
| 10 | Tilt Angle (Kerala) | $\beta \approx \phi - 5°$ to $\phi + 10°$ | degrees | $\phi$ ≈ 8°–12° for Kerala |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Constants to Use (KTU Approved):**
> - Resistivity of copper $\rho_{Cu} = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}$ Ω·m
> - Standard Test Conditions (STC): Irradiance $G = 1000$ W/m², Cell temp = 25°C, AM 1.5
> - Kerala Peak Sun Hours (PSH) ≈ **4.5–5.0 hrs/day**
> - Battery Round-trip Efficiency $\eta_{bat} \approx 0.80$ (lead-acid), 0.95 (Li-ion)
> - System Efficiency $\eta_{sys} \approx 0.75$

---

## 🏭 Real-World Engineering Utility

Standalone PV systems are critical for:
- **Rural electrification** in remote Kerala hamlets (e.g., Wayanad, Idukki tribal areas)
- **Telecom tower power backup** in forest regions
- **Solar water pumps** for agricultural irrigation
- **Disaster-resilient power** (Kerala floods 2018 use-case)
- **Off-grid homes & hill stations** (Munnar, Vagamon)
- **Solar street lighting** (Kerala Haritha Keralam mission)
