> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Always clearly state the PF value used in the formula *before* plugging in numbers. Students often skip this and lose 1–2 marks. Also, state whether penalty applies or not based on the threshold stated in the problem.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 4 - ESE Pattern]`

#### (a) [7 Marks] With neat block diagrams, explain the Three-Part Tariff. Compare it with the Two-Part Tariff.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The Three-Part Tariff, also known as the **Doherty Rate**, splits the consumer's bill into three distinct components:

$$\boxed{\text{Total Bill (₹)} = \alpha L + \beta P + \gamma E}$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = fixed charge per kW of connected load (₹/kW)
- $\beta$ = charge per kW of actual maximum demand (₹/kW)
- $\gamma$ = running charge per kWh (₹/kWh)
- $L$ = connected load (kW)
- $P$ = maximum demand (kW)
- $E$ = energy consumed (kWh)

**Block Diagram (describe in answer):**

```
            CONSUMER BILL (THREE-PART)
   ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │                                        │
   │   ┌──────────────┐                     │
   │   │ α·L (Connect │ ← Fixed charge      │
   │   │  ed Load)    │   (readiness cost)  │
   │   └──────────────┘                     │
   │                                        │
   │   ┌──────────────┐                     │
   │   │ β·P (Max     │ ← Semi-fixed        │
   │   │  Demand)     │   (capacity cost)   │
   │   └──────────────┘                     │
   │                                        │
   │   ┌──────────────┐                     │
   │   │ γ·E (Energy) │ ← Running charge    │
   │   │              │   (fuel + O&M cost) │
   │   └──────────────┘                     │
   │                                        │
   │   TOTAL = α·L + β·P + γ·E              │
   └────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Comparison with Two-Part Tariff:**

| Feature | Two-Part | Three-Part |
|---|---|---|
| Cost pools separated | 2 | 3 |
| Charges for connected load | Not separately billed | Billed via $\alpha L$ |
| Charges for MD only | Billed via $a \cdot MD$ | Billed via $\beta P$ |
| Equity | Moderate | High |
| Billing complexity | Lower | Higher |
| Used for | LT industries | HT industries |

**Advantages of Three-Part Tariff:**
1. Most equitable to both utility and consumer.
2. Discourages installation of unnecessarily high connected load.
3. Recoveries match the cost-causation principle (each cost pool has a corresponding charge).

**Disadvantage:**
1. Requires accurate measurement of $L$, $P$, and $E$ — multiple meters.

#### (b) [7 Marks] A HT industrial consumer has the following data:

- Connected load: 1500 kW
- Maximum demand: 1200 kW
- Monthly energy consumption: 5,00,000 kWh
- PF: 0.85 lagging

**Tariff:**
- $\alpha = $ ₹100 per kW of connected load per month
- $\beta = $ ₹150 per kW of MD per month
- $\gamma = $ ₹4.00 per kWh
- PF penalty: extra ₹0.20 per kWh for every 0.01 fall in PF below 0.90

**Calculate the monthly electricity bill.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply Three-Part formula:**

$$\alpha L = 100 \times 1500 = \text{₹}1{,}50{,}000$$

$$\beta P = 150 \times 1200 = \text{₹}1{,}80{,}000$$

$$\gamma E = 4.00 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}20{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Bill without PF penalty} = 1{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000 + 20{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}23{,}30{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Apply PF penalty:**

PF is 0.85, which is 0.05 below the threshold 0.90. Penalty applies for every 0.01 drop.

$$\text{Number of 0.01 drops} = \frac{0.90 - 0.85}{0.01} = 5$$

$$\text{Extra charge per kWh} = 5 \times 0.20 = \text{₹}1.00/\text{kWh}$$

$$\text{Penalty amount} = 1.00 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}5{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Total bill:**

$$\boxed{\text{Total Monthly Bill} = 23{,}30{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}28{,}30{,}000}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Many students compute $\alpha L$ and $\beta P$ but forget to *add them separately*. Show all three components in a summation form.
> 2. PF penalty calculation: explicitly show the **number of 0.01 drops**. Writing just "₹1.00" without justification loses 1 mark.
> 3. Final answer without unit (₹) or with wrong unit → 0.5 mark deducted.

---

## 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Methods of Fixing Power Tariff**

- ☐ **Tariff = Price structure** for electricity, designed to recover cost + encourage efficient use.
- ☐ **Two main objectives:** (1) cost recovery, (2) load management.
- ☐ **Flat Rate Tariff** → single ₹/kWh rate; simplest; rarely used for large consumers.
- ☐ **Block Rate Tariff** → slabs (0–250, 251–500, etc.); used for domestic consumers; promotes conservation.
- ☐ **Two-Part Tariff (Hopkinson):** $\text{Bill} = a \cdot MD + b \cdot E$. **Most asked topic.**
- ☐ **Three-Part Tariff (Doherty):** $\text{Bill} = \alpha L + \beta P + \gamma E$. Used for HT industries.
- ☐ **kVA Tariff:** $\text{Bill} \propto \dfrac{1}{\cos\phi}$ → PF penalty.
- ☐ **Average PF Tariff formula:** $\text{Rate} = r \left[\dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{1}{2\cos\phi}\right]$. At PF = 1, factor = 1.
- ☐ **Sliding Scale:** stepwise penalty as PF drops in increments (e.g., per 0.05 drop).
- ☐ **Most Economical PF:** typically 0.95–1.0 for industrial consumers.
- ☐ **Breakeven equation:** $E_{\text{breakeven}} = \dfrac{a \cdot MD}{r_1 - b}$ for comparing flat vs two-part.
- ☐ **Key linkage to solar:** Net metering tariffs (a Module 4 / 5 extension) are based on the same fixed + running components discussed here.
- ☐ **Always show units** (₹, kW, kWh, kVAh) — examiner-friendly practice.
- ☐ **Distinguish MD vs Connected Load:** MD is *measured*; Connected load is *installed*.

---

## 5.7 Quick-Reference Comparison Table (For Last-Minute Revision)

| Tariff | Formula | Bill is Fair? | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flat | $r \cdot E$ | ❌ No | Domestic, BPL |
| Block | Slab-wise $r_i \cdot E_i$ | ⚠ Partial | Domestic |
| Two-Part | $a \cdot MD + b \cdot E$ | ✅ Yes | LT Industrial |
| Three-Part | $\alpha L + \beta P + \gamma E$ | ✅✅ Most fair | HT Industrial |
| kVA Tariff | $r \cdot kVAh$ | ✅ PF-aware | Poor PF industries |
| Avg PF | $r\left(\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2\cos\phi}\right) \cdot kWh$ | ✅✅ Most accurate | Large LT/HT consumers |

> [!TIP]
> **Final Tip for the KTU Exam:** When a problem says *"compare two tariff options"*, structure your answer as: (1) Compute Bill_A, (2) Compute Bill_B, (3) Compute Difference, (4) State which is cheaper and *by how much*. A 14-mark question expects at least 4 such computation steps plus an interpretation line. Showing this structure clearly can fetch 2–3 extra marks on the valuation scale.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple Methods to Calculate the Plant Economy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET617 - SOLAR ENERGY CONSERVATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Simple Methods to Calculate the Plant Economy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Plant Economy** (also termed *Solar Plant Economic Analysis* or *Project Feasibility Assessment*) refers to the systematic, quantitative evaluation of the financial viability of a solar energy power plant over its entire operational lifetime. It integrates engineering performance parameters (energy yield, capacity factor, degradation rate) with macroeconomic indicators (interest/discount rate, inflation, tariff escalation) to determine whether an investment in solar infrastructure is economically justified.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context (Course OEMET617, Module 4), *simple methods* specifically denote the **time-independent and elementary time-dependent techniques** that allow a rapid first-pass judgment of plant viability without resorting to complex stochastic modelling or Monte-Carlo simulations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU OEMET617 M4):**
> Simple methods include — *Simple Payback Period*, *Return on Investment (ROI)*, *Life Cycle Costing (LCC)*, *Net Present Value (NPV)*, *Internal Rate of Return (IRR)*, and the **Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE)**. Mastery of these is mandatory as they appear in nearly every KTU board examination under Module 4.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tapri Shop" Intuition

Imagine a street-side tea shop owner, Mr. Sharma, who wants to install a **solar PV system** on his shop's roof to cut down his ₹8,000/month electricity bill. Before spending ₹3,00,000 on the panels and inverter, he asks three simple questions:

1. *"In how many months will I recover my ₹3 lakh?"* → **Simple Payback Period**
2. *"After discounting the future savings to today's rupees, am I actually profitable, or is inflation eating my profits?"* → **Net Present Value (NPV)**
3. *"At what rate of return is this project exactly break-even?"* → **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)**

**Plant Economy is the engineering discipline that answers exactly these three questions — but for MW-scale solar power plants.** Where Mr. Sharma thinks in months, we think in years, cash flows, and discounted rupees.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants Used

The following standard economic indicators are used throughout this module:

- **Discount Rate (i)** — typically **8 % to 12 %** in Indian renewable energy projects (regulated by SECI / state DISCOMs).
- **Project Lifetime (n)** — **25 years** (standard for crystalline silicon PV as per MNRE guidelines).
- **Degradation Rate** — **0.5 % to 0.7 % per annum** (first-year + linear degradation model).
- **Inflation/Electricity Tariff Escalation (e)** — **3 % to 5 %** per annum in India.
- **Plant Load Factor / Capacity Utilization Factor (CUF)** — **15 % to 22 %** for Indian grid-connected solar PV.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Constants (from MNRE & SECI, India):**
> - Benchmark CUF for solar PV in India: **19 %** (NIC reference).
> - Standard project horizon: **n = 25 years**.
> - Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC): **≈ 10 %** for utility-scale solar in India.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of Simple Plant Economy Methods

Plant economy calculation is built on six interlinked pillars. Each method answers a slightly different managerial question, and a complete KTU answer typically combines **two or more** of them.

### Pillar 1 — Simple Payback Period (SPP)
Measures **how fast** the initial capital is recovered, ignoring the time value of money.

**Logic Steps:**
- Step 1 → Compute total initial investment ($C_0$).
- Step 2 → Compute net annual cash inflow (savings or revenue minus O&M).
- Step 3 → Divide $C_0$ by annual cash flow.

**Limitation:** Ignores the fact that **₹100 today is worth more than ₹100 five years from now** — this is the central flaw corrected by discounted methods.

### Pillar 2 — Return on Investment (ROI)
The simplest profitability ratio — total lifetime profit expressed as a fraction of initial cost.

### Pillar 3 — Net Present Value (NPV)
The **gold standard** of project evaluation. Discounts every future cash flow back to *Year 0* using the discount rate $i$.

**Decision Rule:** Accept the project if **NPV > 0**.

### Pillar 4 — Internal Rate of Return (IRR)
The discount rate at which **NPV becomes exactly zero**. A project's IRR must exceed the hurdle rate (cost of capital) for acceptance.

### Pillar 5 — Life Cycle Cost (LCC) / Annualized Cost
Converts the entire lifetime cost stream into a single **uniform annual cost** using the Capital Recovery Factor (CRF). This is the foundation of LCOE.

### Pillar 6 — Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE)
The **per-unit cost of electricity** generated over the plant's lifetime — the single most quoted metric in solar project reports.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Method | Formula | Decision Rule | Standard Unit |
|---|--------|---------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | Simple Payback Period | $SPP = \dfrac{C_0}{A_{net}}$ | Lower is better | years |
| 2 | ROI | $ROI = \dfrac{\sum CashInflows - C_0}{C_0} \times 100$ | Higher is better | % |
| 3 | Discount Factor (Year $t$) | $DF_t = \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^t}$ | Always $< 1$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Net Present Value | $NPV = -C_0 + \displaystyle\sum_{t=1}^{n}\dfrac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | $NPV \geq 0$ | ₹ (currency) |
| 5 | Capital Recovery Factor | $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Used in LCC | yr$^{-1}$ |
| 6 | Sinking Fund Factor | $SFF = \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | For replacement reserve | yr$^{-1}$ |
| 7 | Annualized Life-Cycle Cost | $LCC_{annual} = C_0 \cdot CRF + C_{O\&M} + C_{fuel}$ | Lower is better | ₹/year |
| 8 | Levelized Cost of Energy | $LCOE = \dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{t=0}^{n}\dfrac{C_t}{(1+i)^t}}{\displaystyle\sum_{t=0}^{n}\dfrac{E_t}{(1+i)^t}}$ | Compare to tariff ₹/kWh | ₹/kWh |
| 9 | Discounted Payback | Find smallest $t$ s.t. $\sum_{k=1}^{t}\dfrac{CF_k}{(1+i)^k} \geq C_0$ | Lower is better | years |
| 10 | Profitability Index | $PI = \dfrac{\sum PV(\text{inflows})}{C_0}$ | $PI \geq 1$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRF vs Annuity Factor:**
> The Capital Recovery Factor $(A/P, i, n)$ is the *reciprocal* of the Present Worth Annuity Factor $(P/A, i, n)$. Many KTU students confuse these — *remember, CRF converts a Present Value into an Annual Value; the Annuity Factor converts an Annual Value into a Present Value.*

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SECI Tender Evaluation:** Bidders quote LCOE; the lowest qualifying bidder wins.
- **Bank Loan Appraisal:** PSU banks (SBI, PNB) require **IRR > 14 %** and **DSCR > 1.2** before disbursing solar project loans.
- **DISCOM Tariff Petitions:** Before state electricity regulatory commissions (e.g., KSERC, RERC), generators must justify tariff using **Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) → LCOE methodology** as mandated by CERC (Central Electricity Regulatory Commission) Regulations, 2019.
- **Rooftop Solar Subsidy (PM-Surya Ghar Yojana):** Payback period is the key metric consumers use to decide on adoption.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)

Consider a present sum $P$ invested at a compound interest rate $i$ for $n$ years. We wish to find the equivalent **uniform end-of-year payment $A$** that, when compounded at rate $i$, exactly recovers $P$ over $n$ years.

**Step 1 — Future Value of the Present Sum $P$:**

$$
FV = P \cdot (1+i)^n
$$

**Step 2 — Future Value of a Uniform Series $A$ (ordinary annuity, end-of-year payments):**

The future value of an annuity is the sum of each payment compounded to year $n$:

$$
FV_A = A + A(1+i) + A(1+i)^2 + \cdots + A(1+i)^{n-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Factor Out $A$ and Recognize the Geometric Series:**

$$
FV_A = A \cdot \left[ 1 + (1+i) + (1+i)^2 + \cdots + (1+i)^{n-1} \right]
$$

This is a finite geometric progression with first term $= 1$, common ratio $r = (1+i)$, and number of terms $n$. The sum of a geometric series is:

$$
S_n = a \cdot \dfrac{r^n - 1}{r - 1}
$$

Applying this identity:

$$
FV_A = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{(1+i) - 1} = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate the Two Future Values (Single Payment = Annuity):**

$$
P \cdot (1+i)^n = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $A/P$ (this is the CRF):**

$$
\dfrac{A}{P} = \dfrac{i \cdot (1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; CRF(i,n) \;=\; \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1} \;}
$$

This completes the rigorous derivation of the Capital Recovery Factor used in every LCOE and LCC calculation.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Complete Plant Economy Assessment

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example: 1 MW Grid-Connected Solar PV Plant (KTU Module 4 Style)**

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| Initial Investment | $C_0$ | ₹ 4,50,00,000 (₹ 4.5 Crore) |
| Annual Energy Generation (Year 1) | $E_1$ | 15,00,000 kWh (≈ 17.1 % CUF) |
| Annual Degradation | $d$ | 0.5 % per year |
| Tariff (Revenue) | $R$ | ₹ 4.00 / kWh |
| Annual O&M Cost | $C_{O\&M}$ | ₹ 5,00,000 / year (escalating at 3 %) |
| Project Life | $n$ | 25 years |
| Discount Rate | $i$ | 10 % |

**Step 1 — Compute the Capital Recovery Factor at $i = 10\%, n = 25$:**

$$
CRF = \dfrac{0.10 \cdot (1.10)^{25}}{(1.10)^{25} - 1}
$$

First compute $(1.10)^{25}$:

$$
(1.10)^{25} = 10.8347
$$

Therefore:

$$
CRF = \dfrac{0.10 \cdot 10.8347}{10.8347 - 1} = \dfrac{1.08347}{9.8347} = 0.11017 \; \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Annualized Capital Cost:**

$$
C_{capital,annual} = C_0 \cdot CRF = 4,50,00,000 \cdot 0.11017 = \text{₹ } 49,57,650 \; / \text{year}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Year-wise Energy Generation with Degradation:**

$$
E_t = E_1 \cdot (1 - d)^{t-1} = 15,00,000 \cdot (0.995)^{t-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Year-wise Revenue (tariff held constant for simplicity):**

$$
Rev_t = E_t \cdot R = 15,00,000 \cdot (0.995)^{t-1} \cdot 4.00
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Simple Payback Period (ignoring time value):**

Year-1 revenue (approx.) = ₹ 60,00,000
Year-1 net cash inflow (Year 1) ≈ ₹ 60,00,000 − ₹ 5,00,000 = ₹ 55,00,000

$$
SPP = \dfrac{4,50,00,000}{55,00,000} \approx 8.18 \; \text{years}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute NPV (compact closed-form using present-worth-of-gradient series):**

The Present Worth of a geometrically decreasing series at rate $g$ is:

$$
PW = A \cdot \dfrac{1 - \left(\dfrac{1+g}{1+i}\right)^n}{i - g}
$$

With $A = Rev_1 - C_{O\&M,1} = 60,00,000 - 5,00,000 = 55,00,000$, $g = -0.005$ (energy degradation), $i = 0.10$:

$$
PW_{inflows} = 55,00,000 \cdot \dfrac{1 - \left(\dfrac{0.995}{1.10}\right)^{25}}{0.10 - (-0.005)}
$$

Compute the ratio:

$$
\dfrac{0.995}{1.10} = 0.9045
$$

$$
(0.9045)^{25} = 0.0901 \quad \text{(approx.)}
$$

Therefore:

$$
PW_{inflows} = 55,00,000 \cdot \dfrac{1 - 0.0901}{0.105} = 55,00,000 \cdot \dfrac{0.9099}{0.105}
$$

$$
PW_{inflows} = 55,00,000 \cdot 8.666 = \text{₹ } 4,76,63,000
$$

$$
NPV = PW_{inflows} - C_0 = 4,76,63,000 - 4,50,00,000 = \text{₹ } 26,63,000
$$

Since **NPV > 0**, the project is **economically viable**.

**Step 7 — Compute LCOE:**

The total discounted lifetime cost is dominated by the initial investment and CRF:

$$
LCOE = \dfrac{C_0 \cdot CRF + C_{O\&M,annual}}{E_{annual,avg}} = \dfrac{49,57,650 + 5,00,000}{14,12,000} \approx \text{₹ } 3.87 \; / \text{kWh}
$$

(Where average annual energy $= 15,00,000 \cdot \dfrac{1-(0.995)^{25}}{0.005 \cdot 25} \approx 14,12,000$ kWh.)

**Conclusion:** At a tariff of ₹ 4.00/kWh, the LCOE of ₹ 3.87/kWh yields a healthy margin of ₹ 0.13/unit.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Method

| Situation in Exam | Best Method to Quote | Reasoning |
|-------------------|----------------------|-----------|
| "How long to recover cost?" | Simple Payback Period | Direct answer; one-line formula |
| "Is the project profitable considering money's time value?" | NPV | Theoretically correct; KTU favourite |
| "Compare two solar plants" | LCOE | Allows direct unit-cost comparison |
| "Find break-even discount rate" | IRR | Algebraically: set NPV = 0 |
| "Find annual equivalent cost" | LCC with CRF | Required for LCOE step |
| "Bank asks for project safety" | Discounted Payback + IRR | Conservative + risk-aware |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Plant Economy Calculation Methodology — Functional Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **logical decision flow** a KTU engineer follows when performing a complete plant economy assessment of a solar PV system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Plant Economy Problem] --> B1[Step 1: Identify Inputs]
    B1 --> B2[Initial Cost C0]
    B1 --> B3[Annual Cash Flows CFt]
    B1 --> B4[Discount Rate i]
    B1 --> B5[Project Life n]
    B1 --> B6[Energy Yield Et]

    B2 --> C1{Choose Method}
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C1
    B6 --> C1

    C1 --> D1[Simple Payback Period]
    C1 --> D2[NPV Calculation]
    C1 --> D3[IRR by Iteration]
    C1 --> D4[LCOE via CRF]

    D1 --> E1[Output: Years]
    D2 --> E2[Output: Rs in Lakhs]
    D3 --> E3[Output: Percent]
    D4 --> E4[Output: Rs per kWh]

    E1 --> F1{Decision Check}
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
    E4 --> F1

    F1 --> G1[Accept if NPV greater than 0]
    F1 --> G2[Reject if IRR less than WACC]
    F1 --> G3[Compare LCOE to Grid Tariff]

    G1 --> H1[End: Project Viable Report]
    G2 --> H2[End: Project Rejected Report]
    G3 --> H3[End: Economic Recommendation]
```

## 4.2 Input-Output Architecture Block Diagram

This block diagram shows how raw engineering inputs (irradiance, capacity, costs) feed into the economic engine and produce bank-grade deliverables.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN[Engineering Inputs]
        I1[Solar Irradiance kWh per m2 per day]
        I2[Plant Capacity MWp]
        I3[Capital Cost Rs Crore]
        I4[O and M Cost Rs per year]
        I5[Degradation percent per year]
        I6[Discount Rate i]
    end

    IN --> CORE[Economic Engine: Time Value of Money]

    subgraph OUT[Economic Outputs]
        O1[Simple Payback Years]
        O2[NPV in Crore Rs]
        O3[IRR Percent]
        O4[LCOE Rs per kWh]
        O5[Discounted Payback Years]
        O6[Profitability Index]
    end

    CORE --> O1
    CORE --> O2
    CORE --> O3
    CORE --> O4
    CORE --> O5
    CORE --> O6

    O1 --> DEC[Final Investment Decision]
    O2 --> DEC
    O3 --> DEC
    O4 --> DEC
    O5 --> DEC
    O6 --> DEC
```

## 4.3 LCOE Derivation Chain — Logical Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Initial Cost C0] --> Q1[Compute CRF from i and n]
    Q1 --> Q2[Annualized Capital Cost]
    R0[Annual O and M Cost] --> Q3[Sum Annual Costs]
    Q2 --> Q3
    Q3 --> Q4[Total Annual Cost]
    S0[Annual Energy kWh] --> Q5[Average Energy with Degradation]
    Q5 --> Q6[Annual Energy Output]
    Q4 --> Q7[Divide Cost by Energy]
    Q6 --> Q7
    Q7 --> Q8[Final LCOE in Rs per kWh]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Simple Payback Period. State its major limitation. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Simple Payback Period** is the time required for the gross cumulative cash inflows from a project to equal the initial capital investment, ignoring the time value of money.

$$
SPP = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment } C_0}{\text{Annual Net Cash Inflow}}
$$

**Major Limitation:** It neglects the **time value of money** and ignores cash flows that occur *after* the payback period. A project with a short payback but a poor long-term tail may still be selected over a more profitable long-term project. Hence, it is used only as a first-pass screening tool, not as a final decision metric.

*(3 marks: 1 definition + 1 formula + 1 limitation)*

---

### Q2. What is the Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE)? Write its mathematical expression. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**LCOE** is the per-unit cost of electricity generated over the entire operational lifetime of an energy plant, expressed in ₹/kWh, that equalizes the present value of total lifetime costs to the present value of total lifetime energy output.

$$
LCOE = \dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+i)^t}}{\displaystyle\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{E_t}{(1+i)^t}}
$$

where $C_t$ is the cost in year $t$, $E_t$ is the energy produced in year $t$, $i$ is the discount rate, and $n$ is the project life. For solar PV, typical Indian LCOE values range from **₹ 2.50 to ₹ 4.00 / kWh** as of 2024.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1**

#### (a) Explain the Net Present Value (NPV) method with its decision rule. A solar project requires an initial investment of ₹ 5,00,000 and generates annual cash inflows of ₹ 1,20,000 for 5 years. Compute the NPV at a discount rate of 10 %. `[7 Marks]`

**Step 1 — Concept of NPV:**

The **Net Present Value** is the algebraic sum of the present values of all cash inflows minus the initial outflow, evaluated at the chosen discount rate $i$.

$$
NPV = -C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}
$$

**Decision Rule:**

- $NPV \geq 0$ → **Accept** the project.
- $NPV < 0$ → **Reject** the project.
- If two projects are mutually exclusive, select the one with the **higher NPV**.

**Step 2 — Tabulate the Discounted Cash Flows:**

| Year $t$ | Cash Flow (₹) | Discount Factor $\dfrac{1}{(1.10)^t}$ | Present Value (₹) |
|-----------|---------------|---------------------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | 1,20,000 | 0.9091 | 1,09,090 |
| 2 | 1,20,000 | 0.8264 | 99,173 |
| 3 | 1,20,000 | 0.7513 | 90,158 |
| 4 | 1,20,000 | 0.6830 | 81,962 |
| 5 | 1,20,000 | 0.6209 | 74,510 |

**Step 3 — Sum the Present Values:**

$$
\sum PV = 1,09,090 + 99,173 + 90,158 + 81,962 + 74,510 = 4,54,893
$$

**Step 4 — Compute NPV:**

$$
NPV = -5,00,000 + 4,54,893 = -\text{₹ } 45,107
$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**

Since $NPV < 0$, the project **must be rejected** at $i = 10\%$.

**Alternative Verification using Annuity Factor:**

$$
(P/A, 10\%, 5) = \dfrac{(1.10)^5 - 1}{0.10 \cdot (1.10)^5} = \dfrac{0.6105}{0.16105} = 3.7908
$$

$$
NPV = -5,00,000 + 1,20,000 \cdot 3.7908 = -5,00,000 + 4,54,896 = -\text{₹ } 45,104
$$

(Approximately equal; small rounding difference.) ✓

---

#### (b) A 100 kWp rooftop solar PV system costs ₹ 45,00,000 (including inverter and BOS). The system generates 1,40,000 kWh/year and offsets grid electricity at ₹ 7.50/kWh. Annual O&M is ₹ 60,000. If the discount rate is 8 % and life is 25 years, calculate the **LCOE** and **Simple Payback Period**. State if the project is viable. `[7 Marks]`

**Step 1 — Capital Recovery Factor:**

$$
CRF = \dfrac{0.08 \cdot (1.08)^{25}}{(1.08)^{25} - 1}
$$

Compute $(1.08)^{25} = 6.8485$.

$$
CRF = \dfrac{0.08 \cdot 6.8485}{6.8485 - 1} = \dfrac{0.5479}{5.8485} = 0.0937 \; \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Annualized Capital Cost:**

$$
C_{cap,annual} = 45,00,000 \cdot 0.0937 = \text{₹ } 4,21,650
$$

**Step 3 — Total Annual Cost:**

$$
C_{annual,total} = 4,21,650 + 60,000 = \text{₹ } 4,81,650
$$

**Step 4 — LCOE (neglecting degradation for simplicity):**

$$
LCOE = \dfrac{4,81,650}{1,40,000} = \text{₹ } 3.44 \; / \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 5 — Simple Payback Period:**

Annual savings (assuming self-consumption) = ₹ 7.50 × 1,40,000 = ₹ 10,50,000

Net annual cash inflow = ₹ 10,50,000 − ₹ 60,000 = ₹ 9,90,000

$$
SPP = \dfrac{45,00,000}{9,90,000} \approx 4.55 \; \text{years}
$$

**Step 6 — Viability Check:**

Since $LCOE = \text{₹ } 3.44 < \text{tariff of ₹ } 7.50/\text{kWh}$, and the project recovers cost in **~ 4.5 years**, the system is **highly viable**. The lifetime savings comfortably exceed the investment even after accounting for time-value of money.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 (Alternative Choice)**

#### (a) Define Internal Rate of Return (IRR). For a project with $C_0 = $ ₹ 4,00,000 and uniform annual cash inflow of ₹ 1,00,000 for 6 years, compute the IRR by trial-and-error. `[7 Marks]`

**Step 1 — Definition of IRR:**

The **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** is the discount rate at which the Net Present Value of all cash flows equals zero. Mathematically, IRR is the value of $i$ that satisfies:

$$
0 = -C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}
$$

**Step 2 — Trial at $i = 12\%$:**

$$
(P/A, 12\%, 6) = \dfrac{(1.12)^6 - 1}{0.12 \cdot (1.12)^6} = \dfrac{0.9738}{0.2366} = 4.1114
$$

$$
NPV_{12\%} = -4,00,000 + 1,00,000 \cdot 4.1114 = +11,140
$$

Since $NPV > 0$, IRR must be **higher than 12 %**.

**Step 3 — Trial at $i = 13\%$:**

$$
(P/A, 13\%, 6) = \dfrac{(1.13)^6 - 1}{0.13 \cdot (1.13)^6} = \dfrac{1.0498}{0.2499} = 3.9015
$$

Wait, recompute: $(1.13)^6 = 2.08195$.

$$
(P/A, 13\%, 6) = \dfrac{2.08195 - 1}{0.13 \cdot 2.08195} = \dfrac{1.08195}{0.27065} = 3.9976
$$

$$
NPV_{13\%} = -4,00,000 + 1,00,000 \cdot 3.9976 = -\text{₹ } 240
$$

NPV slightly negative → IRR is **slightly less than 13 %**.

**Step 4 — Linear Interpolation (Bracketing between 12 % and 13 %):**

$$
IRR \approx 12\% + \dfrac{11,140}{11,140 + 240} \cdot (13\% - 12\%) = 12\% + \dfrac{11,140}{11,380} \cdot 1\%
$$

$$
IRR \approx 12\% + 0.979\% = 12.98\% \approx 13\%
$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**

If the cost of capital (hurdle rate) is 10 %, then $IRR = 13\% > 10\%$ → **Project Accepted**.

---

#### (b) Explain the **Life Cycle Costing (LCC)** method for solar PV plants. A solar water heater system costs ₹ 60,000 installed and has annual maintenance of ₹ 1,500. Major inverter replacement costs ₹ 12,000 at year 10. If the lifetime is 20 years and the discount rate is 9 %, compute the LCC. `[7 Marks]`

**Step 1 — Concept of LCC:**

**Life Cycle Costing** aggregates *all* costs incurred over the plant's lifetime — initial cost, recurring O&M, periodic replacements, decommissioning — and discounts them to a single **Present Value (LCC)** or equivalently an **Annualized Cost (using CRF)**.

**Step 2 — Compute Each Component's Present Value:**

*(i) Initial cost (at year 0):*

$$
PV_0 = 60,000
$$

*(ii) Present value of annual O&M (ordinary annuity):*

$$
PV_{O\&M} = 1,500 \cdot (P/A, 9\%, 20)
$$

Compute $(1.09)^{20} = 5.6044$.

$$
(P/A, 9\%, 20) = \dfrac{5.6044 - 1}{0.09 \cdot 5.6044} = \dfrac{4.6044}{0.5044} = 9.1285
$$

$$
PV_{O\&M} = 1,500 \cdot 9.1285 = \text{₹ } 13,693
$$

*(iii) Present value of inverter replacement at year 10:*

$$
PV_{inv} = \dfrac{12,000}{(1.09)^{10}} = \dfrac{12,000}{2.3674} = \text{₹ } 5,069
$$

*(iv) Decommissioning cost (assume ₹ 5,000 at year 20):*

$$
PV_{decom} = \dfrac{5,000}{(1.09)^{20}} = \dfrac{5,000}{5.6044} = \text{₹ } 892
$$

**Step 3 — Total LCC:**

$$
LCC = 60,000 + 13,693 + 5,069 + 892 = \text{₹ } 79,654
$$

**Step 4 — Annualized LCC (optional):**

$$
LCC_{annual} = 79,654 \cdot CRF(9\%, 20) = 79,654 \cdot 0.1095 = \text{₹ } 8,722 / \text{year}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

The ₹ 60,000 system *actually costs* ₹ 79,654 in present-value terms over its life — the **₹ 19,654 "hidden" lifetime cost** is the price of operating it for two decades.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to negate $C_0$ in NPV.** The initial investment is an outflow — write it as $-C_0$, not $+C_0$.
> 2. **Mixing up CRF and Annuity Factor.** CRF $= (A/P, i, n)$; Annuity $= (P/A, i, n)$. They are reciprocals.
> 3. **Using Simple Payback Period as a stand-alone decision metric.** Examiners expect you to *also* compute NPV or LCOE — SPP alone never justifies a project.
> 4. **Ignoring degradation in LCOE.** A 0.5 %/year degradation over 25 years reduces lifetime energy by **~ 12 %** — failure to account for it inflates LCOE estimates and loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Wrong interpolation in IRR.** The interpolation formula is $IRR \approx i_L + \dfrac{NPV_L}{NPV_L - NPV_H} \cdot (i_H - i_L)$ — many students reverse $L$ and $H$.
> 6. **Forgetting units in the final answer.** Always quote LCOE in **₹/kWh**, NPV in **₹ (or Lakhs/Crore)**, and Payback in **years**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU OEMET617 Module 4**

- **Six core methods:** Simple Payback, ROI, NPV, IRR, LCC (with CRF), LCOE.
- **Capital Recovery Factor** — $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$; converts present cost into annual cost.
- **Annuity Factor** — $(P/A, i, n) = \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$; converts annual cash flow into present value.
- **NPV Decision Rule:** Accept if $NPV \geq 0$; Reject if $NPV < 0$.
- **IRR Decision Rule:** Accept if $IRR \geq \text{hurdle rate}$; found by trial-and-error + interpolation.
- **LCOE Formula:** $LCOE = \dfrac{\sum \dfrac{C_t}{(1+i)^t}}{\sum \dfrac{E_t}{(1+i)^t}}$ — comparison metric for competing plants.
- **Standard Reference Values for India (commit to memory):** $n = 25$ years, $i = 10\%$, degradation $= 0.5\%$/year, CUF $= 17$–$19\%$.
- **Simple Payback Period does NOT consider time value of money** — its biggest limitation.
- **Discounted Payback Period does** — but KTU asks it less often.
- **Typical Solar LCOE in India (2024):** ₹ 2.5 – ₹ 4.0 / kWh (utility-scale); ₹ 3.5 – ₹ 6.0 / kWh (rooftop).
- **Most Important for KTU Board Exam:** NPV and LCOE (these two alone account for ~ 60 % of marks in Module 4 questions).
- **Most-Forgotten Topic:** Effect of **degradation** and **inflation/escalation** on NPV — always mention them in long answers to score full marks.
- **Sanity check rule of thumb:** A solar plant is "good" if **SPP < 6 years**, **NPV > 0**, and **LCOE < prevailing grid tariff**.
- **Always state assumptions:** Project life, discount rate, degradation, tariff escalation — examiners explicitly award marks for these in the "Given Data" and "Conclusion" sections.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Life Cycle Cost
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET617 - SOLAR ENERGY CONSERVATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Life Cycle Cost

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Life Cycle Cost (LCC)** is the total discounted cost of owning, operating, maintaining, and disposing of a solar energy system (or any engineered asset) over its entire operational lifespan. It is an economic evaluation methodology that sums all costs incurred from initial conception to final decommissioning, expressed in terms of **Present Value (PV)** or **Annualized Cost**, thereby enabling a true "apples-to-apples" comparison between competing energy alternatives with different capital outlays and operating horizons.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> Life Cycle Cost (LCC) analysis is defined as the sum of all costs (initial, operating, maintenance, replacement, fuel, and salvage) associated with a solar energy system over its defined analysis period, reduced to a common time-equivalent monetary base (present worth) using a suitable discount rate.

### 1.2 The Governing Economic Philosophy

Solar PV and solar thermal systems typically demand a **high initial capital expenditure (CapEx)** but boast very low recurring operating expenditure (OpEx) because sunlight is free. Conventional grid electricity or diesel systems, conversely, require low initial CapEx but accumulate significant OpEx through fuel purchases. LCC bridges this asymmetry.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy: "The Car Ownership Analogy"**
>
> Imagine you are choosing between two cars:
> - **Car A (Electric / Solar Equivalent):** Costs ₹20,00,000 upfront, but you spend only ₹10,000/year on "fuel" (sunlight) and ₹5,000/year on maintenance.
> - **Car B (Diesel / Conventional Equivalent):** Costs only ₹8,00,000 upfront, but you spend ₹1,50,000/year on diesel and ₹30,000/year on servicing.
>
> If you only looked at the **showroom price**, Car B looks cheaper. However, if you plan to keep the car for **20 years**, Car A actually becomes dramatically cheaper because you avoid the compounding diesel cost. **Life Cycle Cost is the equivalent of calculating the "true 20-year total cost of ownership" rather than just the sticker price.** In solar engineering, sunlight replaces diesel as the "fuel," and LCC reveals the long-term financial supremacy of solar systems.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics in LCC

The following physical and economic constants are universally employed in KTU-endorsed LCC calculations:

- **Discount Rate (d or i):** The time-value-of-money factor, typically between **4% and 12%** for Indian renewable energy projects.
- **Analysis Period (N):** The economic life of the system, conventionally **20 to 25 years** for solar PV and **15 to 20 years** for solar thermal.
- **Escalation Rate (e):** The annual percentage increase in operating costs (often 5% to 7% for electricity tariff in India).
- **Inflation Rate (f):** The general annual rate of price rise of goods and services.

### 1.5 Why LCC is the KTU-Mandated Evaluation Tool

> [!NOTE]
> The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) and KTU's Outcome-Based Education framework both insist on LCC-based decision-making for solar installations because the *Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE)* — which is a direct derivative of LCC — is the internationally accepted metric for comparing heterogeneous energy sources.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of Life Cycle Cost

LCC is the aggregate of six distinct cost streams. Each stream must be discounted to the **present (Year 0)** if the analysis is in *Present Worth* form, or converted to an *Uniform Annual Series* if annualized.

1. **Initial Cost (IC / First Cost):** Capital expenditure for procurement, civil works, mounting, electrical BOS, installation, and commissioning. This occurs at Year 0.
2. **Operating Cost (OC):** Recurring costs such as insurance, taxes, water for cleaning (in solar thermal), and any parasitic electricity consumption.
3. **Maintenance Cost (MC):** Scheduled preventive maintenance, spares replacement, panel cleaning, and unscheduled corrective maintenance.
4. **Replacement Cost (RC):** Costs of major component overhauls that occur mid-life. In a 25-year solar PV system, the **string inverters** must be replaced at least once (around Year 12-15).
5. **Fuel Cost (FC):** Applies primarily to conventional systems being compared. For pure solar, FC = 0.
6. **Salvage Value (SV):** The residual monetary worth of the system at the end of its analysis period. It is a *negative* cost (a credit).

### 2.2 The Three Time-Value-of-Money Conversion Factors

To bring all future costs back to a single, comparable base, we use **discount factors**. Let `i` be the annual discount rate and `N` be the year of occurrence.

#### A. Single Payment Present Worth Factor (SPPWF)
Converts a future lump-sum cost at year `N` to its present value at Year 0.
$$\text{SPPWF} = \frac{1}{(1+i)^{N}}$$

#### B. Uniform Series Present Worth Factor (USPWF)
Converts an annual recurring cost (a uniform series `A` for `N` years) into a single present value.
$$\text{USPWF} = \frac{(1+i)^{N} - 1}{i(1+i)^{N}}$$

#### C. Sinking Fund Factor (SFF)
Converts a future lump-sum cost (e.g., replacement or salvage) into an equivalent uniform annual amount.
$$\text{SFF} = \frac{i}{(1+i)^{N} - 1}$$

### 2.3 The Generic LCC Equation (Present Worth Method)

The total Life Cycle Cost in present worth terms is expressed as:

$$\text{LCC} = \text{IC} + \text{PV(OC)} + \text{PV(MC)} + \text{PV(RC)} - \text{PV(SV)}$$

When costs are **escalating** at rate `e` (where `e ≠ i`), the discount formula must be modified using the *constant-escalation* factor:

$$\text{PV of Escalating Series} = A \cdot \left[ \frac{1 - \left(\frac{1+e}{1+i}\right)^{N}}{i - e} \right]$$

### 2.4 Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE) — The LCC Output Metric

The most direct practical use of LCC in solar engineering is the computation of **LCOE**, expressed in **₹/kWh** (or $/kWh). LCOE tells us the per-unit cost of electricity generated over the system's lifetime.

$$\text{LCOE} = \frac{\sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{C_t}{(1+i)^{t}}}{\sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{E_t}{(1+i)^{t}}}$$

where `C_t` is the cost in year `t` and `E_t` is the electricity generated in year `t`. For a uniform annual energy yield `E`:

$$\text{LCOE} = \frac{\text{LCC (Annualized)}}{E_{\text{annual}}}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for LCC (All You Need for KTU Board Exam)**

| **#** | **Formula Name** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Use Case** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SPPWF | $(1+i)^{-N}$ | Discount replacement/salvage cost | Dimensionless |
| 2 | USPWF | $\frac{(1+i)^{N}-1}{i(1+i)^{N}}$ | Discount annual O&M to present | Dimensionless |
| 3 | SFF | $\frac{i}{(1+i)^{N}-1}$ | Annualize future replacement cost | Year$^{-1}$ |
| 4 | Capital Recovery (CR) | $\frac{i(1+i)^{N}}{(1+i)^{N}-1}$ | Annualize the initial cost | Year$^{-1}$ |
| 5 | LCC (Total) | $\text{IC} + \text{PV(OC)} + \text{PV(MC)} + \text{PV(RC)} - \text{PV(SV)}$ | Master life cycle cost | ₹ or $ |
| 6 | Annualized LCC | $\text{LCC} \times \text{CR factor}$ | Convert PW to annual series | ₹/year or $/year |
| 7 | LCOE | $\text{Annualized LCC} / E_{\text{annual}}$ | Compare energy alternatives | ₹/kWh or $/kWh |
| 8 | Escalating Series PV | $A \cdot \left[ \frac{1 - \left(\frac{1+e}{1+i}\right)^{N}}{i - e} \right]$ | O&M with annual price rise | ₹ or $ |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why engineers actually use LCC:**
> - **Bid Evaluation (EPC Tenders):** LCC is used to evaluate the lowest *lifetime* bidder, not just the lowest CapEx bidder.
> - **MNRE Subsidy Decisions:** Viability Gap Funding (VGF) is computed using LCC and LCOE models.
> - **ISO 15686-5 Compliance:** Internationally, LCC is mandated for service-life planning of building-integrated solar systems.
> - **Industrial Decision Support:** A factory comparing rooftop solar vs. captive DG sets will always use LCC to justify CapEx.

### 2.7 Assumptions and Limitations

> [!WARNING]
> Students must explicitly state these assumptions in KTU answers:
> - The discount rate `i` is constant over the analysis period.
> - The annual energy output `E_t` is assumed constant (degradation is either ignored or modeled as a separate degradation factor `d_degrade` per year, typically 0.5%–0.8% for PV).
> - All salvage values are positive (i.e., scrap value, not disposal liability).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Uniform Series Present Worth Factor (USPWF)

**Goal:** Derive the factor that converts an annual uniform amount `A` (occurring at the end of each of `N` years) into a single present value `P` at year 0.

**Step 1 — Conceptual Setup**
Let us imagine the cash flow. At the end of Year 1, you pay `A`. At the end of Year 2, you pay `A` again, and so on, until the end of Year `N`. We wish to find what is the *equivalent single lump sum* `P` at Year 0 that has the exact same economic value as this entire stream.

**Step 2 — Apply Single Payment Present Worth to Each Year**
Using the SPPWF for each individual payment:

$$P = A \cdot \frac{1}{(1+i)^{1}} + A \cdot \frac{1}{(1+i)^{2}} + A \cdot \frac{1}{(1+i)^{3}} + \dots + A \cdot \frac{1}{(1+i)^{N}}$$

**Step 3 — Factor Out the Common Term**
Since `A` is common, factor it out:

$$P = A \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{(1+i)} + \frac{1}{(1+i)^{2}} + \frac{1}{(1+i)^{3}} + \dots + \frac{1}{(1+i)^{N}} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the Geometric Progression**
The bracketed sum is a finite geometric series with first term $x = \frac{1}{1+i}$, common ratio $r = \frac{1}{1+i}$, and number of terms $n = N$.

The sum of a finite geometric series is $S = x \cdot \frac{1 - r^{N}}{1 - r}$.

Substituting our values:

$$S = \frac{1}{1+i} \cdot \frac{1 - \left(\frac{1}{1+i}\right)^{N}}{1 - \frac{1}{1+i}}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the Denominator**
The denominator becomes:

$$1 - \frac{1}{1+i} = \frac{1+i - 1}{1+i} = \frac{i}{1+i}$$

**Step 6 — Combine Numerator and Denominator**

$$S = \frac{1}{1+i} \cdot \frac{1 - \left(\frac{1}{1+i}\right)^{N}}{\frac{i}{1+i}}$$

$$S = \frac{1 - \left(\frac{1}{1+i}\right)^{N}}{i}$$

**Step 7 — Multiply Numerator and Denominator by $(1+i)^{N}$**
Multiply the top and bottom by $(1+i)^{N}$ to clean up the negative exponent:

$$S = \frac{(1+i)^{N} \cdot \left[ 1 - (1+i)^{-N} \right]}{i} = \frac{(1+i)^{N} - 1}{i(1+i)^{N}}$$

**Step 8 — Final Result**

$$\boxed{\text{USPWF} = \frac{(1+i)^{N} - 1}{i(1+i)^{N}}}$$

And therefore: $P = A \times \text{USPWF}$.

---

### 3.2 Comprehensive Numerical Worked Example (KTU Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement**
> A 5 kWp rooftop solar PV system is proposed for a KTU-affiliated engineering college hostel. The economic data is given below. Compute the **Total Life Cycle Cost (Present Worth)** and the **LCOE**.

**Given Data:**
- Initial Cost (IC) = ₹3,50,000 (paid at Year 0)
- Annual Operating Cost (OC) = ₹2,000/year
- Annual Maintenance Cost (MC) = ₹4,000/year (escalating at 5% per year)
- Inverter Replacement Cost at Year 12 = ₹40,000
- Salvage Value at end of Year 25 = ₹25,000
- Discount rate `i` = 8% per year
- Annual Energy Yield `E` = 7,500 kWh/year
- Analysis Period `N` = 25 years

---

#### Step 1: Present Worth of Initial Cost
The initial cost is already at Year 0, so no discounting is needed.

$$\text{PV(IC)} = ₹3,50,000$$

#### Step 2: Present Worth of Annual Operating Cost
Operating cost `A_OC = ₹2,000` is a uniform non-escalating series.

$$\text{PV(OC)} = 2000 \times \text{USPWF}_{8\%, 25}$$

Compute USPWF using the formula:

$$\text{USPWF} = \frac{(1.08)^{25} - 1}{0.08 \times (1.08)^{25}}$$

Compute $(1.08)^{25}$:

$$(1.08)^{25} = 6.8485$$

Substitute:

$$\text{USPWF} = \frac{6.8485 - 1}{0.08 \times 6.8485} = \frac{5.8485}{0.5479} = 10.6748$$

Therefore:

$$\text{PV(OC)} = 2000 \times 10.6748 = ₹21,349.6$$

#### Step 3: Present Worth of Escalating Maintenance Cost
Maintenance cost `A_MC = ₹4,000`, escalating at `e = 5%`, discount rate `i = 8%`. Use the escalating series formula:

$$\text{PV(MC)} = A \cdot \left[ \frac{1 - \left(\frac{1+e}{1+i}\right)^{N}}{i - e} \right]$$

Compute the ratio $\frac{1+e}{1+i}$:

$$\frac{1.05}{1.08} = 0.97222$$

Compute $(0.97222)^{25}$:

$$(0.97222)^{25} = 0.4923$$

Substitute into the formula:

$$\text{PV(MC)} = 4000 \times \left[ \frac{1 - 0.4923}{0.08 - 0.05} \right] = 4000 \times \left[ \frac{0.5077}{0.03} \right] = 4000 \times 16.9233 = ₹67,693.2$$

#### Step 4: Present Worth of Inverter Replacement at Year 12
Replacement cost is a one-time future lump sum of ₹40,000. Use SPPWF:

$$\text{PV(RC)} = 40000 \times (1.08)^{-12}$$

Compute $(1.08)^{12}$:

$$(1.08)^{12} = 2.5182$$

Therefore:

$$\text{PV(RC)} = \frac{40000}{2.5182} = ₹15,884.8$$

#### Step 5: Present Worth of Salvage Value
Salvage value is a *credit* (negative cost) at Year 25:

$$\text{PV(SV)} = 25000 \times (1.08)^{-25} = \frac{25000}{6.8485} = ₹3,650.5$$

#### Step 6: Total Life Cycle Cost (Present Worth)
Sum all present values, subtracting the salvage credit:

$$\text{LCC} = 350000 + 21349.6 + 67693.2 + 15884.8 - 3650.5$$

$$\boxed{\text{LCC} = ₹4,51,277.1}$$

#### Step 7: Convert LCC to Annualized Cost
Use the Capital Recovery factor to annualize:

$$\text{CR factor} = \frac{i(1+i)^{N}}{(1+i)^{N} - 1} = \frac{0.08 \times 6.8485}{6.8485 - 1} = \frac{0.5479}{5.8485} = 0.09368$$

$$\text{Annualized LCC} = 451277.1 \times 0.09368 = ₹42,275.4 \text{ per year}$$

#### Step 8: Compute the LCOE

$$\text{LCOE} = \frac{\text{Annualized LCC}}{E_{\text{annual}}} = \frac{42275.4}{7500} = ₹5.64 \text{ per kWh}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Interpretation of Result

| **Component** | **Present Value (₹)** | **% of Total LCC** | **Comment** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial Cost | 3,50,000 | 77.6% | Dominant cost in any solar LCC |
| Operating Cost | 21,349.6 | 4.7% | Low — characteristic of solar |
| Maintenance Cost | 67,693.2 | 15.0% | Significant because of escalation |
| Replacement Cost | 15,884.8 | 3.5% | Inverter mid-life replacement |
| Salvage (credit) | (3,650.5) | (0.8%) | Small positive credit |
| **Total LCC** | **4,51,277.1** | **100%** | **Final LCC in Present Worth** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight from the Matrix:** The Initial Cost contributes **~78%** of the total LCC, confirming the KTU textbook's claim that solar systems are *capital-intensive but operationally cheap*. Any reduction in CapEx (e.g., via MNRE subsidies) dramatically lowers the LCOE.

### 3.4 Detailed Derivation of LCOE from LCC

**Goal:** Show that LCOE is the natural per-unit energy derivative of LCC.

Starting from:

$$\text{LCOE} = \frac{\text{LCC}_{annualized}}{E_{annual}}$$

Substitute $\text{LCC}_{annualized} = \text{LCC}_{PW} \cdot \text{CR factor}$:

$$\text{LCOE} = \frac{\text{LCC}_{PW}}{E_{annual}} \cdot \text{CR factor}$$

Substitute $\text{CR factor} = \frac{i(1+i)^{N}}{(1+i)^{N}-1}$:

$$\text{LCOE} = \frac{\text{LCC}_{PW}}{E_{annual}} \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^{N}}{(1+i)^{N}-1}$$

Since $\text{LCC}_{PW} = \text{IC} + A_{annual} \cdot \text{USPWF}$ (for simple cases):

$$\text{LCOE} = \frac{\text{IC} \cdot \text{CR} + A_{annual}}{E_{annual}}$$

This is the **classical LCOE formulation** taught universally in energy economics. It is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Life Cycle Cost Cash Flow Timeline

The diagram below illustrates the temporal distribution of all cost events across the 25-year analysis horizon. Each event is mapped to its year of occurrence and its discounted present worth contribution.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    year0[Year 0 Initial Capital Outlay] -->|IC 350000 INR| pw1[Present Worth Pool]
    year1[Year 1 O and M Start] -->|2000 INR per year| pw1
    year2[Year 2 to 11 O and M] -->|Annual Recurring| pw1
    year3[Year 12 Inverter Replacement] -->|40000 INR Lump Sum| pw1
    year4[Year 13 to 24 Continued O and M] -->|Annual Recurring| pw1
    year5[Year 25 Salvage Credit] -->|Negative 25000 INR| pw1
    pw1[Total LCC Present Worth 451277 INR] --> annualize[Annualize Using CR Factor 0.09368]
    annualize --> annual[Annualized LCC 42275 INR per year]
    annual --> lcoe[LCOE 5.64 INR per kWh]
    energy[Annual Energy Yield 7500 kWh] --> lcoe
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture of LCC Computation

This diagram explains the modular computational architecture used in any professional LCC software (e.g., RETScreen, HOMER Pro, SAM).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputA[Input Module Economic Parameters] --> procA
    inputB[Input Module Energy Parameters] --> procA
    inputC[Input Component Costs] --> procA
    procA[Central Processor Discount Engine] --> outA
    procA --> outB
    procA --> outC
    outA[Output Total LCC Present Worth]
    outB[Output Annualized LCC]
    outC[Output LCOE in INR per kWh]
    inputA --- descA[Discount Rate i, Analysis Period N, Escalation e]
    inputB --- descB[Annual Energy kWh, Degradation factor]
    inputC --- descC[IC, OC, MC, RC, SV]
    outA --- metaA[Used for Bid Evaluation]
    outB --- metaB[Used for Budget Allocation]
    outC --- metaC[Used for Tariff Comparison]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Decision Logic for Solar vs Conventional LCC Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Project Initiated] --> q1{Is LCC of Solar Lower than LCC of Grid DG Set?}
    q1 -->|Yes| sol[Select Solar System Award Contract]
    q1 -->|No| q2{Can Subsidy or Tax Benefit Reduce LCC of Solar?}
    q2 -->|Yes| q3[Recompute LCC with Adjusted CapEx]
    q2 -->|No| conv[Select Conventional Energy Source]
    q3 --> q1
    sol --> out[Final Engineering Decision Based on LCC]
    conv --> out
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Block Diagram instead of a physical sketch?**
> LCC is fundamentally a *computational and decision-making* concept, not a physical hardware concept. Therefore, a functional block diagram communicates the engineering logic far more effectively than a hand-drawn schematic ever could. This is also the format preferred by KTU valuation experts for full-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
**Define the term Life Cycle Cost (LCC) of a solar energy system. List any four cost components included in LCC.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Life Cycle Cost (LCC) is the total discounted monetary cost of a solar energy system over its entire operational lifespan, expressed as the present value of all initial, operating, maintenance, replacement, and disposal costs minus the salvage value, evaluated at a chosen discount rate.

**Four Cost Components (1.5 Marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Initial Cost (IC):** Capital expenditure at Year 0 for modules, BOS, civil works, installation, and commissioning.
2. **Operating Cost (OC):** Recurring annual costs such as insurance, taxes, and water consumption.
3. **Maintenance Cost (MC):** Annual preventive and corrective maintenance expenditure, often escalating.
4. **Replacement Cost (RC):** Cost of replacing major components such as inverters, batteries, or pumps at mid-life.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners award full marks only if students mention *discounting* and *entire lifespan* in the definition. Omitting the time-value-of-money aspect costs 0.5 marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE)? Write its mathematical expression.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
LCOE is the per-unit (₹/kWh) cost of electricity generated by an energy system over its entire lifetime, calculated as the ratio of the annualized Life Cycle Cost to the annual energy output.

**Mathematical Expression (2 Marks):**

$$\text{LCOE} = \frac{\text{LCC}_{annualized}}{E_{annual}} = \frac{\text{LCC}_{PW} \times \text{CR factor}}{E_{annual}}$$

Expanded form:

$$\text{LCOE} = \frac{\text{IC} \times \text{CR} + A_{O\&M}}{E_{annual}}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Suggested Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various cost components that constitute the Life Cycle Cost of a solar PV system. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A 3 kWp solar PV system has the following economic data:
- Initial Cost = ₹2,10,000
- Annual O&M Cost = ₹3,000 (uniform, non-escalating)
- Inverter Replacement at Year 10 = ₹30,000
- Salvage Value at Year 20 = ₹15,000
- Discount Rate = 10% per year
- Annual Energy Yield = 4,500 kWh/year
- Analysis Period = 20 years

Compute (i) the Total Life Cycle Cost in Present Worth, (ii) the Annualized LCC, and (iii) the LCOE. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Cost Components of LCC for a Solar PV System (7 Marks):**

1. **Initial Cost (IC) — 2 Marks:**
   Includes module procurement, inverters, mounting structures, DC and AC cables, balance-of-system (BOS) components, civil foundations, lightning arresters, earthing, installation labor, and commissioning charges. Paid at Year 0, hence no discounting.

2. **Operating and Maintenance (O&M) Cost — 2 Marks:**
   - **Operating Cost:** Insurance premium, taxes, water for panel cleaning, parasitic standby consumption.
   - **Maintenance Cost:** Routine cleaning, periodic I-V curve tracing, tightening of MC4 connectors, replacement of fuses, string monitoring.
   - Often treated as a single O&M stream, sometimes assumed to escalate at 5% annually.

3. **Replacement Cost (RC) — 1.5 Marks:**
   Inverters have a useful life of 10–12 years. In a 25-year project, an inverter must be replaced at least once. Batteries (in off-grid systems) require replacement every 5–7 years. These mid-life replacements must be discounted to present worth.

4. **Salvage Value (SV) — 1.5 Marks:**
   The scrap/resale value of modules, aluminum structures, copper cables, and inverters at the end of the analysis period. It is a negative cost (credit) discounted from Year N.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** IC = ₹2,10,000; O&M = ₹3,000/yr; RC = ₹30,000 at Year 10; SV = ₹15,000 at Year 20; i = 10%; N = 20; E = 4,500 kWh/yr.

**Step 1: PV of Initial Cost (0.5 Mark)**
$$\text{PV(IC)} = ₹2,10,000$$

**Step 2: PV of O&M (Uniform Series) (2 Marks)**
Compute USPWF at 10% for 20 years:
$$\text{USPWF} = \frac{(1.10)^{20} - 1}{0.10 \times (1.10)^{20}} = \frac{6.7275 - 1}{0.10 \times 6.7275} = \frac{5.7275}{0.67275} = 8.5136$$

$$\text{PV(O\&M)} = 3000 \times 8.5136 = ₹25,540.8$$

**Step 3: PV of Replacement Cost at Year 10 (1.5 Marks)**
$$\text{PV(RC)} = 30000 \times (1.10)^{-10} = \frac{30000}{2.5937} = ₹11,566.4$$

**Step 4: PV of Salvage Value at Year 20 (1 Mark)**
$$\text{PV(SV)} = 15000 \times (1.10)^{-20} = \frac{15000}{6.7275} = ₹2,229.5$$

**Step 5: Total LCC (1 Mark)**
$$\text{LCC} = 210000 + 25540.8 + 11566.4 - 2229.5 = ₹2,44,877.7$$

**Step 6: Annualized LCC (0.5 Mark)**
$$\text{CR factor} = \frac{0.10 \times 6.7275}{6.7275 - 1} = \frac{0.67275}{5.7275} = 0.11746$$

$$\text{Annualized LCC} = 244877.7 \times 0.11746 = ₹28,764.2 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 7: LCOE (0.5 Mark)**
$$\text{LCOE} = \frac{28764.2}{4500} = ₹6.39 \text{ per kWh}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — Examiner's Warning:**
> 1. Students often forget to *subtract* the salvage value and instead add it. Salvage is a **credit**, hence a negative term.
> 2. Some students mistakenly apply the **USPWF** to the replacement cost at Year 10. The replacement cost is a **single lump sum** at a *specific year*, not a uniform series. Use **SPPWF** for it.
> 3. Do not confuse the *discount rate* `i` with the *escalation rate* `e`. They are independent. A common KTU trap is providing `e = 5%` in the data without instructing you to use the escalating-series formula — read the question carefully.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the methodology of Life Cycle Cost analysis for a solar PV system. List the economic assumptions involved. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Compare the Life Cycle Cost and LCOE of two competing energy options for a rural health centre:
- **Option 1: 2 kWp Solar PV with Battery Backup** — IC = ₹3,00,000; Annual O&M = ₹5,000 (escalating at 6%); Battery replacement at Year 7 = ₹40,000; SV at Year 20 = ₹20,000.
- **Option 2: 5 kVA Diesel Generator** — IC = ₹1,50,000; Annual Diesel Cost = ₹60,000 (escalating at 8%); Major Overhaul at Year 10 = ₹25,000; SV at Year 20 = ₹10,000.

Common data: Discount rate `i` = 9%; Analysis period `N` = 20 years. Annual energy demand = 6,000 kWh for both options.

Determine which option is economically superior. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution (Key Steps):**

**(a) LCC Methodology (7 Marks):**
The LCC methodology proceeds in five sequential steps, as illustrated in the block diagram below:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1[Step 1 Define Project Boundary and Analysis Period] --> step2[Step 2 Identify All Cost Streams IC OC MC RC SV]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3 Choose Discount Rate i and Escalation Rate e]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4 Apply Discount Factors to Bring All Costs to Present Worth]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5 Sum and Compare to Determine Lowest LCC]
    step5 --> out[Decision Award Contract to Lowest LCC Option]
```

**Economic Assumptions (2 Marks):**
- Discount rate remains constant over the analysis period.
- Energy output is constant (or degrades at a known rate).
- Salvage values are positive scrap credits.
- All costs are deterministic (no probabilistic risk premium added unless specified).

---

**(b) Comparative LCC Computation (7 Marks):**

**Option 1 — Solar PV:**
Compute USPWF at 9% for 20 years:
$$\text{USPWF} = \frac{(1.09)^{20} - 1}{0.09 \times (1.09)^{20}} = \frac{5.6044 - 1}{0.5044} = 9.1285$$

PV of O&M (escalating at 6%):
$$\text{PV(O\&M)} = 5000 \times \left[ \frac{1 - (1.06/1.09)^{20}}{0.09 - 0.06} \right] = 5000 \times \frac{1 - (0.9725)^{20}}{0.03}$$

$(0.9725)^{20} = 0.5737$

$$\text{PV(O\&M)} = 5000 \times \frac{1 - 0.5737}{0.03} = 5000 \times 14.21 = ₹71,050$$

PV of Battery Replacement at Year 7:
$$\text{PV(RC)} = 40000 \times (1.09)^{-7} = 40000 \times 0.5470 = ₹21,880$$

PV of Salvage:
$$\text{PV(SV)} = 20000 \times (1.09)^{-20} = 20000 \times 0.1784 = ₹3,568$$

$$\text{LCC}_{Solar} = 300000 + 71050 + 21880 - 3568 = ₹3,89,362$$

**Option 2 — Diesel Generator:**
PV of Diesel (escalating at 8%):
$$\text{PV(Diesel)} = 60000 \times \left[ \frac{1 - (1.08/1.09)^{20}}{0.09 - 0.08} \right] = 60000 \times \frac{1 - (0.9908)^{20}}{0.01}$$

$(0.9908)^{20} = 0.8248$

$$\text{PV(Diesel)} = 60000 \times \frac{1 - 0.8248}{0.01} = 60000 \times 17.52 = ₹10,51,200$$

PV of Overhaul at Year 10:
$$\text{PV(Overhaul)} = 25000 \times (1.09)^{-10} = 25000 \times 0.4224 = ₹10,560$$

PV of Salvage:
$$\text{PV(SV)} = 10000 \times (1.09)^{-20} = 10000 \times 0.1784 = ₹1,784$$

$$\text{LCC}_{Diesel} = 150000 + 1051200 + 10560 - 1784 = ₹12,09,976$$

**Decision:**
Since $\text{LCC}_{Solar} = ₹3,89,362 \ll \text{LCC}_{Diesel} = ₹12,09,976$, the **Solar PV option is decisively superior** — by a factor of roughly **3× cheaper** over the 20-year horizon.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. In Option 2, the *fuel* (diesel) is escalating faster than the discount rate. This means the **escalating-series denominator `i − e` becomes very small**, causing the PV to balloon. Students often mistakenly use the regular USPWF here, which grossly *underestimates* the diesel cost. Always check whether the question mentions escalation.
> 2. Watch the sign of salvage. Subtract it.
> 3. Do not compute LCOE unless the question explicitly asks for it; the LCC comparison alone is sufficient to choose between the two options.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Life Cycle Cost (Module 4)**

- [x] **LCC = Total discounted cost** of a system over its **entire analysis period**, expressed in **Present Worth (PW)** or **Annualized** form.
- [x] The **six canonical cost streams** are: Initial Cost (IC), Operating Cost (OC), Maintenance Cost (MC), Replacement Cost (RC), Fuel Cost (FC), and Salvage Value (SV).
- [x] **SPPWF = $(1+i)^{-N}$** is used for **single future lump sums** (e.g., inverter replacement, salvage).
- [x] **USPWF = $\frac{(1+i)^{N}-1}{i(1+i)^{N}}$** is used for **uniform annual recurring costs** (e.g., O&M).
- [x] **SFF = $\frac{i}{(1+i)^{N}-1}$** is used to **convert a future lump sum into an annual series** (e.g., for replacement reserve funds).
- [x] **CR factor = $\frac{i(1+i)^{N}}{(1+i)^{N}-1}$** is the **inverse of USPWF** and is used to **annualize the total PW LCC**.
- [x] **LCOE = Annualized LCC / Annual Energy Yield** is the **single most important metric** for solar-vs-conventional comparison.
- [x] **Escalating series formula** is mandatory when O&M, fuel, or electricity tariff is rising annually at a rate `e` different from the discount rate `i`.
- [x] **Initial Cost typically contributes 70–80%** of the total LCC of a solar PV system, making **CapEx reduction (subsidies, technology choice) the most powerful lever** for lowering LCOE.
- [x] **Salvage is a credit** — it is **subtracted**, not added.
- [x] **Standard KTU analysis period:** 20–25 years for solar PV; 15–20 years for solar thermal.
- [x] **Standard KTU discount rate:** 8–12% (often taken as 10% in numerical problems).
- [x] **Common student errors to avoid:** (1) Adding salvage instead of subtracting, (2) Using USPWF for a single replacement cost, (3) Ignoring escalation when given, (4) Confusing discount rate with escalation rate.
- [x] **Practical insight:** Solar systems have **high CapEx, low OpEx**; conventional systems have **low CapEx, high OpEx** — LCC is the only fair way to compare them.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Payback Period
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET617 - SOLAR ENERGY CONSERVATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Payback Period

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Payback Period (PBP)** is a fundamental financial metric used in engineering economics that defines the exact length of time required to recover the initial capital investment of a project from the net cash inflows (or gross savings) generated by that investment. In the specific context of solar energy systems, it represents the time period at the end of which the cumulative monetary savings from electricity bills, renewable energy generation, or fuel displacement exactly equals the upfront installation cost of the solar photovoltaic (PV), solar thermal, or hybrid system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** The Payback Period is the time required for the cumulative net cash flow from a solar energy project to become zero (i.e., the point where total savings equal the initial capital outlay). It is one of the simplest and most widely used investment appraisal techniques for renewable energy systems.

Mathematically, the **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** is expressed as:

$$PBP = \frac{\text{Total Initial Investment (Capital Cost)}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Inflow (Annual Savings)}}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you borrow **₹50,000** from a friend to install a small solar water heater for your home. This friend tells you that the solar water heater will save you **₹10,000 per year** on your electricity bill. The question is: "How many years will it take for me to pay back my friend?"

The answer is straightforward: **5 years** (₹50,000 ÷ ₹10,000 per year).

This "5 years" is exactly the **Payback Period**. It is essentially the *breakeven point in time* — the moment when the project has financially justified its own existence. After this point, every rupee saved is pure financial profit for the owner.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The shorter the payback period, the more financially attractive the solar energy project is considered by investors, banks, and government subsidy programs. KTU specifically emphasizes this metric because it directly answers the homeowner's first question: *"Kab wapas milega paisa?"* (When will I get my money back?)

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

In solar energy economics, the following standard metrics are universally accepted:

- **Initial Investment (I₀):** Measured in **₹ (Indian Rupees)**, **$ (US Dollars)**, or any local currency. Includes panels, inverters, mounting structures, BOS (Balance of System), labor, and grid-tie charges.
- **Annual Savings (S):** Measured in **₹/year**, **$/year**, or **kWh/year × tariff rate**.
- **Payback Period (PBP):** Measured in **years** (or sometimes months for very fast-return projects).
- **System Lifetime:** Typically **25 years** for crystalline silicon PV modules and **15–20 years** for inverters.
- **Average Solar Irradiance Reference:** **5 kWh/m²/day** (peak sun hours for Kerala, per NREL/MNRE data).

## 1.4 Why Payback Period Matters in Solar Energy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Payback period analysis is *mandatory* for:
> - Roof-top solar PV system design
> - Solar water heater installations
> - Solar pump sizing for agricultural applications
> - Industrial solar thermal projects
> - Hybrid renewable energy systems
>
> It forms the foundation for comparing different renewable energy options and is a prerequisite for KTU Module 4 numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Payback Period

There are two principal variations of the payback period used in solar energy engineering:

### 2.1.1 Simple Payback Period (SPP)
This method ignores the time value of money. It assumes that one rupee saved today has the same value as one rupee saved 10 years from now. While not financially rigorous, it is the most commonly used metric in the solar industry for quick, preliminary assessments.

**Assumptions:**
- Constant annual cash inflow (no degradation considered initially)
- No discounting of future savings
- Single, lump-sum initial investment
- No consideration of project salvage value

### 2.1.2 Discounted Payback Period (DPP)
This is a more sophisticated method that accounts for the **time value of money** using a discount rate (often the bank loan interest rate, typically 8–12% in India). It recognizes that money received in the future is worth less than money received today due to inflation and opportunity cost.

**Assumptions:**
- Cash inflows are discounted to present value
- Discount rate reflects opportunity cost of capital
- Useful for large-scale solar farms and industrial installations

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow

The payback period analysis follows a structured methodology:

1. **Determine the Initial Capital Investment (I₀):** Sum up all costs — modules, inverters, BOS, civil works, installation labor, grid interconnection, taxes, and financing costs.

2. **Calculate Annual Energy Generation (E):** Use the formula:
   $$E = P_{rated} \times PSH \times \eta_{system} \times 365$$
   where *P_rated* is the installed capacity in kW, *PSH* is Peak Sun Hours, and *η_system* is the overall system efficiency (typically 75–85%).

3. **Compute Annual Monetary Savings (S):**
   $$S = E \times \text{Electricity Tariff (₹/kWh)}$$
   For grid-tied systems, this is the displaced electricity cost. For off-grid systems, it is the displaced diesel generator cost (₹/liter × diesel consumption).

4. **Apply the Payback Formula:**
   $$PBP = \frac{I_0}{S}$$

5. **Account for System Degradation:** Solar panels degrade at approximately **0.5–0.8% per year**. For rigorous analysis, year-by-year cash flow must be calculated.

6. **Compare with Benchmark:** A solar PV system is considered financially attractive in India if the payback period is **≤ 5 years** for residential, **≤ 4 years** for commercial, and **≤ 6–7 years** for industrial systems.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The payback period is the **lingua franca** of the solar industry. It is used by:

- **MNRE (Ministry of New and Renewable Energy, Government of India):** To design subsidy structures and recovery periods.
- **Banks and NBFCs:** To determine loan eligibility and EMI structures for solar projects.
- **Corporate CFOs:** To justify capital expenditure proposals for rooftop solar installations.
- **Household consumers:** To compare solar vs. grid electricity cost over 25 years.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Benchmark (KTU Expected Value):** Modern rooftop solar PV systems in Kerala have a typical payback period of **4 to 6 years** (post-CFA subsidy), while the panels continue to generate electricity for **20+ years** beyond the payback period — meaning the remaining 19+ years represent essentially *free electricity*.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula Number** | **Equation** | **Description** | **Units** |
|--------------------|--------------|-----------------|-----------|
| 2.1 | $PBP = \frac{I_0}{S}$ | Simple Payback Period | Years |
| 2.2 | $E = P \times PSH \times \eta \times 365$ | Annual Energy Generation | kWh/year |
| 2.3 | $S = E \times T$ | Annual Savings (T = Tariff) | ₹/year |
| 2.4 | $PV_n = \frac{S_n}{(1+r)^n}$ | Present Value of Year-n Savings | ₹ |
| 2.5 | $DPP = \min \left\{ n \mid \sum_{k=1}^{n} PV_k \geq I_0 \right\}$ | Discounted Payback Period | Years |
| 2.6 | $LCoE = \frac{\sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{I_0 + O\&M_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}}$ | Levelized Cost of Energy | ₹/kWh |
| 2.7 | $S_{adjusted} = S \times (1-d)^n$ | Savings Adjusted for Degradation (d = annual degradation rate) | ₹/year |
| 2.8 | $NPV = -I_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{S_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | Net Present Value (for project viability check) | ₹ |

**Where:**
- $I_0$ = Initial capital investment (₹)
- $S$ = Annual net cash inflow / savings (₹/year)
- $P$ = Installed peak power (kWp)
- $PSH$ = Peak Sun Hours (hours/day)
- $\eta$ = System efficiency (dimensionless, 0–1)
- $T$ = Electricity tariff (₹/kWh)
- $r$ = Discount rate (decimal, e.g., 0.10 for 10%)
- $N$ = Project lifetime (years)
- $d$ = Annual panel degradation rate (typically 0.005)

## 2.5 Critical Notes on Formula Application

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget to subtract the **annual Operation & Maintenance (O&M) cost** from the gross savings. The correct formula is:
> $$S_{net} = S_{gross} - O\&M_{annual}$$
> Using gross savings will artificially shorten the payback period and is considered a valuation error by KTU examiners.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Fundamental Derivation of the Simple Payback Period

The Simple Payback Period is derived from the concept of **cumulative cash flow breakeven analysis**. Let us derive it rigorously from first principles.

### Derivation Step 1: Define the Cumulative Cash Flow Function

Let $I_0$ be the initial investment made at time $t = 0$ (a cash outflow, hence negative). Let $S$ be the uniform annual savings (cash inflow, positive) received at the end of each year. The cumulative net cash flow at the end of year $n$ is given by:

$$C(n) = -I_0 + \sum_{k=1}^{n} S = -I_0 + n \cdot S$$

### Derivation Step 2: Apply the Breakeven Condition

The payback period is defined as the smallest integer $n$ at which the cumulative cash flow becomes non-negative:

$$C(PBP) \geq 0 \quad \text{and} \quad C(PBP - 1) < 0$$

Substituting our cumulative cash flow equation:

$$-I_0 + PBP \cdot S \geq 0$$

### Derivation Step 3: Solve for PBP

$$PBP \cdot S \geq I_0$$

$$PBP \geq \frac{I_0}{S}$$

Since we want the minimum time for breakeven, the **Simple Payback Period** is:

$$\boxed{PBP = \frac{I_0}{S}}$$

### Derivation Step 4: Verification of Dimensional Consistency

The units check is a critical KTU evaluation step:

$$[PBP] = \frac{[\text{Rupees}]}{[\text{Rupees/Year}]} = \text{Year}$$

This confirms the dimensional validity of the formula. The KTU examiner will award full marks only if the student demonstrates this unit check.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Discounted Payback Period

The Simple Payback Period ignores the time value of money. For a rigorous analysis, we use the Discounted Payback Period.

### Derivation Step 1: Present Value of Future Cash Flows

A rupee received $n$ years in the future is worth less than a rupee today. The present value of $S$ received at the end of year $n$ is:

$$PV_n = \frac{S}{(1+r)^n}$$

### Derivation Step 2: Cumulative Discounted Cash Flow

$$C_{disc}(n) = -I_0 + \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{S}{(1+r)^k}$$

### Derivation Step 3: Discounted Payback Period

$$\boxed{DPP = \min \left\{ n \in \mathbb{Z}^+ \;\middle|\; \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{S}{(1+r)^k} \geq I_0 \right\}}$$

The DPP is always **greater than or equal to** the SPP because discounting reduces the value of future savings.

## 3.3 Comprehensive Worked Example (KTU Pattern Problem)

**Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):**

A homeowner in Kochi, Kerala, installs a **5 kWp** rooftop solar PV system. The total installed cost is **₹2,50,000** (after MNRE subsidy). The system has an overall efficiency of **80%**, and the location receives an average of **4.5 Peak Sun Hours per day**. The electricity tariff is **₹7.50 per kWh**. The annual O&M cost is **₹3,000**. The panel degradation rate is **0.5% per year**. Calculate the **Simple Payback Period** and the **Discounted Payback Period** (at a discount rate of 8%).

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Calculate Annual Energy Generation (E)**

$$E = P \times PSH \times \eta \times 365$$

$$E = 5 \text{ kWp} \times 4.5 \text{ h/day} \times 0.80 \times 365 \text{ days}$$

$$E = 5 \times 4.5 \times 0.80 \times 365$$

$$E = 6,570 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Gross Annual Savings**

$$S_{gross} = E \times T = 6,570 \times 7.50 = ₹49,275/\text{year}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Net Annual Savings (Subtracting O&M)**

$$S_{net} = S_{gross} - O\&M = 49,275 - 3,000 = ₹46,275/\text{year}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Simple Payback Period**

$$PBP = \frac{I_0}{S_{net}} = \frac{2,50,000}{46,275}$$

$$PBP = 5.402 \text{ years} \approx 5.4 \text{ years}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The homeowner recovers the entire ₹2,50,000 investment in approximately **5 years and 5 months**.

**Step 5: Discounted Payback Period Calculation**

We construct a year-by-year discounted cash flow table:

| **Year (n)** | $S_{net,n}$ (₹) | Discount Factor $\frac{1}{(1.08)^n}$ | Present Value $PV_n$ (₹) | Cumulative PV (₹) |
|--------------|------------------|--------------------------------------|---------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | 46,275 | 0.9259 | 42,847 | 42,847 |
| 2 | 46,275 | 0.8573 | 39,673 | 82,520 |
| 3 | 46,275 | 0.7938 | 36,734 | 1,19,254 |
| 4 | 46,275 | 0.7350 | 34,013 | 1,53,267 |
| 5 | 46,275 | 0.6806 | 31,494 | 1,84,761 |
| 6 | 46,275 | 0.6302 | 29,161 | 2,13,922 |
| 7 | 46,275 | 0.5835 | 27,001 | 2,40,923 |
| 8 | 46,275 | 0.5403 | 25,001 | 2,65,924 |

The cumulative present value exceeds ₹2,50,000 between year 7 and year 8. Linear interpolation:

$$DPP = 7 + \frac{2,50,000 - 2,40,923}{2,65,924 - 2,40,923}$$

$$DPP = 7 + \frac{9,077}{25,001}$$

$$DPP = 7 + 0.363 = 7.36 \text{ years}$$

**Conclusion:** The Discounted Payback Period is approximately **7.4 years**, which is **2 years longer** than the Simple Payback Period, reflecting the true economic reality when money has time value.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix for KTU Numerical Problems

| **Scenario** | **Use SPP When** | **Use DPP When** |
|--------------|------------------|------------------|
| Residential rooftop | Quick home assessment, short project life | Loan-financed purchase, high inflation |
| Commercial installation | Preliminary proposal submission | Bank loan appraisal, NPV-based decision |
| Industrial solar farm | Comparison of two technologies | Tender bidding, large capital outlay |
| Solar pump for agriculture | Farmer-friendly quick decision | Government subsidy program design |
| Solar water heater | Domestic application, ≤10 year life | Long-term hospitality/hospital project |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart: Payback Period Calculation Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Project Identified] --> B[Calculate Initial Investment I0]
    B --> C[Determine Installed Capacity in kWp]
    C --> D[Identify Peak Sun Hours PSH from MNRE Data]
    D --> E[Compute Annual Energy E = P x PSH x eta x 365]
    E --> F[Determine Electricity Tariff T in Rs per kWh]
    F --> G[Calculate Gross Annual Savings Sg = E x T]
    G --> H[Subtract Annual O and M Cost]
    H --> I[Net Annual Savings Sn = Sg - OandM]
    I --> J{Choose Method}
    J --> K[Simple Payback: PBP = I0 / Sn]
    J --> L[Discounted Payback: Year-wise PV calculation]
    K --> M[Compare with Benchmark 4 to 6 Years]
    L --> N[Find DPP using Interpolation]
    M --> O{Is PBP Acceptable?}
    N --> O
    O --> P[Yes: Project Approved]
    O --> Q[No: Redesign or Reject]
```

```mermaid

    end

    end

    subgraph OutputLayer[FINANCIAL DECISION METRICS]
        O1[Simple Payback Period in years]
        O2[Discounted Payback Period in years]

        O5[Investment Decision GO NO GO]
    end

    I1 --> P3
    I1 --> P4
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P1
    I4 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    I5 --> P2
    I7 --> P2
    I2 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
```
