
## 4.3 Cash Flow Timeline Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Year 0: Investment Outflow minus I0] -->|Capital deployed| B(Year 1: First Savings plus S)
    B --> C(Year 2: Cumulative minus I0 plus 2S)
    C --> D(Year 3: Cumulative minus I0 plus 3S)
    D --> E(Year N: Breakeven Point PBP)
    E --> F(Year N plus 1: Net Profit Begins)
    F --> G(Year 25: Total Lifetime Savings)
    style A fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style B fill:#4ecdc4,color:#fff
    style C fill:#4ecdc4,color:#fff
    style D fill:#4ecdc4,color:#fff
    style E fill:#ffe66d,color:#000
    style F fill:#95e1d3,color:#000
    style G fill:#38ada9,color:#fff
```

**Description of the Cash Flow Timeline:**

- **Red Zone (Year 0):** The homeowner invests the capital. Net cash flow is negative.
- **Teal Zone (Years 1 to PBP−1):** Annual savings accumulate, but the cumulative position is still negative.
- **Yellow Zone (PBP):** The breakeven point. Cumulative savings equal initial investment.
- **Green Zone (Years PBP+1 to 25):** Pure profit zone. The system generates "free" electricity for the remaining lifetime.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Define the term "Payback Period" as applied to solar energy systems. State its significance in evaluating a rooftop solar PV installation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Payback Period** is defined as the time required for the cumulative monetary savings from a solar energy system to equal the initial capital investment made on the system. Mathematically, it is the ratio of the initial investment (₹) to the annual net cash inflow (₹/year), giving the result in years.

**Significance:**
1. It is the simplest metric to assess the financial viability of a solar project.
2. A shorter payback period indicates a more attractive investment.
3. For Kerala rooftop solar systems, an acceptable payback period is typically 4 to 6 years, after which the system provides essentially free electricity for its remaining 20+ year lifetime.
4. It helps homeowners and investors compare solar energy with alternative investments.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**Distinguish between Simple Payback Period and Discounted Payback Period. Why is DPP always greater than or equal to SPP?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** | **Discounted Payback Period (DPP)** |
|------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Time value of money | Ignored | Considered via discount rate |
| Formula | $I_0 / S$ | Year-wise PV summation until breakeven |
| Complexity | Simple arithmetic | Requires iterative calculation |
| Result accuracy | Over-optimistic | Realistic, financially rigorous |

**Why DPP ≥ SPP:** Discounting reduces the present value of all future cash flows received after year 1. Since each future rupee is worth less in present terms, it takes *more years* of cumulative discounted cash flows to equal the initial investment, making DPP always equal to or longer than SPP.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** A 3 kWp rooftop solar PV system is installed at a residence in Thiruvananthapuram. The total cost is ₹1,80,000 after subsidy. Peak Sun Hours available are 4.8 hours/day. System efficiency is 78%. Electricity tariff is ₹6.50/unit. Annual maintenance cost is ₹2,500. Calculate the **Simple Payback Period**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Discounted Payback Period** with a suitable example. How does it differ from the Simple Payback Period in the context of solar energy investments? (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1:** Annual Energy Generation
$$E = 3 \text{ kWp} \times 4.8 \text{ h/day} \times 0.78 \times 365$$
$$E = 4,098.72 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 2:** Gross Annual Savings
$$S_{gross} = 4,098.72 \times 6.50 = ₹26,641.68/\text{year}$$

**Step 3:** Net Annual Savings
$$S_{net} = 26,641.68 - 2,500 = ₹24,141.68/\text{year}$$

**Step 4:** Simple Payback Period
$$PBP = \frac{1,80,000}{24,141.68} = 7.456 \text{ years} \approx 7.46 \text{ years}$$

**Answer:** The payback period is approximately **7.46 years** (7 years and 5.5 months).

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

The **Discounted Payback Period (DPP)** is the time required for the cumulative *present value* of future cash inflows to equal the initial investment, where future cash flows are discounted at a chosen rate (typically the cost of capital, 8–12% in India).

**Example:** Consider a ₹1,00,000 solar installation generating ₹25,000 annual savings. With SPP = 4 years, this looks attractive. However, if the discount rate is 10%, the present values are:
- Year 1: 25,000 / 1.10 = ₹22,727
- Year 2: 25,000 / 1.21 = ₹20,661
- Year 3: 25,000 / 1.331 = ₹18,783
- Year 4: 25,000 / 1.464 = ₹17,076
- **Cumulative at Year 4 = ₹79,247** (still short of ₹1,00,000)
- Year 5: 25,000 / 1.611 = ₹15,519 → **Cumulative = ₹94,766**
- Year 6: 25,000 / 1.772 = ₹14,108 → **Cumulative = ₹1,08,874** (breakeven)

So DPP = **5.6 years**, which is longer than the 4-year SPP.

**Key Difference:** DPP accounts for the fact that money received in the future is worth less today due to inflation and opportunity cost. SPP ignores this and overstates project attractiveness. For large solar investments financed by bank loans, DPP is the more accurate decision metric.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a neat cash flow diagram, explain the concept of **Payback Period** for a solar PV system. A 10 kWp commercial solar installation costs ₹4,50,000. The system generates 14,600 kWh/year. The commercial electricity rate is ₹9.00/kWh. Annual O&M is ₹5,000. Find the payback period. (7 Marks)

**(b)** What is **Net Present Value (NPV)**? How is it related to the payback period decision? If the discount rate is 9%, will the project in part (a) be financially viable over 25 years? (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Cash Flow Diagram (Conceptual):**

- **Year 0:** Cash outflow = −₹4,50,000
- **Years 1 to N:** Annual cash inflow = (14,600 × 9.00) − 5,000 = ₹1,31,400 − 5,000 = **₹1,26,400**
- **Breakeven Point:** Where cumulative cash flow crosses zero.

**Calculation:**

$$PBP = \frac{I_0}{S_{net}} = \frac{4,50,000}{1,26,400} = 3.56 \text{ years}$$

**Answer:** The Simple Payback Period is **3.56 years (≈ 3 years 7 months)**, which is excellent for a commercial solar system.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Net Present Value (NPV):** NPV is the difference between the present value of all cash inflows and the present value of all cash outflows over the project lifetime. A project is **financially viable** if NPV > 0.

$$NPV = -I_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{S_t}{(1+r)^t}$$

**Calculation for 25 years at r = 9%:**

The present value of an annuity of ₹1,26,400 for 25 years at 9% discount rate is:

$$PV_{annuity} = S_{net} \times \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-N}}{r}$$

$$PV_{annuity} = 1,26,400 \times \frac{1 - (1.09)^{-25}}{0.09}$$

$$PV_{annuity} = 1,26,400 \times \frac{1 - 0.11597}{0.09}$$

$$PV_{annuity} = 1,26,400 \times 9.8226 = ₹12,41,576$$

**NPV Calculation:**

$$NPV = -4,50,000 + 12,41,576 = ₹7,91,576$$

**Decision:** Since **NPV = ₹7,91,576 > 0**, the project is **highly financially viable**. The positive NPV indicates that the solar installation will generate ₹7,91,576 in present-value wealth over its 25-year lifetime beyond recovering the initial investment.

**Relation to Payback Period:** A project with a short payback period (3.56 years) typically also has a positive NPV, confirming its financial attractiveness. The payback period tells us *how quickly* the investment is recovered, while NPV tells us *how much total wealth* is created.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Payback Period Problems:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract O&M cost:** Students often use gross savings instead of net savings in the denominator, leading to an artificially short payback period. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
> 
> 2. **Wrong unit of PSH:** Peak Sun Hours must be in *hours per day*. Using annual values will give incorrect answers. **(−1 mark)**
> 
> 3. **Ignoring system efficiency:** The formula requires the *overall system efficiency* (typically 75–85%), not just the panel efficiency. **(−1 mark)**
> 
> 4. **Forgetting to convert to present value in DPP:** Simply dividing the initial cost by the nominal annual savings and applying the discount factor to the result is WRONG. You must discount each year's savings individually. **(−3 marks)**
> 
> 5. **No unit check:** KTU examiners specifically look for the dimensional analysis step $₹ / (₹/\text{year}) = \text{year}$. **(−0.5 marks)**
> 
> 6. **Confusing PBP with LCoE:** Payback period is in *years*; LCoE is in *₹/kWh*. Do not interchange them. **(−1 mark)**
> 
> 7. **Not stating the assumption of constant savings:** For SPP, explicitly state that annual cash flows are uniform. **(−0.5 marks)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Payback Period:**

- **Definition:** Payback Period is the time taken for cumulative savings to equal the initial investment.

- **Master Formula:** $PBP = I_0 / S_{net}$ (always use **net** savings after O&M deduction).

- **Energy Generation Formula:** $E = P \times PSH \times \eta \times 365$ — memorize this!

- **Two Variants:** SPP (ignores time value of money) and DPP (accounts for time value of money using discount rate).

- **DPP > SPP always:** Because discounting reduces the present value of future cash flows.

- **Kerala Reference Data:** PSH ≈ 4.5 to 5.0 hours/day; residential tariff ≈ ₹6.50 to ₹8.00/kWh.

- **Industry Benchmark:** Residential payback = 4 to 6 years; Commercial = 3 to 5 years; Industrial = 4 to 7 years.

- **Degradation Factor:** Solar panels lose 0.5% to 0.8% efficiency per year — account for this in long-term analysis.

- **System Efficiency:** Use 75% to 85% (includes inverter losses, wiring losses, dust, temperature derating).

- **Decision Rule:** Accept the solar project if PBP ≤ 6 years (residential), ≤ 4 years (commercial), and NPV > 0 over 25 years.

- **Always Show:** Unit analysis, assumption statement, and a final interpretation sentence in your answer.

- **Related Metrics to Know:** NPV (Net Present Value), IRR (Internal Rate of Return), LCoE (Levelized Cost of Energy), ROI (Return on Investment), BCR (Benefit-Cost Ratio).

- **Don't Confuse:** Payback Period is in *years*, not months. If your answer is in months, convert it (e.g., 5.4 years = 5 years 5 months).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relevance of Financial and Economic Feasibility Evaluation of Energy Technologies and Systems, Energy-Economy Interaction, Energy Policy Related Acts and Regulation. Economic Analysis for the Selection of Alternative Decisions and the Future of the Power Plants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET617 - SOLAR ENERGY CONSERVATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Relevance of Financial and Economic Feasibility Evaluation of Energy Technologies and Systems, Energy-Economy Interaction, Energy Policy Related Acts and Regulation. Economic Analysis for the Selection of Alternative Decisions and the Future of the Power Plants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Financial and Economic Feasibility Evaluation

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> *Financial feasibility* evaluates whether a project can be executed with the available capital, expected returns, and acceptable risk profile (firm-level, private investor perspective). *Economic feasibility* (also called *national economic analysis* or *social cost-benefit analysis*) evaluates the project from the perspective of the entire economy, including externalities, social costs, environmental impacts, employment generation, and import substitution.

**Key Distinction:**
- **Financial Analysis** → Cash inflows/outflows to the project owner; uses *market prices*, taxes, subsidies, and interest rates.
- **Economic Analysis** → Uses *shadow prices* (corrected for taxes, subsidies, distortions) to capture true resource cost to the nation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters for Solar Engineers:** Every solar PV plant (rooftop, utility-scale, off-grid) requires crores of rupees upfront. A wrong economic decision can wipe out investor capital. KTU examiners expect students to justify technology selection using *both* financial indicators (NPV, IRR, Payback) and economic indicators (LCOE, BC ratio, NPV at economic prices).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Mango Orchard Decision"**
> Imagine a farmer choosing between planting mango trees (₹5 lakh investment, 10-year payback, steady income) or renting out the land (₹50,000/year, no risk). *Financial analysis* = personal cash flow to the farmer. *Economic analysis* = what is the impact on the village (employment, food security, groundwater use, carbon footprint)?
> 
> A solar power plant is the same: financial analysis tells the promoter "will I make profit?" while economic analysis tells the nation "is this the best use of land, capital, and sunlight for India's welfare?"

### 1.3 Energy-Economy Interaction

The **energy-economy interaction** describes the two-way relationship between a country's energy supply and its Gross Domestic Product (GDP). Energy is both an *input* to economic production and an *output* of economic activity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definitions:**
> - **Energy Intensity (EI):** The amount of energy required to produce one unit of economic output.
>   $$EI = \frac{\text{Total Primary Energy Consumption (TPES)}}{\text{GDP}}$$
> - **Energy Elasticity (ε):** The percentage change in energy demand for a 1% change in GDP.
>   $$\varepsilon = \frac{\% \Delta \text{Energy Demand}}{\% \Delta \text{GDP}}$$
> - **Decoupling:** When GDP grows faster than energy consumption (EI falls) — a sign of an efficient, modern economy.

**India-Specific Context:** India has an energy elasticity of approximately **0.7 to 0.8**, meaning a 10% GDP growth requires only a 7–8% rise in energy consumption. Solar PV directly contributes to *decoupling* by providing high-output, low-fuel-cost electricity.

### 1.4 Energy Policy Related Acts and Regulation (India)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Indian Energy Policy & Regulatory Framework (High-Yield for KTU):**
> 
> | Act / Policy | Year | Key Provision |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Electricity Act** | 2003 | De-licensed generation; open access; mandatory SERC regulations |
> | **National Solar Mission (JNNSM)** | 2010 | Target of 100 GW solar by 2022 (achieved); 500 GW non-fossil by 2030 |
> | **National Tariff Policy** | 2016 (amended 2018) | Solar RPO must be met; prosumers allowed |
> | **Renewable Purchase Obligation (RPO)** | — | Obligates DISCOMs/open-access users to procure % from renewables |
> | **Renewable Energy Certificate (REC)** | 2010 (CERC) | Tradable certificate for RPO compliance |
> | **PM-KUSUM Scheme** | 2019 | Solar pumps for farmers; solarization of grid pumps |
> | **MNRE Guidelines** | Ongoing | Benchmark costs, CFA, tendering standards |
> | **CERC / SERC Tariff Regulations** | 2014–2024 | Determine solar tariff ceilings, single-axis tracker premiums, BESS adders |
> | **National Electricity Plan (NEP)** | 2023 | 500 GW RE; 86 GW solar addition by 2026–27 |
> | **Energy Conservation Act (amended)** | 2022 | Mandatory carbon trading; ECBC for buildings |
> | **Perform, Achieve, Trade (PAT)** | 2012 | Energy efficiency trading for industries |

**International Benchmark Standards:**
- **IRENA** — International Renewable Energy Agency
- **IEA** — International Energy Agency
- **World Bank ESMAP** — Energy Sector Management Assistance Program
- **UNFCCC / Paris Agreement** — NDC commitments driving solar adoption

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Time Value of Money (TVM) — Foundation of All Economic Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Foundation:**
> A rupee today is worth more than a rupee one year from now, because today's rupee can be invested to earn interest (or used to avoid borrowing at interest). This is the *Time Value of Money (TVM)* principle. All project appraisal formulas in this module are derived from TVM.

**Why it matters for Solar:**
A 1 MW solar plant incurs ₹4 crore capital cost in Year 0 but generates electricity revenue for 25 years. Comparing Year 0 outflow with Year 25 inflow *directly* is meaningless. TVM provides the *discounting* mechanism to make them comparable in **present-day rupees**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Project Cash Flow Analysis

1. **Identify all cash flows** — Capital expenditure (CapEx), operating expenditure (OpEx), revenue (electricity sale), salvage value, working capital.
2. **Construct the cash flow timeline** — Year 0 (investment) through project life *N* years.
3. **Select the discount rate (*r*)** — Either Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC) for financial analysis, or Social Discount Rate (SDR, typically 8–12% in India) for economic analysis.
4. **Apply the appropriate indicator** — NPV, IRR, BC ratio, LCOE, or Payback Period.
5. **Accept/Reject decision:**
   - NPV > 0 → Accept (financial)
   - IRR > hurdle rate → Accept
   - LCOE < prevailing grid tariff → Accept
6. **Conduct sensitivity analysis** — Vary discount rate, tariff, capacity utilization factor (CUF), cost overruns, O&M escalation.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield — Memorize These!)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All KTU-board-critical formulas in one table. Memorize units and sign conventions.**

| # | Indicator | Formula | Decision Rule | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Present Value (PV)** | $PV = \dfrac{FV}{(1+r)^n}$ | — | Convert future value to today's money |
| 2 | **Future Value (FV)** | $FV = PV \cdot (1+r)^n$ | — | Compound a present sum forward |
| 3 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | NPV $\geq$ 0 → Accept | Gold standard for project acceptance |
| 4 | **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | $\sum_{t=0}^{N} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t} = 0$ | IRR $\geq r_{hurdle}$ → Accept | Effective % yield of the project |
| 5 | **Simple Payback Period** | $PBP = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Inflow}}$ | Lower is better | Quick screening; ignores TVM |
| 6 | **Discounted Payback Period** | Time until $\sum \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} = 0$ | Lower is better | Accounts for TVM |
| 7 | **Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE)** | $LCOE = \dfrac{\sum_{t=0}^{N} \dfrac{(CapEx_t + OpEx_t)}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t=0}^{N} \dfrac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}}$ | LCOE $\leq$ tariff | Industry-standard metric |
| 8 | **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** | $BCR = \dfrac{\sum \dfrac{B_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum \dfrac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}}$ | BCR $\geq$ 1 → Accept | Often used in economic analysis |
| 9 | **Annuity Present Value Factor** | $APVF = \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-N}}{r}$ | — | Convert equal annual cash flows to PV |
| 10 | **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** | $CRF = \dfrac{r(1+r)^N}{(1+r)^N - 1}$ | — | Annualize a present sum |
| 11 | **Energy Intensity** | $EI = \dfrac{TPES}{GDP}$ | Lower is better | Macro indicator |
| 12 | **Energy Elasticity** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\%\Delta E}{\%\Delta GDP}$ | — | Macro indicator |
| 13 | **Capacity Utilization Factor (CUF)** | $CUF = \dfrac{\text{Actual Energy Generated (kWh)}}{P_{installed} \times 8760}$ | — | Solar plant performance metric |
| 14 | **Discount Factor (DF)** | $DF_t = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^t}$ | — | Multiplier for each year's cash flow |

**Where:**
- $CF_t$ = net cash flow in year $t$ (revenue – cost)
- $r$ = discount rate (decimal, e.g., 0.10 for 10%)
- $N$ = project life in years
- $E_t$ = electricity generated in year $t$ (kWh)
- $CapEx_t$ = capital expenditure in year $t$ (₹)
- $OpEx_t$ = operating expenditure in year $t$ (₹)

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Reminder for Students:**
> In KTU answer sheets, never write `\vert x \vert` for absolute value — use `|x|` in plain text, or `|x|` in math mode. Avoid vertical pipes inside table cells.

### 2.4 Why These Formulas Matter in Engineering Practice

- **PV installers (Rooftop/Utility):** Need **LCOE** to bid in SECI/NTPC Vidyut Vyapar Nigam (NVVN) tenders. India's solar tariffs have dropped from ₹17/kWh (2010) to ₹2.00–2.50/kWh (2023–24) — driven entirely by LCOE optimization.
- **DISCOMs & IPPs:** Use **NPV and IRR** to compare solar vs. coal vs. gas; payback period for rooftop owners.
- **Policy Makers (CEA, MNRE, CERC):** Use **BCR and Economic NPV** to justify subsidies under JNNSM/PM-KUSUM.
- **Banks/PFC/REC:** Use **DSCR (Debt Service Coverage Ratio)** — which is itself a cash-flow ratio between operating income and debt obligations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Deriving LCOE from First Principles (Most Frequently Asked Long-Answer Topic)

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Premise:** A solar plant produces $E_t$ kWh in year $t$ and incurs costs $(CapEx_t + OpEx_t)$. We want the *constant per-kWh cost* that, when applied to every kWh, exactly recovers all costs over the plant's life at discount rate $r$.

**Step 1 — Write total discounted cost (numerator of LCOE):**

$$LCOE_{num} = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{CapEx_t + OpEx_t}{(1+r)^t}$$

**Step 2 — Write total discounted electricity (denominator):**

$$LCOE_{den} = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}$$

**Step 3 — Define LCOE as the ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LCOE &= \frac{\sum_{t=0}^{N} \dfrac{CapEx_t + OpEx_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t=0}^{N} \dfrac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify for constant annual generation and constant OpEx (very common in KTU problems):**

Let $CapEx_0 = I$ (initial investment), $OpEx_t = O$ (constant), $E_t = E$ (constant), then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
LCOE_{num} &= I + \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{O}{(1+r)^t} = I + O \cdot APVF(r,N) \\
LCOE_{den} &= \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{E}{(1+r)^t} = E \cdot APVF(r,N)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute APVF and simplify:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LCOE &= \frac{I + O \cdot \left(\dfrac{1-(1+r)^{-N}}{r}\right)}{E \cdot \left(\dfrac{1-(1+r)^{-N}}{r}\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $r$ (CRF transformation):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LCOE &= \frac{I \cdot r + O \cdot \left(1-(1+r)^{-N}\right)}{E \cdot \left(1-(1+r)^{-N}\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Use Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) to absorb $I$ into annual terms:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CRF &= \frac{r(1+r)^N}{(1+r)^N - 1} \\
LCOE &= \frac{I \cdot CRF + O}{E}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final, KTU-Exam-Ready Form:**
> $$\boxed{LCOE = \frac{I \cdot CRF + O}{E}}$$
> This is the **most-cited formula** in the KTU Solar Energy syllabus. Memorize the derivation steps and the box.

**Interpretation:** $I \cdot CRF$ converts the upfront CapEx into an *equivalent annual cost*; $O$ is the actual annual OpEx; $E$ is the annual energy generated. The ratio gives ₹/kWh.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — LCOE of a 1 MW Solar Plant

**Problem Statement:**
> A 1 MW solar PV plant has an initial investment of ₹4 crore, annual O&M of ₹5 lakh (escalating 3% per year), and generates 15 lakh kWh/year. Discount rate is 10%, life 25 years. Compute LCOE assuming flat generation and a *levelized* OpEx of ₹5 lakh/year (for simplicity).

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify inputs:**
- $I = 4{,}00{,}00{,}000$ ₹ (₹4 crore)
- $O = 5{,}00{,}000$ ₹/year
- $E = 15{,}00{,}000$ kWh/year
- $r = 0.10$, $N = 25$

**Step 2 — Compute CRF:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CRF &= \frac{0.10 \times (1.10)^{25}}{(1.10)^{25} - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $(1.10)^{25}$:
$(1.10)^{10} = 2.5937$
$(1.10)^{20} = (2.5937)^2 = 6.7275$
$(1.10)^{25} = 6.7275 \times (1.10)^5 = 6.7275 \times 1.6105 = 10.835$

So:
$$
\begin{aligned}
CRF &= \frac{0.10 \times 10.835}{10.835 - 1} = \frac{1.0835}{9.835} = 0.11017
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute annualized CapEx:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I \cdot CRF &= 4 \times 10^7 \times 0.11017 = 44.07 \times 10^5 = ₹44.07 \text{ lakh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total annualized cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total} &= 44.07 + 5.00 = ₹49.07 \text{ lakh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — LCOE:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LCOE &= \frac{49.07 \times 10^5}{15 \times 10^5} = \frac{49.07}{15} = ₹3.27 \text{ per kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** A 1 MW solar plant in India typically has LCOE of ₹2.5–3.5/kWh. ₹3.27/kWh is realistic for a 2024-era plant with 15% CUF, 10% WACC, 25-year life. If the PPA tariff is ₹3.50/kWh, the project NPV is positive and IRR > 10%.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem — NPV Decision Between Two Alternatives

**Problem Statement:**
> A 100 kW rooftop solar system has two financing options:
> - **Option A (CAPEX):** Pay ₹40 lakh upfront, no loan. Annual savings = ₹6 lakh for 25 years, no salvage.
> - **Option B (Loan-financed RESCO):** Pay ₹0 upfront, pay ₹4.5 lakh/year for 25 years. Annual savings = ₹6.5 lakh for 25 years.
> - Discount rate = 10%. Recommend.

**Solution — Option A:**

**Step 1 — Cash flows:** $CF_0 = -40$ lakh; $CF_{1..25} = +6$ lakh

**Step 2 — PV of annual savings (annuity):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
APVF(10\%, 25) &= \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-25}}{0.10} = \frac{1 - 0.0923}{0.10} = 9.077
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — PV of savings:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_{savings} &= 6 \times 9.077 = ₹54.46 \text{ lakh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — NPV:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_A &= -40 + 54.46 = +₹14.46 \text{ lakh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Option B:**

**Step 1 — Cash flows:** $CF_0 = 0$; $CF_{1..25} = +6.5 - 4.5 = +2$ lakh/year

**Step 2 — NPV:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_B &= 0 + 2 \times 9.077 = +₹18.15 \text{ lakh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Decision:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_B - NPV_A &= 18.15 - 14.46 = +₹3.69 \text{ lakh}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Option B (RESCO / Loan-financed) is superior by ₹3.69 lakh, *despite* paying ₹4.5 lakh/year to the developer. **Why?** The owner conserves ₹40 lakh capital for other uses (opportunity cost at 10%).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Indicator

> [!NOTE]
> **When to use which indicator (a frequent KTU short-answer question):**
> 
> | Situation | Best Indicator | Reason |
> |---|---|---|
> | Comparing two mutually-exclusive alternatives with different lifetimes | NPV with LCM (least common multiple) repeat assumption | Ensures fair lifecycle comparison |
> | Quoting solar tariff in a tender | LCOE | Industry standard; reflects true unit cost |
> | Investor deciding whether to fund a project | IRR | Easy comparison with hurdle rate |
> | Cash-constrained small business | Simple Payback | Quick, intuitive |
> | Govt. policy / public project | BCR + Economic NPV | Includes social benefits |
> | Sensitivity to cost overruns | NPV with tornado chart | Captures which variable matters most |
> | Debt-financed project | DSCR + NPV | Banks require DSCR > 1.2 typically |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Differentiate between financial feasibility and economic feasibility of a solar energy project. Why are both required?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Financial Feasibility** evaluates a project from the perspective of the project owner / investor using *market prices*, taxes, financing costs (interest), and applicable subsidies. Indicators used are NPV, IRR, Payback, and LCOE — all in *market rupees*. It answers: *"Will the investor make money?"*
>
> **Economic Feasibility** (also called *national economic analysis* or *social cost-benefit analysis*) evaluates the project from the *whole-economy* perspective. It uses *shadow prices* (corrected for taxes, subsidies, market distortions), applies a *social discount rate*, and quantifies externalities (CO₂ avoided, jobs created, import substitution). Indicators used are Economic NPV, BCR, and Economic IRR. It answers: *"Is this the best use of national resources?"*
>
> **Why both are required:** A project may be financially attractive (high IRR) but economically poor (heavy subsidy, no net social benefit), or vice versa. For example, a remote off-grid solar project in a tribal village may have negative financial NPV (no revenue) but very high economic BCR (avoids diesel transport, reduces emissions, improves health and education). The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to appreciate this dual perspective, especially for **government-tendered solar plants** where both MNRE's CFA and CERC's tariff regulations apply. *(1.5 + 1 + 0.5 marks)*

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"Define LCOE. Why is it the most preferred indicator for solar power plants?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **LCOE (Levelized Cost of Energy)** is the per-unit (₹/kWh) cost of generating electricity from a power plant over its entire lifetime, calculated as the *discounted sum of all lifetime costs* divided by the *discounted sum of all lifetime electricity generated*.
>
> $$LCOE = \frac{\sum_{t=0}^{N} \dfrac{CapEx_t + OpEx_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t=0}^{N} \dfrac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}}$$
>
> **Why preferred for solar:**
> (a) **Technology-agnostic** — allows fair comparison between solar, coal, gas, wind, nuclear on a *per-kWh* basis.
> (b) **Captures full lifecycle** — includes CapEx, OpEx, fuel (zero for solar), and decommissioning.
> (c) **Discounting** — accounts for time value of money.
> (d) **Tender/bid standard** — CERC, SECI, and NVVN use LCOE-based tariff discovery; reverse-auction winners quote the lowest LCOE.
> (e) **Investor-friendly** — directly comparable with PPA tariff. *(1 + 2 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up the discount rate** — use WACC for financial, social discount rate (8–12%) for economic.
> 2. **Forgetting Year-0 cash flow** — many students miss $CF_0 = -I$ and compute only annual PVs.
> 3. **LCOE unit mismatch** — answer should be in ₹/kWh, not ₹/year.
> 4. **No sensitivity analysis** — KTU expects at least a brief mention of how NPV changes with CUF, tariff, or discount rate.
> 5. **Confusing energy elasticity and energy intensity** — these are distinct concepts; students often write one for the other.
> 6. **Forgetting salvage value** at Year $N$ in NPV computation.
> 7. **Conflating RPO and REC** — RPO is the obligation, REC is the tradable instrument.
> 8. **Payback period ≠ Discounted payback period** — simple payback ignores TVM; discounted does not.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With neat derivation, obtain the expression for the **Levelized Cost of Electricity (LCOE)** for a solar PV plant. State clearly all assumptions. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 500 kW rooftop solar PV system is proposed for a manufacturing unit. The data are:
- Capital cost: ₹1.5 crore
- Annual O&M: ₹1.8 lakh/year (constant for 25 years)
- Annual energy generation: 6.5 lakh kWh (CUF ≈ 14.85%)
- Discount rate: 9%
- Project life: 25 years
- Electricity tariff saved: ₹6.00/kWh

Compute (i) LCOE, (ii) NPV, (iii) IRR (qualitatively), (iv) Simple Payback Period, and (v) Recommend whether the unit should invest. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer to Question A:**

**(a) — Derivation of LCOE (7 marks):**

> **Assumptions:**
> 1. Discount rate *r* is constant over the project life.
> 2. Annual energy generation $E_t$ may be constant or may degrade (e.g., 0.5% per year for PV).
> 3. Annual OpEx may be constant or escalate at a known rate *g*; for KTU problems we assume constant OpEx unless specified.
> 4. CapEx is incurred at Year 0 (single investment at start).
> 5. No fuel cost (PV has no fuel).
> 6. Project horizon is *N* years; decommissioning cost or salvage value is included if any.

> **Step 1:** Write total discounted lifetime cost ($PV_{cost}$).
>
> $$PV_{cost} = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{CapEx_t + OpEx_t}{(1+r)^t}$$

> **Step 2:** Write total discounted lifetime energy ($PV_{energy}$).
>
> $$PV_{energy} = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}$$

> **Step 3:** By definition, LCOE is the constant per-kWh cost that recovers $PV_{cost}$:
>
> $$LCOE = \frac{PV_{cost}}{PV_{energy}} = \frac{\sum_{t=0}^{N} \dfrac{CapEx_t + OpEx_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t=0}^{N} \dfrac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}}$$

> **Step 4:** Simplifying for constant $E$ and constant $OpEx = O$, with $CapEx_0 = I$ only:
>
> $$\boxed{LCOE = \frac{I \cdot CRF + O}{E}, \quad CRF = \frac{r(1+r)^N}{(1+r)^N - 1}}$$

> **Step 5:** Interpretation — $I \cdot CRF$ annualizes the upfront CapEx; $O$ is annual OpEx; $E$ is annual energy; ratio = ₹/kWh. *(1 + 1.5 + 1.5 + 1.5 + 1.5 = 7 marks)*

**(b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

> **(i) LCOE:**
>
> Compute CRF: $r = 0.09$, $N = 25$, $(1.09)^{25} = 8.623$.
>
> $$CRF = \frac{0.09 \times 8.623}{8.623 - 1} = \frac{0.7761}{7.623} = 0.1018$$
>
> Annualized CapEx = $1.5 \times 10^7 \times 0.1018 = ₹15.27$ lakh/year.
>
> Annual OpEx = ₹1.8 lakh/year.
>
> Total annual cost = ₹17.07 lakh/year.
>
> $$LCOE = \frac{17.07 \times 10^5}{6.5 \times 10^5} = ₹2.626 \text{ per kWh}$$

> **(ii) NPV:**
>
> Annual cash saving = $6.5 \times 10^5 \times 6 = ₹39$ lakh/year.
>
> APVF(9%, 25) = $\frac{1 - (1.09)^{-25}}{0.09} = \frac{1 - 0.1160}{0.09} = 9.823$
>
> PV of savings = $39 \times 9.823 = ₹383.1$ lakh.
>
> $$NPV = 383.1 - 150 = +₹233.1 \text{ lakh} = ₹2.33 \text{ crore}$$
>
> Strongly positive NPV → project is **financially viable**.

> **(iii) IRR (qualitative):** Since NPV at 9% discount rate is *strongly positive* (+₹2.33 crore), IRR must be **substantially higher** than 9%, likely in the range of **20–25%**, well above WACC. *(1 mark)*

> **(iv) Simple Payback Period:**
>
> $$PBP = \frac{1.5 \text{ crore}}{39 \text{ lakh/year}} = \frac{150}{39} = 3.85 \text{ years} \approx 3 \text{ years, 10 months}$$

> **(v) Recommendation:** All four indicators are positive and well within the project's attractive range. The unit **should invest** in the rooftop solar system. Additional benefits (not monetized here): reduced carbon footprint, energy security, peak-load reduction, 40% accelerated depreciation tax shield, and potential REC income. *(1 mark)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Energy-Economy Interaction** in detail. Define *Energy Intensity* and *Energy Elasticity*. Discuss how solar energy contributes to *decoupling* in the Indian context. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the major **energy policy acts and regulations** in India governing solar power. Explain the role of **CERC, SERC, MNRE, JNNSM, RPO, and REC** in promoting solar energy. Briefly outline the *future of power plants* in India. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer to Question B:**

**(a) — Energy-Economy Interaction (7 marks):**

> **Conceptual Framework:** Energy and the economy are linked in a *feedback loop*. Economic growth (GDP rise) → increased industrial, agricultural, and domestic activity → higher energy demand → increased power generation → emissions and resource depletion. Conversely, energy supply constraints → higher prices → inflation → slower GDP growth. The strength of this linkage is measured by **Energy Intensity (EI)** and **Energy Elasticity (ε)**. *(2 marks)*

> **Energy Intensity (EI):**
>
> $$EI = \frac{\text{Total Primary Energy Supply (TPES) [in toe or MJ]}}{\text{GDP [in ₹ or US\$]}}$$
>
> EI = energy needed to produce one unit of economic output. India's EI ≈ **0.20 kgoe/US\$ (2023)** — much higher than the global average of **0.18** and the OECD's **0.13**. Higher EI = energy-inefficient economy. *(1.5 marks)*

> **Energy Elasticity (ε):**
>
> $$\varepsilon = \frac{\%\Delta \text{ Energy Demand}}{\%\Delta \text{ GDP}}$$
>
> India has ε ≈ **0.7–0.8** (2010–2023) — meaning 1% GDP growth demands only 0.7–0.8% more energy. This is *less than 1* (inelastic), indicating an *improvement* in energy efficiency. *(1.5 marks)*

> **Decoupling & Role of Solar:** Decoupling occurs when GDP grows *faster* than energy consumption (i.e., EI falls over time). Solar PV contributes to decoupling in three ways: (i) it provides *high-capacity, low-fuel-cost* electricity, reducing marginal energy cost; (ii) it displaces coal-based generation, reducing both primary energy use and emissions; (iii) rooftop solar avoids transmission losses, lowering the energy needed per unit of delivered electricity. India's *green GDP* (GDP from renewables) is growing at 8–10% CAGR, accelerating decoupling. *(2 marks)*

**(b) — Energy Policy Acts & Future of Power Plants (7 marks):**

> **Electricity Act 2003:** The umbrella legislation that *de-licensed* power generation, mandated *open access* to the transmission/distribution network, and required each state to set up a *State Electricity Regulatory Commission (SERC)*. Crucial for IPP entry. *(1 mark)*

> **CERC (Central Electricity Regulatory Commission):** Sets the *inter-state* generation/transmission tariff guidelines, including the *Generic Tariff for Solar* (e.g., CERC Order dated 30.03.2023 set the benchmark rooftop solar tariff at ₹3.00–3.50/kWh, varying by category). CERC also issues **REC regulations** and regulates the *Power Exchange* (IEX, PXIL). *(1 mark)*

> **SERC (State Electricity Regulatory Commissions):** Each state's regulator (KSERC in Kerala, RERC in Rajasthan, etc.) sets *intra-state* tariffs, approves PPAs, and enforces RPO at the state level. *(0.5 mark)*

> **MNRE (Ministry of New and Renewable Energy):** Apex nodal ministry. Implements schemes like **JNNSM (Jawaharlal Nehru National Solar Mission)** — launched 2010 with a 100 GW target by 2022 (achieved in January 2021, ahead of schedule), revised to **500 GW non-fossil by 2030** under the Panchamrit declaration at COP-26. MNRE also handles PM-KUSUM, CPSU Scheme, and Rooftop Solar Phase-II. *(1.5 marks)*

> **RPO & REC:** **Renewable Purchase Obligation** is a mandate on DISCOMs and large consumers to procure a minimum % of their energy from renewables (currently ~29% for FY 2024–25, going up). **Renewable Energy Certificates** are the tradable compliance instruments issued by NLDC and traded on IEX/PXIL — one REC = 1 MWh of RE injected. RPO non-compliance penalty: ₹0.25–1.00/kWh shortfall under CERC forgoing charges. *(1.5 marks)*

> **Future of Power Plants in India:** Three distinct horizons. (1) **Near-term (2024–30):** 500 GW RE, BESS (Battery Energy Storage Systems) becoming central; hybrid solar-wind-storage tenders at SECI. (2) **Medium-term (2030–40):** Storage-dominant grid; **green hydrogen** production from solar PV; coal capacity operating at 40–50% PLF as "peakers." (3) **Long-term (2040–70):** Coal nearly phased out from baseload; **100% clean grid** with solar + storage + hydro; integration with EV-to-grid (V2G); Power-to-X (P2X) for synthetic fuels. AI-based dispatch, blockchain RECs, and *peer-to-peer* (P2P) solar trading through distribution licensees will dominate. *(1.5 marks)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory 2-minute KTU Exam Revision Checklist (Module 4 Economic Analysis):**
> 
> - [ ] **Time Value of Money (TVM):** PV, FV, APVF, CRF, discount factor — know all five formulas and their inverses.
> - [ ] **NPV formula:** Sum of discounted cash flows; NPV > 0 → Accept.
> - [ ] **IRR:** Discount rate that makes NPV = 0; IRR > hurdle → Accept.
> - [ ] **Payback Period:** Initial investment ÷ annual net cash inflow (simple); uses discounting (discounted payback).
> - [ ] **LCOE formula (Box formula):** $\dfrac{I \cdot CRF + O}{E}$ — must be in ₹/kWh.
> - [ ] **BCR:** Sum PV of benefits ÷ sum PV of costs; > 1 → Accept.
> - [ ] **CRF formula:** $\dfrac{r(1+r)^N}{(1+r)^N - 1}$ — must know derivation and computation.
> - [ ] **Energy Intensity:** TPES/GDP; lower is better; India is more intensive than OECD.
> - [ ] **Energy Elasticity:** %ΔE / %ΔGDP; < 1 implies decoupling; India ~ 0.7–0.8.
> - [ ] **CUF of solar plant in India:** Typically 15–22% (Rajasthan highest, Kerala ~ 16%).
> - [ ] **Electricity Act 2003:** De-licensed generation, open access, SERC framework.
> - [ ] **JNNSM (2010):** 100 GW solar; expanded to 500 GW non-fossil by 2030 (COP-26).
> - [ ] **RPO:** Obligation on DISCOMs to buy % from RE; non-compliance = penalty.
> - [ ] **REC:** Tradable certificate; 1 REC = 1 MWh; traded on IEX/PXIL.
> - [ ] **CERC vs. SERC:** Central vs. State regulatory jurisdiction.
> - [ ] **MNRE:** Nodal ministry; implements JNNSM, PM-KUSUM, rooftop phases.
> - [ ] **PM-KUSUM:** Solar pumps + solarization of grid pumps + agri-feeder.
> - [ ] **LCOE of Indian solar (2024):** ~ ₹2.5–3.5/kWh; tenders discovered tariff ₹2.00–2.50/kWh (Bhadla, Pavagada).
> - [ ] **Future power plant = Solar + Storage + Green H₂** (P2X).
> - [ ] **Decoupling** = GDP grows without proportional energy growth — solar's biggest macro contribution.
> - [ ] **Sensitivity analysis** — always test ±20% on tariff, CUF, CapEx, and discount rate.
> - [ ] **Sunk cost** is *never* included in NPV; only incremental future cash flows.
> - [ ] **WACC** for financial analysis; **SDR (8–12%)** for economic analysis.
> - [ ] **Shadow prices** strip out taxes, subsidies, and market distortions for economic analysis.
> - [ ] **Externalities:** CO₂ avoided = ~0.7 kg/kWh for coal displaced; social cost of carbon ~ US\$ 50/tCO₂ (World Bank).
> - [ ] **DSCR** = Operating cash flow ÷ Debt service; banks require DSCR > 1.2 for project loans.
> - [ ] **Discounted vs. Simple Payback** — always state which one you are computing.
> - [ ] **NEP 2020 alignment:** Module 4 directly supports KTU outcome CO4 (engineering economics) and CO5 (sustainability & environment).

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy Tip:**
> In a 14-mark question on this module, the examiner typically allocates ~7 marks to **derivation** (LCOE or NPV) and ~7 marks to **numerical application** or **policy discussion**. Always begin long answers with the *definition*, then state *assumptions*, then *derive*, then *apply*. This structure fetches full marks.
